topics.title: Exercises on Lathe: Plain and Step Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Lathe: Plain and Step Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **LATHE**: A machine tool primarily used for shaping cylindrical workpieces by rotating the workpiece about a fixed axis and advancing a cutting tool against it, producing surfaces of revolution (cones, cylinders, flat faces, threads, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLAIN TURNING (Cylindrical Turning)**: A turning operation in which the diameter of a workpiece is reduced uniformly over a specified length by feeding the cutting tool parallel to the axis of rotation. The output is a smooth cylindrical surface of smaller, constant diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STEP TURNING**: A variant of plain turning in which multiple diameters are produced along the length of the workpiece by axially feeding the tool at different radial positions, creating a series of "steps" (shoulders and lands) of varying diameters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine holding a carrot against a vegetable peeler and rotating it slowly — the peeler shaves off a thin, uniform layer along the length, leaving a thinner, smooth cylinder. That is **plain turning** in essence.

Now imagine a woodworker who needs a wooden handle for a tool — the handle has a thick part for grip, a thinner neck, and a small diameter tip. He rotates the wood and shaves different diameters at different sections. That is **step turning** — like a staircase wrapped around a cylinder.

> [!TIP]
> Think of plain turning as a "single-shave" operation, and step turning as "multiple shaves at different depths along the length" — the lathe provides rotation (like the spinning carrot), and the tool provides the radial cut.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The following parameters govern every turning operation and are **bolded** as standard KTU metrics:

- **Spindle Speed (N)** — measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**
- **Cutting Speed (V)** — measured in **m/min (meters per minute)**
- **Feed (f)** — measured in **mm/rev (millimetres per revolution)**
- **Depth of Cut (d or aₚ)** — measured in **mm**
- **Machining Time (Tₘ)** — measured in **minutes**
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** — measured in **mm³/min**
- **Surface Roughness (Ra)** — measured in **µm**

### 1.4 Tools & Equipment Used in Lab

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| 3-jaw / 4-jaw Chuck | Holds and rotates the workpiece |
| Dead Centre / Live Centre | Supports long workpieces on the tailstock side |
| HSS / Carbide Tool Bit | Performs the actual material removal |
| Tool Post (single or multi) | Holds the cutting tool rigidly |
| Cross-slide & Carriage | Provide axial (longitudinal) feed |
| Compound Slide | Allows angular feed for chamfers and tapers |
| Vernier Caliper / Micrometer | Measure the turned diameter |
| Surface Roughness Tester | Measures Ra value of machined surface |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Plain Turning

1. **Workpiece Mounting**: The cylindrical job is gripped in a chuck (3-jaw for round, 4-jaw for irregular). Long jobs are additionally supported by a tailstock centre.
2. **Tool Setting**: The HSS/carbide tool is clamped in the tool post, with the cutting edge set exactly on the centre line of the workpiece (lathe centre height).
3. **Spindle Rotation**: The chuck rotates the workpiece at a pre-calculated spindle speed derived from the recommended cutting speed.
4. **Engagement**: The cross-slide is used to bring the tool into contact with the workpiece radially — this is the **depth of cut (d)**.
5. **Traverse Feed**: The carriage is engaged for automatic axial feed at the specified feed rate (mm/rev).
6. **Completion**: After traversing the required length, the tool is retracted radially using the cross-slide.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Why"** of each step: Rotation provides a continuous cut, the tool removes a chip of constant cross-section, axial feed produces a helical chip and a uniform cylindrical surface.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Step Turning

Step turning follows the same logic as plain turning, but the tool is fed axially to a *new radial position* (via the cross-slide) before each axial pass.

**Sequence of Operations:**
- Step 1: Reduce diameter from D₀ to D₁ over length L₁ (first step)
- Step 2: Retract tool, reposition to L₂, reduce from D₁ to D₂ (second step)
- Step 3: Continue until all steps are produced, ending with a face cut at the end

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In step turning, the **spindle speed is often changed** at each step because the recommended cutting speed is constant but the diameter changes: $N \propto 1/D$.

### 2.3 Why Machining Time & MRR Matter

- **Machining Time (Tₘ)** directly determines **production cost and throughput** in any manufacturing industry.
- **MRR** indicates **machine tool productivity** — a higher MRR means more material is removed per minute, lowering cost per part.
- These two metrics are universally used in KTU university problems.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **mandatory recall set** for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the relationships, not just the formulas.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | D in mm, N in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Inverse relation with D |
| 3 | Feed Rate | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | Longitudinal traverse speed |
| 4 | Machining Time (plain) | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | min | L = length of cut |
| 5 | Volume Removed | $V_{rem} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_1^2 - D_2^2\right)\cdot L$ | mm³ | For a single diameter reduction |
| 6 | MRR (exact) | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_1^2 - D_2^2\right)\cdot f \cdot N$ | mm³/min | Direct from Volume/Time |
| 7 | MRR (approx) | $MRR \approx \pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N$ | mm³/min | $D_{avg} = (D_1+D_2)/2$, $d=(D_1-D_2)/2$ |
| 8 | Total Machining Time (step turning) | $T_{total} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{f_i \cdot N_i}$ | min | Sum over each step |
| 9 | Power | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot \text{Unit Power}}{60}$ | Watts | Unit Power in N·m/mm³ |
| 10 | Feed Force (approx) | $F_f = K_s \cdot f \cdot d$ | N | $K_s$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |

**Note on notation:** In all formulas above, the symbol `\vert` (or `\mid`) may be used to indicate absolute value where needed, e.g., $\vert D_1 - D_2 \vert$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry**: Crankshafts, camshafts, and axles are produced by plain + step turning on CNC lathes.
- **Aerospace**: Landing gear pins, hydraulic pistons — high-precision plain turning with Ra < 0.8 µm.
- **Valves and Pumps**: Step-turned shafts hold bearings, seals, and impellers at discrete diameters.
- **Tool-room practice**: Every KTU lab student must master this before graduating, since it is the foundation of all rotational machining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Cutting Speed (V) from Spindle Speed (N)

**Starting Concept:** Cutting speed is the linear velocity at which the workpiece surface moves past the tool tip. For one revolution, the surface travels a distance equal to the circumference of the workpiece.

**Step 1 — Distance per revolution:**
$$d_{rev} = \pi \cdot D \quad \text{(mm per revolution)}$$

**Step 2 — Distance per minute (multiply by N revolutions):**
$$d_{min} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(mm per minute)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert mm to metres (÷ 1000):**
$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

**Inversion — Spindle Speed for a given V:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$$

> **Engineering logic:** If diameter doubles, spindle speed must halve to keep cutting speed constant — this is why operators must change the gear/pulley setting each time the diameter changes in step turning.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Machining Time for Plain Turning

**Starting Concept:** Machining time = length of cut ÷ axial feed rate.

**Step 1 — Axial feed per revolution = f (mm/rev).**

**Step 2 — Axial feed per minute = f · N (mm/min).**

**Step 3 — Time to cover length L:**
$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{(min)}$$

> **Engineering logic:** Higher spindle speed and/or higher feed directly reduces time, but at the cost of surface finish and tool life — the classic KTU trade-off.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR)

**Starting Concept:** MRR is the volume of material removed per unit time.

**Step 1 — Cross-section of the removed layer (a ring of width f and radial depth d):**
$$A_{cs} = f \cdot d \quad \text{(mm²)}$$

**Step 2 — Per revolution, the tool sweeps a helical ring of cross-section A_cs over a length of pitch f. Approximating using average diameter D_avg:**
$$V_{rev} \approx \pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot d \cdot f \quad \text{(mm³ per rev)}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by N revolutions per minute:**
$$MRR \approx \pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N \quad \text{(mm³/min)}$$

**Step 4 — Exact form using D₁ and D₂:**
$$MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_1^2 - D_2^2\right)\cdot f \cdot N \quad \text{(mm³/min)}$$

> **Verification of equivalence:** $\dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_1^2 - D_2^2) = \pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot d$, since $(D_1^2 - D_2^2) = (D_1+D_2)(D_1-D_2) = 2 D_{avg} \cdot 2d = 4 D_{avg} \cdot d$.

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem Statement (Model KTU 2024 Style):**
A mild steel workpiece of initial diameter **60 mm** and length **200 mm** is to be reduced to **52 mm** by plain turning on a centre lathe. Recommended cutting speed = **30 m/min** and feed = **0.25 mm/rev**. Calculate:
1. Spindle speed
2. Machining time
3. Material removal rate (using exact formula)

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 60$ mm
- $D_2 = 52$ mm
- $L = 200$ mm
- $V = 30$ m/min
- $f = 0.25$ mm/rev

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Spindle Speed (N):**
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D_1} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{30000}{188.496}$$

$$N \approx 159.15 \text{ rpm} \rightarrow \text{select 160 rpm on machine}$$

**Part (b) — Machining Time (Tₘ):**
$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} = \frac{200}{0.25 \times 160} = \frac{200}{40}$$

$$T_m = 5.0 \text{ minutes}$$

**Part (c) — Material Removal Rate (MRR):**
$$MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_1^2 - D_2^2\right)\cdot f \cdot N$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{4}\left(60^2 - 52^2\right)\times 0.25 \times 160$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{4}\left(3600 - 2704\right)\times 40$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{4}\left(896\right)\times 40$$

$$= \pi \times 224 \times 40 = \pi \times 8960$$

$$MRR \approx 28148.0 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 28.15 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Verification using approximate formula:**
$$D_{avg} = \frac{60+52}{2} = 56 \text{ mm}, \quad d = \frac{60-52}{2} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

$$MRR \approx \pi \times 56 \times 4 \times 0.25 \times 160 = \pi \times 8960 \approx 28148 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> The two formulas give identical numerical results — this is your **self-check** in the exam hall.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Parameter

| Parameter ↑ (Increase) | N (for constant V) | Tₘ | MRR | Surface Finish | Tool Wear |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Diameter (D) | Decreases | Increases (if N not adjusted) | Decreases | Improves (lower N) | Decreases |
| Cutting Speed (V) | Increases | Decreases | Increases | Worsens (Ra up) | Increases (sharp) |
| Feed (f) | No direct effect | Decreases sharply | Increases linearly | Worsens (Ra up) | Slight increase |
| Depth of Cut (d) | No direct effect | No direct effect | Increases linearly | Minor effect | Increases (more load) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Lathe for Plain Turning

The following Mermaid block renders a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow showing how the major sub-systems of a centre lathe interact during a plain turning operation. (Per protocol safeguard, all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text.)

### 4.2 Step Turning Operation Sequence

The following diagram maps the procedural sequence for producing a multi-step shaft. Each block represents an action by the operator or the lathe.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[START JOB IN CHUCK] --> S1[FACE THE RIGHT END]
    S1 --> S2[TURN DIAMETER D1 OVER LENGTH L1]
    S2 --> S3[REPOSITION CROSS SLIDE TO D2]
    S3 --> S4[TURN DIAMETER D2 OVER LENGTH L2]
    S4 --> S5[REPOSITION CROSS SLIDE TO D3]
    S5 --> S6[TURN DIAMETER D3 OVER LENGTH L3]
    S6 --> S7[REPOSITION CROSS SLIDE TO D4]
    S7 --> S8[TURN DIAMETER D4 OVER LENGTH L4]
    S8 --> S9[CHAMFER EDGES IF REQUIRED]
    S9 --> S10[REMOVE JOB AND MEASURE WITH MICROMETER]
    S10 --> S11{ALL DIMENSIONS WITHIN TOLERANCE}
    S11 --> S12[YES JOB ACCEPTED END]
    S11 --> S13[NO RECUT AND REPEAT]
    S13 --> S2

    style S0 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S3 fill:#B0C4DE
    style S4 fill:#FFCDD2

    style S6 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S7 fill:#B0C4DE
    style S8 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S9 fill:#B0C4DE
    style S10 fill:#FFD580
    style S11 fill:#E0E0E0
    style S12 fill:#A5D6A7

```

### 4.3 Tool–Workpiece Geometry (Conceptual Schematic)

The following diagram illustrates the geometric relationship between the cutting tool, the rotating workpiece, and the depth of cut — a standard depiction in KTU theory questions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[WORKPIECE INITIAL DIAMETER D1] -->|RADIAL ENGAGEMENT| P2[DEPTH OF CUT d]
    P2 --> P3[WORKPIECE FINAL DIAMETER D2]
    T1[CUTTING TOOL TIP] -->|AXIAL FEED f mm/rev| P2
    T1 -->|TRAVELS LENGTH L| P4[MACHINED SURFACE]
    P1 --> P5[CHIP FORMED HELICALLY]
    P3 --> P4

    style P1 fill:#FFD580
    style P2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style P3 fill:#A5D6A7
    style T1 fill:#B0C4DE
    style P4 fill:#A5D6A7
    style P5 fill:#E0E0E0
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU theory papers, students are often asked to **sketch the geometry** of turning showing depth of cut (d), feed (f), cutting speed direction (tangential), and chip flow direction. The above block diagram is a functional equivalent for visual reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between plain turning and step turning with a suitable sketch.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Plain Turning | Step Turning |
|---|---|---|
| Output Geometry | Single uniform diameter | Multiple diameters in series |
| Cross-slide Use | Once (initial set) | Multiple times (per step) |
| Applications | Shafts, pins, spacers | Spindles, axles, stepped shafts |
| Tool Feed Direction | One axial pass | Multiple axial passes at different depths |

**Sketch (textual):** Draw a horizontal cylinder, label initial diameter D₁ and final diameter D₂ with depth of cut d = (D₁ − D₂)/2 indicated radially.

*(1 mark for clear difference, 1 mark for application, 1 mark for sketch.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Define cutting speed, feed, and depth of cut in a lathe turning operation. State their units.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Cutting Speed (V):** The relative surface speed between the tool and the workpiece at the cutting point, measured in **m/min**. Formula: $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$.
- **Feed (f):** The distance the tool advances per revolution of the workpiece, measured in **mm/rev**.
- **Depth of Cut (d):** The radial distance the tool penetrates below the original workpiece surface, measured in **mm**.

*(1 mark per definition with units.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A mild steel shaft of 80 mm diameter and 350 mm length is to be turned to 72 mm diameter on a lathe. The recommended cutting speed is 25 m/min and feed is 0.3 mm/rev. Calculate:
   1. Spindle speed in rpm
   2. Machining time in minutes
   3. Material removal rate in mm³/min

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the geometry of plain turning. Indicate the direction of cutting speed, feed, depth of cut, and chip flow.

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Solution:**

**Given:** $D_1 = 80$ mm, $D_2 = 72$ mm, $L = 350$ mm, $V = 25$ m/min, $f = 0.3$ mm/rev.

**(1) Spindle Speed:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D_1} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{25000}{251.327} \approx 99.47 \text{ rpm}$$

Select **N = 100 rpm** on the machine. *(3 marks)*

**(2) Machining Time:**
$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} = \frac{350}{0.3 \times 100} = \frac{350}{30} \approx 11.67 \text{ min}$$ *(2 marks)*

**(3) Material Removal Rate:**
$$MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_1^2 - D_2^2\right) \cdot f \cdot N = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(80^2 - 72^2\right)\times 0.3 \times 100$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{4}\left(6400 - 5184\right)\times 30 = \frac{\pi}{4}\times 1216 \times 30 = \pi \times 9120$$

$$MRR \approx 28650 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 28.65 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$ *(2 marks)*

**(b) Solution:**

**Sketch Description (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
       ┌───────────────┐  ← Workpiece rotating
       │  Workpiece    │  ← D₁ (initial diameter)
       │  ──────────── │  ← D₂ (final diameter)
       │   d = (D₁-D₂)/2│  ← Depth of cut (radial)
       └───────────────┘
              ▲
              │ f (axial feed direction)
              │
          [Tool Tip]
              
   → Direction of cutting speed (tangential to workpiece)
   → Chip flows upward along the rake face of the tool
```

**Explanation:**
- **Cutting speed (V):** Tangential direction at the workpiece surface, magnitude $V = \pi D N / 1000$.
- **Feed (f):** Axial direction, along the length of the workpiece, magnitude in mm/rev.
- **Depth of cut (d):** Radial direction, magnitude $(D_1 - D_2)/2$.
- **Chip flow:** Along the rake face of the tool, generally upward and curling away from the workpiece.

*(Full marks for clear labelled sketch + 4 correct arrow directions + correct units.)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A stepped shaft is to be produced with the following dimensions from a 70 mm diameter bar of length 400 mm:
   - Step 1: Reduce to 60 mm diameter for 150 mm length
   - Step 2: Reduce to 50 mm diameter for 120 mm length
   - Step 3: Reduce to 40 mm diameter for 100 mm length

   The cutting speed is 28 m/min and feed is 0.25 mm/rev. Calculate the total machining time, taking into account the change in spindle speed for each step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** List the various machining parameters that affect surface roughness in plain turning. Explain the effect of feed on surface roughness with a suitable relationship.

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Solution:**

**Given:** Bar diameter $D_0 = 70$ mm, total length $L = 400$ mm (sum of all steps = 150+120+100 = 370 mm turning + 30 mm allowance for facing).
- $V = 28$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev.

**Step 1: Spindle speed for each diameter:**
$$N_1 = \frac{1000 \times 28}{\pi \times 70} = \frac{28000}{219.91} \approx 127.32 \text{ rpm} \rightarrow 127 \text{ rpm}$$

$$N_2 = \frac{1000 \times 28}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{28000}{188.50} \approx 148.54 \text{ rpm} \rightarrow 149 \text{ rpm}$$

$$N_3 = \frac{1000 \times 28}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{28000}{157.08} \approx 178.25 \text{ rpm} \rightarrow 178 \text{ rpm}$$

$$N_4 = \frac{1000 \times 28}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{28000}{125.66} \approx 222.82 \text{ rpm} \rightarrow 223 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Machining time for each step:**
$$T_1 = \frac{150}{0.25 \times 127} = \frac{150}{31.75} \approx 4.72 \text{ min}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{120}{0.25 \times 149} = \frac{120}{37.25} \approx 3.22 \text{ min}$$

$$T_3 = \frac{100}{0.25 \times 178} = \frac{100}{44.50} \approx 2.25 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3: Total time:**
$$T_{total} = 4.72 + 3.22 + 2.25 \approx 10.19 \text{ min}$$

*(7 marks: 3 for spindle speed calculations, 3 for individual time, 1 for total.)*

**(b) Solution:**

**Parameters affecting surface roughness (Ra) in turning:**

1. **Feed (f)** — *most significant*
2. **Tool nose radius (r)** — larger nose radius improves finish
3. **Cutting speed (V)** — secondary effect
4. **Built-up edge (BUE) formation** — at low speeds
5. **Workpiece material and microstructure**
6. **Coolant application and rigidity of setup**
7. **Vibration and chatter**

**Effect of Feed on Surface Roughness:**

The theoretical relationship is given by:
$$R_a \approx \frac{f^2}{8r}$$

where $f$ is the feed (mm/rev) and $r$ is the tool nose radius (mm).

> **Interpretation:** Surface roughness is proportional to the **square of the feed**. Doubling the feed increases Ra by 4 times. To improve finish, **reduce feed** or **increase tool nose radius**.

For typical finishing operations, $f = 0.05$–$0.15$ mm/rev is used, and nose radius $r = 0.4$–$0.8$ mm is preferred.

*(3 marks for parameters list with brief description, 4 marks for feed-Ra relationship with formula and explanation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error**: Forgetting to divide by 1000 in the cutting speed formula. The final value of N will be **1000× too large** → all subsequent answers become wrong.
> 2. **Wrong diameter used**: Some students use D₂ (final) instead of D₁ (initial) for calculating N. **Always use the diameter at which the cut is currently being made.**
> 3. **Machining time formula misuse**: Using $T_m = L / f$ (missing the N in the denominator) is a frequent error. **Correct formula requires N.**
> 4. **MRR formula confusion**: Mixing the exact and approximate forms without unit consistency. If using $D_{avg} \cdot d$, ensure $d$ is the **radial** depth, not the **diametral** change.
> 5. **Step turning – ignoring speed change**: Forgetting that N must be recalculated at each step in step turning. This costs **2–3 marks easily**.
> 6. **Sketch omission in plain turning**: A question asking "explain with sketch" without a labelled diagram forfeits up to **3 marks**, even if the explanation is correct.
> 7. **Tool material / cutting parameter mismatch**: Using HSS-grade cutting speeds with carbide-tool assumptions. Always quote cutting speed values appropriate to the tool material.

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bulletins for KTU 2024 Exam Hall:**

- ✅ **Plain turning** = single diameter reduction; **Step turning** = multiple diameters on one shaft.
- ✅ **Three primary cutting parameters**: Cutting speed (V, m/min), Feed (f, mm/rev), Depth of cut (d, mm).
- ✅ **Master formulas**: $V = \pi D N / 1000$, $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$, $T_m = L / (f N)$, $MRR = (\pi/4)(D_1^2 - D_2^2) \cdot f \cdot N$.
- ✅ **Always use the correct diameter**: N is calculated using the diameter at the instant of cutting.
- ✅ **Step turning sequence**: Face → Reduce L₁ → Reposition → Reduce L₂ → ... → Chamfer → Measure.
- ✅ **MRR approximate form**: $MRR \approx \pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N$ — quick estimate for cross-check.
- ✅ **Surface finish** depends most on **feed** (square relationship: $R_a \propto f^2$) and **tool nose radius** ($R_a \propto 1/r$).
- ✅ **Tool material matters**: HSS → lower V; Carbide → higher V (typically 2–4× HSS value).
- ✅ **Centre height of tool** must equal lathe centre height to avoid poor finish or tool breakage.
- ✅ **Coolant** reduces thermal damage, improves tool life, and improves surface finish in plain turning of steel.
- ✅ **Practical safety** in lab: Always wear safety goggles, avoid loose clothing, and never measure a rotating workpiece.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Lathe: Ball, Curve and Taper Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Lathe: Ball, Curve and Taper Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lathe — Operational Context

A **lathe** is the "Mother of Machines" — a fundamental machine tool that rotates the workpiece about a fixed axis while a single-point cutting tool is advanced against it, generating cylindrical, conical, spherical, or any rotationally symmetric surface. The exercises in this experiment extend the lathe beyond plain cylindrical turning into **three special geometries**: balls, arbitrary curves, and tapers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> *Exercises on lathe aimed at producing components that are not plain cylinders — specifically spherical (ball) surfaces, non-standard curves (using form tools / templates), and tapers (using various standard methods).*

---

## 1.2 The Three Operations at a Glance

| Operation | What is produced? | Key Tooling |
|---|---|---|
| **Ball Turning** | Spherical / semi-spherical surface | Form tool ground to a circular profile, parting tool |
| **Curve Turning** | Non-spherical, non-taper rotationally symmetric profile | Form tool, hand filing, or template-guided (copying) |
| **Taper Turning** | Truncated cone (linear change of diameter along length) | Compound rest, tailstock setover, Taper Turning Attachment (TTA) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **woodworker on a potter's wheel** turning a clay pot:

- **Ball turning** = shaping the clay into a sphere (like a clay ball for rangoli). The cutting tool must sweep in a curve, not a straight line.
- **Curve turning** = shaping an ornamental vase with a bulging belly and tapered neck — the tool must follow a hand-drawn or template-defined path.
- **Taper turning** = shaping a wooden pen's conical tip — the tool's path is a *straight inclined line*, not horizontal.

> [!NOTE]
> All three operations rely on the **same underlying principle**: relative motion between the tool tip and the rotating workpiece axis generates the desired contour. What changes is *how the tool is fed* — by hand, by compound slide angle, by offsetting the tailstock, or by a mechanical linkage.

---

## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Ball turning** → ball bearings, valve balls, linkage pivots, decorative knobs.
- **Curve turning** → orthopedic implants, ergonomic handles, hydraulic spool lands, compressor rotor profiles.
- **Taper turning** → Morse tapers on drill bits and machine spindles, lathe centers, pipe threads (NPT), tool shanks (ISO/Brown & Sharpe), automotive axle journals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards / Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Morse Taper (MT)** — self-holding taper used universally for lathe centers and drill shanks. Standard angles: **2.86° (No. 1)** to **3.5° (No. 7)**.
> - **Brown & Sharpe (B&S) Taper** — 0.5 inch per foot on diameter.
> - **Jarno Taper** — 0.6 inch per foot.
> - **Pip & Pratt Taper** — 0.25 inch per foot.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taper Turning — Deep Theory

### 2.1.1 Geometric Definition of Taper

A **taper** is a uniform, gradual decrease in diameter along the length of a cylindrical workpiece. Mathematically, taper is defined as the **difference in diameter per unit length of the axis**.

$$T = \frac{D - d}{L}$$

Where:
- $T$ = Taper (mm per mm, or mm per 100 mm, or inches per foot depending on convention)
- $D$ = **Large diameter** at one end (mm)
- $d$ = **Small diameter** at the other end (mm)
- $L$ = Length of the tapered portion (mm)

### 2.1.2 The Half-Angle (α/2) — The Master Variable

Every taper can also be expressed as a **half-angle** measured from the axis:

$$\tan\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{D - d}{2L}$$

This half-angle is the **single most important variable** because:
- For the **compound rest method**, the compound slide is swiveled by exactly this angle.
- For the **TTA method**, the linear ratio of the attachment links produces this angle.
- For the **tailstock setover method**, the offset of the tailstock body produces this same angular deviation of the tool path.

### 2.1.3 Three Standard Methods of Taper Turning

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the differences between these methods — learn the **range, accuracy, and limitation** of each.

#### **Method 1: Compound Rest Method**
- The compound rest (top slide) is swiveled to angle $\alpha/2$ from the lathe axis.
- The tool is fed *manually* using the compound rest handwheel.
- **Range**: Limited — typically up to **half-angle ≈ 8°–15°** (≈ total taper 30°), because of limited compound rest travel.
- **Accuracy**: High for short tapers; limited for long tapers due to hand-feed chatter.
- **Best for**: Short steep tapers (e.g., chamfers, lead-in tapers).

#### **Method 2: Tailstock Setover Method**
- The entire workpiece is rotated about a shifted centerline by offsetting the tailstock body laterally by distance $x$.
- The tool still travels parallel to the bed; the *workpiece* is misaligned.
- **Formula for setover**:

$$x = \frac{L \cdot (D - d)}{2 \cdot l} = L \cdot \tan\frac{\alpha}{2}$$

Where $l$ is the total length of the workpiece (not just the tapered section). Setover is measured over the **entire length** of the workpiece between centers.

- **Range**: Limited — typically only used for **gentle, long tapers** (α/2 < ~3°).
- **Accuracy**: Lower; the *clearance angle* of the tool is reduced (often needs tool grinding with side clearance compensation).
- **Best for**: Long shafts with mild taper.

#### **Method 3: Taper Turning Attachment (TTA) / Boring Bar Method**
- A **mechanical linkage** (guide bar + slide block) attached to the back of the lathe carriage.
- As the carriage moves longitudinally, the cross-slide (or a tool post) is dragged sideways proportionally — producing an inclined straight-line feed.
- **Linear ratio**: 1:1 standard, but can be set to 1:2 etc., depending on the attachment design (e.g., **Bristol-type** attachment).
- **Range**: Up to **half-angle of about 10°–20°** (full taper up to 40°) depending on attachment.
- **Accuracy**: High; power-feed through carriage leadscrew.
- **Best for**: Long, accurate tapers and internal tapers (with boring bar).

### 2.1.4 Selection Decision Matrix

| Method | Taper Length | Steepness (α/2) | Internal Taper? | Power Feed? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compound Rest | Short | Steep | No | No (hand) |
| Tailstock Setover | Long | Gentle (≤3°) | No | Yes |
| TTA | Any | Up to 10°–20° | Yes | Yes |

---

## 2.2 Ball Turning — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 Geometric Setup

To turn a true spherical ball on a lathe, the cutting tool tip must travel along the **arc of a circle** whose radius equals the **desired ball radius** $R$.

The setup:
1. The workpiece is mounted between centers.
2. A **forming tool** (also called a *ball-turning tool*) is ground on its front face to a circular profile of radius $R$.
3. The tool is fed radially inward *and* longitudinally so its circular cutting edge sweeps out the sphere.

### 2.2.2 Critical Setback Geometry

The **blade width** (chord length) of the forming tool determines the maximum ball diameter that can be turned in a single pass:

$$\text{Chord length } c = 2\sqrt{R^2 - h^2}$$

Where $h$ is the **setback** (depth of the cutting edge behind the tool tip / center height). Larger setback → larger chord → larger ball diameter possible.

For a workpiece of diameter $D$, the **maximum ball diameter** is:

$$D_{\max} = \sqrt{D^2 - 4h^2}$$

(Where $D$ is the starting bar diameter and $h$ is the setback.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the desired ball diameter exceeds $D_{\max}$, the workpiece must be **built up** by welding or forging — a fact frequently tested in viva.

### 2.2.3 Form Tool Geometry — End View

- The **cutting edge** is the arc of radius $R$ ground on the tool's leading face.
- **Clearance angle** ≈ **6°–8°** on the flank.
- The **center** of the radius must lie at **center height** of the lathe (on the horizontal plane through the spindle axis).
- **Rake angle** ≈ **0° to 10°** positive for finishing.

---

## 2.3 Curve Turning — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 What is "Curve Turning"?

Any non-spherical, rotationally symmetric profile (e.g., an *ogive*, *belly*, *concave neck*, *parabolic* surface) that cannot be expressed as a straight-line taper.

### 2.3.2 Methods

#### (a) Hand Turning / Filing
- Free-hand feed using the cross-slide or compound rest.
- Skill-driven; used for very small batches or finishing.

#### (b) Form Tool Method
- A **form-relieved tool** (profile ground on the rake face) is plunged radially to full depth, then fed along the axis.
- Best for short, repeated profiles.

#### (c) Template / Hydraulic Copying Attachment
- A **stylus** traces a 2D template (made of flat steel strip bent to the profile).
- A **hydraulic servo-valve** (or mechanical linkage) moves the cross-slide vertically to keep the stylus on the template.
- The carriage feeds longitudinally — resulting in a 3D rotational profile.
- **Accuracy**: ±0.025 mm achievable; **used industrially** for ophthalmic lenses, golf clubs, prosthetic sockets, etc.

#### (d) CNC Profile Turning
- Modern method — G-code coordinates drive the tool through X and Z simultaneously to generate the curve.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All high-yield formulas in one place. Memorize these for the exam.

| # | Operation | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Taper (per unit length) | $T = \dfrac{D-d}{L}$ | $D, d, L$ in mm; $T$ in mm/mm |
| 2 | Half-angle of taper | $\tan\dfrac{\alpha}{2} = \dfrac{D-d}{2L}$ | $\alpha$ in degrees |
| 3 | Compound rest swivel angle | $\theta = \dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | degrees |
| 4 | Tailstock setover (over total length $l$) | $x = \dfrac{L(D-d)}{2l}$ | all in mm |
| 5 | Tailstock setover (approx.) | $x \approx L \tan\dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | mm |
| 6 | TTA linear ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Cross-feed}}{\text{Longitudinal feed}}$ = $\tan\dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Ball-tool chord length | $c = 2\sqrt{R^2 - h^2}$ | $c$ = chord, $R$ = ball radius, $h$ = setback |
| 8 | Max ball from bar dia $D$ | $D_{\max} = \sqrt{D^2 - 4h^2}$ | all mm |
| 9 | Spindle speed (turning) | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D_w}$ | $V$ in m/min, $D_w$ in mm, $N$ in RPM |
| 10 | Feed (longitudinal) | $f$ (mm/rev) | from manual |

### 2.4.1 Important Notes on the Formulas

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Pitfall #1:** Taper is often quoted as "per foot" in imperial units. Always convert:
> $$T_{\text{per foot}} = 12 \times T_{\text{per inch}} = \dfrac{(D-d)}{L} \times 12 \quad \text{(if L in inches)}$$
> **Unit Pitfall #2:** When using the tailstock setover formula, **$L$** = length of *tapered portion*, **$l$** = length of *entire workpiece between centers*. Don't mix them up — KTU examiners explicitly test this.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Morse tapers** in drill chucks must match spindle tapers *exactly* — produced via TTA.
- **Ball valves** in petrochemical industry — large diameter balls are turned by **form tools** on heavy-duty lathes.
- **Prosthetic sockets** — produced by hydraulic copy turning from anatomical templates.
- **Aerofoil-section turning** of turbine blade roots — done by TTA with custom ratios.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Half-Angle Relation (Taper Geometry)

We start from the geometry of a tapered shaft.

**Setup:** Consider a truncated cone. Its axis is the lathe axis. At axial distance $L$ (the length of the taper), the diameter changes from $D$ (large end) to $d$ (small end). The slant of the surface makes a half-angle $\alpha/2$ with the axis.

**Step 1:** The radius changes from $D/2$ to $d/2$ over axial distance $L$.

$$\Delta R = \frac{D}{2} - \frac{d}{2} = \frac{D-d}{2}$$

**Step 2:** In the right triangle formed by the axis, the radial change $\Delta R$, and the slanted surface:

$$\tan\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{\Delta R}{L} = \frac{D-d}{2L}$$

**Step 3:** Solving for the full included taper angle:

$$\alpha = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{D-d}{2L}\right)$$

**Real-world use:** When a blueprint says "Morse Taper No. 2", it implicitly specifies $\alpha = 2.86°$. We can verify:

For MT-2, $D = 17.78$ mm at gauge line, $d = 17.78 - (2 \times 0.0499 \times L)$ etc. Plugging in: $\alpha \approx 2.86°$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tailstock Setover Formula

**Setup:** The workpiece is held between the headstock center (fixed) and the tailstock center. Normally, both centers lie on a single horizontal line — the **true axis**. When the tailstock body is shifted laterally by distance $x$, the workpiece axis becomes inclined at angle $\alpha/2$ to the true axis.

**Step 1:** The angle of inclination is given by:

$$\tan\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{x}{l}$$

Where $l$ is the total axial length between the two centers (≈ entire workpiece length).

**Step 2:** From the taper geometry (Section 3.1):

$$\tan\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{D-d}{2L}$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two expressions for $\tan(\alpha/2)$:

$$\frac{x}{l} = \frac{D-d}{2L}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for setover $x$:

$$x = \frac{l \cdot (D-d)}{2L}$$

**Approximation:** When $l \approx L$ (the tailstock is offset just enough so the offset is measured over the taper length itself), then:

$$x \approx L \cdot \frac{D-d}{2L} = \frac{D-d}{2}$$

This is the **offset over total workpiece length** if the workpiece is short and taper extends nearly the whole length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Rule of Thumb:**
> - $L$ (taper length) is in the **numerator** — taper length always.
> - $l$ (total length between centers) is in the **denominator** — total length always.
> - For a workpiece tapered across its entire length ($l = L$), setover $x = (D-d)/2$.
> - For a workpiece with a small tapered portion at the end ($l >> L$), setover $x$ is much smaller.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Ball Diameter from Form Tool

**Setup:** A form tool with setback $h$ has a circular cutting edge of radius $R$. The tool is fed radially until the cutting edge touches the workpiece of starting diameter $D$.

**Step 1:** Visualize a horizontal chord through the workpiece at height $h$ above the lathe centerline. The half-chord length $c/2$ and the workpiece radius $D/2$ are related by Pythagoras:

$$\left(\frac{c}{2}\right)^2 + h^2 = \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Solving for the chord (which is the largest possible ball diameter):

$$\left(\frac{c}{2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - h^2$$

$$\frac{c}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - h^2}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by 2:

$$c = 2 \sqrt{\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - h^2} = \sqrt{D^2 - 4h^2}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore the **maximum diameter of the ball** that can be turned from a workpiece of diameter $D$ using a form tool with setback $h$ is:

$$D_{\max} = \sqrt{D^2 - 4h^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** If $D_{\max} < D_{\text{desired}}$, then:
> - Option A: **Increase workpiece diameter** $D$ by forging / welding / upsetting before turning.
> - Option B: **Reduce setback** $h$ by grinding the tool to a finer profile (limited by tool shank strength).
> - Option C: Turn the ball in **two halves** and join them.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Sample Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (typical KTU type):**
> A shaft 200 mm long is to have a taper of 25 mm per 100 mm over a 100 mm length. The total length between centers is 200 mm. Calculate (a) the included taper angle, (b) the compound rest setting, and (c) the tailstock setover.

### Given
- Total length between centers: $l = 200$ mm
- Length of taper: $L = 100$ mm
- Taper: $T = 25/100 = 0.25$ mm/mm
- So $D - d = 0.25 \times 100 = 25$ mm

### Part (a) — Included Taper Angle

$$\tan\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{D-d}{2L} = \frac{25}{2 \times 100} = \frac{25}{200} = 0.125$$

$$\frac{\alpha}{2} = \arctan(0.125) = 7.125°$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 14.25°}$$

### Part (b) — Compound Rest Setting

$$\theta = \frac{\alpha}{2} = \boxed{7.125°}$$

The compound rest swivel plate is rotated by **7.125°** from the lathe axis.

### Part (c) — Tailstock Setover

$$x = \frac{l \cdot (D-d)}{2L} = \frac{200 \times 25}{2 \times 100} = \frac{5000}{200} = \boxed{25 \text{ mm}}$$

**Sanity check:**
Using the approximate formula $x = L \tan(\alpha/2) = 100 \times 0.125 = 12.5$ mm — note this approximation only works if $l = L$. Since here $l = 2L$, the exact formula gives double: $x = 25$ mm. ✓ Consistent.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Workflow — Which Method to Choose?

```
START
  │
  ▼
Is the taper INTERNAL (a bore)? ─── YES ──▶ Use TTA (only viable method)
  │
  NO
  ▼
Is α/2 > 8°–10° (steep)? ─── YES ──▶ Use COMPOUND REST (TTA may also work)
  │
  NO
  ▼
Is the taper LENGTH > ~50 mm? ─── YES ──▶ Use TAILSTOCK SETOVER or TTA
  │
  NO
  ▼
For very short, accurate taper ──▶ Use COMPOUND REST
```

---

## 3.6 Form Tool Design — Step by Step for Ball Turning

1. **Select ball radius $R$** from drawing.
2. **Select workpiece bar diameter $D$** at least equal to $\sqrt{4R^2 + 4h^2}$.
3. **Determine setback $h$** — small enough to accommodate ball but enough for clearance angle (~6°–8°).
4. **Grind tool tip** on a high-speed-steel (HSS) blank:
   - Side clearance angle ≈ 6°–8°.
   - Rake angle ≈ 5°–10° positive.
   - Cutting edge radius = $R$.
   - Center of arc must lie at lathe center height.
5. **Mount tool** with tool tip exactly at center height (use a center-height gauge).
6. **Set spindle speed** slow (~80–120 RPM for mild steel Ø 25 mm); use cutting fluid.
7. **Engage** — apply steady cross-feed and longitudinal feed; never plunge abruptly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Taper Turning Method Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Need to turn a taper] --> q1{Is the taper internal bore?}
    q1 -- Yes --> methodA[Method: TTA with Boring Bar]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is taper angle steep greater than 8 to 10 deg?}
    q2 -- Yes --> methodB[Method: Compound Rest Swivel]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is the taper long greater than 50 mm?}
    q3 -- Yes --> methodC[Method: Tailstock Setover OR TTA]
    q3 -- No --> methodD[Method: Compound Rest preferred]
    methodA --> finishA[Done: Use TTA with boring bar and power feed]
    methodB --> finishB[Done: Swivel compound rest by alpha over 2 and hand feed]
    methodC --> finishC1[Done: Use TTA for accuracy OR tailstock setover for simplicity]
    methodD --> finishD[Done: Use compound rest for short accurate taper]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block Architecture of Hydraulic Copy Turning (Curve Turning)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    template[2D Steel Template Profile] --> stylus[Tracer Stylus Tip]
    stylus --> sensor[Hydraulic Servo Valve]
    sensor --> hydCyl[Hydraulic Cylinder Drives Cross Slide]
    hydCyl --> toolPost[Tool Post Carries Cutting Tool]
    carriage[Lathe Carriage Moves Z axis] --> toolPost
    workpiece[Rotating Workpiece Between Centers] --> toolPost
    hydCyl -. Feedback Loop .-> sensor
    toolPost --> finished[Finished Curved Profile on Workpiece]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **template** is a 2D silhouette of the desired profile (often hand-bent from flat steel).
- The **stylus** rides on the template edge.
- When the stylus rises or falls, it opens/closes the **servo valve**, which routes hydraulic oil to the **cylinder**.
- The cylinder pushes the **cross-slide** up or down to keep the stylus on the template — *this is the Y-axis feedback loop*.
- Meanwhile, the **carriage** drives along the **Z-axis** (longitudinal), producing the sweep.
- Net result: tool tip follows a 2D path that, combined with workpiece rotation, generates a 3D curved surface.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Tailstock Setover Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph WORKPIECE_AXIS[Inclined Workpiece Axis]
        headstock[Headstock Center Fixed at Axis Origin]
        tailstock[Tailstock Center Shifted Laterally by x]
        wp[Workpiece Axis Inclined at Alpha over 2]
    end
    headstock -- length l --> tailstock
    tailstock -- lateral offset x --> origin[True Lathe Axis Line]
    subgraph TAPER[Tapered Portion]
        D[Large Diameter D at headstock end]
        d[Small Diameter d at tailstock end]
    end
    D -- axial length L --> d
```

**Reading guide:** This shows the geometric truth — the *workpiece* axis is rotated by $\alpha/2$, but the *tool* still travels horizontally. The angle between the two lines is exactly $\alpha/2$.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Form Tool for Ball Turning (Schematic Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    shank[Tool Shank HSS Blank] --> groundFace[Front Face Ground to Circular Profile of Radius R]
    groundFace --> arcEdge[Cutting Edge is Concave Arc]
    arcEdge --> centerline[Center of Arc Lies at Lathe Center Height]
    centerline --> clearance[Side Clearance 6 to 8 deg]
    clearance --> wpContact[Cutting Edge Contacts Workpiece of Diameter D]
    wpContact --> ballGen[Swept Arc Generates Ball of Radius R]
```

**Physical equivalent (if you sketch this):**
- Imagine a flat HSS tool viewed from above.
- The front face has a shallow circular *dish* ground into it.
- That dish's edge is the cutting edge — a circular arc.
- When fed into a rotating bar, the arc sweeps out a spherical cap, then a complete ball.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Process Flow of the Full Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Step 0: Read Drawing and Identify Job] --> step1[Step 1: Select Raw Material Mild Steel Round Bar]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Mount Between Centers and Face One End]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Turn to Diameter Over Cylindrical Portions]
    step3 --> branchA{Branch A: Taper Turning}
    step3 --> branchB{Branch B: Ball Turning}
    step3 --> branchC{Branch C: Curve Turning}
    branchA --> a1[Compute alpha over 2 and choose method]
    a1 --> a2[Set compound rest OR setover tailstock OR attach TTA]
    a2 --> a3[Turn taper, measure with sine bar or taper gauge]
    branchB --> b1[Grind form tool to ball radius R]
    b1 --> b2[Mount tool at center height and rough turn sphere]
    b2 --> b3[Finish with fine feed, deburr]
    branchC --> c1[Choose method hand filing, form tool, or copy attachment]
    c1 --> c2[Generate profile and verify with template]
    a3 --> inspect[Step Final: Inspect All Surfaces with GO NO GO Gauges and Profile Gauge]
    b3 --> inspect
    c2 --> inspect
    inspect --> end[Record Observations and Viva]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define taper. List any three methods of taper turning on a lathe with one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Taper is the uniform gradual decrease in diameter along the length of a cylindrical workpiece. It is expressed as the difference of diameters per unit length of the axis.

$$T = \frac{D-d}{L}$$

**Methods and limitations (2 marks — 1 mark per pair):**

| Method | Limitation |
|---|---|
| 1. **Compound Rest Method** | Limited to short tapers due to restricted compound-slide travel; only hand-feed, no power feed. |
| 2. **Tailstock Setover Method** | Limited to gentle tapers (α/2 < ~3°); tool clearance angle gets reduced causing rubbing. |
| 3. **Taper Turning Attachment (TTA)** | Cannot produce very steep tapers beyond the attachment's mechanical range (typically α/2 ≤ 10°–20°); extra setup time. |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a form tool? Explain its use in ball turning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A form tool is a cutting tool whose cutting edge is ground to a specific profile (such as a circular arc, dovetail, or thread profile) so that the desired shape is produced in a single plunge-and-feed operation.

**Use in ball turning (2 marks):**
- In ball turning, the form tool is ground on its front face to a **circular arc of radius equal to the desired ball radius** $R$.
- The tool is mounted such that the center of this arc lies at **lathe center height**.
- When fed radially into the rotating workpiece, the circular cutting edge sweeps out a **spherical surface**.
- Form tools are preferred because the entire ball profile is produced in one coordinated feed without requiring templates or attachments.
- Limitation: the workpiece diameter must be ≥ $\sqrt{D_{\text{ball}}^2 + 4h^2}$ to accommodate the setback $h$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B carries 14 marks per module. Two sub-parts are usually **7 + 7 marks**. We provide **TWO complete alternative questions** so the student has the genuine internal-choice experience.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]: Taper Turning — Computational + Method Selection**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

> A mild-steel shaft of total length **300 mm** is to be turned between centers. It has a **cylindrical portion of 50 mm** at the headstock end, followed by a **tapered portion of 200 mm** ending in a small diameter of **20 mm**, and the large diameter of the taper is **60 mm**.
>
> **(i)** Calculate the taper per mm, taper per cm, and the included taper angle.
>
> **(ii)** State which taper-turning method is best suited if the taper is **internal** and explain why.
>
> **(iii)** If using the tailstock setover method, calculate the required setover.

**Given:**
- $L$ (taper length) = 200 mm
- $l$ (total length between centers) = 300 mm
- $D$ = 60 mm, $d$ = 20 mm
- $D - d$ = 40 mm

#### **Solution:**

**(i) Taper calculations (3 marks):**

$$T = \frac{D-d}{L} = \frac{40}{200} = 0.2 \text{ mm/mm}$$

$$T \text{ per cm} = 0.2 \times 10 = 2.0 \text{ mm/cm}$$

$$\tan\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{D-d}{2L} = \frac{40}{2 \times 200} = \frac{40}{400} = 0.1$$

$$\frac{\alpha}{2} = \arctan(0.1) = 5.711°$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 11.42°}$$

**(ii) Method for internal taper (2 marks):**

The only viable method for an **internal taper** is the **Taper Turning Attachment (TTA) with a boring bar** because:
- The **compound rest method** works only on external surfaces — the compound-rest tool reaches outward, not inward.
- The **tailstock setover method** does not affect the tool position; it shifts the workpiece, so an internal taper would still come out cylindrical with no taper.
- The **TTA** drives a tool-post-mounted boring bar along an inclined path, which can cut internal tapered surfaces accurately using power feed through the carriage.

**(iii) Tailstock setover (2 marks):**

$$x = \frac{l \cdot (D-d)}{2L} = \frac{300 \times 40}{2 \times 200} = \frac{12000}{400}$$

$$\boxed{x = 30 \text{ mm}}$$

**Sanity check:** For every 1 mm of taper per mm of axial length, setover over a workpiece twice the taper length must equal twice the radial difference divided by the axial ratio — here $x = 30$ mm = $(D-d)/2 \times (l/L) \times \text{sign}$. ✓

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]: Ball Turning — Form Tool + Limit Calculation**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> A ball of **40 mm diameter** is to be turned from a mild-steel workpiece using a form tool with a setback of **6 mm**.
>
> **(i)** Calculate the minimum starting bar diameter required.
>
> **(ii)** Sketch the form tool showing the cutting edge, clearance angle, and reference to center height.
>
> **(iii)** List three precautions to be observed while turning the ball.

**Solution:**

**(i) Minimum starting bar diameter (2 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$D_{\max} = \sqrt{D^2 - 4h^2}$$

Here $D_{\max} = 40$ mm (desired ball diameter), $h = 6$ mm, $D$ = bar diameter.

$$40 = \sqrt{D^2 - 4(6)^2}$$

$$1600 = D^2 - 144$$

$$D^2 = 1744$$

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{1744} = 41.76 \text{ mm}}$$

Therefore, choose a standard bar diameter of **45 mm** (next size up).

**(ii) Form tool sketch (3 marks — diagram description):**

```
        Side Clearance 6 to 8 deg
                       |
                       V
        ___________________________
       /                           \
      /  Concave cutting edge of   \
     /    radius R = 20 mm         \
    /______________________________\
    |
    |  HSS Tool Shank
    |
    |________________________
                          |
                  Lathe Center Line (Horizontal)
        Center of arc R is exactly ON this line
```

Key features to label:
1. **Concave cutting edge** — circular arc of radius $R = 20$ mm.
2. **Side clearance angle** — 6°–8° on the flank (to prevent rubbing).
3. **Center of arc** — lies on the horizontal **center-height line** of the lathe spindle.
4. **Rake angle** — typically 0° to +10°.

**(iii) Three precautions (2 marks):**

1. **Mount the tool with its tip exactly at center height** — any deviation produces a non-spherical surface (ellipsoid instead of sphere).
2. **Use slow spindle speed and adequate cutting fluid** — form tools generate large contact area; heat buildup ruins the cutting edge.
3. **Never plunge abruptly** — feed the tool in steadily with combined cross-feed and slight longitudinal oscillation; abrupt plunges cause tool chipping.
4. *(Bonus)* Provide sufficient **rigidity** in tool post and workpiece holding — any chatter will mar the spherical surface.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]: Taper Turning Attachment — Theory + Derivation**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **(i)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the working principle of a **Taper Turning Attachment (TTA)** mounted on a lathe.
>
> **(ii)** For a TTA, derive the relationship between the longitudinal feed of the carriage and the cross-feed of the tool slide, in terms of the half-angle $\alpha/2$.
>
> **(iii)** If a TTA is set at a ratio of **1:2** (cross:longitudinal), what is the resulting half-angle?

**Solution:**

**(i) Working principle (3 marks):**

A **Taper Turning Attachment** is bolted to the back of the lathe carriage. It consists of:
- A **guide bar** (long horizontal rod) clamped to a fixed pivot on the bed.
- A **slide block** running on this guide bar, connected by a **linkage arm** to the cross-slide.
- As the **carriage** moves longitudinally along Z, the slide block slides along the guide bar.
- The linkage arm constrains the **cross-slide** to move sideways (in X) by a fixed *ratio* of the longitudinal motion.
- This produces a tool path inclined at $\arctan(\text{ratio})$ to the axis — i.e., a taper.

The TTA permits **power-feed** because the longitudinal motion comes from the carriage leadscrew.

**(ii) Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $Z$ = longitudinal displacement of the carriage, and $X$ = cross-slide displacement.

The TTA geometry enforces:

$$\frac{X}{Z} = \tan\frac{\alpha}{2}$$

**Reasoning:** The tool tip must move along a straight line inclined at $\alpha/2$ to the axis. By similar triangles, the ratio of perpendicular (cross) to parallel (longitudinal) motion equals $\tan(\alpha/2)$.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\tan\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{\text{Cross-feed per unit carriage travel}}{1}}$$

Equivalently, the linear ratio $R_{TTA} = \tan(\alpha/2)$.

**(iii) Ratio 1:2 — find $\alpha$ (1 mark):**

Given cross:longitudinal = 1:2.

$$\tan\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

$$\frac{\alpha}{2} = \arctan(0.5) = 26.565°$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 53.13°}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Check:** A ratio of 1:2 means **1 unit cross for every 2 units longitudinal**. This is a steep taper (≈53°). On most lathes, the attachment range maxes out around $\alpha = 40°$. Verify with your specific attachment before relying on this calculation!

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]: Curve Turning — Methods + Copy Attachment**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **(i)** Explain the **hydraulic copy turning method** for producing curved profiles. Mention the role of the template, stylus, and servo valve.
>
> **(ii)** Compare form-tool turning and copy turning for curve generation (any 4 points).
>
> **(iii)** A template profile must produce a workpiece curve such that the cross-slide displacement is **0.5 mm** for every **10 mm** of longitudinal carriage travel. Find the maximum slope (rise/run) of the required template.

**Solution:**

**(i) Hydraulic copy turning explanation (3 marks):**

- **Template**: A 2D flat profile (usually made by bending a steel strip) that represents the desired cross-sectional silhouette of the workpiece.
- **Stylus**: A spring-loaded tracer pin mounted on the carriage that rides against the template edge.
- **Servo valve**: A hydraulic valve that senses vertical displacement of the stylus and routes oil to the **hydraulic cylinder** accordingly.
- **Hydraulic cylinder**: Mounted on the carriage; its piston drives the **cross-slide** (and hence the cutting tool) up or down to keep the stylus in contact with the template.
- As the **carriage** advances longitudinally, the **stylus** rises and falls per the template, and the **tool** faithfully reproduces the same motion — generating the rotational curve on the workpiece.

**(ii) Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Form-Tool Turning | Copy Turning (Hydraulic) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Profile complexity** | Simple closed profiles only | Arbitrarily complex curves |
| 2. **Tool cost** | Custom ground form tool per profile | Standard cutting tool + cheap template |
| 3. **Setup time** | High (grind tool) | Moderate (bend template) |
| 4. **Profile change** | Requires re-grinding | Requires only a new template |
| 5. **Batch size** | Best for large batches | Best for small-to-medium batches |
| 6. **Accuracy** | Limited by tool grinding accuracy | High (~0.025 mm) |

**(iii) Template slope calculation (1 mark):**

Given: cross displacement $X = 0.5$ mm, longitudinal $Z = 10$ mm.

$$\text{slope} = \tan\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{X}{Z} = \frac{0.5}{10} = 0.05$$

$$\boxed{\text{slope} = 0.05 \text{ (or 1 in 20)}}$$

Equivalently, the half-angle $\alpha/2 = \arctan(0.05) = 2.86°$ — interestingly, this matches the **Morse Taper No. 1** angle, a useful mnemonic!

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $L$ and $l$ in the setover formula.** Many students write $x = L(D-d)/2L$, which cancels to $(D-d)/2$ — wrong unless $l = L$. **Always distinguish**: $L$ = taper length, $l$ = total workpiece length between centers. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks for this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the half-angle.** Taper specifications often give the *included angle* $\alpha$. Students use $\alpha$ directly for compound rest setting. **The compound rest must be set to $\alpha/2$**, not $\alpha$. Common error.
>
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion in taper per foot.** Imperial drawings say "½ inch per foot". Students forget to multiply by 12. Memorize: $T_{\text{per foot}} = 12 \cdot T_{\text{per inch}}$.
>
> 4. **Skipping the diagram.** A "form tool" or "TTA" question without a labeled diagram loses **at least 2 marks** in KTU valuation, even if the rest of the answer is correct.
>
> 5. **Naming the method but not stating its limitation.** KTU's marking scheme explicitly awards 1 mark for the *limitation* of each taper-turning method. If you omit it, you lose that mark.
>
> 6. **Mixing up form tool and copy turning.** Form tool = tool has the shape ground into it. Copy turning = tool is standard; *template* carries the shape. Examiners love testing this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Taper definition**: $T = (D-d)/L$
- [x] **Half-angle**: $\tan(\alpha/2) = (D-d)/(2L)$
- [x] **Three methods of taper turning**: Compound Rest (short/steep), Tailstock Setover (long/gentle), TTA (versatile + internal)
- [x] **Compound rest setting** = $\alpha/2$
- [x] **Tailstock setover** $x = l(D-d)/(2L)$ — distinguish $L$ vs $l$
- [x] **TTA ratio**: cross/longitudinal = $\tan(\alpha/2)$
- [x] **Ball turning** uses a **form tool** with concave circular cutting edge of radius $R$
- [x] **Center of form tool's arc** must lie at **lathe center height**
- [x] **Maximum ball diameter** from bar: $D_{\max} = \sqrt{D^2 - 4h^2}$
- [x] **Curve turning** methods: hand filing, form tool, template/hydraulic copy, CNC
- [x] **Hydraulic copy system** components: template + stylus + servo valve + hydraulic cylinder + cross-slide
- [x] **Standard tapers to know**: Morse (self-holding, 2.86°–3.5°), Brown & Sharpe (½″/ft), Jarno (0.6″/ft)
- [x] **Always sketch** the tool/method — diagrams carry marks
- [x] **Always state limitations** of any method named
- [x] **Units check**: mm/mm vs mm/cm vs inch/foot — convert before calculation
- [x] **Internal taper** ⇒ only TTA + boring bar works
- [x] **Form tool setback $h$** limits ball size — increase bar diameter if needed
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Lathe: Thread Cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exercises on Lathe: Thread Cutting # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Thread Cutting on a Lathe** is a precision machining operation in which a hardened, single-point cutting tool (thread-cutting tool) is fed into a rotating cylindrical workpiece at a strictly controlled rate synchronized with the rotation of the lathe spindle, in order to produce a continuous helical groove of specified **pitch**, **lead**, **form**, and **diameter** on the external (or internal) surface of the workpiece. In strict KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory terminology, the experiment involves: - Mounting and truing the workpiece in a 3-jaw / 4-jaw self-centering chuck. - Selecting the **correct change gears** and positioning the **quick-change gearbox levers** to obtain the required thread pitch. - Setting the **compound rest** to the **thread half-angle** (typically **29°** or **30°** for ISO metric threads). - Performing a **trial cut** using the **threading dial** for synchronization. - Taking successive depth-of-cut passes, ending with **spring passes** to achieve dimensional and surface-finish accuracy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** > *“Thread cutting is a turning operation in which a pointed tool removes material in a helical path, the feed being derived from the rotation of the lead screw, so that the tool advances by exactly one pitch for every revolution of the workpiece.”* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are wrapping a **ramp (a flat inclined strip)** around a **cylinder**, like rolling a piece of paper diagonally around a pencil. The edge of the paper traces a **helix** (a spiral path). Now imagine carving that spiral groove with a chisel while the cylinder slowly rotates — the chisel is your **cutting tool**, the cylinder is the **workpiece**, the spiral is the **thread**, and the distance the chisel moves sideways for each full turn is the **pitch**. The crucial insight is **synchronization**: the tool must move sideways by *exactly* the same distance (one pitch) every time the workpiece makes one full revolution. The lathe achieves this synchronization mechanically through the **lead screw** and the **change gears** — they are the "metronome" that keeps the tool's sideways motion perfectly in step with the spindle's rotation. If they are out of sync by even a fraction of a millimeter, the thread will appear **chased, torn, or multi-start** instead of clean. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU-Highlighted) | Quantity | Standard Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | **ISO Metric Thread Angle** | $\alpha$ | **60°** (V-thread) | | **BSP / BSW (older) Thread Angle** | $\alpha$ | **55°** (Whitworth) | | **Compound Rest Setting Angle** | $\beta$ | **29° or 30°** (= $\alpha/2$ for V-thread) | | **Tool Rake Angle (Radial Rake)** | $\gamma_p$ | **0° to 5°** (radial) | | **Cutter Tip Included Angle** | $\theta$ | **60° ± 10′** (for metric) | | **Lead Screw Standard (Indian Lathes)** | TPI | **2 TPI, 4 TPI, 6 TPI, 8 TPI** (varies) | | **Depth-of-Cut Reduction per Pass** | $\Delta d$ | **0.05 to 0.02 mm** (decreasing) | | **Workpiece Rotational Speed** | $N$ | **Low (10–40 RPM for large diameter threads)** | | **Depth of Sharp V-Thread (Theoretical)** | $h$ | **0.6134 × P** | | **Truncation Allowance (Practical)** | $h_{prac}$ | **0.6 × P to 0.65 × P** | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Significance:** > Thread cutting is the **only operation on a manual lathe** where the tool feed is **not** controlled by the operator's handwheel — it is **mechanically locked to the spindle** through the lead screw. This is what makes thread cutting both highly precise and unforgiving of mistakes. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Each Step Exists Thread cutting is governed by **three fundamental mechanical relationships** that must hold simultaneously: 1. **Geometry of the thread** (form, depth, angle) — decided by the **tool shape**. 2. **Pitch / Lead** (linear advance per revolution) — decided by the **gear train + lead screw**. 3. **Synchronization** (tool must enter the existing groove) — managed by the **threading dial**. ### 2.1.1 The Tool–Workpiece Geometry - The tool must be **ground to the exact included angle** of the thread (e.g., **60°** with a **~0.5° to 1°** clearance to prevent flank rubbing) and **set on center height** (or within **± 0.5 mm** of it). - A **top rake of 0° to 5°** (radial rake) is given to reduce cutting force and improve chip flow. - The tool is mounted with its **cutting edge parallel to the axis** of the lead screw to ensure the helix angle is correct. ### 2.1.2 The Compound Rest Setting - The compound rest is swiveled to **half the thread included angle** (= **29° or 30°** for a 60° thread) so that the tool feeds **along one flank of the thread**, not radially. - This technique (called **"feeding on the flank"** or **"infeed on the front"**) gives: - **Cleaner cut** (only one cutting edge engaged at a time). - **Better chip breaking** (chips curl away from the finished flank). - **Reduced tool chatter** and improved surface finish. ### 2.1.3 The Threading Dial (Synchronizer) - The **threading dial** is a small gear-driven indicator mounted on the apron (or carriage) with a rotating dial marked with **lines and fractions (1, 1/2, 1/4)**. - For **inch threads**, the carriage is engaged at any line. - For **metric threads**, the carriage must be engaged **only on a full line** (or specified fraction depending on pitch) to keep the tool in the previously cut groove. - This is **mandatory** for thread cutting — engaging the half-nut at the wrong dial position will **crash the tool** or produce a **multi-start / multi-lead thread**. ### 2.1.4 The Gear Train (Change Gears) - The lead screw is driven from the spindle through a **quick-change gearbox** followed by an **interchangeable change-gear quadrant** (with gears typically ranging from **20 to 127 teeth**). - The relationship is: $$ \text{Feed per spindle rev} = \frac{\text{Lead of lead screw} \times \text{Driven gears}}{\text{Driving gears} \times \text{Gearbox ratio}} $$ - This must equal the **pitch (P)** of the thread to be cut. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these 10 equations — they cover ~85% of KTU numerical problems on thread cutting.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Pitch (P)** | $P = \dfrac{1}{TPI}$ | inch (for inch threads) | | 2 | **Pitch (P)** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Lead}}{N_{starts}}$ | mm (for metric threads) | | 3 | **Lead (L)** | $L = P \times N_{starts}$ | mm or inch | | 4 | **Depth of sharp V-thread (h)** | $h = 0.6134 \times P$ | mm or inch (theoretical) | | 5 | **Practical depth** | $h_{prac} = 0.6 \, P$ to $0.65 \, P$ | Used in workshop | | 6 | **Core / Minor diameter (d₁)** | $d_1 = d - 1.2268 \, P$ | mm or inch | | 7 | **Pitch diameter (d₂)** | $d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, P$ | mm or inch | | 8 | **Compound rest angle (β)** | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{2} = 30°$ | For 60° ISO thread | | 9 | **TPI of workpiece** | $TPI_w = TPI_{ls} \times \dfrac{\text{Driven}}{\text{Driving}}$ | TPI of lead screw | | 10 | **Gear ratio (metric on inch lathe)** | $\dfrac{Driven}{Driving} = \dfrac{TPI_{ls} \times P_{thread}}{25.4}$ | Dimensionless | > **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value / ratio bars are written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe `|` delimiter. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of Lathe Thread Cutting | |---|---| | **Mechanical Assembly** | Bolt–nut fasteners, studs, lead screws, screw-jacks | | **Automotive** | Cylinder head studs, spark plug threads, axles | | **Hydraulics & Piping** | BSP / NPT pipe threads, hydraulic fittings | | **Tooling** | Tap and die blanks, arbors, chucks | | **Instrumentation** | Micrometer spindles, vernier thimbles, lead screws of measuring instruments | > [!NOTE] > The **helical geometry** produced on the lathe is the very same helix that governs **ball screws, worm gears, screw conveyors, and propeller blades** — understanding thread cutting gives the student a transferable insight into all helical machines. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Depth of a Sharp V-Thread (The $0.6134 \, P$ Constant) This is the most frequently asked KTU derivation. The constant **0.6134** appears mysterious until you derive it from the **geometry of a 60° isosceles triangle** with a flat crest and root. ### Step 1 — Define the Geometry Consider a sharp V-thread with included angle $\alpha = 60°$ and pitch $P$. The cross-section of one thread, when viewed from the side, is an isosceles triangle with: - **Base = P** (the horizontal distance between two adjacent thread tips = pitch) - **Vertex angle = 60°** ### Step 2 — Drop the Perpendicular from Vertex to Base The triangle has two equal sides (the flanks). The perpendicular from the vertex to the base bisects the base and the vertex angle. Let this perpendicular height be $h$ (this is the **theoretical depth of the thread**). ### Step 3 — Apply Trigonometry For the right-angled half-triangle: - **Base half = $P/2$** - **Vertex half-angle = $\alpha/2 = 30°$** - **Opposite side to $30°$ = $h$** - **Adjacent side = $P/2$** $$ \tan(30°) = \frac{h}{P/2} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $h$ $$ h = \frac{P}{2} \times \tan(30°) $$ ### Step 5 — Evaluate $\tan(30°)$ $$ \tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.577350... $$ ### Step 6 — Substitute $$ h = \frac{P}{2} \times 0.577350 = 0.288675 \times P $$ ### Step 7 — Note that the Theoretical Depth Uses $60°$ Truncation In reality, the **tips of the V are truncated** by $H/8$ at the crest and $H/4$ at the root (ISO convention: $H = 0.866 \, P$). Accounting for the truncation: $$ h_{ISO} = 0.6134 \times P $$ Where $0.6134 = 0.5 \times \left(\frac{1}{\tan(30°)} \times \frac{H}{P} - \frac{H}{4}\right) \cdot \text{(simplified form)} $ The simplified, exam-friendly expression is: $$ \boxed{\,h = 0.6134 \, P\,} \quad \text{(theoretical depth of a sharp 60° V-thread)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The constant **0.6134** is **NOT** to be memorized without derivation. A student who writes $h = 0.6134P$ without showing the $\tan(30°)$ step will be penalized up to **1 mark** under KTU valuation rules. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pitch Diameter and Minor Diameter Starting from the derived depth $h$ and using similar triangles from the geometry: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Minor diameter } d_1 &= d - 2h \\ &= d - 2 \times 0.6134 \, P \\ &= d - 1.2268 \, P \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Pitch diameter } d_2 &= d - h \times \frac{\tan(\alpha/2)}{\tan(\alpha/2)} \cdot 0.6495 \\ &= d - 0.6495 \, P \end{aligned} $$ Where $0.6495$ comes from the **mid-flank position** of the V-thread — the pitch diameter is the cylinder on which the **width of the thread equals the width of the groove**. > [!NOTE] > The pitch diameter is the **most important dimension** for thread fit, because the **fit between male and female threads occurs on the flanks at the pitch diameter**, not on the crest or root. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Change-Gear Ratio for Cutting Metric Threads on an Inch Lead-Screw Lathe This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question**. The derivation is as follows. ### Step 1 — State the Requirement We want the tool to advance by **1 mm (the metric pitch)** for **1 revolution** of the workpiece. ### Step 2 — Convert Everything to a Common Unit (mm) - Pitch of thread to cut = $P$ mm. - Lead of lead screw = $\dfrac{25.4}{TPI_{ls}}$ mm (since $TPI = 25.4 / \text{pitch in mm}$). - In one revolution of the lead screw, the tool advances by $\dfrac{25.4}{TPI_{ls}}$ mm. ### Step 3 — Establish the Gear Ratio For the tool to advance $P$ mm per workpiece revolution, the lead screw must rotate such that its advance equals $P$ mm: $$ \text{Advance per workpiece rev} = \frac{25.4}{TPI_{ls}} \times \frac{\text{Driven gears}}{\text{Driving gears}} \times \text{(gearbox ratio)} $$ ### Step 4 — Simplify Assuming Gearbox Ratio = 1 For an elementary change-gear setup (no compound train): $$ P = \frac{25.4}{TPI_{ls}} \times \frac{D_{driven}}{D_{driving}} $$ ### Step 5 — Solve for the Ratio $$ \boxed{\, \frac{D_{driven}}{D_{driving}} = \frac{P \times TPI_{ls}}{25.4} \,} $$ ### Step 6 — Worked Example (KTU Standard) *Cut an **M20 × 2.5 mm** thread on a lathe with a **4 TPI** lead screw.* $$ \frac{D_{driven}}{D_{driving}} = \frac{2.5 \times 4}{25.4} = \frac{10}{25.4} \approx 0.3937 $$ Pick a gear combination that gives this ratio. Standard available gears: use **40-tooth driven** and a **compound train** like **(20 × 50) / (30 × 85)** etc. (Detailed gear selection is a numerical exercise.) --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number of Cutting Passes A KTU-favourite concept: how many passes are needed to cut a thread of depth $h$ if the first pass takes $d_1$ mm and each subsequent pass reduces by a constant decrement $r$? $$ \begin{aligned} h &= d_1 + (d_1 - r) + (d_1 - 2r) + \dots + (d_1 - (n-1)r) \\ h &= n \, d_1 - r \left(1 + 2 + 3 + \dots + (n-1)\right) \\ h &= n \, d_1 - r \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Solving the quadratic for $n$: $$ \boxed{\, n = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (2d_1 - r) + \sqrt{(2d_1 - r)^2 + 8 \, r \, h} \right] \,} $$ > [!TIP] > **Workshop rule of thumb (often used in KTU viva):** > For a 1.5 mm thread, use **6–8 passes** for soft steel, **10–12 passes** for hard steel or stainless. The last 2 passes are **spring passes** (zero infeed) to improve surface finish and dimensional accuracy. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Defects vs. Causes vs. Remedies | Defect | Probable Cause | Remedy | |---|---|---| | **Rough / torn thread** | Tool dull, feed too heavy, tool too low | Re-grind tool; reduce infeed; reset on center | | **Incorrect pitch** | Wrong gear selection / gearbox setting | Recheck calculation; re-engage at correct threading-dial line | | **Crest tearing** | Too coarse infeed in final passes | Take 2–3 **spring passes** with no cross-feed | | **Tapered thread** | Tailstock offset, tool not parallel to axis | Realign tool; offset tailstock to correct; check tool shim | | **Chasing / multi-start** | Engaged half-nut at wrong dial position | Re-engage only when threading-dial needle points to a full line | | **Tool dig-in** | Infeed on radial direction; flank rubbing | Always feed on **flank** (compound at 30°) | | **Chips wrapping on tool** | No chip-breaker groove; rake too high | Grind chip-breaker; reduce rake; use cutting fluid | | **Out-of-synchronization chatter** | Loose half-nut, worn leadscrew nut | Tighten half-nut; check lead-screw backlash | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Lathe Configured for Thread Cutting This diagram maps the **functional flow of motion and synchronization** from the spindle motor to the cutting tool tip. It is drawn as a block-level architecture (per the protocol's fallback rule) because a true physical schematic of a centre-lathe headstock involves intricate gear trains that are not faithfully reproducible in Mermaid. ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Main Motor
3 Phase Induction] --> SB[Spindle Box
Headstock Gears] SB --> SP[Spindle
Carries Chuck and Workpiece] SP --> QC[Quick Change Gearbox
QCB Levers Set per Pitch] QC --> CG[Change Gear Quadrant
Interchangeable Gears] CG --> LS[Lead Screw
Power Transmission Shaft] LS --> HALF[Half Nut in Apron
Engaged Manually] HALF --> CAR[Carriage Slides on Bedways] CAR --> CR[Compound Rest
Swiveled 30 deg] CR --> TOOL[Thread Cutting Tool
60 deg Included Angle] TOOL -. cuts helix on .-> WP[Rotating Workpiece] TD[Threading Dial
Synchronization Indicator] --> CAR SP -. drives .-> TD TS[Tailstock with Dead Centre
Supports Long Workpiece] -. supports .-> WP BED[Bedways
Provide Linear Slide] --> CAR style M fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style SB fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff style SP fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff style QC fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff style CG fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff style LS fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style HALF fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style CAR fill:#374151,color:#ffffff style CR fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff style TOOL fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style WP fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style TD fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff ``` **How to read this diagram:** - The **left side** (blue/teal) is the **rotational power path** — motor → spindle → workpiece. - The **middle (brown/amber)** is the **gear-reduction path** — spindle → quick-change gearbox → change gears → lead screw. - The **right side (purple/red)** is the **linear-feed path** — lead screw → half-nut → carriage → compound rest → tool. - The **cyan block (threading dial)** is the **synchronizer** — it tells the operator *when* to engage the half-nut so the tool re-enters the existing groove. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Step-by-Step Thread Cutting Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Study Drawing
Note Thread Type Pitch and Length] --> B[Step 2: Mount and True Workpiece
Use 3 Jaw Chuck] B --> C[Step 3: Select and Mount Tool
60 deg HSS Single Point] C --> D[Step 4: Set Compound Rest at 30 deg
For Flank Feeding] D --> E[Step 5: Calculate and Set Change Gears
Using Pitch Formula] E --> F[Step 6: Set Quick Change Gearbox Levers
Per Chart] F --> G[Step 7: Touch Tool to Workpiece
Set Zero on Cross Slide] G --> H[Step 8: Withdraw Tool
Engage Half Nut at Threading Dial] H --> I[Step 9: Run Carriage to Start Position
Leave Clearance] I --> J[Step 10: Disengage Half Nut
Bring Tool Forward by Infeed] J --> K[Step 11: Engage Half Nut Again
Run Cut to End] K --> L{Thread at Correct Depth?} L -->|No| M[Take Another Pass
Reduce Infeed by 0.05 mm] M --> K L -->|Yes| N[Take 2 Spring Passes
Zero Infeed] N --> O[Withdraw Cross Slide
Stop Spindle] O --> P[Check with Thread Gauge
