Measure Pitch Diameter] P --> Q[Part Off if Required
Use Parting Tool] Q --> R[Done] style A fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style B fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style C fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style D fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style E fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style F fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style G fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff style H fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff style I fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff style J fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff style K fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff style L fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff style M fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff style N fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style O fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style P fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style Q fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style R fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cross-Section of a 60° V-Thread (Geometry Reference) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["Sharp V Thread Geometry Cross Section"] direction TB CREST["Crest Outer Diameter d
Truncated by H slash 8"] PITCHD["Pitch Diameter d2
Flank Contact Cylinder"] MINORD["Minor Diameter d1
Root Truncated by H slash 4"] PITCH["Horizontal Pitch P
Between Adjacent Crest Points"] HALFANG["Half Angle 30 deg
Each Flank with Vertical"] HDEPTH["Theoretical Depth h = 0.6134 P
Measured Radially from Crest to Root"] end CREST --> PITCHD --> MINORD PITCH -. bottom .-> MINORD HALFANG -. flank .-> PITCHD HDEPTH -. axis .-> CREST style CREST fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000 style PITCHD fill:#22c55e,color:#000000 style MINORD fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff style PITCH fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff style HALFANG fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff style HDEPTH fill:#14b8a6,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#f3f4f6,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The thread is bounded by **three diameters** (crest = $d$, pitch = $d_2$, root = $d_1$) separated radially by fractions of the **theoretical depth $h$**. The **flanks** make an angle of **30° with the vertical axis** (= half of the 60° included angle). The **pitch $P$** is the horizontal repeat distance between two crests. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Differentiate between Pitch and Lead of a screw thread. When are they equal?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Pitch (P) | Lead (L) | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Distance between two adjacent thread crests measured parallel to the axis. | Distance the nut (or tool) advances axially in **one full revolution** of the screw. | | **Symbol** | $P$ | $L$ | | **For single-start thread** | $P = L$ | $P = L$ | | **For multi-start thread** | $L = N_{starts} \times P$ | Same formula | | **Unit** | mm or TPI⁻¹ | mm or TPI⁻¹ | **When are they equal?** **Pitch and lead are equal for a single-start thread.** For a multi-start thread (e.g., 2-start, 3-start), the lead is an integer multiple of the pitch. **3-mark split (KTU pattern):** Definition of pitch — **1 mark**; definition of lead — **1 mark**; equality condition with example — **1 mark**. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Why is the compound rest of a lathe set at 30° (or 29°) during thread cutting?** **Model Answer:** The compound rest is set at **half the included angle of the thread** so that the cutting tool feeds along **one flank of the V-thread** instead of radially. For a standard ISO metric thread with included angle **60°**, the half-angle is **30°** (some textbooks use **29°** to compensate for practical clearance). **Three advantages of this "flank-feeding" technique:** 1. **Only one cutting edge is engaged** at a time → cleaner chip formation, no rubbing on the finished flank. 2. **Chips curl away** from the already-cut surface → better surface finish on the working flank. 3. **Reduced cutting force and chatter** → improved tool life and dimensional accuracy. > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Writing "30° is the thread angle." This is **wrong** — the thread angle is **60°**; the compound rest is set to **half** of that. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this confusion. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Explain, with neat sketches, the **procedure for cutting an external V-thread on a lathe**. Mention the tool geometry, compound-rest setting, and the role of the **threading dial**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A metric thread **M 30 × 3.5 mm** is to be cut on a lathe whose lead screw has **4 TPI**. Calculate: **(i)** the theoretical depth of thread, **(ii)** the minor diameter, and **(iii)** the required change-gear ratio (assume a single-pair gear train). **(7 marks)** --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B-A #### Part (a) — Procedure and Tool Setup (7 marks) **1. Workpiece Preparation (1 mark)** - Mount the cylindrical workpiece in a **3-jaw self-centering chuck** (or 4-jaw chuck for non-circular blanks). - Use a **dial indicator** to check run-out — it should be **< 0.02 mm**. - For long workpieces, support the free end on a **dead centre** in the tailstock. **2. Tool Selection and Grinding (2 marks)** - Use a **high-speed-steel (HSS) single-point thread-cutting tool** (e.g., M2 or M42 grade). - Grind the **included angle to 60° ± 10′** for ISO metric thread. - Provide a **top (radial) rake of 0° to 5°**. - Provide a **side (clearance) angle of 1° to 1.5°** to prevent flank rubbing. - Honed cutting edge ensures surface finish. **3. Tool Setting on the Lathe (1 mark)** - Set the tool tip **exactly on the centre line** of the workpiece (use a **centre gauge** or **gauge block**). - Mount the tool with the **cutting edge parallel to the axis of the lead screw** (use a **travelling angle gauge**). - Set the **compound rest swivel to 30°** (= $\alpha/2$ for 60° thread) and lock it. **4. Gear and Speed Setup (1 mark)** - Set the **quick-change gearbox levers** to the position that gives a feed close to the required pitch. - Calculate the **change-gear ratio** using the formula (Part b of this question). - Mount the change gears on the **tumbler-reverse and gear-quadrant** with the correct **intermediate gear** (idler). - Select a **low spindle speed** — typically **15 to 40 RPM** for threading. **5. Threading Dial and First Cut (1 mark)** - Touch the tool tip to the workpiece surface and **set the cross-slide graduated collar to zero**. - Withdraw the cross slide by ~0.5 mm. - Move the carriage to the **starting position** (leaving ~2–3 mm clearance before the thread length). - **Engage the half-nut** only when the **threading dial** pointer is **exactly on a full line** (for metric threads, only full-line positions are valid; for inch threads, any line is acceptable). **6. Successive Passes and Spring Passes (1 mark)** - Take successive cuts, **decreasing the infeed** by **0.05 mm per pass** (for soft steel) down to **0.02 mm**. - When the thread is **nearly at depth**, take **2–3 spring passes** with **zero infeed** to refine the surface. - Stop the spindle, withdraw the cross slide, **disengage the half-nut**, and return the carriage. **7. Inspection (not for marks, but viva)** - Check with a **thread pitch gauge** (leaf gauge) and a **screw pitch micrometer** (or **three-wire method** for pitch diameter). #### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** - Thread: **M 30 × 3.5 mm** (nominal diameter $d = 30$ mm, pitch $P = 3.5$ mm). - Lead screw: **4 TPI** (i.e., 4 threads per inch). **(i) Theoretical depth of thread:** (2 marks) $$ h = 0.6134 \times P = 0.6134 \times 3.5 = 2.1469 \text{ mm} $$ **Answer:** $h \approx 2.147$ mm. **(ii) Minor diameter:** (2 marks) $$ d_1 = d - 1.2268 \, P = 30 - 1.2268 \times 3.5 $$ $$ d_1 = 30 - 4.2938 = 25.7062 \text{ mm} $$ **Answer:** $d_1 \approx 25.71$ mm. **(iii) Change-gear ratio:** (3 marks) First, convert the lead-screw pitch to mm: $$ P_{ls} = \frac{25.4}{TPI} = \frac{25.4}{4} = 6.35 \text{ mm} $$ The ratio formula for cutting a metric thread on an inch-threaded lead screw: $$ \frac{D_{driven}}{D_{driving}} = \frac{P_{thread}}{P_{ls}} = \frac{3.5}{6.35} $$ $$ \boxed{\, \frac{D_{driven}}{D_{driving}} = 0.5512 \,} $$ **Practical gear selection (1 mark bonus):** Use a **compound gear train** since this is less than 1. For example: - A driver gear of **40 teeth** and a driven gear of **22 teeth** gives ratio 0.55 (close to 0.5512). - For exact ratio, use a **trio of gears** (e.g., 50/65 × 40/55 = 0.559) or a vernier dial on the lathe's quick-change gearbox. **Final gear combination example:** **Driver = 80, Driven = 44** → ratio = 0.55 (slight error of 0.22% is acceptable in workshop practice). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. Forgetting to **convert TPI of lead screw to mm** — loses **2 marks** out of 3 in part (b)(iii). > 2. Using the **nominal diameter 30 mm** as the thread depth reference — the formula for $d_1$ is $d - 1.2268P$, not $d - P$. > 3. Not stating the **practical depth** in part (b)(i) — for workshop cutting, the depth used is **0.6P to 0.65P**, not the sharp-V depth. > 4. Writing "**change gear ratio = pitch**" without showing the **gear-train equation** — examiners expect the explicit formula, not just the number. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** What are the different **types of thread-cutting defects** observed in lathe operations? List **at least six defects** with their **causes and remedies**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **geometry of an ISO metric screw thread**. Derive the expression for the **theoretical depth of a sharp V-thread** and the **minor diameter**. **(7 marks)** --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B-B #### Part (a) — Six Thread-Cutting Defects (7 marks) *(Distribute marks: 1 mark per defect + 0.5 mark for the remedy = 1.5 × 4 fully-correct = 6 marks; 1 mark for additional/bonus defects.)* | # | Defect | Cause | Remedy | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Rough / torn thread surface** | Dull tool, excessive infeed, insufficient cutting fluid | Re-grind tool, reduce infeed, use sulphurized cutting oil | | 2 | **Incorrect pitch (wrong TPI / mm pitch)** | Wrong gear selection, gearbox lever mis-set | Recheck calculation; verify dial position | | 3 | **Chasing / multi-starting** | Half-nut engaged at wrong threading-dial position | Engage only at full line; ensure tool re-enters groove | | 4 | **Tapered thread** | Tailstock offset, tool not parallel to axis | Realign tool; check tailstock alignment | | 5 | **Crest tearing / flaking** | Too coarse infeed in last passes; tool rubs on flank | Take spring passes; flank-feed instead of radial feed | | 6 | **Tool dig-in / breakage** | Radial infeed; chip packing | Always feed on flank (compound at 30°); use chip-breaker | | 7 | **Out-of-synchronization chatter** | Worn lead-screw nut; loose half-nut | Tighten half-nut; check lead-screw nut condition | | 8 | **Burrs at thread end** | Tool overrun at end of cut | Use chamfered tool; add relief at end; use shoulder stop | #### Part (b) — Geometry and Derivation (7 marks) **Geometry of ISO Metric Thread (3 marks):** - **Included angle (α):** **60°** (V-thread). - **Flank angle (each flank with vertical):** **30°**. - **Pitch (P):** Distance between two adjacent crests. - **Truncation:** Crest is truncated by $H/8$, root by $H/4$ (where $H = 0.866 \, P$). - **Theoretical depth (h):** from crest tip to root tip of the sharp V. - **Diameters:** Major ($d$), Pitch ($d_2$), Minor ($d_1$). **Derivation of Theoretical Depth (2 marks):** Show the perpendicular from the V-vertex to the base: $$ \tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{P/2}{h} $$ $$ h = \frac{P}{2 \tan(30°)} = \frac{P}{2 \times 0.5774} = 0.866 \, P $$ *(Wait — this gives the un-truncated depth. The ISO standard then **truncates** the V at crest and root, reducing the effective depth to:)* $$ h_{ISO} = 0.6134 \, P $$ *(For full marks, students should show the truncation step.)* **Derivation of Minor Diameter (2 marks):** $$ d_1 = d - 2h = d - 2 \times 0.6134 \, P = d - 1.2268 \, P $$ **Substitution example (for clarity):** For M20 × 2.5 mm: $$ d_1 = 20 - 1.2268 \times 2.5 = 20 - 3.067 = 16.933 \text{ mm} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (All-Question Type) > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks in thread-cutting questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion** — TPI ↔ mm conversions catch ~40% of students. > 2. **Confusing pitch and lead** — pitch is between adjacent crests; lead is per revolution. Marks deducted for each wrong usage. > 3. **Not specifying the change-gear ratio equation** — examiners expect the **formula** in addition to the numerical answer. > 4. **Using sharp-V depth in practical calculations** — workshop depth is **0.6P to 0.65P**, not 0.6134P. The 0.6134 constant is **theoretical only**. > 5. **Skipping the threading-dial explanation** — in any procedure question, the **synchronization step** is worth **at least 1 mark** and is often missed. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Thread angle (ISO metric)** = **60°**; BSP / BSW = **55°**; ACME = **29°**. - [x] **Compound rest** is set to **half the thread angle** (= **30°** for 60° thread). - [x] **Theoretical depth** $h = 0.6134 \, P$; **practical depth** $h = 0.6 \, P$ to $0.65 \, P$. - [x] **Minor diameter** $d_1 = d - 1.2268 \, P$. - [x] **Pitch diameter** $d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, P$ (most important for thread fit). - [x] **Lead = Pitch × Number of starts**; for single-start, **Lead = Pitch**. - [x] **Change-gear ratio for metric on inch lathe:** $D_{driven}/D_{driving} = P_{thread}/P_{ls}$, where $P_{ls} = 25.4/TPI_{ls}$. - [x] **Threading dial:** Metric threads — engage only at **full lines**; Inch threads — any line. - [x] **Spring passes** (2–3 passes with zero infeed) are essential for surface finish. - [x] **Infeed direction** = **flank feed** (compound at 30°), not radial — gives cleaner cut, better chip flow. - [x] **Spindle speed** for threading is **low** (15–40 RPM for typical workpieces). - [x] **Tool material:** **HSS** (M2 / M42 grade) is standard; carbide is used for production CNC, not hand lathe. - [x] **Cutting fluid:** **Sulphurized cutting oil** (for steel); **soluble oil** (for aluminium, copper, brass). - [x] **Three-wire method** is the standard for **measuring pitch diameter** accurately. - [x] **Causes of failure in exam:** missing unit conversion, wrong formula for $d_1$, ignoring threading-dial, confusing pitch/lead, not sketching the geometry.

topics.title: Exercises on Lathe: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Turning Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Lathe: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Turning Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cutting Force in Turning:** The force generated at the tool-workpiece interface during material removal on a lathe, resulting from the relative motion between the cutting tool and the rotating workpiece. It is resolved into three orthogonal components along mutually perpendicular axes.

In orthogonal cutting reference frame (Merchant's convention), the resultant cutting force **F** is resolved into three measurable components:

| Component | Symbol | Direction | Physical Action |
|-----------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Tangential (Main) Cutting Force** | **F<sub>c</sub>** | Direction of cutting velocity (tangential to workpiece surface) | Performs the actual material cutting. Largest magnitude. |
| **Feed (Axial/Longitudinal) Force** | **F<sub>f</sub>** | Along the feed direction (longitudinal, parallel to workpiece axis) | Overcomes friction under the tool flank; resists feed motion. |
| **Radial (Thrust/Deflection) Force** | **F<sub>r</sub>** | Perpendicular to the machined surface (radial, pushing tool away from workpiece) | Causes tool deflection, chatter, and affects surface integrity. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Resultant Force Relation:** $F = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_r^2}$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are peeling a potato with a vegetable peeler. As the blade slices through the potato skin:

- The **force your hand pushes along the blade (sideways motion)** corresponds to **F<sub>c</sub>** — this is the force doing the "cutting" work.
- The **forward pushing force (advancing the blade along the potato)** corresponds to **F<sub>f</sub>** — this moves the tool forward against friction.
- The **inward pressing force (pressing blade into potato)** corresponds to **F<sub>r</sub>** — this keeps the blade engaged with the surface.

In a lathe, the **workpiece rotates** (replacing your sideways slicing motion) and the **tool advances linearly** (the carriage feed). The cutting tool's geometry (rake angle, nose radius) and material properties dictate how the potato-equivalent "chip" curls and separates.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook — "CFR" Rule:** Cutting (tangential) force is always the **C**ompounding largest, Feed force is medium, Radial force is usually the smallest. Remember: **C > F > R** in magnitude under standard turning conditions.

## 1.3 Need for Measuring Cutting Forces (Why This Experiment?)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cutting force measurement is essential for:
> 1. **Machine tool design** — sizing spindles, slides, and drives to withstand operational loads.
> 2. **Power calculation** — determining motor rating and energy efficiency.
> 3. **Tool life prediction** — empirical models (Taylor's equation) link force to wear.
> 4. **Process optimization** — selecting cutting parameters (v, f, d) for minimum cost.
> 5. **Vibration & chatter control** — radial force directly affects dynamic stability.
> 6. **Validation of analytical models** (Merchant, Ernst–Merchant, Lee–Shaffer).

## 1.4 Units and Standard Metrics

- **SI Unit of Force:** **Newton (N)**; commonly expressed in **kN** for heavy-duty machining.
- **Specific Cutting Energy:** **N/mm²** or **J/mm³** (energy per unit volume removed).
- **Cutting Power:** **kW**.
- **Standard Reference:** **ISO 3685** (single-point turning tools with carbide and ceramic inserts).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Chip Formation (Background Theory)

When a tool advances into a rotating workpiece, the material ahead of the tool undergoes **plastic deformation** along a thin zone called the **primary shear zone** (inclined at the **shear angle $\phi$**). The chip then slides along the **tool face (rake face)**, generating friction in the **secondary deformation zone**.

### Step-by-Step Physics of Force Generation:

1. **Workpiece rotates** at cutting velocity $v_c = \pi D N / 1000$ m/min.
2. **Tool is fed** at rate $f$ mm/rev, creating an **uncut chip thickness $t_1 = f \sin \kappa_r$** (where $\kappa_r$ is the tool cutting edge angle, usually 75°).
3. Material plastically shears along a plane inclined at **shear angle $\phi$** to the cutting velocity direction.
4. The **resultant force R** acts on the tool face, inclined at **friction angle $\beta$** to the normal of the rake face.
5. **R** is resolved along the tool reference frame to give **F<sub>c</sub>, F<sub>f</sub>, F<sub>r</sub>**.

## 2.2 Merchant's Force Circle (Orthogonal Cutting Model)

For **orthogonal cutting** (tool edge perpendicular to cutting velocity — an idealization of turning), the **Merchant Circle** provides a graphical and analytical relationship between:

- Cutting force **F<sub>c</sub>**
- Thrust (feed) force **F<sub>t</sub>**
- Shear force **F<sub>s</sub>**
- Normal force on shear plane **F<sub>n</sub>**
- Friction force **F**
- Normal force on tool face **N**
- Resultant force **R**
- Rake angle **$\alpha$**, shear angle **$\phi$**, friction angle **$\beta$**

### Key Force Balance Equations:

- $F_s = F_c \cos \phi - F_t \sin \phi$
- $F_n = F_c \sin \phi + F_t \cos \phi$
- $F = F_n \sin \beta$
- $N = F_n \cos \beta$
- $R = F_s / \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$
- $F_c = R \cos(\beta - \alpha)$
- $F_t = R \sin(\beta - \alpha)$

## 2.3 Shear Angle Relationship (Merchant's Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Merchant's Shear Angle Equation:**
> $$\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}$$

This is the foundational equation linking **rake angle**, **friction angle**, and **shear angle**, derived by minimizing cutting energy.

An **extended form** (Lee–Shaffer) for oblique cutting is:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{r_c \cos \alpha}{1 - r_c \sin \alpha}$$

where $r_c$ is the **chip thickness ratio**: $r_c = t_1 / t_2$ (uncut chip thickness to chip thickness).

## 2.4 Empirical Force Model (Kienzle's Equation — Most Used in Industry)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kienzle's Specific Cutting Force Equation:**
> $$F_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot b \cdot h^{1 - m_c}$$
> - $k_{c1.1}$ = **specific cutting force at $h = 1$ mm chip thickness, $b = 1$ mm chip width** (N/mm²)
> - $h$ = **uncut chip thickness (mm)**
> - $b$ = **chip width (mm)** = depth of cut $d$ for 90° tool
> - $m_c$ = **Kienzle exponent** (typically 0.20–0.35 for steels)

**Feed Force Component:**
$$F_f \approx (0.3 \text{ to } 0.5) \cdot F_c$$

**Radial Force Component:**
$$F_r \approx (0.2 \text{ to } 0.4) \cdot F_c$$

## 2.5 Power and Energy Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cutting Power Equation:**
> $$P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot v_c}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(kW)}$$
> where $v_c$ is in m/min and $F_c$ in N.

**Specific Cutting Energy:**
$$u = \frac{P_c}{Q} = \frac{F_c \cdot v_c}{v_c \cdot f \cdot d \cdot 1000} = \frac{F_c}{1000 \cdot f \cdot d} \quad \text{(J/mm³ or N/mm²)}$$

where $Q$ is the material removal rate (MRR): $Q = v_c \cdot f \cdot d / 1000$ mm³/s.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $v_c = \pi D N / 1000$ | Cutting speed | m/min |
| 2 | $F = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_r^2}$ | Resultant force | N |
| 3 | $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ | Merchant's shear angle | degrees |
| 4 | $F_c = \tau_s \cdot A_s = \tau_s \cdot \frac{b \cdot t_1}{\sin \phi}$ | Cutting force via shear | N |
| 5 | $r_c = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$ | Chip thickness ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $F_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot b \cdot h^{1-m_c}$ | Kienzle's force model | N |
| 7 | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_c}{60{,}000}$ | Cutting power | kW |
| 8 | $u = \dfrac{F_c}{1000 \cdot f \cdot d}$ | Specific cutting energy | N/mm² |
| 9 | $MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot d / 1000$ | Material removal rate | mm³/s |
| 10 | $\tan \beta = \dfrac{F_f}{F_c}$ | Approx. friction angle | degrees |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Kienzle's equation is used in **CNC programming** and **CAM software (Mastercam, NX, CATIA)** to predict cutting forces before machining. It directly drives **spindle load**, **feed rate optimization**, and **tool path planning** in modern smart factories (Industry 4.0).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Cutting, Thrust, Shear, and Normal Forces

### Setup (Orthogonal Cutting)
- Tool moves with velocity $V$ against a stationary workpiece (or vice versa).
- Rake angle: $\alpha$ (positive for sharp tools).
- Shear plane inclined at $\phi$ to cutting velocity.
- Friction angle: $\beta$ on the tool face.

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of the Chip

The chip is acted upon by four forces in equilibrium:
- $F_s$ along the shear plane (shear force).
- $F_n$ normal to the shear plane.
- $F$ along the tool face (friction force).
- $N$ normal to the tool face.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolving along cutting direction (V direction):} \quad & F_c = F_s \cos \phi + F_n \sin \phi \\
\text{Resolving perpendicular to cutting direction:} \quad & F_t = F_s \sin \phi - F_n \cos \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $F_s$ and $F_n$ in terms of $F_c$, $F_t$

Inverting the matrix by multiplying first equation by $\cos \phi$ and second by $\sin \phi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c \cos \phi &= F_s \cos^2 \phi + F_n \sin \phi \cos \phi \\
F_t \sin \phi &= F_s \sin^2 \phi - F_n \cos \phi \sin \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding the two equations (the $F_n$ terms cancel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c \cos \phi + F_t \sin \phi &= F_s (\cos^2 \phi + \sin^2 \phi) \\
F_c \cos \phi + F_t \sin \phi &= F_s
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{F_s = F_c \cos \phi - F_t \sin \phi}
$$

Similarly, multiplying the first equation by $\sin \phi$ and the second by $\cos \phi$, then adding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c \sin \phi &= F_s \cos \phi \sin \phi + F_n \sin^2 \phi \\
-F_t \cos \phi &= -F_s \sin \phi \cos \phi + F_n \cos^2 \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding:

$$
\boxed{F_n = F_c \sin \phi + F_t \cos \phi}
$$

### Step 3 — Resolve $F_n$ into $F$ and $N$ using friction angle $\beta$

The resultant of $F$ and $N$ equals $R$ (the tool-chip reaction force):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= F_n \sin \beta \\
N &= F_n \cos \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Express $R$ and relate to $F_c$, $F_t$

From the Merchant circle, $R$ is the resultant and makes angle $(\beta - \alpha)$ with the cutting direction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= R \cos(\beta - \alpha) \\
F_t &= R \sin(\beta - \alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{F_c}{\cos(\beta - \alpha)} = \frac{F_s}{\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Merchant's Shear Angle Equation (Energy Minimization)

### Step 1 — Express $F_s$ in terms of $F_c$ and $F_t$

From above: $F_s = F_c \cos \phi - F_t \sin \phi$.

### Step 2 — Express $F_c$ and $F_t$ in terms of $R$

Using $F_c = R \cos(\beta - \alpha)$ and $F_t = R \sin(\beta - \alpha)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= R \cos(\beta - \alpha) \cos \phi - R \sin(\beta - \alpha) \sin \phi \\
F_s &= R \left[ \cos(\beta - \alpha) \cos \phi - \sin(\beta - \alpha) \sin \phi \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the cosine addition formula $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$
\boxed{F_s = R \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}
$$

### Step 3 — Shear Plane Area

The shear plane area is:

$$
A_s = \frac{b \cdot t_1}{\sin \phi}
$$

where $b$ = chip width, $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness.

### Step 4 — Cutting Energy Per Unit Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{F_s \cdot V_s}{V \cdot b \cdot t_1} \\
\text{where } V_s &= \frac{V \cos \alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} \text{ (velocity along shear plane)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplifying using $V_s / V$ ratio:

$$
U = \frac{\tau_s}{\sin \phi} \cdot \frac{\cos \alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

where $\tau_s$ is the shear flow stress on the shear plane.

### Step 5 — Minimize $U$ with respect to $\phi$

Setting $dU/d\phi = 0$ yields the famous **Merchant's equation**:

$$
\boxed{\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- **Higher positive rake angle $\alpha$** → **larger $\phi$** → thinner chip → less force.
- **Higher friction angle $\beta$** (more friction on tool face) → **smaller $\phi$** → thicker chip → more force.
- This is why **coated tools, high-pressure coolant, and polished rake faces** reduce cutting force.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical)

**Problem:** In an orthogonal turning operation on mild steel, the following are measured:
- Rake angle $\alpha = 10°$
- Friction angle $\beta = 30°$
- Uncut chip thickness $t_1 = 0.3$ mm
- Chip thickness $t_2 = 0.5$ mm
- Width of cut $b = 4$ mm
- Shear strength $\tau_s = 400$ MPa

**Find:** (a) Shear angle $\phi$ (b) Cutting force $F_c$ (c) Thrust force $F_t$ (d) Friction force $F$

### Solution:

**(a) Shear angle using chip thickness ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_c &= \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.3}{0.5} = 0.6 \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{r_c \cos \alpha}{1 - r_c \sin \alpha} = \frac{0.6 \cos 10°}{1 - 0.6 \sin 10°} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.6 \times 0.9848}{1 - 0.6 \times 0.1736} = \frac{0.5909}{0.8958} = 0.6596 \\
\phi &= \tan^{-1}(0.6596) = 33.4°
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Shear plane area:**

$$
A_s = \frac{b \cdot t_1}{\sin \phi} = \frac{4 \times 0.3}{\sin 33.4°} = \frac{1.2}{0.5505} = 2.180 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Shear force:**

$$
F_s = \tau_s \cdot A_s = 400 \times 2.180 = 872 \text{ N}
$$

**(c) Friction angle relation: $\beta = \phi + \beta - \alpha - \phi = ...$ — Compute $F_c$ and $F_t$ via $F_s$:**

Using $F_s = R \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{F_s}{\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)} = \frac{872}{\cos(33.4° + 30° - 10°)} \\
R &= \frac{872}{\cos(53.4°)} = \frac{872}{0.5962} = 1462.6 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= R \cos(\beta - \alpha) = 1462.6 \times \cos(20°) = 1462.6 \times 0.9397 = 1374.4 \text{ N} \\
F_t &= R \sin(\beta - \alpha) = 1462.6 \times \sin(20°) = 1462.6 \times 0.3420 = 500.2 \text{ N} \\
F &= F_n \sin \beta, \text{ where } F_n = R \sin(\beta - \alpha + \phi - \alpha) \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

Alternative direct method using $\tan \beta = F/F_c \Rightarrow$ friction force estimation is covered in theory classes.

> [!TIP]
> **Check:** Force ratio $F_t / F_c = 500.2 / 1374.4 \approx 0.364$ — typical value for steel turning, lies in expected range (0.3–0.5).

## 3.4 KTU 2024 Numerical Problem Pattern (Worked)

**Problem:** A turning operation is conducted with the following parameters:
- Workpiece diameter $D = 60$ mm
- Spindle speed $N = 1000$ rpm
- Feed $f = 0.2$ mm/rev
- Depth of cut $d = 2$ mm
- Specific cutting energy $u = 2.5$ N·m/mm³

**Find:** (a) Cutting speed (b) MRR (c) Cutting force (d) Cutting power

### Solution:

**(a) Cutting speed:**

$$
v_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 60 \times 1000}{1000} = 188.5 \text{ m/min}
$$

**(b) Material removal rate:**

$$
MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot d / 1000 = 188.5 \times 0.2 \times 2 / 1000 = 0.0754 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

Converting $v_c$ to mm/s: $v_c = 188.5 \times 1000 / 60 = 3141.6$ mm/s.

So $MRR = 3141.6 \times 0.2 \times 2 = 1256.6$ mm³/s (equivalent).

**(c) Cutting force from specific energy:**

$$
F_c = u \times f \times d \times 1000 = 2.5 \times 0.2 \times 2 \times 1000 = 1000 \text{ N}
$$

(Note: dimensional check — specific energy is J/mm³ = N/mm².)

**(d) Cutting power:**

$$
P_c = \frac{F_c \times v_c}{60{,}000} = \frac{1000 \times 188.5}{60{,}000} = 3.14 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** A 3 kW motor is sufficient. Including machine efficiency (typically 70–80%), the required motor rating is ~4 kW.

## 4.4 Description of the Experimental Lathe Setup

> [!NOTE]
> **Component Layout (Descriptive Block):**
> - **Workpiece:** Mounted in lathe chuck, rotating at controlled RPM via headstock spindle motor.
> - **Cutting Tool:** Single-point HSS or carbide tool held in a tool post mounted on a **3-component piezoelectric dynamometer**.
> - **Dynamometer:** Bolted under the tool post; senses forces in **X (feed), Y (radial), Z (tangential)** directions.
> - **Charge Amplifier:** Converts the high-impedance piezoelectric charge signal into a low-impedance voltage signal.
> - **DAQ System:** National Instruments / HBM / Kistler data acquisition card samples at 1–10 kHz.
> - **Display:** Real-time plots of $F_c$, $F_f$, $F_r$ versus time; export to CSV for further analysis.
> - **Coolant nozzle** is positioned to flood the cutting zone (to minimize thermal distortion of force readings).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the three components of cutting force in a turning operation. State which component is the largest in magnitude and justify why.**

**Model Answer:**

The three orthogonal components of cutting force in turning are:

1. **Tangential (Main) Cutting Force $F_c$:** Acts in the direction of cutting velocity, i.e., tangentially to the workpiece surface. It performs the actual material removal and is the **largest** of the three components.
2. **Feed (Longitudinal) Force $F_f$:** Acts along the feed direction, parallel to the axis of the workpiece. It resists the tool's axial advancement.
3. **Radial (Thrust) Force $F_r$:** Acts perpendicular to the machined surface, pushing the tool away from the workpiece. It causes tool deflection.

**Justification for $F_c$ being the largest:**
- $F_c$ acts in the direction of the largest velocity component (cutting velocity) and is the primary force overcoming the **shear strength** of the workpiece material.
- The relation $P_c = F_c \cdot v_c$ confirms that $F_c$ directly determines the cutting power required, which is the dominant energy consumer in the process.
- Typically, $F_c \approx (2 \text{ to } 5) F_f$ and $F_c \approx (2.5 \text{ to } 6) F_r$ for steel turning.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is Merchant's Circle? List the forces and angles involved in it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Merchant's Circle** is a graphical-analytical construction used in **orthogonal cutting theory** to relate the various forces, velocities, and angles at the tool-chip interface in a turning process. It provides a closed-form solution to compute cutting and thrust forces from measurable quantities (rake angle, friction angle, and chip thickness ratio).

**Forces in Merchant's Circle:**
- $F_c$ — Cutting force (along cutting velocity)
- $F_t$ — Thrust force (perpendicular to cutting velocity)
- $F_s$ — Shear force (along shear plane)
- $F_n$ — Normal force on shear plane
- $F$ — Friction force on tool face
- $N$ — Normal force on tool face
- $R$ — Resultant force

**Angles:**
- $\alpha$ — Rake angle
- $\phi$ — Shear angle
- $\beta$ — Friction angle

The key relationship: $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **Merchant's shear angle equation** $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ starting from the orthogonal cutting force diagram. Clearly state the assumptions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] In an orthogonal turning experiment, the following readings are recorded:
- Uncut chip thickness $t_1 = 0.25$ mm
- Chip thickness $t_2 = 0.45$ mm
- Width of cut $b = 3$ mm
- Tool rake angle $\alpha = 12°$
- Cutting force $F_c = 1200$ N
- Thrust force $F_t = 480$ N

**Compute:** (i) Chip thickness ratio (ii) Shear angle (iii) Friction angle (iv) Cutting power if cutting speed is 150 m/min.

#### Model Solution for Q-A:

**(a) Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
- Orthogonal cutting (single straight cutting edge, 90° cutting edge angle).
- Continuous chip with uniform thickness.
- Tool is sharp (no flank wear).
- Shear plane is a single thin plane of constant thickness.
- Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys a single shear flow stress.

**Steps (as derived in Section 3.2 above):**
- Force balance on the chip gives $F_s = R \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$.
- Shear plane area: $A_s = b t_1 / \sin \phi$.
- Shear velocity relation: $V_s = V \cos \alpha / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$.
- Cutting energy per unit volume: $U = (\tau_s / \sin \phi) \cdot [\cos \alpha / \cos(\phi - \alpha)]$.
- Minimize $U$ w.r.t. $\phi$: set $dU/d\phi = 0$.
- This yields $\boxed{2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°}$, or $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Chip thickness ratio:**

$$
r_c = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.25}{0.45} = 0.5556
$$

**(ii) Shear angle (using chip thickness formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \phi &= \frac{r_c \cos \alpha}{1 - r_c \sin \alpha} = \frac{0.5556 \cos 12°}{1 - 0.5556 \sin 12°} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5556 \times 0.9781}{1 - 0.5556 \times 0.2079} = \frac{0.5434}{0.8845} = 0.6144 \\
\phi &= \tan^{-1}(0.6144) = 31.56° \approx 31.6°
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Friction angle (from Merchant's equation rearranged):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= 90° - 2\phi + \alpha = 90° - 2(31.56°) + 12° = 90° - 63.12° + 12° = 38.88° \\
\tan \beta &= \tan 38.88° = 0.806
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification from force ratio:**

$$
\tan \beta_{approx} = \frac{F_t}{F_c} = \frac{480}{1200} = 0.40 \Rightarrow \beta_{approx} = 21.8° \text{ (lower bound; the difference accounts for rake angle effects)}
$$

**Accurate $\beta$ using $F_c = R\cos(\beta - \alpha)$, $F_t = R\sin(\beta - \alpha)$:**

$$
\tan(\beta - \alpha) = \frac{F_t}{F_c} = \frac{480}{1200} = 0.4 \Rightarrow \beta - \alpha = 21.8° \Rightarrow \beta = 33.8°
$$

The slight discrepancy (33.8° vs 38.88°) is due to real cutting effects not in the ideal model.

**(iv) Cutting power:**

$$
P_c = \frac{F_c \times v_c}{60{,}000} = \frac{1200 \times 150}{60{,}000} = 3.0 \text{ kW}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a 3-component piezoelectric lathe dynamometer** used to measure cutting forces. List its advantages and limitations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A CNC turning operation uses a carbide tool on mild steel. The following data is given:
- Workpiece diameter: 80 mm (initial)
- Spindle speed: 800 rpm
- Feed: 0.25 mm/rev
- Depth of cut: 1.5 mm
- Kienzle's specific cutting force $k_{c1.1} = 2200$ N/mm²
- Kienzle exponent $m_c = 0.25$

**Calculate:** (i) Uncut chip thickness $h$ and width $b$ (assume $\kappa_r = 75°$) (ii) Cutting force $F_c$ using Kienzle's equation (iii) Material removal rate (iv) Specific cutting energy.

#### Model Solution for Q-B:

**(a) 3-Component Piezoelectric Dynamometer:**

**Construction:**
- It consists of **three sets of piezoelectric sensors** (typically quartz or PZT crystals) mounted in a **preloaded cylindrical housing**.
- The four sensors are placed at 90° intervals around the central axis.
- The tool holder is mounted on the top platform; the bottom platform is bolted to the lathe cross-slide.
- Preload bolts keep the crystals under compression so that both tensile and compressive forces can be measured.

**Working Principle:**
- When cutting forces act on the tool, the housing deforms slightly.
- This deformation is transmitted to the piezo crystals, generating proportional **electrical charge** ($Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric coefficient).
- Each pair of crystals senses force along one axis: tangential, feed, or radial.
- The charges are converted to voltages by **charge amplifiers** and recorded by a DAQ system.
- The three components are computed in real time:
  - $F_c = $ Output of tangential pair
  - $F_f = $ Output of feed pair
  - $F_r = $ Output of radial pair

**Advantages:**
- High **stiffness** → very small deflection (< 10 µm) → minimal interference with cutting.
- Excellent **frequency response** (DC to 10 kHz) → can capture dynamic cutting events.
- High **sensitivity** and good **linearity**.
- **Compact** and can fit in standard tool posts.
- Measures **all three force components simultaneously**.

**Limitations:**
- Sensitive to **temperature** (pyroelectric effect) — requires temperature compensation.
- **Charge leakage** — needs high-input-impedance amplifiers.
- **Expensive** compared to strain-gauge types.
- Sensitive to **vibration** and **electromagnetic noise** — needs careful shielding.
- **Overload damage** is irreversible.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Uncut chip thickness and width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= f \sin \kappa_r = 0.25 \times \sin 75° = 0.25 \times 0.9659 = 0.2415 \text{ mm} \\
b &= d / \sin \kappa_r = 1.5 / \sin 75° = 1.5 / 0.9659 = 1.553 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Cutting force (Kienzle's equation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= k_{c1.1} \cdot b \cdot h^{1 - m_c} \\
F_c &= 2200 \times 1.553 \times (0.2415)^{1 - 0.25} \\
F_c &= 2200 \times 1.553 \times (0.2415)^{0.75} \\
(0.2415)^{0.75} &= e^{0.75 \ln 0.2415} = e^{0.75 \times (-1.4205)} = e^{-1.0654} = 0.3446 \\
F_c &= 2200 \times 1.553 \times 0.3446 = 1177.3 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Material removal rate:**

$$
v_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 800}{1000} = 201.06 \text{ m/min} = 3351 \text{ mm/s}
$$

$$
MRR = v_c \times f \times d = 3351 \times 0.25 \times 1.5 = 1256.6 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(iv) Specific cutting energy:**

$$
u = \frac{F_c}{1000 \times f \times d} = \frac{1177.3}{1000 \times 0.25 \times 1.5} = \frac{1177.3}{375} = 3.139 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $v_c$** from m/min to mm/s when computing MRR.
> 2. **Mixing units of $k_{c1.1}$** (N/mm²) with chip area in mm² to get N — many students lose 1–2 marks by writing inconsistent units.
> 3. **Using $\kappa_r = 90°$** instead of the given 75° — the problem specifies a non-standard tool, so don't assume 90°.
> 4. **In Kienzle's equation, students often forget the exponent $1 - m_c$**, and simply multiply $k_{c1.1} \times b \times h$ — this costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **For Q-A part (a), skipping the energy minimization step** — Examiner expects the full calculus derivation, not just stating the final formula.
> 6. **In dynamometer questions**, students forget to mention **preload** and **charge amplifier** — both are mandatory in the KTU 2024 marking scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall — Cutting Forces in Turning:**

- **Three force components:** $F_c$ (tangential, largest), $F_f$ (feed/axial), $F_r$ (radial/thrust).
- **Resultant force:** $F = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_r^2}$.
- **Cutting speed:** $v_c = \pi D N / 1000$ m/min.
- **Material removal rate:** $MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot d$ mm³/s.
- **Cutting power:** $P_c = F_c \cdot v_c / 60{,}000$ kW.
- **Specific cutting energy:** $u = F_c / (1000 \cdot f \cdot d)$ N/mm².
- **Merchant's shear angle:** $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$.
- **Chip thickness ratio:** $r_c = t_1 / t_2 = \sin \phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$.
- **Kienzle's force model:** $F_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot b \cdot h^{1-m_c}$.
- **Uncut chip thickness:** $h = f \sin \kappa_r$ (for $\kappa_r \neq 90°$).
- **Chip width:** $b = d / \sin \kappa_r$.
- **Friction angle (approx):** $\tan \beta \approx F_t / F_c$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Exam Facts (KTU 2024):**
> 1. $F_c$ is **directly responsible for power consumption** — multiply by $v_c$ to get $P_c$.
> 2. $F_r$ causes **tool deflection** and **chatter** — relevant to surface finish questions.
> 3. Increasing **rake angle** reduces $F_c$ (and hence power) by increasing $\phi$.
> 4. Increasing **friction** (worn tool, low cutting speed) **increases** $F_c$.
> 5. Kienzle's $k_{c1.1}$ values (memorize a few): Mild steel ≈ 2000–2500 N/mm²; Cast iron ≈ 1100 N/mm²; Aluminum ≈ 750 N/mm²; Stainless steel ≈ 2400–2800 N/mm².
> 6. Kienzle's $m_c$ values: Steels ≈ 0.20–0.30; Cast iron ≈ 0.30–0.40; Aluminum ≈ 0.20.
> 7. **Piezoelectric dynamometer** measures all three components simultaneously; requires charge amplifier.
> 8. **Strain gauge dynamometer** is cheaper but has lower frequency response.
> 9. **Merchant's Circle** is the cornerstone theoretical model for KTU exam derivations.
> 10. For $m_c = 1$, Kienzle's equation reduces to $F_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot b \cdot h \cdot 1$ (a special simple case).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Lab Note (for Viva & Record):**
> - Always **zero the dynamometer** before each run to remove bias.
> - Run each cutting parameter combination at least **3 times** and average.
> - Record $F_c$, $F_f$, $F_r$ on a **digital storage oscilloscope (DSO)** for stable readings.
> - Use a **fresh tool insert** for each new workpiece material to avoid wear-related errors.
> - Calibration of the dynamometer is done with a **dead-weight or hydraulic press** with known loads.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Shaping Machine: Flat Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Shaping Machine: Flat Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Shaping Machine** is a reciprocating-type machine tool primarily used for machining **flat surfaces**, slots, keyways, and contours on small to medium-sized workpieces. It operates on the principle of **linear reciprocating motion** of a single-point cutting tool held in a ram, combined with an **intermittent cross-feed** of the worktable perpendicular to the tool's stroke direction. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCSFL308)**, exercises on flat surfaces form the foundational experiment for understanding metal-removal mechanics, kinematics, and tool-workpiece interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The shaping machine is classified under *mechanical metal-removal processes* and is widely used for batch production of flat surfaces due to its simplicity, low cost, and versatility compared to planers (which are used for larger workpieces).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a woodworker using a hand plane to smooth a wooden plank. The carpenter pushes the plane **forward** to shave off wood, lifts it, and pulls it **backward** without cutting. Now imagine this action performed **1,000 times per minute** by a powered mechanism, with the wood automatically advancing a tiny bit sideways after each stroke — this is exactly how a shaping machine works on metal!

- **Forward Stroke** = Cutting action (chip removed)
- **Return Stroke** = Idle motion (no cutting, tool lifted)
- **Cross Feed** = The workpiece moves sideways by a tiny amount (feed per stroke) after every double stroke
- **Depth of Cut** = How deep the tool plunges into the workpiece

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Bench-Shaping Machine Specs:**
> - **Stroke length range:** 0 – 700 mm
> - **Number of strokes per minute:** 10 – 90 strokes/min
> - **Cross-feed range:** 0.1 – 3.0 mm/stroke
> - **Table size (typical):** 450 mm × 250 mm

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition | Symbol |
|------|------------|--------|
| Cutting Velocity | Speed of the ram during forward stroke | $v_c$ |
| Feed | Lateral movement of table per stroke | $f$ |
| Depth of Cut | Radial penetration of tool into workpiece | $d$ |
| Stroke Length | Total reciprocating distance of ram | $L$ |
| Number of Strokes | Reciprocations per minute | $N$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Mounting the Workpiece:**
The raw workpiece (typically a rectangular mild steel block) is clamped onto the machine table using **step clamps, T-bolts, and parallels** to ensure it is rigid and at the correct height relative to the cutting tool.

**Step 2 — Tool Setup:**
A **single-point high-speed steel (HSS)** tool is mounted on the tool post attached to the ram. The tool is set such that the **rake angle**, **clearance angle**, and **side clearance angle** allow clean chip formation.

**Step 3 — Adjusting the Stroke:**
The ram stroke length and position are adjusted so that:
- Stroke starts **5–10 mm ahead** of the workpiece edge (overrun for tool clearance)
- Stroke ends **5–10 mm beyond** the workpiece edge
- This ensures the tool does not crash into the table or workpiece body

**Step 4 — Setting Depth of Cut:**
The cross-slide is advanced to set the required **depth of cut** ($d$). For roughing operations: $d = 2$ to $5$ mm; for finishing: $d = 0.2$ to $0.5$ mm.

**Step 5 — Selecting Cutting Speed and Feed:**
The **stroke rate** (strokes per minute) is selected based on the workpiece material and tool material. Typical values:
- Mild steel: $v_c = 15$ to $25$ m/min
- Cast iron: $v_c = 10$ to $18$ m/min
- Feed per stroke: $f = 0.5$ to $1.5$ mm/stroke

**Step 6 — Machining:**
The machine is started. During the **forward stroke**, cutting occurs. During the **return stroke**, the tool is automatically lifted (via clapper box mechanism) to avoid rubbing. After each double stroke, the table feeds by $f$ mm laterally.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU 2024 Exam Formulas** — These must be memorized verbatim.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Length of Stroke | $L = L_w + L_o + 2C$ | mm |
| 2 | Number of Strokes per minute | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{L}$ | strokes/min |
| 3 | Time for one Double Stroke | $T_{ds} = \dfrac{1}{N} = \dfrac{L}{1000 \cdot v_c}$ | min |
| 4 | Feed per minute | $f_m = f \times N$ | mm/min |
| 5 | Length of Job (per pass) | $L_j = W + \Delta$ | mm |
| 6 | Number of Passes | $n_p = \dfrac{W_{job}}{f}$ (width feed) | passes |
| 7 | Total Machining Time | $T_m = \dfrac{L \cdot n_p}{1000 \cdot v_c}$ | min |
| 8 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot d \cdot k$ | mm³/min |
| 9 | Power Required | $P = \dfrac{MRR \times U_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW |
| 10 | Specific Cutting Energy (M.S.) | $U_s \approx 2.0$ to $2.5$ | N·m/mm³ |

Where:
- $L_w$ = Length of workpiece
- $L_o$ = Tool overhang
- $C$ = Clearance (typically **5–10 mm**)
- $W$ = Width of workpiece
- $\Delta$ = Overrun allowance
- $k$ = Strokes-to-feed conversion factor (≈ 1)

### 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter (Real-World Utility)

In KTU's evaluation key, students often confuse **forward stroke time** vs **double stroke time**. Since cutting occurs **only on the forward stroke**, the effective cutting time per stroke = $\dfrac{L}{v_c}$ (in mm/min terms). The return stroke is **idle** and typically takes only **40–50%** of the forward stroke time due to quick-return mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** In actual industrial practice, the shaping machine is used for producing **tool room jobs, prototypes, fixtures, and small-batch components** where the cost of a planer or milling machine is unjustified. The machining time directly determines **labor cost and machine-hour billing**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Machining Time for Flat Surface

**Problem Setup:**
A flat surface of dimensions $W$ (width) $\times L_w$ (length) needs to be shaped on a workpiece. The cutting tool has feed $f$ mm/stroke and depth of cut $d$ mm. The cutting velocity during forward stroke is $v_c$ m/min, and the length of stroke is $L$ mm.

**Step 1 — Determine Number of Passes**

Since each double stroke removes material of width equal to the **feed per stroke** ($f$), the total number of strokes (passes) required to cover the full width $W$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_p &= \frac{W}{f} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine Time per Stroke**

The cutting velocity $v_c$ is in m/min and the stroke length $L$ is in mm. We must convert:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_c \left[\frac{\text{m}}{\text{min}}\right] &= \frac{v_c \times 1000}{L} \left[\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{min}}\right] \\
\text{Time per stroke} \quad T_s &= \frac{L}{v_c \times 1000} \quad [\text{min/stroke}] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Machining Time**

Multiplying the number of strokes by time per stroke:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_m &= n_p \times T_s \\
T_m &= \frac{W}{f} \times \frac{L}{1000 \cdot v_c} \quad [\text{min}] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **final machining time formula** for one flat surface in shaping.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Length of workpiece: $L_w = 200$ mm
- Width of workpiece: $W = 50$ mm
- Cutting velocity: $v_c = 18$ m/min
- Feed per stroke: $f = 1.0$ mm/stroke
- Depth of cut: $d = 2.5$ mm
- Overrun (clearance): $C = 10$ mm (each side)
- Tool overhang: $L_o = 30$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute Stroke Length**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= L_w + L_o + 2C \\
L &= 200 + 30 + 2(10) \\
L &= 250 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Number of Passes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_p &= \frac{W}{f} \\
n_p &= \frac{50}{1.0} \\
n_p &= 50 \text{ passes} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Time per Stroke**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= \frac{L}{1000 \cdot v_c} \\
T_s &= \frac{250}{1000 \times 18} \\
T_s &= \frac{250}{18000} \\
T_s &= 0.01389 \text{ min/stroke} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Machining Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_m &= n_p \times T_s \\
T_m &= 50 \times 0.01389 \\
T_m &= 0.6944 \text{ min} \\
T_m &\approx 41.67 \text{ seconds} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute MRR (Optional)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= v_c \cdot f \cdot d \\
\text{(Note: } v_c \text{ in mm/min for this)} &= 18000 \times 1.0 \times 2.5 \\
MRR &= 45000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \\
MRR &= 45 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Power Required**

For mild steel, specific cutting energy $U_s = 2.0$ N·m/mm³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{MRR \times U_s}{60 \times 10^6} \quad [\text{kW}] \\
P &= \frac{45000 \times 2.0}{60 \times 10^6} \\
P &= \frac{90000}{60000000} \\
P &= 0.0015 \text{ kW} \\
P &= 1.5 \text{ W (apparent) — but practical power is higher} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The small theoretical power is due to MRR being relatively low in shaping. In practice, **frictional losses, idle time, and acceleration/deceleration** of the ram significantly increase the required motor power, so **practical motor rating ≈ 1.5–2 kW** for bench shaping machines.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on Output

| Parameter ↑ (Increase) | Machining Time ↓ | Surface Finish ↑ (Better) | Tool Wear ↑ |
|------------------------|------------------|----------------------------|-------------|
| Cutting velocity $v_c$ | Decreases | Slightly worse | Increases |
| Feed $f$ | Decreases | Worsens | Increases |
| Depth of cut $d$ | Decreases (for same volume) | Slightly worsens | Increases |
| Stroke length $L$ | Increases | No effect | No effect |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Shaping Machine Kinematics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Motor Power Supply 1.5 to 2 kW AC] --> nodeB[Bull Gear Drive]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Quick Return Mechanism Crank and Slotted Lever]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Ram Carrying Tool Post]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Cutting Tool HSS Single Point]
    nodeF[Cross Feed Mechanism] --> nodeG[Cross Slide and Table Saddle]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Work Table Reciprocating Perpendicular]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Workpiece Clamped with T Bolts]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Cutting Action on Workpiece Forward Stroke Only]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Flat Surface Generation]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram of Shaping Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 Identify Workpiece and Mark Datum] --> step2[Step 2 Clamp on Machine Table with Parallels]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Mount HSS Tool on Tool Post]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Set Stroke Length and Position]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Set Depth of Cut Using Cross Slide]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Select Feed and Stroke Rate]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 Start Machine Run Forward Stroke Cutting]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8 Return Stroke Idle Tool Lifted by Clapper Box]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9 Cross Feed by f mm After Each Double Stroke]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10 Check Dimension with Vernier After Roughing]
    step10 --> step11[Step 11 Take Finishing Pass at Reduced d]
    step11 --> step12[Step 12 Inspect Flatness and Surface Finish]
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    baseA[Base and Column] --> baseB[Table Vertical and Cross Slide]
    baseB --> baseC[Worktable Swiveling]
    baseC --> baseD[Workpiece Holding Zone]
    baseD --> baseE[Cutting Zone Interaction]
    baseE --> baseF[Ram Horizontal Reciprocation]
    baseF --> baseG[Tool Head and Clapper Box]
    baseG --> baseH[Single Point HSS Tool]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In the absence of a real CAD drawing, the block diagrams above faithfully represent the kinematic chain and operational workflow of a shaping machine as mandated by the KTU 2024 Machine Shop syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is a shaping machine? List any two advantages and one limitation.*

**Model Answer:**
A shaping machine is a reciprocating-type machine tool used to produce flat surfaces, slots, keyways, and contours by moving a single-point cutting tool in a linear forward-and-return motion over a stationary (or fed) workpiece.

**Advantages:**
1. Simple in construction and low cost compared to planers.
2. Suitable for small, medium-sized workpieces and tool room jobs.

**Limitation:**
1. Limited to small/medium workpieces due to stroke length constraints; not suitable for very long flat surfaces.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*Why is the return stroke non-productive in shaping? How is it minimized?*

**Model Answer:**
During the return stroke, the tool merely retracts without cutting, so no material removal occurs. This makes it **non-productive time**. However, the return stroke is **faster** than the forward stroke due to the **quick-return mechanism** (crank and slotted lever), which reduces this non-productive time. Additionally, the **clapper box** lifts the tool during return, preventing rubbing and tool wear.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a shaping machine. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flat surface of length 250 mm and width 80 mm is to be shaped on a mild steel workpiece. The cutting velocity is 20 m/min, feed is 1.2 mm/stroke, depth of cut is 3 mm. Tool overhang is 25 mm and clearance on each side is 8 mm. Calculate the **total machining time**, **number of strokes per minute**, and the **material removal rate**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $L_w = 250$ mm, $W = 80$ mm
- $v_c = 20$ m/min, $f = 1.2$ mm/stroke, $d = 3$ mm
- $L_o = 25$ mm, $C = 8$ mm

**Step 1 — Stroke Length**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= L_w + L_o + 2C \\
L &= 250 + 25 + 2(8) \\
L &= 291 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Strokes per Minute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \times v_c}{L} \\
N &= \frac{1000 \times 20}{291} \\
N &= 68.73 \approx 69 \text{ strokes/min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Passes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_p &= \frac{W}{f} = \frac{80}{1.2} = 66.67 \approx 67 \text{ passes} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Machining Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_m &= \frac{L \cdot n_p}{1000 \cdot v_c} \\
T_m &= \frac{291 \times 67}{1000 \times 20} \\
T_m &= \frac{19497}{20000} \\
T_m &= 0.975 \text{ min} \\
T_m &\approx 58.5 \text{ seconds} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — MRR**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= v_c \times f \times d \quad (\text{in mm/min}) \\
MRR &= (20 \times 1000) \times 1.2 \times 3 \\
MRR &= 72000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \\
MRR &= 72 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $N = 69$ strokes/min, $T_m = 0.975$ min, $MRR = 72000$ mm³/min.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **clapper box mechanism** and **quick-return mechanism** with sketches. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shaping machine is used to machine a flat surface. Stroke length is 300 mm, cutting velocity is 15 m/min, feed is 0.8 mm/stroke, depth of cut is 2 mm. The workpiece is 150 mm long and 60 mm wide. Calculate the **machining time** and **MRR**. Tool overhang = 20 mm; clearance = 10 mm each side. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $L_w = 150$ mm, $W = 60$ mm
- $v_c = 15$ m/min, $f = 0.8$ mm/stroke, $d = 2$ mm
- $L_o = 20$ mm, $C = 10$ mm

**Step 1 — Stroke Length**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= L_w + L_o + 2C = 150 + 20 + 20 = 190 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Passes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_p &= \frac{W}{f} = \frac{60}{0.8} = 75 \text{ passes} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Machining Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_m &= \frac{L \cdot n_p}{1000 \cdot v_c} = \frac{190 \times 75}{15000} = 0.95 \text{ min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — MRR**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= 15000 \times 0.8 \times 2 = 24000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $T_m = 0.95$ min, $MRR = 24000$ mm³/min.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students often forget to convert m/min to mm/min when calculating MRR, leading to incorrect orders of magnitude. **Always remember:** $v_c$ (m/min) × 1000 = $v_c$ (mm/min).
> 2. **Stroke Length vs. Workpiece Length:** Many students use $L = L_w$ only, forgetting tool overhang and clearance. **Correct formula:** $L = L_w + L_o + 2C$.
> 3. **Direction of Feed Confusion:** Some students divide $W$ by feed incorrectly (using $f/W$ instead of $W/f$). Always remember: **number of passes = total width / feed per pass**.
> 4. **Return Stroke in MRR:** MRR should be calculated only on cutting velocity, not on average velocity — the return stroke is idle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Shaping machine = Reciprocating tool + Cross-fed workpiece**
- ✅ **Cutting only on forward stroke; return stroke is idle**
- ✅ **Stroke Length Formula:** $L = L_w + L_o + 2C$ (always include tool overhang + clearance)
- ✅ **Number of Passes:** $n_p = W / f$
- ✅ **Machining Time:** $T_m = \dfrac{L \cdot n_p}{1000 \cdot v_c}$ (in minutes)
- ✅ **Strokes per Minute:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{L}$
- ✅ **MRR:** $MRR = (1000 \cdot v_c) \times f \times d$ mm³/min
- ✅ **Power:** $P = \dfrac{MRR \times U_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ kW ($U_s$ for mild steel ≈ 2.0 N·m/mm³)
- ✅ **Quick-return mechanism** reduces idle return stroke time (typically 40–50% of forward stroke)
- ✅ **Clapper box** lifts tool during return to prevent rubbing & wear
- ✅ **Mild Steel cutting velocity:** 15–25 m/min; **Cast Iron:** 10–18 m/min
- ✅ **HSS tool** is standard for shaping; always check rake & clearance angles
- ✅ **Always perform finishing pass** with reduced depth of cut (0.2–0.5 mm)
- ✅ **Safety:** Never start machine without clamping workpiece; use guards; keep hands away from ram stroke zone
- ✅ **Inspect flatness** with a surface plate and dial indicator; **surface finish** with a surface roughness tester (typically achieved: 1.6–3.2 µm Ra in shaping)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Shaping Machine: Grooves and Key Ways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Shaping Machine: Grooves and Key Ways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shaping Machine (Shaper):** A reciprocating type machine tool that removes metal from a workpiece by the linear, backward-and-forward (reciprocating) motion of a single-point cutting tool held in a ram, while the workpiece is fed intermittently against the tool, primarily used for producing flat surfaces, **grooves, and keyways** on small to medium-sized workpieces.

A **groove** is a narrow channel or recess cut along a surface, running parallel to the axis of a cylindrical workpiece. A **keyway** is a specific type of groove — a precisely dimensioned slot cut either **on the shaft (external keyway)** or **in the hub/bore (internal keyway)** to accommodate a **key**, which is the mechanical element that locks a shaft to a rotating component (gear, pulley, coupling) and transmits torque without slippage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Ram:** The sliding member that carries the tool and provides the cutting (forward) and return (idle) strokes.
> - **Stroke Length (L):** The distance travelled by the ram during one complete forward-and-return cycle.
> - **Quick-Return Mechanism:** A crank-and-rocker (Whitworth) or hydraulic arrangement that makes the return stroke faster than the cutting stroke, improving productivity.
> - **Feed (f):** The small lateral movement of the workpiece (or tool, in vertical shapers) given at the end of each return stroke to advance the cut.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Think of a **shaping machine like a hand-saw held by a robotic arm**:
- A carpenter pushing a saw forward and pulling it back across a piece of wood is essentially doing what a shaper does — but with **infinite precision, infinite patience, and zero fatigue**.
- The **ram** is the "robotic arm" holding the saw (cutting tool). It pushes the tool **forward** into the metal (cutting stroke — slow & powerful) and pulls it **back** quickly (return stroke — fast, no cutting) to save time.
- Now imagine the wood clamped on a table that **shuffles a tiny bit sideways every time the saw comes back**. That tiny sideways shuffle is called **feed** — it removes another thin sliver of metal on the next forward push.
- A **groove** is what you get when the carpenter wants to cut a narrow channel along the grain — the table keeps shuffling in the **same direction** for multiple strokes.
- A **keyway** is just a **precision groove** of a specific, standardized width and depth (e.g., 10 mm wide × 5 mm deep on a 50 mm shaft) — it is the "groove where a key will sit" to lock two parts together.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world connection:** Every time you open a door handle, ride a bicycle pedal, or start a ceiling fan — a key hidden in a keyway is transferring the rotational force from a shaft to the handle/gear/pulley. The shaping machine is the workshop workhorse that creates those keyways in service workshops and tool rooms.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical References

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Cutting Speed for Mild Steel | **15 to 25 m/min** | Used in speed/stroke calculations |
| Cutter Material | **HSS (High-Speed Steel)** | Standard for shaping tools |
| Typical Keyway Width | **8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm, 14 mm** | As per IS 2048 / ISO 773 |
| Standard Keyway Depth (shaft) | **h/2 (half of key height)** | Default in design tables |
| Approach & Return Ram Angles | **30°–45°** | Determines effective cutting length |
| Clearance Angle of Tool | **6° to 10°** | Prevents rubbing on return stroke |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — How a Shaper Cuts a Groove / Keyway

The shaping of a groove or keyway is fundamentally a **multi-stroke incremental cutting process**. Each forward (cutting) stroke removes a thin layer of material from a localized zone, and after every return stroke the workpiece is **fed laterally by a fixed increment (feed per stroke)**. After a sufficient number of strokes, the full width and depth of the groove are achieved.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Groove/Keyway Production:

1. **Workpiece Holding:** The cylindrical job (shaft) is clamped in a **machine vice** such that the marked-out keyway line is horizontal and parallel to the ram stroke direction. A **V-block + clamps** or a **prism jaw vice** is preferred to hold round shafts.
2. **Tool Selection:** A **narrow parting-type tool** or a **keyway-cutting tool** (HSS, ground to the exact groove width for through keyways; undersize for finishing) is mounted in the toolpost. For **internal keyways** in bores, a **slotting tool** with a special bar is used.
3. **Alignment & Zero Setting:** The tool tip is brought to the start of the marked groove using a **tracer/vernier height gauge**. The cross-slide is set to the **starting depth**.
4. **First Cut (Light Cut):** A shallow depth of cut (0.5–1 mm) is taken to confirm alignment and chip flow.
5. **Incremental Depth Cutting:** Depth is increased in steps (typically **1 mm to 3 mm per pass**) until full depth is reached. Multiple strokes at each depth widen the groove.
6. **Side Wall Finishing:** For an undersize tool approach, the side walls are finished by taking very light cuts after reaching depth, ensuring the keyway width is exact.
7. **Final Sizing:** Width is checked with **internal calipers / plug gauge**, depth with a **depth micrometer / vernier depth gauge**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multi-stroke and not single pass?** Because shaping uses **linear reciprocating motion**, the chip cross-section is limited by the **stroke length and rigidity** of the tool. Hence, a groove is never machined in one stroke; it is built up by **N × (feed × depth)** removal cycles.

## 2.2 Role of Quick-Return Mechanism (QRM)

The **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** (crank and slotted lever) ensures the **return stroke is faster** than the cutting stroke. This is critical because:
- No cutting happens during return — so going slow wastes time.
- Going fast reduces heat-soak in the tool.
- Time ratio is typically **2:1 to 3:1** (cutting : return).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Velocity $V$ | $V = \dfrac{L \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right) \cdot N_s}{1000}$ | m/min | $m$ = QRM ratio, $N_s$ = strokes/min |
| 2 | Strokes per minute $N_s$ | $N_s = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{L\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right)}$ | strokes/min | Inverse formula |
| 3 | QRM Time Ratio $m$ | $m = \dfrac{T_{\text{cutting}}}{T_{\text{return}}} = \dfrac{\theta_{\text{cut}}}{\theta_{\text{ret}}}$ | — | Typically 2 to 3 |
| 4 | Feed per stroke $f$ | $f = 0.1$ to $1.5$ mm/stroke | mm | Horizontal feed on most shapers |
| 5 | Feed rate $f_r$ | $f_r = f \times N_s$ | mm/min | Equivalent linear feed |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = L_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | $L_c$ = cutting length of stroke, $d$ = depth of cut |
| 7 | Time per Stroke $T$ | $T = \dfrac{1}{N_s}$ | min/stroke | Useful for time estimation |
| 8 | Approx. Cutting Length $L_c$ | $L_c = L - 0.4L \approx 0.6L$ | mm | Subtract approach + over-run (≈ 40%) |
| 9 | Stroke Length $L$ | $L = L_{\text{job}} + (15 \text{ to } 25 \text{ mm clearance})$ | mm | Clearance for approach & over-run |
| 10 | Power $P$ | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60\,000}$ | kW | $F_c$ in N, $V$ in m/min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem says *"Find the machining time for shaping a 100 mm long keyway on a 50 mm diameter shaft"*, students often forget to **add clearance** to the stroke length. The stroke must be at least **100 + 20 = 120 mm**, and the cutting length is then ≈ 0.6 × 120 = 72 mm. This is the most common deduction in KTU valuation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tool Rooms & Maintenance Workshops:** Shapers are still the **fastest low-cost solution** for cutting one-off keyways on shafts during breakdown maintenance, where a horizontal milling machine is unavailable.
- **Prototype Manufacturing:** During R&D of gearbox assemblies, keyways and slots are often shaped before tooling up for mass production.
- **Die & Fixture Making:** Grooves for T-slots, dovetail guides (in vertical shapers), and slide-ways are commonly shaped.
- **Educational Workshops:** Shaping is the **first machine tool** students operate because it directly demonstrates the three fundamental motions — **primary (cutting), secondary (feed), and tertiary (depth)** — without the safety hazards of rotating spindles.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Strokes per Minute ($N_s$) for Shaper

> The **cutting speed** in shaping is defined as the **mean speed of the ram during the cutting stroke**, expressed in metres per minute.

### Step 1 — Define the cutting stroke time

Let:
- $L$ = length of stroke (mm)
- $N_s$ = number of complete double strokes per minute
- $m$ = quick-return ratio = (Time of cutting stroke) / (Time of return stroke)

The time for one complete cycle (cutting + return) is:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{cut}} + T_{\text{ret}} = \dfrac{1}{N_s} \text{ minutes}
$$

### Step 2 — Express cutting and return times in terms of the ratio

By definition of $m$:

$$
T_{\text{ret}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{cut}}}{m}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{\text{cut}} + \dfrac{T_{\text{cut}}}{m} = \dfrac{1}{N_s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{cut}} \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right) = \dfrac{1}{N_s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{1}{N_s \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right)} \text{ minutes}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to seconds for velocity calculation

$$
T_{\text{cut (sec)}} = \dfrac{60}{N_s \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right)} \text{ seconds}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute mean cutting velocity

The mean velocity during the cutting stroke is:

$$
V = \dfrac{\text{Stroke length (mm)}}{\text{Cutting stroke time (min)}} = \dfrac{L}{T_{\text{cut}}}
$$

Substituting $T_{\text{cut}}$:

$$
V = L \cdot N_s \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right) \text{ mm/min}
$$

Converting mm/min → m/min by dividing by 1000:

$$
\boxed{V = \dfrac{L \cdot N_s \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right)}{1000} \text{ m/min}}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve the inverse for $N_s$

$$
\boxed{N_s = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{L \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right)} \text{ strokes/min}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out KTU Numerical — Shaping a Keyway

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern Question]**
> A keyway of length **80 mm** and depth **5 mm** is to be cut on a **mild steel shaft of 60 mm diameter** using a shaping machine. The recommended cutting speed is **18 m/min**, quick-return ratio is **2**, feed per stroke is **0.6 mm/stroke**, and depth of cut per pass is **1.25 mm**. Calculate:
> 1. Strokes per minute ($N_s$)
> 2. Feed rate ($f_r$)
> 3. Material removal rate (MRR) per stroke
> 4. Approximate machining time

### Given Data

- Job length $L_{\text{job}} = 80$ mm
- Stroke clearance $= 15$ mm each side → total clearance = 30 mm
- Stroke length $L = 80 + 30 = 110$ mm
- Cutting speed $V = 18$ m/min
- QRM ratio $m = 2$
- Feed $f = 0.6$ mm/stroke
- Depth per pass $d = 1.25$ mm
- Total depth required = 5 mm → **Number of passes** = 5 / 1.25 = **4 passes**
- Effective cutting length $L_c = 0.6 \times L = 0.6 \times 110 = 66$ mm

### (a) Strokes per minute

$$
N_s = \dfrac{1000 \times 18}{110 \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\right)}
$$

$$
N_s = \dfrac{18000}{110 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{18000}{165}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_s \approx 109.09 \text{ strokes/min}}
$$

### (b) Feed rate

$$
f_r = f \times N_s = 0.6 \times 109.09
$$

$$
\boxed{f_r \approx 65.45 \text{ mm/min}}
$$

### (c) Material Removal Rate per stroke (per pass)

MRR is volume removed per minute. Per stroke, the volume is $L_c \times f \times d$:

$$
V_{\text{stroke}} = 66 \times 0.6 \times 1.25 = 49.5 \text{ mm}^3 \text{ per stroke}
$$

MRR per minute:

$$
MRR = V_{\text{stroke}} \times N_s = 49.5 \times 109.09
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR \approx 5400 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 5.4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

### (d) Machining Time

The width of the keyway (say **12 mm**) is achieved by **3 strokes per pass** in the feed direction (since 0.6 mm × 3 = 1.8 mm; assuming 5 strokes = 3 mm; we approximate).

> **Simplified KTU Approach (Board expects):** For a keyway where the **tool width = keyway width**, only **one stroke per pass** is needed laterally. Then strokes per pass = 1.

Strokes per pass = 1 (since tool width = keyway width).
Number of passes = 4.
Time per stroke = $1 / N_s = 1/109.09 = 0.00917$ min.
Total time for the keyway:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \text{Passes} \times \text{Strokes/pass} \times T_{\text{stroke}} = 4 \times 1 \times 0.00917
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{total}} \approx 0.0367 \text{ min} \approx 2.2 \text{ seconds}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Width:** In a real KTU answer, mention that if the tool width is **less than** the keyway width, additional lateral feed strokes are needed. For an exact-width HSS tool, one stroke per depth pass is sufficient.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tool Geometry for Keyway Cutting

| Tool Parameter | Groove/Keyway Tool | Reason / KTU Point |
|---|---|---|
| Rake Angle ($\alpha$) | **6° to 12° (positive)** | Reduces cutting force on mild steel |
| Clearance Angle ($\gamma$) | **6° to 10°** | Prevents rubbing on return stroke |
| Cutting Edge | **Straight, parallel to stroke** | For straight-walled grooves |
| Tool Tip Radius | **0.2 to 0.4 mm** | Sharp, but not zero (to avoid chipping) |
| Tool Material | **HSS grade M2 / M3** | Standard workshop tool |
| Tool Width (for through keyway) | **= Keyway width (h11 tolerance)** | Achieves width in single pass |
| Tool Width (for finishing) | **0.05–0.1 mm undersize** | Allows side-finishing cuts |
| Nose Angle for Roughing | **Slight nose radius** | Improves tool life |

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Feed Rate Concept

The **feed** in a shaper is the lateral displacement given to the workpiece (or tool) at the **end of each return stroke**, before the next cutting stroke begins. This is implemented by a **ratchet-and-pawl mechanism** linked to the ram.

If $f$ = feed per stroke (mm) and $N_s$ = strokes/min, then in one minute, the workpiece advances $N_s$ times by $f$ mm each time:

$$
\boxed{f_r = f \times N_s \text{ mm/min}}
$$

> **Why feed is given only on return stroke?** Because during the cutting stroke the tool is engaged; any feed then would cause the tool to **skid, chatter, or break**. The feed mechanism is **automatically disengaged** during cutting via the ratchet mechanism.
