# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Major Components of a Shaping Machine

The physical layout of a standard horizontal shaper is mapped below as a **functional block architecture**, showing the interaction between subsystems that ultimately enable keyway/groove cutting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    base[BASE AND BED] --> column[VERTICAL COLUMN]
    column --> crossrail[CROSS RAIL WITH TABLE]
    crossrail --> table[WORK TABLE WITH VICE]
    table --> job[WORKPIECE SHAFT]
    column --> ram[SLIDE AND RAM ASSEMBLY]
    ram --> toolpost[TOOL POST WITH HSS TOOL]
    ram --> bullgear[BULL GEAR DRIVE]
    bullgear --> qrm[WHITWORTH QUICK RETURN MECH]
    qrm --> bullgear
    base --> motor[MAIN DRIVE MOTOR]
    motor --> gearbox[GEAR BOX FOR SPEEDS]
    gearbox --> bullgear
    ram --> feed[FEEED RATCHET PAWL MECH]
    feed --> crossrail
    bullgear --> apron[APRON AND HAND FEED]
    apron --> crossrail
    table --> coolant[COOLANT TRAY AND PUMP]
    coolant --> job
    job -.shaping operation.-> toolpost
    toolpost --> ram
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Power flows: **Motor → Gearbox → Bull Gear → Quick-Return Mechanism → Ram (cutting & return strokes)**.
- Feed flows: **Ram motion → Ratchet-pawl → Cross-rail → Table** (intermittent lateral feed).
- Coolant flows: **Coolant tray → Pump → Workpiece/Tool interface**.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Keyway Cutting on Shaping Machine

This flowchart captures the **complete procedural sequence** students must follow during the experiment and mention in their **viva voce / lab record**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read Drawing] --> B[Identify Keyway Length Width Depth]
    B --> C[Select HSS Keyway Tool of Correct Width]
    C --> D[Set Cutting Speed and Strokes per Min]
    D --> E[Mount Shaft in V-Block Vice on Table]
    E --> F[Mark Keyway Center Line with Scriber]
    F --> G[Align Tool Tip with Marked Line using Height Gauge]
    G --> H[Set Quick Return Ratio to 2 or 3]
    H --> I[Set Feed per Stroke 0.5 to 0.8 mm]
    I --> J[Take First Light Cut Depth 0.5 mm]
    J --> K{Check Alignment OK?}
    K -- No --> L[Correct Alignment and Re-cut] --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[Increase Depth in Steps up to Required Depth]
    M --> N[Achieve Full Depth in 3 to 4 Passes]
    N --> O[Finish Side Walls with Light Cuts]
    O --> P[Measure Width with Internal Caliper]
    P --> Q[Measure Depth with Depth Micrometer]
    Q --> R{Dimensions within Tolerance?}
    R -- No --> S[Take Correction Cut] --> P
    R -- Yes --> T[Stop Machine and Remove Job]
    T --> U[Record Observations in Lab Manual]
    U --> V[END]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Force System Acting on the Shaping Tool

A keyway-cutting tool experiences a **three-component force system** at the cutting edge. Understanding this is critical for KTU viva and for selecting tool geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    workpiece[WORKPIECE SHAFT] -->|Fc| tool[TIP OF HSS KEYWAY TOOL]
    workpiece -->|Ff| tool
    workpiece -->|Fp| tool
    tool --> ram[RAM PROVIDES CUTTING MOTION]
    table[WORK TABLE PROVIDES FEED MOTION]
    feed[F] --> workpiece
    ram -->|V cutting| tool
    Fc[Fc = Cutting Force N] -.horizontal resistance.-> workpiece
    Ff[Ff = Feed Force N] -.vertical down.-> workpiece
    Fp[Fp = Passive Force N] -.lateral on side.-> workpiece
    tool -->|R resultant| tool
```

Where:
- $F_c$ = **Cutting force** (resists the cutting motion of the ram) — primary, largest component.
- $F_f$ = **Feed/Thrust force** (acts vertically downward on the tool).
- $F_p$ = **Passive force** (acts laterally, pushes the tool sideways).
- $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_p^2}$ = resultant force on the tool tip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"Why does the side wall of a shaped keyway taper slightly?"*
> **Answer:** Because of the **passive force $F_p$**, the tool deflects elastically. As the tool is fed laterally, the uncut chip thickness increases, hence $F_p$ increases, causing more deflection — leading to a slight **taper or barrel-shape** in the keyway side wall. This is why **multiple light finishing cuts** are given at the final depth.

---

## 4.4 Schematic of Tool Engagement in Keyway (Sectional View)

The diagram below shows how the tool engages the workpiece during a keyway cut, and the **two distinct cutting edges** that shape the side walls.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Shaft_Cross_Section
        shaft[CYLINDRICAL SHAFT DIA D]
        shaft --> top[TOP SURFACE]
        shaft --> bot[BOTTOM SURFACE]
    end
    keyway[KEYWAY WIDTH b DEPTH t] --> shaft
    tool[HSS TOOL] -->|cutting edge 1| leftwall[LEFT WALL OF KEYWAY]
    tool -->|cutting edge 2| rightwall[RIGHT WALL OF KEYWAY]
    tool -->|tip| bottom[KEYWAY BOTTOM]
    feed[FEED DIRECTION] --> tool
    cutting[CUTTING DIRECTION RAM STROKE] --> tool
```

- **Cutting edge 1** removes material on the **left side wall**.
- **Cutting edge 2** removes material on the **right side wall**.
- **Tool tip** clears the **bottom of the keyway** to required depth $t$.
- Feed $f$ is given **perpendicular** to the cutting direction, taking the tool from one side of the keyway to the other.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a shaping machine and list any two operations commonly performed on it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **shaping machine** is a reciprocating type machine tool in which a single-point cutting tool moves in a linear, back-and-forth motion (cutting stroke and return stroke) across a stationary or intermittently-fed workpiece to remove metal and produce a flat or contoured surface. *(2 marks)*

Two common operations: *(1 mark)*
1. **Machining flat surfaces** (horizontal, vertical, or inclined) on small workpieces.
2. **Cutting grooves and keyways** on shafts and in hubs.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the operating principle, and 1 mark for listing operations. Avoid one-line vague answers.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the function of a quick-return mechanism in a shaper? State its typical ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **quick-return mechanism (QRM)** ensures that the **return stroke of the ram is completed in a shorter time** than the cutting stroke, since no metal cutting occurs during the return. This increases productivity and reduces tool heat exposure. *(2 marks)*

The typical QRM ratio is **2:1 to 3:1** (cutting stroke time : return stroke time) for crank-and-slot type (Whitworth) shapers. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that within each module, students are offered an **internal choice** between two questions. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** of equivalent marks distribution.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a horizontal shaping machine**. List the various mechanisms used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure to cut a keyway on a shaft using a shaping machine**, including tool selection, work-holding, and inspection methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

A horizontal shaping machine consists of the following major parts:

1. **Base and Bed:** Heavy cast iron base that supports the column and absorbs vibrations. The bed has accurately machined ways (V-flat) on which the table slides. *(1 mark)*
2. **Column:** Vertical member at one end of the base that supports the ram, cross-rail, and drive mechanism. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Cross-rail and Table:** The cross-rail moves vertically on the column; the table mounted on it moves horizontally and clamps the workpiece in a vice. *(1 mark)*
4. **Ram:** The reciprocating slide that carries the toolpost. It moves on accurately-ground guideways in the column. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Toolpost:** Swiveling tool-holder mounted on the ram face. Can be rotated to cut at any angle. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Drive Mechanism:** Includes motor, gearbox, and bull-gear, which converts rotary motion into reciprocating motion of the ram. *(1 mark)*

**Mechanisms used:** *(2 marks)*
- **Quick-Return Mechanism** (Whitworth crank-and-slotted-lever) for differential stroke timing.
- **Ratchet-and-Pawl Feed Mechanism** for intermittent lateral feed of the table.
- **Apron Mechanism** for manual cross-feed.
- **Clutch and Brake** for starting/stopping the ram within the stroke.

**Working:** The motor drives the bull-gear through a gearbox. The crankpin on the bull-gear engages the slotted lever (Whitworth), which converts rotation into to-and-fro motion of the ram. The feed ratchet advances the table by a small increment at the end of each return stroke, building up the cut over multiple strokes.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Procedure for Keyway Cutting on Shaper:**

1. **Study the drawing** and identify keyway length, width, depth, and tolerance. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Select the HSS keyway tool** of width equal to the keyway width (or 0.1 mm undersize for finishing). *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Work-holding:** Mount the shaft in a V-block or a 3-jaw chuck mounted on the table, ensuring the marked keyway center-line is **horizontal and parallel to the ram stroke**. *(1 mark)*
4. **Marking out:** Scribe the keyway length on the shaft using a height gauge and vernier. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Tool alignment:** Bring the tool tip to the start of the keyway using a height gauge; set the cross-slide to give the correct starting depth. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Set parameters:** Adjust the stroke length to cover the keyway + 15 mm clearance each side. Set the cutting speed and feed per stroke. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **First light cut (0.5 mm depth):** Verify alignment and chip formation. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Progressive depth cutting:** Increase depth in steps of 1–1.5 mm until the full keyway depth is reached (typically 3–4 passes). *(1 mark)*
9. **Side wall finishing:** With an undersize tool, take 2–3 light cuts (0.1 mm feed) to true up the side walls. *(0.5 mark)*
10. **Inspection:** Measure width with an **internal caliper or plug gauge**, depth with a **depth micrometer or vernier depth gauge**. Check parallelism of keyway to shaft axis using a dial indicator. *(1 mark)*
11. **Corrective action:** If undersize, recut with a wider tool or shift; if oversize, the part is rejected. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram and the quick-return mechanism**, derive the relationship between cutting speed, stroke length, and number of strokes per minute in a shaping machine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A keyway of **120 mm length, 12 mm width, and 6 mm depth** is to be cut on a **mild steel shaft of 75 mm diameter** in a shaping machine. Given: cutting speed = **20 m/min**, QRM ratio = **2.5**, feed = **0.8 mm/stroke**, depth of cut per pass = **1.5 mm**. Calculate: **(i)** Stroke length, **(ii)** Strokes per minute, **(iii)** Feed rate, **(iv)** Machining time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The final relationship is:

$$
\boxed{V = \dfrac{L \cdot N_s \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right)}{1000} \text{ m/min}}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_s = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{L \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m}\right)} \text{ strokes/min}}
$$

**Explanation of terms (1 mark each):**
- $V$ = mean cutting velocity (m/min) during the cutting stroke.
- $L$ = total stroke length (mm) = job length + approach + over-run.
- $N_s$ = number of complete double strokes per minute.
- $m$ = quick-return ratio (time of cutting stroke / time of return stroke).
- $1 + 1/m$ = factor accounting for the asymmetry between cutting and return strokes.

**Whitworth QRM:** A crankpin on the bull gear slides in a slotted lever. As the crank rotates, the slotted lever oscillates, driving the ram. Since the crankpin moves through a **larger angle during cutting** than during return, the return stroke is faster. *(1 mark for block diagram description)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**
- Keyway length = 120 mm → Job length $L_{\text{job}} = 120$ mm
- Width = 12 mm (tool width = 12 mm, so 1 lateral stroke suffices)
- Total depth = 6 mm → Number of passes = 6 / 1.5 = **4 passes**
- Cutting speed $V = 20$ m/min
- QRM ratio $m = 2.5$
- Feed $f = 0.8$ mm/stroke
- Clearance for stroke = 15 mm each side → total clearance = 30 mm

**(i) Stroke length:** *(2 marks)*

$$
L = L_{\text{job}} + \text{clearance} = 120 + 30 = \mathbf{150 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(ii) Strokes per minute:** *(2 marks)*

$$
N_s = \dfrac{1000 \times 20}{150 \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{2.5}\right)} = \dfrac{20000}{150 \times 1.4} = \dfrac{20000}{210}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_s \approx 95.24 \text{ strokes/min}}
$$

**(iii) Feed rate:** *(1 mark)*

$$
f_r = f \times N_s = 0.8 \times 95.24 = \mathbf{76.19 \text{ mm/min}}
$$

**(iv) Machining time:** *(2 marks)*

Since the tool width = keyway width, only **1 stroke per pass** is required laterally. Number of passes = 4.

Time per stroke = $1 / N_s = 1 / 95.24 = 0.0105$ min/stroke.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 4 \text{ passes} \times 1 \text{ stroke/pass} \times 0.0105 \text{ min/stroke}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{total}} \approx 0.042 \text{ min} \approx 2.52 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add clearance to stroke length** (loses 2 marks). Always: $L = L_{\text{job}} + 2 \times 15 \text{ mm (minimum)}$.
> 2. **Wrong units in cutting speed formula** — $L$ must be in **mm**, $N_s$ in **strokes/min**, $V$ in **m/min**. Mixing units is the #1 reason for wrong answers.
> 3. **Ignoring the $(1 + 1/m)$ factor** — students often write $N_s = 1000V/L$, which is correct **only for equal cutting and return times** (no QRM). This loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning the Whitworth mechanism by name** — generic "crank mechanism" answers lose the 1 mark allotted to QRM identification.
> 5. **In practicals:** Students frequently fail to set the **stroke-end correctly** so that the tool **runs off the workpiece** on the return side, leading to **no cut on part of the keyway** — this is heavily penalized in the lab viva.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam & Lab Viva:**

- [ ] **Shaper = reciprocating linear cutting motion**; cutting tool moves, workpiece feeds.
- [ ] **Three motions:** Primary (ram reciprocation), Secondary (table feed at end of return stroke), Tertiary (cross-slide vertical feed for depth).
- [ ] **Whitworth QRM** makes return stroke faster; ratio typically 2:1 to 3:1.
- [ ] **Keyway = precision groove** on shaft (external) or in hub (internal); used with a key to transmit torque.
- [ ] **Tool selection:** HSS parting-type tool; tool width = keyway width (for one-pass width) or 0.05–0.1 mm undersize (for finishing).
- [ ] **Work-holding:** V-block + machine vice for round shafts; mark the keyway line using a height gauge.
- [ ] **Cutting speed for mild steel:** **15–25 m/min**; for cast iron: **10–18 m/min**.
- [ ] **Depth of cut per pass:** **1 to 3 mm** typical; use shallow passes for accuracy and longer tool life.
- [ ] **Feed per stroke:** **0.5 to 1.0 mm** for roughing, **0.1 to 0.3 mm** for finishing.
- [ ] **Key Formula:** $V = \dfrac{L \cdot N_s (1 + 1/m)}{1000}$ — must be applied in every numerical.
- [ ] **Inspection tools:** Depth micrometer, vernier depth gauge, internal caliper, plug gauge, dial indicator (for parallelism).
- [ ] **Common defect to check:** **Tapered side walls** due to passive force deflection; remedy = multiple light finishing cuts.
- [ ] **Coolant:** Use soluble oil for mild steel cutting; dry cutting is acceptable for cast iron.
- [ ] **Safety:** Always use a **chip guard**, wear **goggles**, and ensure the **stroke is disengaged** before mounting/dismounting the workpiece.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Drilling Machine: Drilling, Boring, Reaming, Tapping and Counter Sinking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Drilling Machine: Drilling, Boring, Reaming, Tapping and Counter Sinking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the Radial Drilling Machine

The **Radial Drilling Machine** is a heavy-duty, multi-purpose machine tool designed to perform a variety of hole-making operations on large, heavy, or irregularly shaped workpieces that cannot be easily accommodated on a conventional upright drilling machine. Unlike a pillar drill where the workpiece must be moved to align the tool, in a radial drill the **spindle head itself can be positioned** over the workpiece via a sliding radial arm. This makes it ideal for workshop-floor production environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PCSFL308):** This module is purely **practically-oriented (Lab/Practice)**. You are expected to demonstrate hands-on competency in identifying the tool, setting up the workpiece, selecting cutting parameters, and executing the operation safely. The viva questions are framed around tool nomenclature, operating sequences, and calculation of cutting parameters.

The five principal hole-making operations performed on this machine are:

1. **Drilling** – Producing a fresh cylindrical hole in a solid workpiece.
2. **Boring** – Enlarging an already existing hole to a precise diameter.
3. **Reaming** – Finishing a previously drilled/bored hole to obtain a smooth, accurate internal surface.
4. **Tapping** – Cutting internal threads inside a pre-drilled hole.
5. **Counter Sinking** – Enlarging the entry of a drilled hole conically to accommodate a flat-head screw.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to make a hole in a wooden log lying on the ground.

- **Drilling** is like using a hand auger to bore the very first hole — you push a twisted tool with cutting edges into solid wood.
- **Boring** is like using a single-point cutter to widen that existing hole to a precise, larger diameter — only possible *after* the hole already exists.
- **Reaming** is like running a smoothing tool (reamer) gently through the hole to scrape away the tiny imperfections, leaving a polished, accurate finish.
- **Tapping** is like twisting a threaded screw tap into the hole to cut spiral grooves inside — these grooves become the mating threads for a bolt.
- **Counter Sinking** is like using a conical tool at the entry of the hole so a flat-head screw sits flush with the surface, like a countersunk bolt on a wooden cabinet door.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Clarity:** Drilling is the *only* operation that starts in solid material. **Boring, Reaming, Tapping, and Counter Sinking are all secondary operations** performed *after* an initial hole has been made.

## 1.3 Standard Definitions as per KTU Lab Manual Terminology

| Operation | Standard Definition |
|-----------|---------------------|
| **Drilling** | A chip-removal process that uses a **multi-point rotating cutter** (twist drill) to produce a cylindrical hole in solid metal. |
| **Boring** | A chip-removal process that uses a **single-point cutting tool** held in a boring bar to enlarge or finish an existing hole. |
| **Reaming** | A sizing and finishing operation using a **multi-fluted rotary cutting tool (reamer)** to produce a hole of accurate size and smooth surface finish. |
| **Tapping** | A thread-cutting operation using a **multi-point tool (tap)** to produce internal threads in a previously drilled hole of slightly smaller diameter (the *tap drill size*). |
| **Counter Sinking** | A machining operation using a **conical multi-tooth cutter (counter-sink)** to produce a flared, conical enlargement at the entry of a hole, typically at an included angle of **60°, 82°, 90°, 100°, 110°, or 120°**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** Standard counter-sink angles per ISO 7721 are **90°** (most common for metric flat-head screws) and **120°** (for rivets and imperial standards). Countersunk screws manufactured to ISO 7721 use the **90°** included angle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Operation-Wise Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Drilling Operation

The drilling operation is the foundational hole-making process. A standard **twist drill** (the most common drill bit) has the following structural elements:

- **Point / Dead Centre** — The very tip that provides initial contact.
- **Cutting Lips** — Two cutting edges formed by the intersection of the flank and the leading face.
- **Chisel Edge** — The short edge connecting the two cutting lips; it does not cut but extrudes material.
- **Flutes** — Helical grooves that evacuate the chips and allow cutting fluid to reach the cutting edge.
- **Body / Shank** — The cylindrical portion; for larger drills, a *taper shank* (Morse Taper) is used.
- **Neck** — A relief area for grinding the flutes without altering the shank.
- **Margin / Land** — A narrow axial strip that provides guidance inside the hole.

**Standard Drill Nomenclature (KTU Manual Standard):**
- Drill Point Angle: **118°** (general purpose for mild steel); **135°** for hard materials (split-point drill).
- Helix Angle: **24° to 32°** (standard); **40°** for soft, gummy materials; **10°–15°** for hard materials.

**Working Principle:**
$$\text{Rotary motion (spindle)} + \text{Axial feed motion (quill)} \longrightarrow \text{Cutting action}$$

The drill rotates at the spindle RPM while the operator (or auto-feed mechanism) pushes it axially into the workpiece at a prescribed feed rate.

### 2.1.2 Boring Operation

Boring is performed with a **boring bar** holding a **single-point cutting tool (SPCT)**. It is used for:
- Enlarging an existing drilled hole.
- Correcting misalignment of a previously drilled hole.
- Producing internal profiles (steps, grooves) that drilling cannot achieve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Boring **does not start from solid material**. The starting hole must always be pre-made, typically by drilling or by casting/forging.

### 2.1.3 Reaming Operation

Reaming is a **sizing operation**, not a stock-removal operation. The reamer removes only a tiny amount of material (typically **0.1 mm to 0.4 mm** on diameter) to:
- Improve dimensional accuracy (tolerances of **IT7 to IT8**).
- Improve surface finish (typically **Ra 0.8 to Ra 3.2 μm**).
- Correct minor roundness errors.

A reamer has **6 to 12 cutting flutes** (more than a drill of the same diameter) to ensure smoother finish and better guidance.

### 2.1.4 Tapping Operation

A **tap** is a hardened, threaded cutting tool that cuts internal threads. Standard tap types:
- **Taper tap** (No. 1) — Starts the thread.
- **Plug tap** (No. 2) — Intermediate.
- **Bottoming tap** (No. 3) — Cuts threads close to a blind-hole bottom.

**Tap Drill Size Formula:**

$$\boxed{D_{\text{tap}} = D_{\text{major}} - P}$$

Where:
- $D_{\text{tap}}$ = Tap drill diameter (mm)
- $D_{\text{major}}$ = Nominal major diameter of the thread (mm)
- $P$ = Pitch of the thread (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tapping Speed Rule:** The spindle speed for tapping should be **50% to 70%** of the equivalent drilling speed for the same diameter, because tap geometry and chip evacuation are critical.

### 2.1.5 Counter Sinking Operation

Counter sinking is performed using a **counter-sink tool**, which is essentially a conical cutter. Common applications:
- Providing a seating for flat-head machine screws.
- Deburring the entry edge of a drilled hole.
- Creating chamfers for assembly ease.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA SHEET** — These equations are essential for both numerical viva questions and the Part B calculations in the KTU End Semester Exam (ESE). Memorize the symbols and units carefully.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbols & Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Cutting Speed** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D$ = tool diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle speed (RPM) |
| 2 | **Spindle Speed** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | All symbols as above; $N$ in RPM |
| 3 | **Feed Rate (Linear)** | $f_r = N \cdot f$ | $f_r$ = table feed (mm/min), $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev) |
| 4 | **Feed per Revolution** | $f = \dfrac{f_r}{N}$ | Same units as above |
| 5 | **Machining Time (Drilling)** | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r} = \dfrac{L}{N \cdot f}$ | $T_m$ = time (min), $L$ = effective cutting length (mm) |
| 6 | **Effective Drilling Length** | $L = L_{\text{hole}} + 0.3 \cdot D$ | The **0.3·D term** accounts for drill point overshoot |
| 7 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot f_r$ | MRR in mm³/min |
| 8 | **Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot K_s}{60 \cdot 10^6}$ | $P$ = power (kW), $K_s$ = specific cutting energy (N·m/mm³) ≈ **2.0 to 2.5 N·m/mm³ for mild steel** |
| 9 | **Thrust Force (Empirical)** | $F_t = K_s \cdot \text{MRR}$ | $F_t$ in Newtons |
| 10 | **Tap Drill Diameter** | $D_{\text{tap}} = D_{\text{major}} - P$ | $P$ = thread pitch (mm) |
| 11 | **Tap Drill Diameter (Metric, %)** | $D_{\text{tap}} \approx D_{\text{major}} \cdot 0.85$ | Approximation for ~75% thread engagement |
| 12 | **Counter-Sink Diameter at Surface** | $D_{\text{cs}} = D_{\text{hole}} + 2 \cdot h \cdot \tan(\theta/2)$ | $h$ = depth of countersink, $\theta$ = included angle |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Typographical Safeguard:** In the table above, every vertical bar that normally denotes absolute value or modulus has been written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table renderer. In your exam answer sheets, write the modulus as ordinary vertical bars.

## 2.3 Recommended Cutting Parameters for Mild Steel (KTU Reference Values)

| Operation | Cutting Speed $V$ (m/min) | Feed $f$ (mm/rev) | Coolant |
|-----------|---------------------------|-------------------|---------|
| Drilling | 25 – 35 | 0.05 – 0.30 | Cutting oil / soluble oil |
| Boring | 30 – 50 | 0.10 – 0.40 | Cutting oil |
| Reaming | 8 – 15 | 0.10 – 0.50 | Cutting oil |
| Tapping | 5 – 10 | Equal to pitch (P) | Sulphurised oil |
| Counter Sinking | 20 – 30 | 0.05 – 0.15 | Cutting oil |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These five operations collectively form the **backbone of mechanical assembly manufacturing**. Without drilling, you cannot bolt components. Without tapping, you cannot fasten screws to a frame. Without boring, large diameters cannot be machined economically. Without reaming, precision fits (H7/g6) are impossible. Without counter sinking, flush-mounted fasteners cannot be installed. Every automobile engine block, gearbox housing, and aerospace structural component depends on this exact sequence of operations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Machining Time Formula for Drilling

This is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU university viva and Part B questions. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define Cutting Speed

Cutting speed $V$ is defined as the **linear distance traversed by a cutting edge per unit time** at the periphery of the tool.

$$\text{In one revolution, the drill periphery travels a distance} = \pi \cdot D$$

Therefore, in $N$ revolutions per minute (i.e., $N$ minutes of time):

$$\boxed{V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(in mm/min)}}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Standard Unit m/min

Since $V$ is conventionally expressed in m/min and $D$ in mm, we divide by **1000**:

$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}}$$

### Step 3 — Define Feed per Revolution

Feed per revolution $f$ is the **axial advance of the drill per spindle revolution**, measured in mm/rev.

### Step 4 — Define Linear Feed Rate

Linear feed rate $f_r$ is the **axial advance per minute**:

$$f_r = N \cdot f \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

### Step 5 — Define Effective Length of Cut

The drill point has a chisel edge and a conical point. The drill must travel a distance equal to the hole depth $L_h$ **plus** the overshoot required for the point to clear the workpiece. Empirically:

$$L = L_h + 0.3 \cdot D$$

### Step 6 — Derive Machining Time

By definition:

$$\text{Time} = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed (feed rate)}}$$

Applying this to the drilling operation:

$$T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$$

Substituting $f_r = N \cdot f$:

$$\boxed{T_m = \dfrac{L_h + 0.3 \cdot D}{N \cdot f} \quad \text{(minutes)}}$$

### Step 7 — Express $T_m$ Purely in Cutting Parameters

Substitute $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$:

$$T_m = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot (L_h + 0.3 \cdot D)}{1000 \cdot V \cdot f}$$

$$\boxed{T_m = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot (L_h + 0.3 \cdot D)}{1000 \cdot V \cdot f} \quad \text{(minutes)}}$$

This is the **most general form** and is directly usable in numerical problems.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem Statement (KTU ESE Style):**
A hole of **20 mm diameter** and **40 mm depth** is to be drilled in a mild steel workpiece on a radial drilling machine. The cutting speed is **25 m/min** and the feed is **0.2 mm/rev**. Calculate: **(a)** Spindle speed, **(b)** Linear feed rate, **(c)** Machining time.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $D = 20$ mm
- $L_h = 40$ mm
- $V = 25$ m/min
- $f = 0.2$ mm/rev

### Part (a) — Spindle Speed $N$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 20}$$

$$N = \dfrac{25000}{62.832}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 398 \text{ RPM}}$$

In practice, we round down to the nearest available machine speed: **400 RPM**.

### Part (b) — Linear Feed Rate $f_r$

$$f_r = N \cdot f = 398 \cdot 0.2$$

$$\boxed{f_r = 79.6 \text{ mm/min}}$$

### Part (c) — Machining Time $T_m$

First, compute effective length:

$$L = L_h + 0.3 \cdot D = 40 + 0.3 \cdot 20 = 40 + 6 = 46 \text{ mm}$$

Now:

$$T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r} = \dfrac{46}{79.6}$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx 0.578 \text{ minutes} \approx 34.7 \text{ seconds}}$$

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example — Material Removal Rate and Power

**Problem Statement:**
For the same drilling operation above, calculate: **(a)** Material Removal Rate, **(b)** Power required if specific cutting energy $K_s = 2.2$ N·m/mm³.

### Solution:

### Part (a) — MRR

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot f_r$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (20)^2 \cdot 79.6$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 400 \cdot 79.6$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 31840$$

$$\text{MRR} \approx 25007 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 25007 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 25 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}}$$

### Part (b) — Power Required

$$P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot K_s}{60 \cdot 10^6}$$

$$P = \dfrac{25007 \cdot 2.2}{60 \cdot 10^6}$$

$$P = \dfrac{55015.4}{60 \cdot 10^6}$$

$$P \approx 9.17 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 0.917 \text{ W}}$$

(Forces during drilling are small but multiplied by RPM produce significant torque.)

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Operation

| If you want to… | Use this operation | Tool employed | Starts from solid? |
|-----------------|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Make a new hole | **Drilling** | Twist drill | ✅ Yes |
| Enlarge an existing hole | **Boring** | Boring bar + SPCT | ❌ No |
| Improve accuracy of existing hole | **Reaming** | Reamer | ❌ No |
| Cut internal threads | **Tapping** | Tap | ❌ No |
| Chamfer the hole entry | **Counter Sinking** | Counter-sink cutter | ✅ Yes (on existing hole entry only) |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Procedure — Setting Up the Radial Drilling Machine

> [!NOTE]
> This procedure is asked verbatim in KTU viva voce. Memorize the sequence.

1. **Identify** the workpiece material and select the appropriate cutting speed from a handbook (KTU lab manual).
2. **Calculate** the spindle speed $N$ using $N = (1000V)/(\pi D)$.
3. **Set** the machine spindle to the calculated RPM using the speed-change levers.
4. **Mount** the drill in the spindle using a drill chuck (for parallel shank) or a sleeve/collet (for taper shank).
5. **Clamp** the workpiece securely on the machine table using suitable clamps; ensure no overhang beyond the table edge.
6. **Position** the radial arm so the spindle is directly above the marked hole center.
7. **Lower** the spindle (quill) to bring the drill point just above the workpiece surface.
8. **Peck** (if deep hole) — periodically retract the drill to evacuate chips.
9. **Apply** cutting fluid liberally during the cut.
10. **Withdraw** the drill while it is still rotating to avoid breaking the cutting edges.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Hole-Making Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Hole Making Operations"]:::root --> B["Primary Operation"]
    A --> C["Secondary Operations"]
    
    B --> B1["DRILLING"]:::primary
    B1 --> B1a["Twist Drill"]
    B1 --> B1b["Starts in solid material"]
    B1 --> B1c["Multi point rotary cutter"]
    
    C --> D["Boring"]:::secondary
    C --> E["Reaming"]:::secondary
    C --> F["Tapping"]:::secondary
    C --> G["Counter Sinking"]:::secondary
    
    D --> D1["Enlarges existing hole"]
    D --> D2["Single point tool"]
    E --> E1["Sizing finishing op"]
    E --> E2["Multi flute reamer"]
    F --> F1["Cuts internal threads"]
    F --> F2["Tap tool"]
    G --> G1["Conical entry chamfer"]
    G --> G2["90 degree or 120 degree"]
    
    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef primary fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef secondary fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Sequential Operations on a Workpiece

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Raw Workpiece"]:::start --> S2["Step 2: Mark Hole Centers"]:::mark
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Centre Punch"]:::mark
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: DRILLING"]:::drill
    S4 --> S5{"Further Machining Needed?"}:::decision
    S5 -- Yes, Larger Dia --> S6["BORING"]:::bore
    S5 -- Yes, Sizing --> S7["REAMING"]:::ream
    S5 -- Yes, Threads --> S8["TAPPING"]:::tap
    S5 -- Yes, Flush Head --> S9["COUNTER SINKING"]:::csk
    S6 --> S10["Finished Part"]:::finish
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S5 -- No --> S10
    
    classDef start fill:#37474f,color:#fff
    classDef mark fill:#fbc02d
    classDef decision fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
    classDef drill fill:#c62828,color:#fff
    classDef bore fill:#1565c0,color:#fff
    classDef ream fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    classDef tap fill:#ef6c00,color:#fff
    classDef csk fill:#5d4037,color:#fff
    classDef finish fill:#1b5e20,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Radial Drilling Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RAD["RADIAL DRILLING MACHINE"]
        M1["Base and Column"]:::base
        M2["Radial Arm"]:::arm
        M3["Drill Head with Spindle"]:::head
        M4["Quill Feed Mechanism"]:::feed
        M5["Work Table"]:::table
        M6["Coolant Pump"]:::cool
    end
    
    OP1["Operator Console"]:::op --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5
    M5 --> M1
    M6 --> M5
    
    classDef base fill:#5d4037,color:#fff
    classDef arm fill:#1565c0,color:#fff
    classDef head fill:#c62828,color:#fff
    classDef feed fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
    classDef table fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    classDef cool fill:#0277bd,color:#fff
    classDef op fill:#37474f,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Tool Nomenclature Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T["TWIST DRILL ANATOMY"]:::title --> P1["Point Angle 118 degree"]:::tip
    T --> P2["Chisel Edge"]:::tip
    T --> P3["Cutting Lips"]:::tip
    T --> P4["Flutes Helical"]:::body
    T --> P5["Body Diameter D"]:::body
    T --> P6["Margin Land"]:::body
    T --> P7["Neck"]:::neck
    T --> P8["Shank Taper or Straight"]:::shank
    T --> P9["Tang for taper shank"]:::shank
    
    classDef title fill:#1a237e,color:#fff
    classDef tip fill:#b71c1c,color:#fff
    classDef body fill:#0d47a1,color:#fff
    classDef neck fill:#1b5e20,color:#fff
    classDef shank fill:#e65100,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Comparison of Operation Tooling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph O1["DRILLING"]
        TD["Twist Drill"]:::tool1
    end
    subgraph O2["BORING"]
        BB["Boring Bar with SPCT"]:::tool2
    end
    subgraph O3["REAMING"]
        RM["Multi Flute Reamer"]:::tool3
    end
    subgraph O4["TAPPING"]
        TP["Hand or Machine Tap"]:::tool4
    end
    subgraph O5["COUNTER SINKING"]
        CS["Conical Counter Sink"]:::tool5
    end
    
    classDef tool1 fill:#c62828,color:#fff
    classDef tool2 fill:#1565c0,color:#fff
    classDef tool3 fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    classDef tool4 fill:#ef6c00,color:#fff
    classDef tool5 fill:#5d4037,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** Part A contains 5 short questions of 3 marks each; you must answer any **3**. The questions below are framed exactly in that style with complete model answers.

---

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any five operations that can be performed on a drilling machine.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The five principal operations performed on a drilling machine are:

1. **Drilling** — Producing a fresh cylindrical hole in a solid workpiece using a multi-point rotating tool (twist drill).
2. **Boring** — Enlarging an already existing hole to a precise diameter using a single-point cutting tool on a boring bar.
3. **Reaming** — Sizing and finishing a previously drilled/bored hole to improve dimensional accuracy and surface finish using a multi-fluted reamer.
4. **Tapping** — Cutting internal threads inside a pre-drilled hole using a threaded cutting tool called a tap.
5. **Counter Sinking** — Producing a conical enlargement at the entry of a drilled hole to accommodate flat-head screws, typically at 90° or 120° included angle.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between drilling and reaming (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Drilling | Reaming |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| **Purpose** | Creates a new hole in solid material | Sizes and finishes an existing hole |
| **Tool** | Twist drill (2 flutes typical) | Reamer (6 to 12 flutes) |
| **Material Removal** | Removes large volume (bulk removal) | Removes very small amount (0.1 to 0.4 mm) |
| **Tolerance Achieved** | IT10 to IT12 (loose) | IT7 to IT8 (tight) |
| **Surface Finish** | Ra 3.2 to 12.5 μm (rough) | Ra 0.8 to 3.2 μm (smooth) |
| **Cutting Speed** | High (25 to 35 m/min for mild steel) | Low (8 to 15 m/min for mild steel) |
| **Feed per Rev** | 0.05 to 0.30 mm/rev | 0.10 to 0.50 mm/rev |

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. You must answer **any one** from each pair. Below we provide two complete alternative questions (Question A and Question B) with model answers for every sub-part.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]` — **14 Marks**

**Q: With neat sketches, explain in detail the following hole-making operations performed on a drilling machine: (a) Drilling and Boring (7 Marks); (b) Reaming and Counter Sinking (7 Marks).**

#### Part (a) — Drilling and Boring (7 Marks)

**Drilling:**
Drilling is a chip-removal process in which a multi-point rotating cutter called a **twist drill** is used to produce a cylindrical hole in a solid workpiece. The drill rotates at a calculated spindle RPM while being advanced axially into the workpiece at a prescribed feed. Standard drill point angle is **118°** for mild steel. Drilling is the **only** hole-making operation that begins in solid material.

The cutting speed for mild steel is **25 to 35 m/min** and feed is **0.05 to 0.30 mm/rev**. Drilling oil or soluble oil is used as cutting fluid.

**Boring:**
Boring is the process of enlarging an already existing hole using a **single-point cutting tool** mounted on a boring bar. It is used when:
- The required hole diameter is too large to drill economically.
- The drilled hole is slightly off-center and needs correction.
- A precise internal profile or stepped hole is needed.

Cutting speed for boring mild steel is **30 to 50 m/min**. Boring can achieve IT7 accuracy.

#### Part (b) — Reaming and Counter Sinking (7 Marks)

**Reaming:**
Reaming is a sizing and finishing operation performed after drilling or boring. The **reamer** is a multi-fluted (typically 6 to 12 flutes) rotary cutting tool that removes only a small amount of material (0.1 to 0.4 mm on diameter) to:
- Improve dimensional accuracy to IT7 or IT8.
- Improve surface finish to Ra 0.8 to 3.2 μm.
- Correct minor roundness errors.

Cutting speed for reaming mild steel is **8 to 15 m/min** (much lower than drilling to avoid chatter and tool wear). Reaming is **never** used to start a hole.

**Counter Sinking:**
Counter sinking is the operation of producing a conical enlargement at the entry of a drilled hole using a **counter-sink tool**. The most common included angles are **90°** (metric flat-head screws per ISO 7721) and **120°** (for rivets and imperial standards). It is used to:
- Provide a flush seating for flat-head screws.
- Deburr the hole entry.
- Ease assembly of mating parts.

Cutting speed for counter sinking mild steel is **20 to 30 m/min** with feed of **0.05 to 0.15 mm/rev**.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` — **14 Marks**

**Q: A hole of 25 mm diameter and 50 mm depth is to be drilled in a mild steel workpiece. The cutting speed is 30 m/min and feed is 0.25 mm/rev. Calculate (a) Spindle speed, feed rate, and machining time (7 Marks); (b) Material removal rate and power required if specific cutting energy is 2.5 N·m/mm³ (7 Marks).**

#### Part (a) — Spindle Speed, Feed Rate, Machining Time (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 25$ mm
- $L_h = 50$ mm
- $V = 30$ m/min
- $f = 0.25$ mm/rev
- $K_s = 2.5$ N·m/mm³ (for part b)

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed $N$:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot 25}$$

$$N = \dfrac{30000}{78.54}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 382 \text{ RPM} \approx 380 \text{ RPM (nearest standard)}}$$

**Step 2 — Linear Feed Rate $f_r$:**

$$f_r = N \cdot f = 380 \cdot 0.25$$

$$\boxed{f_r = 95 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Length:**

$$L = L_h + 0.3 \cdot D = 50 + 0.3 \cdot 25 = 50 + 7.5 = 57.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time $T_m$:**

$$T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r} = \dfrac{57.5}{95}$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx 0.605 \text{ min} \approx 36.3 \text{ seconds}}$$

#### Part (b) — Material Removal Rate and Power (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Material Removal Rate MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot f_r$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (25)^2 \cdot 95$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 625 \cdot 95$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 59375$$

$$\text{MRR} \approx 46631 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 46631 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

**Step 2 — Power Required $P$:**

$$P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot K_s}{60 \cdot 10^6}$$

$$P = \dfrac{46631 \cdot 2.5}{60 \cdot 10^6}$$

$$P = \dfrac{116577.5}{60 \cdot 10^6}$$

$$P \approx 1.943 \cdot 10^{-3} \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 1.94 \text{ W}}$$

This low power is consistent because MRR for a single drill is small, but the torque requirement on the spindle is significant (torque ≈ 0.048 N·m at 380 RPM).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.3·D overshoot in machining time** — Most students compute $T_m$ using only $L_h$, missing the drill point overshoot. This **costs 2 marks** in calculation problems.
>
> 2. **Unit mismatch in Cutting Speed formula** — Writing $V = \pi D N$ without dividing by 1000. Cutting speed must be in **m/min**, diameter in **mm**, so the **1000 is mandatory**.
>
> 3. **Confusing Boring with Drilling** — Examiners **repeatedly deduct marks** when students say "boring creates a new hole." Boring **cannot** start in solid material.
>
> 4. **Wrong tap drill formula** — The tap drill diameter is $D_{\text{major}} - P$, not $D_{\text{major}} + P$. Adding the pitch is a common error.
>
> 5. **Skipping cutting fluid mention** — Always mention the **coolant used** (cutting oil for drilling/reaming, sulphurised oil for tapping) for full marks.
>
> 6. **No mention of safety** — In KTU practical exams, omitting the **safety glasses, loose-clothes, chip evacuation** points can **lose 1 mark** in viva.
>
> 7. **Incorrect unit for MRR** — Always convert final MRR to **mm³/min** (or cm³/min), not mm³/s.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it on your machine shop observation notebook for quick revision before the practical exam and viva.**

### 🔹 Core Operations Summary
- **Drilling** = Fresh hole in solid material (twist drill, 118° point, multi-point).
- **Boring** = Enlarges existing hole (single-point tool on boring bar).
- **Reaming** = Sizing and finishing (multi-flute reamer, removes only 0.1 to 0.4 mm).
- **Tapping** = Internal threads (tap, tap drill = $D_{\text{major}} - P$).
- **Counter Sinking** = Conical chamfer at hole entry (90° or 120°).

### 🔹 Key Formula Flashcards
- $V = \pi D N / 1000$
- $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$
- $f_r = N \cdot f$
- $T_m = (L_h + 0.3D) / (N f)$
- $\text{MRR} = (\pi / 4) D^2 f_r$
- $P = \text{MRR} \cdot K_s / (60 \cdot 10^6)$

### 🔹 Standard Speeds for Mild Steel (Handbook Values)
- Drilling → 25–35 m/min
- Boring → 30–50 m/min
- Reaming → 8–15 m/min
- Tapping → 5–10 m/min
- Counter Sinking → 20–30 m/min

### 🔹 Standard Drill Point Angles
- General purpose (mild steel, cast iron) → **118°**
- Hard materials (stainless, tool steel) → **135°** (split-point drill)

### 🔹 Standard Counter-Sink Angles
- Metric flat-head screws (ISO 7721) → **90°**
- Imperial and rivets → **120°**

### 🔹 Tap Drill Rules
- Tap drill = Major diameter − Pitch
- For ~75% thread engagement: Tap drill ≈ 0.85 × Major diameter
- Three-tap set: Taper (No.1), Plug (No.2), Bottoming (No.3)

### 🔹 Standard Cutting Fluids
- Drilling / Boring / Reaming / Counter Sinking → **Cutting oil** or **Soluble oil emulsion**
- Tapping → **Sulphurised cutting oil** (for better thread finish)

### 🔹 Safety Must-Reads Before the Practical Exam
- Always wear **safety goggles** (chip particles fly at high velocity).
- **Never** wear loose clothing, ties, or gloves near rotating spindles.
- **Clamp** the workpiece firmly — never hold it by hand.
- **Withdraw the drill while rotating** to avoid breakage.
- Use **chip hook or brush** to clear chips — **never use bare hands**.
- Apply **cutting fluid liberally** for tool life and surface finish.

### 🔹 Viva Quick-Fire Questions (Most Asked in KTU)
1. *Why is 118° chosen as the standard drill point angle?* — Optimal compromise for general-purpose steels; reduces thrust force and provides good chip formation.
2. *Why is reaming feed higher than drilling feed?* — Reamer has more flutes (6–12) and removes only a thin layer, so a higher feed is safe and produces better finish.
3. *Why is tapping speed lower than drilling?* — Tap has many cutting edges and small flute volume; lower speed prevents tap breakage and improves thread finish.
4. *Can boring be done on solid material?* — **No.** Boring always requires a pre-existing hole.
5. *What is the difference between a reamer and a drill?* — A drill removes bulk material; a reamer removes only a finishing allowance (0.1 to 0.4 mm) and has many more flutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes.** Always tie your answer back to the **KTU Course Outcome** being tested (typically CO2: *Demonstrate safe operation of basic machine tools* and CO3: *Calculate cutting parameters for machining processes*). Make sure your answer sheet clearly shows the **Given Data**, the **Formula Used**, the **Substitution**, and the **Final Answer with Units** — this four-step structure is what KTU examiners look for when awarding full marks in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Drilling Machine: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Drilling Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Drilling Machine: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Drilling Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛠️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Drilling** is a **material-removal machining process** that uses a **multi-point cutting tool** (the twist drill) to produce a **cylindrical hole** of a given diameter and depth in a solid workpiece by a combination of **rotary motion** (primary motion) and **linear translational motion** (secondary/feed motion) along the tool axis.

The **measurement of cutting forces** in drilling involves capturing the **three orthogonal force components** generated at the drill–workpiece interface — namely the **axial thrust force ($F_z$)**, the **tangential cutting force ($F_c$)**, and the **radial force ($F_r$)** — using a calibrated **drill-tool dynamometer** (most commonly a **strain-gauge-type platform dynamometer** or a **piezoelectric dynamometer**).

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Drill Diameter | $D$ | mm |
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | rpm |
| Feed per Revolution | $f$ | mm/rev |
| Axial Thrust Force | $F_z$ | N |
| Tangential Cutting Force | $F_c$ | N |
| Torque | $T$ | N·m |
| Specific Cutting Energy | $U$ | N·m/mm³ or J/mm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the Machine Shop laboratory, the experiment on drilling-force measurement is considered a **mandatory Module-2 experiment** under the course **PCSFL308 (Machine Shop)**. Students are expected to (i) operate the radial/bench drilling machine, (ii) record forces for at least **two cutting conditions**, and (iii) compute **power, MRR, and specific cutting energy** for viva-voce and record evaluation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — “The Screw That Cuts”

Imagine pushing a **wood-screw** into a wooden block with your hand. Your palm pushes **along the axis** (axial force), the screw threads **rotate** (causing torque), and the **side walls** of the screw press against the wood sideways (radial force). Drilling on a milling/drilling machine is **exactly this** — but instead of threads forcing the tool in, **two sharp cutting lips** at the drill tip carve out a chip, while the **flutes** sweep the chip out.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> - **Axial force ($F_z$)** = the *push-down* force on the drill. It determines how much **thrust** the machine spindle must withstand.
> - **Tangential force ($F_c$)** = the *twisting resistance* offered by the metal. It determines the **torque** the motor must deliver.
> - **Radial force ($F_r$)** = the *sideways push* that tends to deflect the drill and affect **hole accuracy**.

A useful everyday analogy is a **hand-cranked ice-cream freezer**: you push down (axial), you turn the crank (tangential/torque), and the dasher pushes sideways against the cylinder wall (radial). All three forces exist simultaneously, just like in drilling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Drilling Process

The drilling process converts **electrical energy → mechanical energy (rotary) → chip-formation energy (cutting)**. Energy is dissipated as:

$$\text{Input Power} \;=\; \text{Cutting Power} \;+\; \text{Friction Power} \;+\; \text{Heat Loss to Chip} \;+\; \text{Heat Loss to Tool} \;+\; \text{Heat Loss to Workpiece}$$

### 2.1.1 Primary and Secondary Motions

| Motion Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary (Cutting) Motion** | Rotation of the drill bit | Spindle motor |
| **Secondary (Feed) Motion** | Axial advancement into the workpiece | Hand-feed lever or automatic feed mechanism |
| **Tertiary Motion** (some setups) | Workpiece may also rotate (lathe-based drilling) | Lathe spindle |

### 2.1.2 Twist-Drill Geometry — The Cutting Tool

A standard **twist drill** has the following geometric elements that directly influence cutting force:

1. **Point Angle ($2\kappa_r$)** — typically **118°** for general steels; **135°** for hardened steels and cast iron.
2. **Helix Angle ($\beta$)** — typically **30°** for general-purpose drills (lower for aluminum, higher for magnesium).
3. **Lip Clearance Angle ($\alpha_o$)** — typically **8°–12°**, prevents rubbing at the cutting lip.
4. **Rake Angle ($\gamma_o$)** — varies along the lip; negative near the periphery, positive near the chisel edge.
5. **Chisel Edge** — the straight edge connecting the two cutting lips; contributes **~50%** of the total thrust force but does almost no cutting.
6. **Flutes** — provide chip evacuation and coolant passage.
7. **Margins (Lands)** — provide bearing surface for hole guidance.

---

## 2.2 The Force System in Drilling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Unlike turning (which has only two force components $F_c$ and $F_f$), **drilling has THREE orthogonal force components** plus a **torque**. This makes the drill dynamometer more complex than a turning dynamometer.

The **three orthogonal force components** measured at the drill tip are:

$$\boxed{\vec{F}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \vec{F}_z \,+\, \vec{F}_c \,+\, \vec{F}_r}$$

Where:
- $F_z$ — **Axial / Thrust Force** — directed **along the drill axis**; pushes the tool into the workpiece.
- $F_c$ — **Tangential / Cutting Force** — acts **tangentially to the drill periphery**; produces torque.
- $F_r$ — **Radial Force** — acts **radially toward the drill axis**; tends to deflect the drill.

The relationship between the tangential force and the **drilling torque ($T$)** is:

$$T \;=\; F_c \times \frac{D}{2}$$

The **resultant cutting force ($F$)** at the lip is:

$$F \;=\; \sqrt{F_c^{\,2} \,+\, F_r^{\,2} \,+\, F_z^{\,2}}$$

---

## 2.3 Principle of Drill Dynamometer

A **drill dynamometer** is a precision instrument mounted **between the drill spindle and the tool holder** (or as a workstation platform **beneath the workpiece**). It uses:

- **Strain gauges** (foil-type or semiconductor-type) bonded on the **elastic sensing elements** (usually a **Cartesian-ring** or **octagonal ring**) arranged in a **Wheatstone-bridge** configuration.
- When force is applied, the elastic element deforms; the strain gauges convert this deformation into a **resistance change**, which is proportional to the applied force.
- The signal is **amplified** and read on a **digital indicator / oscilloscope / DAQ system**.

> [!NOTE]
> A typical **drill-tool strain-gauge dynamometer** has **four sensing stations** to decouple the three force components ($F_x$, $F_y$, $F_z$) and the **torque ($M_z$)** independently.

---

## 2.4 🔥 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Part-B question on drilling.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi D}$ | rpm | Reverse of (1) |
| 3 | Feed Rate | $f_r \;=\; f \times N$ | mm/min | Linear feed velocity |
| 4 | Feed per Revolution | $f \;=\; \dfrac{f_r}{N}$ | mm/rev | From feed-rate dial |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} f N$ | mm³/min | Volume of chip per minute |
| 6 | Drilling Torque | $T \;=\; F_c \times \dfrac{D}{2}$ | N·m | From tangential force |
| 7 | Cutting Power | $P_c \;=\; \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W (or J/s) | Equivalent to $P_c = F_c \cdot V$ |
| 8 | Specific Cutting Energy | $U \;=\; \dfrac{P_c}{Q}$ | N·m/mm³ or J/mm³ | Also $U = F_c \cdot v / Q$ |
| 9 | Thrust Force (empirical) | $F_z \;=\; C_{F_z} \, D^{\,a} \, f^{\,b}$ | N | Constants depend on workpiece & tool |
| 10 | Torque (empirical) | $T \;=\; C_T \, D^{\,c} \, f^{\,d}$ | N·m | Constants depend on workpiece & tool |
| 11 | Machining Time | $t_m \;=\; \dfrac{L}{f_r} \;=\; \dfrac{L}{f \, N}$ | min | $L$ = total drill travel |
| 12 | Approach + Drilling Depth | $L \;=\; 0.3 D \,+\, \text{actual depth}$ | mm | Includes drill point overshoot |
| 13 | Metal Removal Rate (volumetric) | $Z_R \;=\; Q$ | mm³/min | Synonymous with MRR |
| 14 | Resultant Force | $F_{\text{res}} \;=\; \sqrt{F_c^{\,2} + F_f^{\,2} + F_z^{\,2}}$ | N | All three components included |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Application | Why Drilling-Force Measurement Matters |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** (Ti/Al/composite stack drilling) | Predicts **delamination**, **tool wear**, **chip evacuation** efficiency |
| **Automotive (Cylinder blocks)** | Optimises **spindle power sizing**, **coolant pressure selection** |
| **Biomedical Implants** (Bone drilling) | Limits **thrust force** to avoid **thermal necrosis** of bone tissue |
| **PCB Micro-drilling** | Forces are in **mN range** — demands ultra-sensitive piezoelectric dynamometers |
| **Adaptive Machining / Industry 4.0** | Real-time force signals feed **CNC controllers** to vary feed dynamically |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Show **every algebraic step**. Marks are awarded for **substitution logic** and **unit consistency**, not just the final answer. **Always state the formula before plugging values.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutting Speed ($V$) in Drilling

### Step 1 — Geometric Definition
The drill bit of diameter $D$ rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute. In one revolution, the **peripheral point** travels a circumferential distance of $\pi D$.

### Step 2 — Circumferential Distance per Revolution

$$d_{\text{per rev}} \;=\; \pi D \quad [\text{mm}]$$

### Step 3 — Circumferential Distance per Minute

$$d_{\text{per min}} \;=\; \pi D \times N \quad [\text{mm/min}]$$

### Step 4 — Convert to m/min (since standard cutting-speed unit is m/min)

$$V \;=\; \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}]$$

### ✅ Final Boxed Expression

$$\boxed{\,V \;=\; \frac{\pi D N}{1000}\,} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the **linear velocity of the outermost cutting lip** — the fastest-moving point on the drill. Heat generation is highest here.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feed Rate ($f_r$)

### Step 1 — Definition
Feed per revolution $f$ is the axial distance the drill advances **per revolution**.

### Step 2 — For $N$ revolutions in one minute

$$f_r \;=\; f \times N \quad [\text{mm/min}]$$

### ✅ Final Boxed Expression

$$\boxed{\,f_r \;=\; f \, N\,} \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Removal Rate ($Q$)

### Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Hole
A drilled hole is **cylindrical**, with cross-sectional area:

$$A \;=\; \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \quad [\text{mm}^2]$$

### Step 2 — Volume Removed per Minute
The drill removes a cylindrical column of cross-section $A$ and length $f_r$ per minute:

$$Q \;=\; A \times f_r \;=\; \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $f_r = f N$

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f \times N \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

### ✅ Final Boxed Expression

$$\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2} f N}{4}\,} \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Drilling Torque ($T$) from Cutting Force

### Step 1 — Free-Body Concept
The tangential force $F_c$ acts at the **outer diameter** of the drill. Its moment arm about the drill axis is:

$$r \;=\; \frac{D}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Torque = Force × Moment Arm

$$T \;=\; F_c \times r \;=\; F_c \times \frac{D}{2}$$

### ✅ Final Boxed Expression

$$\boxed{\,T \;=\; F_c \cdot \frac{D}{2}\,} \quad \text{(N·m)}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Cutting Power ($P_c$)

### Step 1 — Mechanical Power Definition
Power = Work done per unit time. Work done per revolution = Torque × angular displacement (in radians):

$$W_{\text{per rev}} \;=\; T \times 2\pi \quad [\text{N·m/rev}]$$

### Step 2 — Multiply by Number of Revolutions per Second
Revolutions per second $= N / 60$:

$$P_c \;=\; T \times 2\pi \times \frac{N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad [\text{W}]$$

### Step 3 — Equivalence Check (must equal $F_c \cdot V$)

$$P_c \;=\; F_c \times V \;=\; F_c \times \frac{\pi D N}{60 \times 1000} \quad [\text{W}]$$

For both expressions to match, $T = F_c \cdot D/2$. (Verify: $2\pi N \cdot F_c D / 2 / 60 = F_c \pi D N / 60$, but units need a factor of $1000$ for $V$ in m/min → final $P_c$ in W after dividing by 60s × 1000 mm/m. This confirms dimensional consistency.)

### ✅ Final Boxed Expression

$$\boxed{\,P_c \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}\,} \quad \text{(W)} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,P_c \;=\; F_c \cdot V\,} \quad \text{(W)}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Specific Cutting Energy ($U$)

### Step 1 — Definition
Specific cutting energy is the **energy required to remove a unit volume of material**:

$$U \;=\; \frac{\text{Cutting Power}}{\text{Material Removal Rate}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$U \;=\; \frac{P_c}{Q} \;=\; \frac{F_c \cdot V}{\frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot f_r}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Reference Values

| Workpiece Material | Typical Specific Cutting Energy $U$ (N·m/mm³) |
|---|---|
| **Aluminum Alloys** | 0.4 – 0.8 |
| **Brass** | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| **Mild Steel** | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| **Stainless Steel** | 2.5 – 3.5 |
| **Cast Iron** | 1.0 – 1.8 |
| **Titanium Alloys** | 3.0 – 4.5 |

> **Engineering Insight:** Specific cutting energy is the **most fundamental machinability indicator** — the lower the $U$, the more easily the material is cut.

### ✅ Final Boxed Expression

$$\boxed{\,U \;=\; \frac{P_c}{Q} \;=\; \frac{F_c \cdot V}{\frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} f_r}\,} \quad \text{(N·m/mm}^3\text{)}$$

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A **$\mathbf{20}$ mm-diameter** HSS twist drill is used to drill mild steel at a cutting speed of **$25$ m/min** and feed **$0.20$ mm/rev**. The measured axial thrust force and torque are **$2200$ N** and **$48$ N·m** respectively. Compute: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) Cutting Power, (v) Specific Cutting Energy, (vi) Machining Time for a **$50$ mm** deep hole (assume pilot drill tip is included).

### Given Data
- $D = 20$ mm, $V = 25$ m/min, $f = 0.20$ mm/rev
- $F_z = 2200$ N, $T = 48$ N·m
- Depth $L_{\text{actual}} = 50$ mm

### (i) Spindle Speed $N$

$$N \;=\; \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi D} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20}$$

$$\boxed{N \;=\; 397.9 \;\approx\; 398 \text{ rpm}}$$

### (ii) Feed Rate $f_r$

$$f_r \;=\; f \cdot N \;=\; 0.20 \times 397.9$$

$$\boxed{f_r \;=\; 79.58 \text{ mm/min}}$$

### (iii) Material Removal Rate $Q$

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \times f_r \;=\; \frac{\pi \times (20)^{2}}{4} \times 79.58$$

$$Q \;=\; 314.159 \times 79.58 \;=\; 25\,000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{Q \;=\; 25\,000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \;=\; 25 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}}$$

### (iv) Cutting Power $P_c$

$$P_c \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi \times 397.9 \times 48}{60}$$

$$P_c \;=\; \frac{120\,017}{60} \;=\; 2000 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_c \;=\; 2.00 \text{ kW}}$$

### (v) Specific Cutting Energy $U$

$$U \;=\; \frac{P_c}{Q} \;=\; \frac{2000 \text{ W}}{25\,000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

Converting $Q$ to mm³/s: $25\,000 / 60 = 416.67$ mm³/s

$$U \;=\; \frac{2000 \text{ J/s}}{416.67 \text{ mm}^3\text{/s}}$$

$$\boxed{U \;=\; 4.80 \text{ J/mm}^3 \;\equiv\; 4.80 \text{ N·m/mm}^3}$$

*(The slightly higher value indicates the chosen cutting parameters are aggressive — typical for mild steel should be 1.5–2.5.)*

### (vi) Machining Time $t_m$

Total drill travel $L = $ approach $+ $ actual depth $= 0.3 \times D + 50 = 0.3 \times 20 + 50 = 56$ mm

$$t_m \;=\; \frac{L}{f_r} \;=\; \frac{56}{79.58}$$

$$\boxed{t_m \;=\; 0.704 \text{ min} \;\approx\; 42.2 \text{ s}}$$

---

## 3.8 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Drilling Parameters

| Workpiece | Recommended $V$ (m/min) | Recommended $f$ (mm/rev) | Coolant | Tool Material |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 25–35 | 0.10–0.30 | Cutting oil | HSS / Coated HSS |
| Cast Iron | 30–50 | 0.15–0.40 | Dry / MQL | HSS / Carbide |
| Aluminum | 60–100 | 0.20–0.50 | Soluble oil | HSS / Carbide |
| Stainless Steel | 10–20 | 0.05–0.15 | Heavy cutting oil | Coated HSS / Carbide |
| Brass | 40–70 | 0.20–0.40 | Dry | HSS |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU viva:** Examiners frequently ask, *“If the workpiece is stainless steel, why must you reduce cutting speed?”* Answer: Stainless steel has **low thermal conductivity** and **high work-hardening tendency** — high speeds cause **built-up edge** formation and **tool failure**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📊 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are **Mermaid functional/architectural diagrams** showing the interaction of components in the drilling and force-measurement system — adapted for clarity since physical engineering drawings cannot be rendered directly in text.

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Drilling Machine Tool & Force Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[AC MAINS SUPPLY] --> B1[MOTOR DRIVE UNIT]
    B1 --> C1[SPINDLE ASSEMBLY]
    C1 --> D1[CHUCK]
    D1 --> E1[TWIST DRILL BIT]
    E1 --> F1[WORKPIECE]
    F1 --> G1[WORKHOLDING VICE OR CLAMP]
    G1 --> H1[MACHINE TABLE]
    H1 --> I1[MACHINE BASE]
    J1[FEED LEVER OR POWER FEED] --> C1
    K1[STRAIN GAUGE DYNAMOMETER] --> C1
    K1 --> L1[STRAIN GAUGE BRIDGE CIRCUIT]
    L1 --> M1[SIGNAL AMPLIFIER]
    M1 --> N1[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    N1 --> O1[DATA ACQUISITION DAQ MODULE]
    O1 --> P1[PERSONAL COMPUTER OR DISPLAY]
```

**Interpretation of Flow:**
- **Upper half (A1 → I1):** Power flow path from mains → spindle → drill → workpiece.
- **Lower-left (J1):** Feed input mechanism.
- **Lower-right (K1 → P1):** **Instrumentation flow** — dynamometer → amplifier → DAQ → PC, where force signals are recorded and processed.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Twist Drill Nomenclature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DRILL BIT ASSEMBLY] --> B1[POINT ANGLE TYP 118 DEG]
    A1 --> C1[LIP CLEARANCE ANGLE 8 TO 12 DEG]
    A1 --> D1[HELIX ANGLE 30 DEG]
    A1 --> E1[CUTTING LIPS LIP RELIEF]
    A1 --> F1[CHISEL EDGE]
    A1 --> G1[FLUTES CHIP CHANNELS]
    A1 --> H1[MARL OR LAND BEARING SURFACE]
    A1 --> I1[WEB THICKER NEAR SHANK]
    A1 --> J1[SHANK TAPERED OR STRAIGHT]
    A1 --> K1[BODY DIAMETER D]
```

**Interpretation of Flow:** This block-level map identifies **every critical geometric feature** of the twist drill, with the **point angle, helix angle, and chisel edge** being the **most force-influencing parameters**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Force Components on a Drill Bit During Cutting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[DRILL BIT IN ACTIVE CUT] --> B1[AXIAL THRUST FORCE FZ]
    A1 --> C1[TANGENTIAL FORCE FC]
    A1 --> D1[RADIAL FORCE FR]
    B1 --> E1[ALONG DRILL AXIS DOWNWARD]
    C1 --> F1[PERPENDICULAR TO AXIS TANGENTIAL]
    D1 --> G1[PERPENDICULAR TO AXIS RADIAL]
    C1 --> H1[PRODUCES DRILLING TORQUE]
    H1 --> I1[T EQUALS FC TIMES D DIVIDED BY 2]
    B1 --> J1[POWER ALONG FEED DIRECTION]
    J1 --> K1[FEED POWER PF EQUALS FZ TIMES F TIMES N]
    C1 --> L1[CUTTING POWER PC]
    L1 --> M1[PC EQUALS FC TIMES V EQUALS 2 PI N T BY 60]
```

**Interpretation of Flow:** This diagram distinguishes the **axial thrust** (which contributes only to **feed power** $P_f$, not cutting power) from the **tangential force** (which is the **only** contributor to true **cutting power** $P_c$). This distinction is often tested in KTU viva.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Experimental Procedure (Step-by-Step Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START] --> B1[FIX WORKPIECE IN VICE]
    B1 --> C1[MOUNT DRILL BIT IN CHUCK]
    C1 --> D1[CALIBRATE DYNAMOMETER ZERO LOAD]
    D1 --> E1[SET SPINDLE SPEED N RPM]
    E1 --> F1[SET FEED F MM PER REV]
    F1 --> G1[START MACHINE ENGAGE FEED]
    G1 --> H1[RECORD THRUST FORCE FZ]
    H1 --> I1[RECORD TORQUE T]
    I1 --> J1[MEASURE HOLE DEPTH]
    J1 --> K1[STOP MACHINE RETRACT DRILL]
    K1 --> L1[CALCULATE V FR Q PC U TM]
    L1 --> M1[REPEAT FOR SECOND CUTTING CONDITION]
    M1 --> N1[TABULATE RESULTS PLOT GRAPHS]
    N1 --> O1[END]
```

**Interpretation of Flow:** This is the **canonical laboratory procedure** used in KTU-affiliated Machine Shop labs. Examiners often ask students to **trace this procedure aloud** during viva-voce.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Force vs. Process Parameter Trends

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[INCREASING FEED F] --> B1[FZ INCREASES NON LINEARLY]
    A1 --> C1[T INCREASES NON LINEARLY]
    D1[INCREASING CUTTING SPEED V] --> E1[FZ DECREASES SLIGHTLY]
    D1 --> F1[T DECREASES SLIGHTLY]
    G1[INCREASING DRILL DIAMETER D] --> H1[FZ INCREASES STRONGLY]
    G1 --> I1[T INCREASES VERY STRONGLY D POWER 2]
    J1[INCREASING TOOL WEAR] --> K1[FZ INCREASES GRADUALLY]
    J1 --> L1[T INCREASES GRADUALLY]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Take-Away:** Feed and drill diameter are the **two most influential parameters** on cutting forces. Doubling the diameter roughly **quadruples** the torque — a critical insight when scaling up drill sizes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📌 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Define the following terms with reference to drilling: (a) cutting speed, (b) feed per revolution, (c) material removal rate. State their units.**

#### Model Answer:

> **(a) Cutting Speed ($V$):** It is the **relative linear velocity between the cutting edge and the workpiece surface** at the point of contact, measured at the **outer diameter of the drill**. Mathematically,
> $$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \text{ m/min}$$
>
> **(b) Feed per Revolution ($f$):** It is the **axial distance travelled by the drill into the workpiece per revolution** of the spindle. It is set on the feed dial of the machine. **Unit: mm/rev.**
>
> **(c) Material Removal Rate (MRR or $Q$):** It is the **volume of material removed per unit time** during drilling. For drilling,
> $$Q = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot f \cdot N \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**List and briefly explain the three orthogonal force components developed during drilling. How is each measured?**

#### Model Answer:

> The three orthogonal force components in drilling are:
>
> **(i) Axial Thrust Force ($F_z$):** Acts **along the drill axis**, pushing the tool into the workpiece. Measured using strain gauges mounted vertically on the dynamometer elastic element.
>
> **(ii) Tangential Cutting Force ($F_c$):** Acts **tangentially at the drill periphery**, perpendicular to the drill axis. It is the **only force that produces torque**. Measured using strain gauges oriented tangentially.
>
> **(iii) Radial Force ($F_r$):** Acts **radially outward (or inward) from the drill axis**, tending to deflect the drill sideways. Measured using strain gauges oriented radially on the dynamometer.
>
> The torque is then derived as $T = F_c \cdot D/2$.

---

## 5.2 📚 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 scheme:** Part-B questions offer **internal choice** between two full 14-mark questions. Both choices are independent.

---

### ⭐ Question B.A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat labelled diagram**, describe the **constructional features and geometry of a standard twist drill**. Highlight the role of **point angle, helix angle, and chisel edge** in determining cutting force. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **$15$ mm diameter** HSS twist drill is used on mild steel at a cutting speed of **$20$ m/min** and feed **$0.15$ mm/rev**. Compute:
   - (i) Spindle speed
   - (ii) Feed rate
   - (iii) MRR
   - (iv) Machining time for a **$40$ mm** deep hole **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to B.A(a):

The **twist drill** consists of the following parts:

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Point** | Conical end with cutting lips and chisel edge |
| **Body** | Cylindrical portion with flutes and margins |
| **Shank** | Held in the chuck; tapered or straight |
| **Flutes** | Helical grooves for chip evacuation |
| **Cutting Lips** | Two sharp edges that perform actual cutting |
| **Chisel Edge** | Connects the cutting lips; contributes mostly to thrust |
| **Margins (Lands)** | Narrow bearing strips that guide the drill in the hole |
| **Web** | Central core of the drill, tapering toward the point |

**Geometric Angles and Their Role:**

- **Point Angle ($2\kappa_r = 118°$ standard):** Determines the **lip geometry** and **chip thickness**. Smaller angles (e.g., 90°) for soft materials; larger angles (e.g., 135°) for hardened materials to reduce thrust force.
- **Helix Angle ($\beta \approx 30°$ standard):** Controls **chip flow** and **rake angle**. Higher helix = better chip evacuation in soft materials; lower helix = stronger cutting edge for hard materials.
- **Chisel Edge:** Although it removes **no material**, it accounts for **~50% of the total thrust force** due to **extrusion and rubbing** at the dead centre of the drill.

The **rake angle** varies along the cutting lip — it is **positive near the chisel edge** and **negative near the periphery**, which is why drilling requires **higher thrust forces** than turning.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to B.A(b):

**Given:** $D = 15$ mm, $V = 20$ m/min, $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, depth = $40$ mm.

### (i) Spindle Speed $N$

$$N \;=\; \frac{1000 V}{\pi D} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 15}$$

$$\boxed{N \;=\; \frac{20\,000}{47.124} \;=\; 424.4 \text{ rpm}}$$

### (ii) Feed Rate $f_r$

$$f_r \;=\; f \times N \;=\; 0.15 \times 424.4$$

$$\boxed{f_r \;=\; 63.66 \text{ mm/min}}$$

### (iii) Material Removal Rate $Q$

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \times f_r \;=\; \frac{\pi \times (15)^{2}}{4} \times 63.66$$

$$Q \;=\; 176.71 \times 63.66 \;=\; 11\,250 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{Q \;=\; 11\,250 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

### (iv) Machining Time $t_m$

Total drill travel $L = 0.3 D + \text{actual depth} = 0.3 \times 15 + 40 = 4.5 + 40 = 44.5$ mm

$$t_m \;=\; \frac{L}{f_r} \;=\; \frac{44.5}{63.66}$$

$$\boxed{t_m \;=\; 0.699 \text{ min} \;\approx\; 41.95 \text{ s}}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B.B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe with a **neat sketch** the working principle of a **strain-gauge-type drill dynamometer** used for measuring cutting forces in drilling. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During drilling of mild steel with a **$12$ mm** diameter drill at **$500$ rpm** and **$0.10$ mm/rev feed**, the thrust force and torque were measured as **$1200$ N** and **$20$ N·m** respectively. Compute: (i) tangential cutting force, (ii) cutting power, (iii) specific cutting energy, (iv) MRR. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to B.B(a):

A **strain-gauge drill dynamometer** is mounted between the **drill spindle and the tool holder**. It typically uses an **elastic sensing element** (Cartesian ring / octagonal ring) with **strain gauges bonded** at critical locations.

**Working Principle:**

1. **Force application:** During drilling, the three force components ($F_x$, $F_y$, $F_z$) and torque ($M_z$) act on the drill.
2. **Elastic deformation:** These forces deform the elastic sensing element of the dynamometer by a **microscopic amount** (within Hooke's law region).
3. **Strain-gauge response:** The bonded **foil-type strain gauges** change their **electrical resistance** in proportion to the strain:
   $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; K \cdot \varepsilon$$
   where $K$ = gauge factor (≈ 2.0 for constantan) and $\varepsilon$ = strain.
4. **Wheatstone bridge:** The strain gauges are connected in a **Wheatstone-bridge circuit** to convert resistance changes into a **measurable voltage output**.
5. **Signal conditioning:** The millivolt signal is **amplified**, **filtered**, and sent to a **DAQ system** for real-time recording on a computer.
6. **Calibration:** Before use, the dynamometer is **calibrated** by applying known loads and recording the corresponding voltages — a **calibration constant** (V/N) is established.
7. **Decoupling:** Multiple strain gauges at **four stations** allow **independent extraction** of $F_x$, $F_y$, $F_z$, and $M_z$ using appropriate algebraic combinations of the four bridge outputs.

**Advantages:**
- High accuracy (within ±1%)
- Measures all four components ($F_x, F_y, F_z, M_z$) simultaneously
- Suitable for dynamic (real-time) measurements

**Limitations:**
- Sensitive to temperature drift
- Requires regular calibration
- Costlier than simple tool-force gauges

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to B.B(b):

**Given:** $D = 12$ mm, $N = 500$ rpm, $f = 0.10$ mm/rev, $F_z = 1200$ N, $T = 20$ N·m

### (i) Tangential Cutting Force $F_c$

$$F_c \;=\; \frac{2T}{D} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 20}{0.012} \quad [\text{since } D = 0.012 \text{ m}]$$

$$\boxed{F_c \;=\; 3333.33 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 3333 \text{ N}}$$

*(Alternatively, if working in mm: $T = F_c \cdot D/2$ where $T$ is in N·mm if $D$ is in mm. Since $T = 20$ N·m = $20\,000$ N·mm,*
$$F_c = \frac{2 \times 20\,000}{12} = 3333.33 \text{ N.}$$)*

### (ii) Cutting Power $P_c$

$$P_c \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi \times 500 \times 20}{60}$$

$$P_c \;=\; \frac{62\,832}{60}$$

$$\boxed{P_c \;=\; 1047.2 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 1.047 \text{ kW}}$$

### (iii) Material Removal Rate $Q$

$$f_r \;=\; f \cdot N \;=\; 0.10 \times 500 \;=\; 50 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \times f_r \;=\; \frac{\pi \times (12)^{2}}{4} \times 50$$

$$Q \;=\; 113.097 \times 50 \;=\; 5654.87 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

Converting to mm³/s:

$$Q \;=\; \frac{5654.87}{60} \;=\; 94.25 \text{ mm}^3\text{/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \;=\; 94.25 \text{ mm}^3\text{/s} \;=\; 5654.87 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

### (iv) Specific Cutting Energy $U$

$$U \;=\; \frac{P_c}{Q} \;=\; \frac{1047.2 \text{ W}}{94.25 \text{ mm}^3\text{/s}}$$

$$\boxed{U \;=\; 11.11 \text{ J/mm}^3}$$

> **Note:** This value of $U$ is **higher than typical mild-steel range** (1.5–2.5 J/mm³), suggesting the cutting parameters may be conservative or the drill may be dull. In practice, such high $U$ values indicate poor machinability conditions.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Torque:** The most common error is mixing mm and m for drill diameter. Always use **consistent units** — either keep $D$ in mm and $T$ in N·mm, or convert $D$ to m and $T$ to N·m.
>
