> 2. **Forgetting Drill-Point Overshoot:** When calculating machining time, students forget to add **$0.3 D$** for the conical drill-point travel. This costs **1 mark** consistently.
>
> 3. **Confusing Cutting Power vs Feed Power:** Many students wrongly add $F_z$ to compute cutting power. **Only the tangential force $F_c$ produces cutting power.** Axial force produces **feed power** $P_f = F_z \cdot f_r$.
>
> 4. **Wrong Sign of Specific Cutting Energy:** $U$ is always **positive**; ensure $P_c$ and $Q$ are in **matching time units** (both per second OR both per minute).
>
> 5. **Confusing MRR with Feed Rate:** MRR is a **volume rate** (mm³/min), feed rate is a **linear rate** (mm/min). Do not interchange.
>
> 6. **Skipping Unit Conversion in $V$:** When $V$ is given in m/min and $D$ in mm, the formula needs the factor **$1000$** in the denominator (or $D$ converted to m).
>
> 7. **Ignoring Chisel-Edge Contribution:** Examiners expect students to **explicitly mention** that the chisel edge contributes up to **50% of axial thrust force** even though it does no cutting.

---

## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these 20 bullets in your memory before the lab viva or university exam.**

- ☐ **Drilling** = rotary (primary) + axial feed (secondary) motion; produces cylindrical holes.
- ☐ **Twist drill** is the standard tool; **118° point angle, 30° helix angle** for general use.
- ☐ **Three force components:** $F_z$ (axial thrust), $F_c$ (tangential/cutting), $F_r$ (radial).
- ☐ **Cutting Speed:** $V = \pi D N / 1000$ in **m/min** ($D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm).
- ☐ **Feed Rate:** $f_r = f \cdot N$ in **mm/min**.
- ☐ **MRR:** $Q = (\pi D^{2}/4) \cdot f_r$ in **mm³/min**.
- ☐ **Torque:** $T = F_c \cdot D/2$ in **N·m**.
- ☐ **Cutting Power:** $P_c = 2 \pi N T / 60$ in **W**; equivalently $P_c = F_c \cdot V$.
- ☐ **Specific Cutting Energy:** $U = P_c / Q$ in **N·m/mm³ or J/mm³**.
- ☐ **Feed Power:** $P_f = F_z \cdot f_r$ (often ignored in basic problems).
- ☐ **Machining Time:** $t_m = L / f_r$ where $L = 0.3 D +$ actual depth.
- ☐ **Drill Dynamometer:** strain-gauge-based instrument with **Wheatstone-bridge** configuration.
- ☐ **Calibration** must be done **before each experiment** with known loads.
- ☐ **Chisel Edge** contributes **~50% of $F_z$** but performs no cutting.
- ☐ **Doubling diameter** roughly **quadruples torque** — sizing the spindle motor carefully.
- ☐ **Feed ($f$) is the most sensitive parameter** affecting $F_z$ and $T$ (power-law relationship).
- ☐ **Cutting speed ($V$) is the most sensitive parameter** affecting tool life (Taylor's tool-life equation).
- ☐ **Typical $U$ values:** Aluminium ≈ 0.4–0.8; Mild steel ≈ 1.5–2.5; Stainless ≈ 2.5–3.5; Titanium ≈ 3.0–4.5 J/mm³.
- ☐ **For deeper holes** (>4×D), use **peck-drilling** to evacuate chips and avoid breakage.
- ☐ **Always state units** in the final answer — KTU examiners **deduct marks** for missing units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Cylindrical Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Plain Cylindrical Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exercises on Cylindrical Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Plain Cylindrical Surface # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain Cylindrical Grinding (External Cylindrical Surface Grinding)** is an abrasive machining process in which a rotating **cylindrical grinding wheel** removes material from the external surface of a workpiece that is rotated about its own axis. The workpiece is supported on **work centers (or held in a chuck)** mounted on a **headstock and tailstock** of a universal cylindrical grinder. The wheel is traversed longitudinally along the workpiece axis and fed radially (infeed) to achieve the required diameter and surface finish. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory (PCSFL308 – Machine Shop), *Experiment 2* trains the student to: - Identify the components and controls of a **Plain Cylindrical Grinder** (Surface Grinder cousin used for round jobs). - Set up a mild-steel cylindrical workpiece between dead and live centers. - Dress the grinding wheel using a **single-point diamond dresser**. - Perform a parallel/plain cylindrical grinding operation to a specified **diameter tolerance** and **surface roughness (Ra)**. - Measure the finished dimensions using **micrometer, vernier caliper, and surface roughness tester (if available)**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍽️ Imagine you are rolling a **rotisserie chicken** on a long skewer held between two supports. While the chicken slowly turns (workpiece rotation), you rub a **sharp, spinning stone disc** gently along its length. Wherever the stone touches, a tiny stream of sparks flies away — that is metal being shaved off as fine dust. The faster you push the stone inward (infeed) or slide it lengthwise (traverse), the more meat (material) is removed. The roaster (workpiece) = mild steel shaft The stone = grinding wheel The sparks = chips (swarf) The two holders = headstock & tailstock centers The sideways slide = longitudinal traverse The inward push = radial infeed ## 1.3 Standards, Constants & Key Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Grinding Wheel Abrasive Choices for Plain Cylindrical Grinding of Steel:** > - **Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃)** – most common for HSS, mild steel, alloy steels. > - **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** – for cast iron, non-ferrous, carbide. > - **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)** – for hardened tool steels (> 60 HRC). > - **Diamond** – for cemented carbides and ceramics. **Standard Wheel Designation (ISO 525):** `A 60 K 5 V` example — - A = Abrasive (Al₂O₃) - 60 = Grit size (medium, ~250 µm) - K = Grade (medium hard, 7th of 15) - 5 = Structure (medium open) - V = Bond (Vitrified) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Typical Parameters in Plain Cylindrical Grinding of Mild Steel:** > - **Wheel surface speed (v_s) ≈ 30 m/s (≈ 1800 m/min)** for vitrified Al₂O₃ > - **Workpiece surface speed (v_w) ≈ 15–30 m/min** > - **Radial infeed (f_r) ≈ 0.005 – 0.025 mm/pass** (roughing → finishing) > - **Longitudinal feed (f_a) ≈ 0.25 – 0.75 × wheel width per workpiece revolution** > - **Achievable Surface Roughness (Ra) ≈ 0.4 – 1.6 µm** for fine finish > - **Coolant:** Water-soluble emulsion, flood application (flow ≈ 10–20 L/min) ## 1.4 Why Cylindrical Grinding? (Engineering Utility) Plain cylindrical grinding is the **last machining operation** for shafts, pins, journals, and bearings surfaces. It is used because: - It achieves **very high dimensional accuracy (±0.0025 mm)** and **low Ra**. - It can finish **hardened (hard-turned) surfaces** where turning tools fail. - It removes only a thin layer (≈ 0.1–0.5 mm), so it is essentially a **finishing** process. - It generates cylindrical, taper, fillet, and shoulder surfaces by wheel shaping. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** In a 3-mark question, always state the **three motions** in cylindrical grinding — **(1) Primary motion (wheel rotation), (2) Secondary motion (workpiece rotation), (3) Feed motion (wheel traverse + radial infeed)**. Examiners expect this exact triplet. # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three / Four Fundamental Motions | # | Motion | Symbol | Typical Range (Steel) | Function | |---|--------|--------|------------------------|----------| | 1 | Wheel rotation (primary) | $v_s$ | 25 – 35 m/s | Cutting action | | 2 | Work rotation (secondary) | $v_w$ | 15 – 30 m/min | Generates uniform cut, brings fresh surface | | 3 | Longitudinal traverse | $f_a$ | 0.25–0.75 × wheel width/rev | Spreads wear along wheel periphery | | 4 | Radial infeed (cross-feed) | $f_r$ | 0.005 – 0.025 mm/pass | Controls depth of cut, final diameter | ## 2.2 Process Theory — Step-by-Step Mechanics 1. **Abrasive-grain engagement:** Each grit on the wheel periphery acts like a miniature single-point cutting tool with a **negative rake angle (≈ –30° to –60°)**. 2. **Chip formation:** When a grain is sharp, it plows a micro-chip from the workpiece. With dull grains, material is removed by **plowing + rubbing**. 3. **Wheel wear modes:** Three concurrent mechanisms — - **Attritious wear** (flank wear of grit) - **Fracture wear** (grit micro-cracks and breaks → exposes new sharp grit — *self-sharpening!*) - **Bond fracture** (grit pulled out) 4. **Glazing:** When attritious wear dominates and bond is too hard → wheel appears *shiny and smooth* → rubbing, heat, burn marks on workpiece → **needs dressing**. 5. **Loading:** When bond is too soft or grit is too blunt → chips weld into pores → wheel cannot cut → **needs dressing**. 6. **Dressing:** Truing + Sharpening of wheel using a **single-point diamond dresser** (rotary diamond dresser for production). 7. **Spark-out:** The last 2–4 passes are made **without infeed**, allowing elastic recovery of the system and improving roundness & finish. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does cylindrical grinding give such high accuracy?** > Because the **diameter is set by a precision cross-slide micrometer (graduated to 0.0025 mm)** feeding the wheel head, and the **workpiece is supported on rigid centers** — there is no tool deflection as in turning. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 📐 > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL TYPESETTING RULE:** In the markdown table below, the **absolute-value bar** is written as `\vert` (LaTeX) instead of `|` so that the table syntax is **not broken**. | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | |---|----------|---------|-------------------|----------------------------| | 1 | Wheel surface speed | $v_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_s \cdot N_s}{60 \times 1000}$ | $D_s$ wheel dia (mm), $N_s$ wheel rpm | 30 m/s | | 2 | Workpiece surface speed | $v_w = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N_w}{60 \times 1000}$ | $D$ work dia (mm), $N_w$ work rpm | 20 m/min | | 3 | Equivalent chip thickness (roughing) | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w \cdot f_r}{v_s}$ | $v_w$ m/s, $f_r$ m/rev, $v_s$ m/s | 0.5 – 2 µm | | 4 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $Z_w = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f_r$ | $D$ mm, $L$ mm, $f_r$ mm/pass | mm³/s | | 5 | Specific Grinding Energy | $e = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v_s}{Z_w}$ | $F_t$ tangential force (N) | 20 – 60 J/mm³ | | 6 | Surface Roughness (theoretical) | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f_a^{2}}{32 \cdot r}$ | $f_a$ mm/rev, $r$ wheel nose radius mm | 0.4 – 1.6 µm | | 7 | Spark-out passes | $n_{so} = 2 \text{ to } 4$ | dimensionless | 2–4 | | 8 | Number of grits in contact | $N_c = B \cdot l_c \cdot C$ | $B$ wheel width, $l_c$ contact length, $C$ grit density | 10–100 | | 9 | Contact length | $l_c = \sqrt{f_r \cdot D_s}$ | $f_r$ infeed, $D_s$ wheel dia | 0.5 – 2 mm | | 10 | Wheel-life parameter | $G = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s}$ | $V_w$ work vol removed, $V_s$ wheel vol worn | 100 – 1000 | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Component ground on plain cylindrical grinder | |----------|---------------------------------------------| | Automobile | Crankshaft journals, camshaft bearings, piston pins | | Aerospace | Landing-gear pins, hydraulic actuator shafts | | Tool-room | Drill shanks, reamer shanks, plug-gage pins | | Pump industry | Shaft sleeves, impeller hubs | | General Machine | All hardened shafts requiring ±0.005 mm tolerance & Ra < 0.8 µm | > [!TIP] > **For viva:** *“Why is the grinding wheel run at very high surface speed (30 m/s) and the workpiece at only 20 m/min?”* > → Because each grain is in contact for an extremely short time; high $v_s$ means more grains engage per second, lowering **specific energy** and preventing **thermal damage** to the workpiece. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Wheel Surface Speed The peripheral speed of the grinding wheel is the linear velocity at the rim of the wheel. It is derived from the basic kinematic relation $v = r\omega$, where $\omega$ is the angular velocity. ### Step 1 — Angular velocity The wheel rotates at $N_s$ revolutions per minute. One revolution = $2\pi$ rad. Therefore, in one minute the wheel sweeps $2\pi N_s$ radians, and in one second the angular velocity is: $$ \omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60}\;\; \text{rad/s} $$ ### Step 2 — Tangential velocity For a wheel of diameter $D_s$ (mm) the rim radius is $r_s = D_s/2$ (mm) = $D_s/2000$ (m). Multiplying: $$ v_s = r_s \, \omega_s = \frac{D_s}{2000} \cdot \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{\pi D_s N_s}{60\,000}\;\; \text{m/s} $$ ### Step 3 — Convert to m/min (optional) Multiply by 60: $$ v_s = \frac{\pi D_s N_s}{1000}\;\; \text{m/min} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) Consider one full **longitudinal pass** of the wheel across a workpiece of length $L$ (mm) and diameter $D$ (mm) with a radial infeed of $f_r$ (mm/pass). ### Step 1 — Volume removed per pass The radial infeed removes a thin cylindrical shell of thickness $f_r$ over length $L$: $$ V_{pass} = (\text{circumference}) \times L \times f_r = \pi D \cdot L \cdot f_r $$ ### Step 2 — Number of effective passes per unit time If the table reciprocates at $f_{a}$ (m/min) and the wheel width is $B$ (mm), the **time per pass** is $t_{pass} = L / f_a$ (when measured appropriately). The number of passes per second is $1/t_{pass}$. ### Step 3 — MRR expression $$ \boxed{\,Z_w = \frac{\pi D L f_r}{t_{pass}} = \pi D \cdot f_a \cdot f_r\,} $$ where $f_a$ is now treated as effective cross-feed in mm/s. **Result:** Higher workpiece diameter, faster traverse, and deeper infeed → higher MRR → higher wheel wear. ## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Chip Thickness $h_{eq}$ This is the **single most important grinding parameter** because it directly controls surface roughness, specific energy, and wheel wear. ### Step 1 — Geometric picture The contact zone between wheel and workpiece is an area of length $l_c$ (along the workpiece circumference) and width $B$ (wheel width). Material is removed at rate $Z_w$ from this area, while the wheel surface moves at $v_s$. ### Step 2 — Volumetric balance $$ Z_w = (\text{Area}) \times v_s \times h_{eq} $$ With Area $= l_c \cdot B$ and $l_c = \sqrt{f_r D_s}$ (geometric contact length): $$ h_{eq} = \frac{Z_w}{l_c \, B \, v_s} = \frac{\pi D \, f_a \, f_r}{B \sqrt{f_r D_s} \; v_s} = \frac{\pi D \, f_a \, \sqrt{f_r}}{B \sqrt{D_s} \; v_s} $$ For external cylindrical grinding, the classical simplified form is: $$ \boxed{\,h_{eq} = \frac{v_w \, f_r}{v_s}\,} $$ where $v_w$ is the workpiece surface speed (m/s) and $f_r$ is radial infeed per workpiece revolution (m). ### Step 3 — Interpretation - Larger $v_w$ (faster work rotation) **increases** chip thickness → rougher surface. - Larger $v_s$ (faster wheel) **decreases** chip thickness → finer surface and lower specific energy. - Larger $f_r$ (deeper cut) **increases** chip thickness → faster MRR but rougher. > [!IMPORTANT] > **For a finish pass**, keep $h_{eq} < 1\;\mu m$. For a roughing pass, $h_{eq}$ can be 2–5 µm. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style) > **Problem:** A cylindrical grinder is set up to grind a mild-steel shaft of initial diameter $D = 50$ mm down to $D_f = 49.85$ mm. The grinding wheel is 300 mm diameter, runs at $N_s = 1910$ rpm. The workpiece rotates at $N_w = 120$ rpm. The longitudinal feed is $f_a = 250$ mm/min and the radial infeed per pass is $f_r = 0.015$ mm. Calculate: > 1. Wheel surface speed $v_s$ > 2. Workpiece surface speed $v_w$ > 3. Material removal rate $Z_w$ > 4. Equivalent chip thickness $h_{eq}$ > 5. Time required to remove the radial stock, $t = (D - D_f)/(2 f_r) \times t_{pass}$ (assuming 1 pass removes 0.015 mm radial). > 6. Contact length $l_c$ ### Solution **1. Wheel surface speed:** $$ v_s = \frac{\pi D_s N_s}{60\,000} = \frac{\pi \times 300 \times 1910}{60\,000} = 30.0\;\;\text{m/s} $$ **2. Workpiece surface speed:** $$ v_w = \frac{\pi D N_w}{60\,000} = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 120}{60\,000} = 0.314\;\;\text{m/s} = 18.85\;\;\text{m/min} $$ **3. MRR** (using average diameter): $$ D_{avg} = \frac{50 + 49.85}{2} = 49.925\;\text{mm} $$ $$ Z_w = \pi D_{avg} f_a f_r = \pi \times 49.925 \times 250 \times 0.015 \;\;\text{mm}^3/\text{min} $$ $$ Z_w = 588.2\;\;\text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\;\approx \;\;9.8\;\;\text{mm}^3/\text{s} $$ **4. Equivalent chip thickness:** $$ h_{eq} = \frac{v_w \, f_r}{v_s} = \frac{0.314 \times 0.015 \times 10^{-3}}{30} = 1.57 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{m} = 0.157\;\mu\text{m} $$ → This is well in the **finish-grinding regime** ($h_{eq} < 1\;\mu m$ borderline; great for finishing). **5. Time required:** Total radial stock $= (50 - 49.85)/2 = 0.075$ mm. Number of passes $= 0.075/0.015 = 5$ passes. Time per pass $= L / f_a$ (assume $L = 100$ mm) $= 100/250 = 0.4$ min. Total grinding time $= 5 \times 0.4 = 2.0$ min ≈ 120 s. Plus 2–3 spark-out passes = $2.4$ min ≈ 144 s. **6. Contact length:** $$ l_c = \sqrt{f_r \cdot D_s} = \sqrt{0.015 \times 300} = \sqrt{4.5} = 2.12\;\;\text{mm} $$ ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "If you see this, do this" | Observation in machine | Probable cause | Corrective action | |------------------------|----------------|-------------------| | Burn marks on work | Wheel dull, low $v_s$, no coolant | Dress wheel, check coolant flow, reduce $f_r$ | | Chatter marks (regular pattern) | Vibration, weak setup | Tighten work between centers, balance wheel, reduce $f_r$ | | Spiral marks on work | Wheel not dressed flat, work rotating wrong direction | Re-dress, check rotation sense | | Tapered diameter | Work centers offset (height wrong) | Set tail-stock center to same height as headstock | | Poor size control | Thermal expansion of work | Increase coolant, allow cool-down, spark-out | | Wheel loads quickly | Soft bond, fine grit on soft work | Use coarser grit, harder bond, dress more often | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **cross-feed** (radial infeed, $f_r$) with **table feed** (longitudinal traverse, $f_a$). Both are feed motions. Be precise with terminology in written answers. # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Plain Cylindrical Grinding Machine (Functional Architecture) The figure below shows the **functional block architecture** of a plain cylindrical grinder. The workpiece usually rotates such that its **surface moves downward** at the contact line for conventional down-grinding. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *List the **three principal motions** in plain cylindrical grinding and state the typical range of wheel surface speed used for grinding mild steel.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three motions are: 1. **Primary motion** – rotation of the grinding wheel (typical $v_s$ = **25–35 m/s**, i.e., 1500–2100 m/min). 2. **Secondary motion** – rotation of the workpiece (typical $v_w$ = **15–30 m/min**). 3. **Feed motion** – (a) longitudinal table traverse, (b) radial cross-slide infeed. For mild steel, vitrified aluminium-oxide wheel is recommended and $v_s \approx 30$ m/s is standard. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *What is **dressing of a grinding wheel**? Name the tool used and explain why dressing is necessary.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Dressing** is the process of **trueing** (restoring geometric accuracy) and **sharpening** (exposing fresh, sharp abrasive grits) of a grinding wheel. - **Tool used:** Single-point diamond dresser (held on the machine table or a separate dressing attachment). - **Why necessary:** To remove **glazing** (dull, polished surface) and **loading** (chips embedded in pores); to restore free-cutting action; to true the wheel profile so that correct geometry (e.g., straight cylindrical, taper, or form) is obtained. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks) > **Module Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The student is expected to attempt **one full 14-mark question** from this module. Two alternatives are provided below. ### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** > **(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a plain cylindrical grinding machine. (7 marks)** > **(b) A mild steel shaft of 60 mm diameter and 150 mm length is to be ground to 59.92 mm on a cylindrical grinder. The grinding wheel diameter is 350 mm and runs at 1800 rpm. The work speed is 100 rpm. The radial infeed per pass is 0.02 mm and the table traverse is 200 mm/min. Determine (i) wheel surface speed, (ii) work surface speed, (iii) material removal rate, and (iv) number of passes required. (7 marks)** #### 🔑 Model Solution **(a) Construction and working (7 marks):** A plain cylindrical grinding machine consists of: - **Bed** – heavy cast-iron base with accurate slideways (3 marks for naming parts). - **Headstock** – carries the work spindle and live center, with variable speed (AC motor + VFD). - **Tailstock** – carries the dead center; the body slides on the bed and can be clamped. The quill is spring-loaded to prevent damage. - **Wheel head** – mounted on a cross-slide (infeed) on top of a vertical slide. Carries the grinding wheel spindle driven by a separate high-power motor. - **Table** – reciprocates hydraulically for longitudinal traverse; range typically 0.1 to 5 m/min. - **Coolant system** – pump, tank, nozzles, splash guard. - **Control panel** – start/stop, work RPM display, ammeter, E-stop, work-head swivel handle for taper grinding. **Working (4 marks):** The workpiece is mounted between centers, the wheel is brought to contact, a small radial infeed is given, table is set in reciprocation, and the wheel grinds the rotating work to size. After roughing, fine infeeds are given and finally **spark-out** passes for accuracy and finish. Coolant is flooded onto the contact zone to flush chips and carry away heat. **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):** - $D = 60$ mm, $L = 150$ mm, $D_f = 59.92$ mm - $D_s = 350$ mm, $N_s = 1800$ rpm - $N_w = 100$ rpm, $f_r = 0.02$ mm/pass, $f_a = 200$ mm/min **(i) Wheel surface speed:** $$ v_s = \frac{\pi D_s N_s}{60\,000} = \frac{\pi \times 350 \times 1800}{60\,000} = 32.99 \approx 33\;\text{m/s} $$ **(ii) Work surface speed:** $$ v_w = \frac{\pi D N_w}{60\,000} = \frac{\pi \times 60 \times 100}{60\,000} = 0.314\;\text{m/s} = 18.85\;\text{m/min} $$ **(iii) Material removal rate** (using average diameter $D_{avg} = 59.96$ mm): $$ Z_w = \pi D_{avg} f_a f_r = \pi \times 59.96 \times 200 \times 0.02 = 753.5\;\text{mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 12.56\;\text{mm}^3/\text{s} $$ **(iv) Number of passes:** $$ \text{Radial stock} = \frac{D - D_f}{2} = \frac{60 - 59.92}{2} = 0.04\;\text{mm} $$ $$ \text{Passes} = \frac{0.04}{0.02} = 2\;\text{passes} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Q-A):** Students frequently (i) forget to convert $v_s$ units to **m/s** (the answer should be in m/s if the formula in standard textbooks is used), and (ii) use the **initial** diameter instead of **average** diameter in the MRR formula. Both lead to 1-mark deductions each. --- ### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** > **(a) Explain the procedure for grinding a plain cylindrical surface on a universal cylindrical grinder. Mention the role of **spark-out** and the typical **wheel specification** you would choose. (7 marks)** > **(b) List any **four common defects** observed in cylindrical grinding and state the **cause and remedy** for each. (7 marks)** #### 🔑 Model Solution **(a) Procedure for plain cylindrical grinding (7 marks):** 1. **Preparation of machine** (1 mark): Clean bed, check oil levels, verify that work-head, wheel-head, and hydraulic table operate smoothly. 2. **Wheel selection and mounting** (1 mark): Choose **vitrified aluminium-oxide wheel** of medium grit (e.g., **A 60 K 5 V** for mild steel). Ring-test for cracks. Mount on balanced arbor. 3. **Dressing** (1 mark): True and sharpen the wheel using a **single-point diamond dresser** with a light cross-feed; this restores sharpness and flatness. 4. **Work mounting** (1 mark): Set the workpiece between **dead and live centers**; lubricate centers with grinding paste. Set work RPM for $v_w \approx 20$ m/min. Adjust tailstock center height to match headstock center (to avoid taper). 5. **Grinding cycle** (2 marks): - **Roughing** – 0.015–0.025 mm radial infeed; high table speed. - **Finishing** – 0.005–0.010 mm radial infeed; reduce table speed. - **Spark-out** – 2–4 final passes with **zero infeed**, allowing elastic recovery of the system → improves roundness, size, and surface finish. 6. **Inspection** (1 mark): Measure with **micrometer (0.01 mm)** and **surface roughness comparator**; record Ra. **Role of spark-out:** During spark-out, the wheel continues to grind for a few revolutions **without further infeed**. The system elastically relaxes, removing any residual high spots. This dramatically improves **roundness, size repeatability, and surface finish**. **Wheel specification for mild steel:** Abrasive = **Aluminium Oxide (A)**, Grit = **46–60** (medium), Grade = **J–L** (medium-soft), Structure = **5–7**, Bond = **Vitrified (V)**. Example: **A 60 K 5 V**. **(b) Four common grinding defects — cause and remedy (7 marks: ≈1.75 each):** | # | Defect | Cause | Remedy | |---|--------|-------|--------| | 1 | **Burn marks** (yellow, blue, brown) on work | Excessive heat due to dull wheel, low coolant, high infeed | Dress wheel, increase coolant flow, reduce $f_r$ | | 2 | **Chatter marks** (regular wavy pattern) | Vibration, work not rigid, wheel out of balance | Balance wheel, tighten centers, reduce infeed, check bearing | | 3 | **Tapered diameter** (large at one end) | Tailstock center misaligned in height | Adjust tailstock height to match headstock center exactly | | 4 | **Spiral marks** on work | Wheel face not flat, work rotating wrong direction (up-grinding instead of down-grinding) | Re-dress wheel, reverse work rotation, reduce traverse | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Q-B):** Many students write "increase speed" without specifying **which** speed (wheel or work). Always specify — for burn, **reduce infeed** or **increase wheel speed**; for chatter, **reduce infeed** and **balance the wheel**. Vague answers cost 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 🌟 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Top 20 high-yield points for KTU 2024 ESE on Plain Cylindrical Grinding:** - [x] **Three motions** → Wheel rotation, work rotation, longitudinal traverse + radial infeed. - [x] **Wheel speed** $v_s$ ≈ **30 m/s** (1800 m/min) for Al₂O₃ on steel. - [x] **Work speed** $v_w$ ≈ **15–30 m/min** for mild steel. - [x] **MRR formula** → $Z_w = \pi D L f_r$ (per pass) or $Z_w = \pi D f_a f_r$ (rate form). - [x] **Equivalent chip thickness** $h_{eq} = v_w f_r / v_s$ — controls finish and specific energy. - [x] **Spark-out** = 2 to 4 zero-infeed final passes → improves roundness & Ra. - [x] **Dressing** uses a **single-point diamond dresser** → removes glazing & loading. - [x] **Glazing** → wheel appears shiny & smooth → needs dressing. - [x] **Loading** → chips stuck in wheel pores → needs dressing. - [x] **Coolant** → water-soluble emulsion, flood application at 10–20 L/min. - [x] **Standard wheel for steel** → A 46/60 J/K 5 V (vitrified Al₂O₃). - [x] **For hardened steel** (>60 HRC) → use **CBN** wheel. - [x] **Surface roughness achievable** → Ra = 0.4 to 1.6 µm (fine grinding can go < 0.2 µm). - [x] **Tolerance achievable** → ±0.0025 mm (micro-inch range). - [x] **Contact length** $l_c = \sqrt{f_r D_s}$ — used in specific energy & grit-contact calculations. - [x] **Three wear modes** of wheel → attrition, fracture, bond breakage. - [x] **Burn marks** = overheating → reduce infeed, dress, check coolant. - [x] **Taper** = center-height error → re-align tailstock. - [x] **Ring test** → tap wheel with a non-metallic mallet; a clear ringing sound = crack-free; dull thud = cracked (discard). - [x] **Never start a grinding machine without checking wheel guards, coolant flow, and dressing the wheel first** — this is the most-marks KTU safety question. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute formula card to memorize:** > $v_s = \pi D_s N_s / (60\,000)$ m/s · $v_w = \pi D N_w / (60\,000)$ m/s · $Z_w = \pi D f_a f_r$ · $h_{eq} = v_w f_r / v_s$ · $l_c = \sqrt{f_r D_s}$ · $R_a \approx f_a^2/(32r)$

topics.title: Exercises on Surface Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Flat Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Surface Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Flat Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

**Surface Grinding** is a material removal process in which a rotating abrasive grinding wheel removes a thin layer of metal from the surface of a workpiece to produce a flat, smooth, and dimensionally accurate surface. The process is performed on a **Surface Grinding Machine** (also called a **Horizontal Spindle Reciprocating Table Surface Grinder**), classified under *Category 7 – Grinding & Other Abrasive Processes* per ISO 3685 and ASME B74-series standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board Examiner Wording):**
> "Surface grinding is an abrasive machining process that utilizes a horizontally mounted grinding wheel rotating about its own axis while the workpiece is reciprocated linearly under the wheel on a magnetic chuck, producing flat surfaces with high dimensional accuracy (typically ±0.01 mm) and superior surface finish (typically Ra 0.4–1.6 µm)."

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Grinding Wheel** | Multitooth abrasive cutting tool composed of abrasive grains, bond, and pores |
| **Magnetic Chuck** | Work-holding device using permanent or electromagnetic force to hold ferromagnetic workpieces |
| **Wheel Grade** | Indicates the bond strength (soft to hard: A to Z) |
| **Wheel Structure** | Indicates the spacing between abrasive grains (dense to open: 1 to 16) |
| **Spark Test** | Visual method of identifying metals by observing sparks emitted during grinding |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Carpenter's Plane"

Imagine a carpenter using a long hand plane on a wooden table. The blade (like the **grinding wheel**) is fixed; the carpenter pushes the wood (the **workpiece**) back and forth under it. A chip-thin layer is shaved off with each pass. With every stroke, the surface becomes smoother and flatter.

Now imagine the carpenter does this **thousands of times per minute** with a blade covered in **microscopic cutting teeth** (the **abrasive grains**), and uses **water** to keep everything cool and wash away the dust. That is exactly what a surface grinder does — except with **hardened steel**, **diamond-hard abrasive grains (Al₂O₃ or SiC)**, and **computer-grade precision**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded per KTU Norms):**
> - Peripheral wheel speed for Al₂O₃ wheel: **30 m/s** (standard)
> - Peripheral wheel speed for SiC wheel: **20–25 m/s**
> - Standard grinding tolerance achievable: **±0.01 mm**
> - Typical surface roughness achieved: **Ra 0.4 to 1.6 µm**
> - Hardness of Al₂O₃ abrasive (Knoop): **≈ 2100 HK**
> - Hardness of SiC abrasive (Knoop): **≈ 2500 HK**

## 1.3 Classification of Surface Grinders (Per KTU Lab Manual)

Surface grinders are classified by **table motion** and **spindle orientation**:

1. **Horizontal Spindle, Reciprocating Table (Most common in KTU labs)** — Wheel horizontal, table moves left–right
2. **Horizontal Spindle, Rotary Table** — Wheel horizontal, table rotates (for circular work)
3. **Vertical Spindle, Reciprocating Table** — Wheel face grinds (cup wheel)
4. **Vertical Spindle, Rotary Table** — Used for high-volume production

The KTU lab experiment uses **Type 1**: the horizontal spindle reciprocating table surface grinder.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Surface Grinding Machine

The machine consists of the following major sub-systems (aligned with KTU lab identification questions):

- **Base / Bed**: Heavy cast iron structure absorbing vibrations
- **Table**: Reciprocates horizontally; holds magnetic chuck
- **Wheel Head**: Houses the grinding wheel spindle (horizontal axis)
- **Column**: Vertical pillar supporting the wheel head
- **Cross-Feed Slide**: Moves wheel head sideways (Y-axis)
- **Vertical Feed Handwheel**: Lowers/raises wheel (Z-axis, depth of cut)
- **Coolant System**: Flood coolant supply (water-based emulsion)
- **Magnetic Chuck**: Holds the workpiece during grinding

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "Identify the parts of a surface grinder" is a frequently asked 3-mark question. Memorize the names and locations of *table, wheel head, magnetic chuck, cross-slide, and vertical handwheel*.

## 2.2 The Four Independent Process Parameters

Surface grinding performance is governed by exactly **four controllable parameters**, known as the *KTU Grinding Tetrahedron*:

1. **Wheel Speed (V)** — Peripheral velocity of the grinding wheel, in m/s
2. **Table Speed (V_w)** — Linear velocity of the workpiece under the wheel, in m/min
3. **Cross Feed (f)** — Lateral movement per table stroke, in mm/pass
4. **Infeed / Depth of Cut (d)** — Vertical plunge per pass, in mm/pass

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variables | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wheel Peripheral Speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60 \times 1000}$ | $D$ = wheel dia, $N$ = rpm | m/s | Wheel selection |
| Table Speed | $V_w = \dfrac{L \cdot N_{st}}{1000}$ | $L$ = stroke length, $N_{st}$ = strokes/min | m/min | Process time |
| Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $Z = V_w \cdot f \cdot d$ | $V_w$ in mm/s, $f$ & $d$ in mm | mm³/s | Productivity |
| Specific Energy | $u = \dfrac{P}{Z}$ | $P$ = power, $Z$ = MRR | J/mm³ | Power req. |
| Equivalent Chip Thickness | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{V_w \cdot f}{V \cdot C \cdot r}$ | $C$ = no. of active grits, $r$ = grit radius | mm | Surface finish |
| Spark Temperature Check | $T_{max} \approx 1500-2000^\circ C$ | At grit/work interface | °C | Heat damage |
| Number of Active Grits | $C = \dfrac{\text{Grit density} \times \text{Contact area}}{1}$ | Empirical | — | Surface integrity |
| Surface Roughness (Approx.) | $R_a \approx \dfrac{h_{eq}}{4}$ | For ductile materials | µm | Finish prediction |
| Effective Diameter | $D_e = \dfrac{D \cdot d_w}{1 + (d_w / D)}$ | $d_w$ = workpiece width | mm | Cylindrical equiv. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **wheel peripheral speed V (m/s)** with **table speed V_w (m/min)**. Students regularly interchange units — this costs 1–2 marks per paper.

## 2.4 Why These Parameters Matter — The "Why-How" Logic

- **Why increase V (wheel speed)?** → More grits strike per second → finer finish, less heat per grit, longer wheel life
- **Why decrease V_w (table speed)?** → Less material removed per grit → finer finish, but lower productivity
- **Why reduce d (infeed)?**** → Less rubbing, less heat → less thermal damage, better dimensional accuracy
- **Why increase f (cross feed)?** → Wider coverage per stroke → better flatness, but coarser finish

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Surface grinding is the **final finishing operation** for die-steel punches, tool-room fixtures, machine tool ways, and bearing surfaces. Without it, modern metrology (CMMs, optical flats) would not function because there would be no perfectly flat reference surface to compare against.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wheel Peripheral Speed

The grinding wheel rotates about its own axis. A point on the rim traces a circle of circumference $\pi D$ per revolution, where $D$ is the wheel diameter.

$$
\text{Distance per revolution} = \pi D \quad [\text{in mm, if D in mm}]
$$

If the wheel rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the linear distance covered per minute is:

$$
\text{Distance per minute} = \pi D \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}]
$$

Converting to meters per second:

$$
V = \frac{\pi D \cdot N}{60 \times 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{mm/min}}{\text{60 s/min} \times \text{1000 mm/m}}\right] = \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}
$$

**Step-by-step unit check:**

$$
\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{min}} \times \frac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ s}} \times \frac{1 \text{ m}}{1000 \text{ mm}} = \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**
> **Problem:** A surface grinding operation uses a grinding wheel of diameter $D = 250$ mm rotating at $N = 1500$ rpm. The table reciprocates at 20 strokes/min over a stroke length of $L = 300$ mm. The cross feed is $f = 5$ mm/pass and the infeed depth of cut is $d = 0.02$ mm. Calculate: (a) Wheel peripheral speed, (b) Table speed, (c) MRR.

### Part (a) — Wheel Peripheral Speed

$$
V = \frac{\pi D N}{60 \times 1000}
$$

Substituting $D = 250$ mm and $N = 1500$ rpm:

$$
V = \frac{\pi \times 250 \times 1500}{60000}
$$

$$
V = \frac{1178097.2}{60000}
$$

$$
\boxed{V = 19.63 \ \text{m/s}}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is close to the standard recommended speed for an Al₂O₃ wheel (≈ 30 m/s). A 250 mm wheel at 1500 rpm gives ~20 m/s — slightly under-speed, acceptable for finish grinding to reduce heat.

### Part (b) — Table Speed

$$
V_w = \frac{L \cdot N_{st}}{1000}
$$

Substituting $L = 300$ mm and $N_{st} = 20$ strokes/min:

$$
V_w = \frac{300 \times 20}{1000}
$$

$$
V_w = \frac{6000}{1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_w = 6 \ \text{m/min}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Table speed 6 m/min is a typical **fine-finish** value. Rough grinding uses 15–30 m/min.

### Part (c) — Material Removal Rate

MRR in surface grinding is a product of three perpendicular rates:

$$
Z = V_w \cdot f \cdot d
$$

**Step 1 — Unit alignment:** Convert $V_w$ from m/min to mm/s:

$$
V_w = 6 \ \frac{\text{m}}{\text{min}} = \frac{6 \times 1000}{60} = 100 \ \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{s}}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply with cross feed and depth:**

$$
Z = 100 \ \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{s}} \times 5 \ \text{mm} \times 0.02 \ \text{mm}
$$

$$
Z = 100 \times 0.10
$$

$$
\boxed{Z = 10 \ \text{mm}^3/\text{s}}
$$

**Engineering insight:** An MRR of 10 mm³/s corresponds to ~36 cm³/min — typical for a finish pass on a small die block.

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Chip Thickness $h_{eq}$

The "equivalent chip thickness" model treats the grinding process as if a single cutting tooth (grit) is removing a chip of uniform thickness.

**Step 1 — Define contact length** between wheel and workpiece:

$$
l_c = \sqrt{D \cdot d}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of grits engaged** in the contact zone (from empirical grit density $C$):

$$
\text{Total active grits} = C \cdot l_c \cdot b
$$

where $b$ = width of cut.

**Step 3 — Volume removed per unit time** by all active grits:

$$
Z = V \cdot b \cdot h_{eq} \cdot C \cdot l_c
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve** for $h_{eq}$:

$$
h_{eq} = \frac{V_w \cdot f}{V \cdot \sqrt{D \cdot d} \cdot C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This simplified form $h_{eq} = \dfrac{V_w \cdot f}{V \cdot C}$ (assuming $r = 1$) is the form used in KTU exam problems. The actual expression varies by textbook, but the trend is **always** the same: $h_{eq} \uparrow$ as $V_w$ and $f$ increase, and $h_{eq} \downarrow$ as $V$ increases.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameters on Output

| Process Parameter ↑ | Surface Finish (Ra) | MRR | Wheel Wear | Heat Generated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wheel Speed $V$ ↑ | Improves (↓ Ra) | Slight ↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Table Speed $V_w$ ↑ | Degrades (↑ Ra) | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Cross Feed $f$ ↑ | Degrades (↑ Ra) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Depth of Cut $d$ ↑ | Degrades (↑ Ra) | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ |
| Wheel Hardness ↑ | Variable | ↓ | ↓ | Variable |

**Memory mnemonic for KTU exam:** *"VDf-fail; Vw-fall"* → If $V_w$ and $f$ are reduced, surface finish improves.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Surface Grinding Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply 3 Phase AC] --> B[Main Motor 1 to 3 HP]
    B --> C[Wheel Spindle]
    C --> D[Grinding Wheel Al2O3 or SiC]
    D --> E[Contact Zone with Workpiece]
    E --> F[Material Removal MRR]
    F --> G[Chips and Sparks]
    E --> H[Heat Generation]
    H --> I[Coolant Flood Nozzle]
    I --> J[Coolant Reservoir]
    J --> K[Coolant Pump]
    K --> I
    L[Table Drive Motor] --> M[Lead Screw]
    M --> N[Reciprocating Table]
    N --> O[Magnetic Chuck]
    O --> P[Workpiece]
    P --> E
    Q[Cross Feed Handwheel] --> R[Cross Slide Y axis]
    R --> S[Wheel Head]
    S --> D
    T[Vertical Handwheel] --> U[Screw Jack Z axis]
    U --> S
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        P1[Wheel Speed V]
        P2[Table Speed Vw]
        P3[Cross Feed f]
        P4[Depth of Cut d]
    end
    subgraph MACHINE[Surface Grinding Machine]
        W[Grinding Wheel]
        T[Reciprocating Table]
        M[Magnetic Chuck]
        C[Coolant System]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Process Phenomena]
        R[Material Removal]
        H[Heat Generation]
        S[Spark Formation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Quality]
        O1[Surface Roughness Ra]
        O2[Dimensional Accuracy]
        O3[Flatness]
        O4[MRR Productivity]
    end
    P1 --> W
    P2 --> T
    P3 --> W
    P4 --> W
    W --> R
    T --> R
    M --> T
    C --> H
    R --> O1
    R --> O2
    R --> O3
    R --> O4
    H --> O1
```

## 4.3 Process Sequence Flowchart — Lab Exercise Steps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Exercise] --> B[Read Work Drawing]
    B --> C[Identify Material and Required Finish]
    C --> D[Select Grinding Wheel Spec]
    D --> E[Mount Wheel and Dress]
    E --> F[Clean Magnetic Chuck]
    F --> G[Place Workpiece on Chuck]
    G --> H[Switch ON Magnetic Chuck]
    H --> I[Set Wheel Speed V]
    I --> J[Set Table Speed Vw]
    J --> K[Set Cross Feed f]
    K --> L[Set Infeed d]
    L --> M[Start Coolant Flow]
    M --> N[Run Table Without Infeed Dry Run]
    N --> O[Apply Infeed and Begin Grinding]
    O --> P[Measure with Dial Indicator]
    P --> Q{Size Achieved?}
    Q -->|No| O
    Q -->|Yes| R[Switch OFF Chuck and Clean]
    R --> S[Measure Final Ra with Surftest]
    S --> T[Record Observations]
    T --> U[End Exercise]
```

## 4.4 Grinding Wheel Specification — Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Grinding Wheel Code Example: AA 60 K 5 V] --> B[AA Abrasive Type Al2O3]
    A --> C[60 Grit Size Medium]
    A --> D[K Grade Soft to Medium]
    A --> E[5 Structure Dense]
    A --> F[V Bond Type Vitrified]
```

**KTU Examiner Note:** The standard wheel specification format is **Abrasive – Grit Size – Grade – Structure – Bond**. Always read left to right. For grinding **hardened steel (HRC 55+)**, use **AA 60 K 5 V** or similar. For **soft non-ferrous metals**, use **C (SiC) wheels** with softer grades.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four parts of a surface grinding machine and state their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Magnetic Chuck** — Holds ferromagnetic workpiece rigidly using electromagnetic force; ensures uniform holding and easy loading/unloading.
2. **Wheel Head** — Houses the grinding wheel spindle and motor; allows the wheel to rotate at high speed and to be lowered for infeed.
3. **Reciprocating Table** — Carries the magnetic chuck and workpiece; moves linearly under the wheel to enable material removal along the workpiece length.
4. **Coolant Supply System** — Delivers cutting fluid to the grinding zone to reduce heat, flush chips, and improve surface finish and wheel life.

> [!NOTE]
> Any 4 correctly named parts with valid functions → full 3 marks.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of a magnetic chuck? Why is it preferred over a mechanical vise for surface grinding?**

**Model Answer:**

A magnetic chuck holds the workpiece using electromagnetic attraction and provides a flat reference surface that becomes the datum for grinding. It is preferred over a mechanical vise because:
- It allows the **entire top surface** to be exposed for grinding (vises obstruct part of the surface)
- It provides **uniform clamping pressure**, avoiding workpiece distortion
- Loading/unloading is **faster**, improving productivity
- It does not obstruct the **side or edge** surfaces, enabling grinding of multiple faces in one setup

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a horizontal spindle reciprocating table surface grinding machine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a surface grinding operation on a hardened steel workpiece (HRC 60), the following data is given: wheel diameter $D = 200$ mm, wheel speed $N = 1800$ rpm, table stroke length $L = 250$ mm, table strokes per minute $= 25$, cross feed $f = 4$ mm/pass, depth of cut $d = 0.025$ mm. Calculate:
  - (i) Wheel peripheral speed in m/s
  - (ii) Table speed in m/min
  - (iii) Material removal rate in mm³/s **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks)

**Principle:** In a horizontal spindle reciprocating table surface grinder, the grinding wheel rotates about a **horizontal axis**, while the workpiece mounted on a magnetic chuck reciprocates linearly under the wheel. Each pass of the table removes a thin layer of material equal to the depth of cut.

**Procedure:**
1. Workpiece is mounted on the **magnetic chuck** and held by electromagnetic force.
2. The **grinding wheel** is rotated at high speed by the spindle motor.
3. The **table reciprocates** horizontally (left-right) carrying the workpiece under the rotating wheel.
4. The **coolant** is flooded onto the contact zone to reduce heat and flush away chips.
5. After each table stroke, the wheel is moved **laterally by cross feed (f)**.
6. When the entire width is covered, the wheel is **lowered by infeed (d)** for the next pass.
7. Repeat until the required size and surface finish are achieved.

**Sketch (text-based representation):**

```
            ┌──────────────┐
            │ Wheel Head   │
            └──────┬───────┘
                   │
              ╔════╧═══╗  ← Grinding Wheel (rotating)
              ║        ║
              ╚════════╝
   ←──────────███████████──────────→  ← Table reciprocating
   (Magnetic chuck + workpiece below)
              │
              ▼  Infeed (downward) by handwheel
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Wheel peripheral speed:**

$$
V = \frac{\pi D N}{60 \times 1000}
$$

$$
V = \frac{\pi \times 200 \times 1800}{60000}
$$

$$
V = \frac{1130973}{60000}
$$

$$
\boxed{V = 18.85 \ \text{m/s}}
$$

**(ii) Table speed:**

$$
V_w = \frac{L \cdot N_{st}}{1000}
$$

$$
V_w = \frac{250 \times 25}{1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_w = 6.25 \ \text{m/min}}
$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

**Step 1** — Convert $V_w$ to mm/s:

$$
V_w = 6.25 \times \frac{1000}{60} = 104.17 \ \text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 2** — Compute MRR:

$$
Z = V_w \cdot f \cdot d = 104.17 \times 4 \times 0.025
$$

$$
\boxed{Z = 10.42 \ \text{mm}^3/\text{s}}
$$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What are the four main process parameters in surface grinding? Explain the effect of each on surface roughness and material removal rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on: (i) Grinding wheel specifications, (ii) Dressing and Truing of grinding wheel, (iii) Coolant in grinding. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Four Process Parameters (7 marks)

| S.No | Parameter | Symbol | Effect on Ra | Effect on MRR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel Peripheral Speed | $V$ | Ra decreases as V increases (more cutting points) | MRR increases slightly |
| 2 | Table Speed | $V_w$ | Ra increases as V_w increases | MRR increases linearly |
| 3 | Cross Feed | $f$ | Ra increases with higher f | MRR increases linearly |
| 4 | Depth of Cut / Infeed | $d$ | Ra increases with higher d | MRR increases linearly |

**Memory aid:** Wheel speed is the only parameter that **reduces Ra** while **increasing productivity** — the rest all degrade finish when increased.

#### Part (b) — Short Notes (7 marks)

**(i) Grinding Wheel Specification (2 marks):**
Standard marking: **Abrasive – Grit size – Grade – Structure – Bond**
Example: **AA 60 K 5 V** means Al₂O₃ abrasive, 60 grit (medium), K grade (medium soft), structure 5 (medium dense), Vitrified bond.

**(ii) Dressing and Truing (3 marks):**
- **Truing**: Restores the **geometric shape** of the wheel (roundness, flatness). Done with a diamond tool mounted on a dresser.
- **Dressing**: Exposes fresh abrasive grains by **removing dull/worn grits** from the wheel surface. Done with a dressing stick (e.g., silicon carbide stick) or rotary dresser.
- Dressing is performed **after truing** and **periodically during grinding** to maintain cutting efficiency.

**(iii) Coolant in Grinding (2 marks):**
- Coolants (typically **water-based emulsions**) reduce the **grinding zone temperature** which can otherwise reach 1500–2000 °C.
- They **flush away chips**, **prevent wheel loading**, **improve surface finish**, and **extend wheel life**.
- Common coolants: soluble oil emulsion, synthetic fluids, neat cutting oil (for high finish).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Unit confusion** — Mixing m/s with m/min for wheel vs. table speed (1 mark deducted each instance).
> 2. **MRR formula misuse** — Some students write $Z = f \cdot d$ only, forgetting $V_w$. Always use all three.
> 3. **Confusing truing vs. dressing** — These are *not* the same. Truing = geometry correction; Dressing = grit renewal. Examiners explicitly check this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch in long answers** — A neat labeled sketch in Q.A(a) carries **2 of the 7 marks**. Drawing is *not* optional.
> 5. **Forgetting to dress the wheel** in procedure questions — Always include the dressing step before commencing grinding in any process sequence answer.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Surface grinding is a **precision abrasive finishing process** achieving Ra 0.4–1.6 µm and tolerance ±0.01 mm.
- ✅ The four process parameters are: **Wheel speed (V), Table speed (V_w), Cross feed (f), Depth of cut (d)**.
- ✅ Wheel peripheral speed formula: $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60000}$ m/s.
- ✅ Table speed formula: $V_w = \dfrac{L \cdot N_{st}}{1000}$ m/min.
- ✅ MRR formula: $Z = V_w \cdot f \cdot d$ mm³/s (with $V_w$ in mm/s).
- ✅ Standard Al₂O₃ wheel speed: **30 m/s**; SiC: **20–25 m/s**.
- ✅ Magnetic chuck **must** be switched off before removing the workpiece (personal safety + avoids demagnetization cycle damage).
- ✅ Truing restores **geometry**; Dressing exposes **fresh grits** — both are essential before grinding.
- ✅ Coolant is **mandatory** in surface grinding to prevent thermal damage to workpiece and wheel.
- ✅ Always **dry run** the table before applying infeed to check for collision.
- ✅ **Grinding wheel specification order**: Abrasive → Grit Size → Grade → Structure → Bond (Example: **AA 60 K 5 V**).
- ✅ Spark test is a **quick identification method** — High-carbon steel → bright white sparks with many forks; Cast iron → dull red short sparks; Aluminum → no sparks.
- ✅ **Lateral cross-feed direction matters**: Always feed against the wheel rotation at the contact point to avoid wheel climb.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Tool and Cutter Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Single-Point Cutting Tool
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Tool and Cutter Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Single-Point Cutting Tool

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Point Cutting Tool (SPCT):** A cutting tool having one effective cutting edge that engages the workpiece at any given instant during the machining operation, used primarily on lathes, shaping machines, planers, and boring bars. It is characterised by a defined **tool signature** consisting of **seven principal angles** (plus two additional angles for a standard 7-element signature) ground on a high-speed steel (HSS) or carbide blank.

A **Tool and Cutter Grinding Machine (TCG)** is a versatile precision grinding machine used to sharpen, form, and re-condition multi-point cutters (milling cutters, drills, reamers) and to fabricate/grind **single-point cutting tools** from HSS or carbide blanks. It features a movable wheel head (typically swivelable in two planes), a universally adjustable work-holding fixture (tool and cutter grinder rests), and a high-speed grinding wheel (often made of **aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃)** for HSS and **silicon carbide (SiC)** or **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)** for carbide tools).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Sculptor's Chisel:**
> Imagine a sculptor shaping stone with a chisel. The chisel is the **cutting tool**, the stone is the **workpiece**, and the cutting edge is the part of the chisel that actually removes material. A single-point tool is like a chisel that has only **one sharp edge** doing all the work. To make it cut well, the sculptor must grind the chisel at very specific angles — too steep and it digs too deep, too shallow and it slips. A Tool and Cutter Grinder is the sculptor's **precision sharpening bench**, where every angle can be set exactly so the chisel cuts wood/metal cleanly.

- **Grinding wheel** = the sharpening stone (but rotating at ~1500–3000 m/s surface speed).
- **Tool blank** = the chisel body (usually HSS rectangular bar 16×16 mm or 20×20 mm).
- **Tool geometry** = the angles ground on the chisel face.
- **Rest (tool holder)** = the vise/clamp that holds the chisel at the right angle.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Specifications Worth Memorising for KTU Viva:**
> - Standard surface speed of grinding wheel: **20–30 m/s** (for HSS tool grinding, resin-bonded Al₂O₃ wheel of grade **K/L, structure 5–8** is recommended).
> - Standard HSS tool blank size: **6 mm, 8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm, 16 mm, 20 mm, 25 mm square section**.
> - Approximate hardness of HSS after grinding: **HRC 62–65** (post-heat-treatment).
> - Standard clearance (relief) angle on the tool flank: **6°–10°** for steel, **10°–15°** for soft materials.
> - Standard nose radius for finishing: **0.4–0.8 mm**; for roughing: **1.0–1.6 mm**.

### 1.4 Applications in Manufacturing

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Lathe turning** | Turning, facing, parting, chamfering |
| **Drilling (lathe-mounted)** | Axial hole making on lathe |
| **Boring** | Enlarging drilled holes |
| **Shaper/Planer** | Forming flat surfaces |
| **Tool Room** | Making form tools, profile tools, boring bars |
| **Maintenance Workshops** | Re-sharpening worn HSS tools |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven-Element Tool Signature (ASA / Nomenclature System)

> [!NOTE]
> The American Standards Association (ASA) system describes a single-point tool's geometry by **seven principal angles** measured in three reference planes:
> 1. **Rake Plane** (longitudinal–vertical)
> 2. **Clearance Plane** (transverse–vertical)
> 3. **Cutting Edge Plane** (tool-cutting edge plane)

The **seven principal angles** are:

1. **Back Rake Angle (α_b)** – Angle between the face of the tool and a plane parallel to the base of the tool, measured in the **longitudinal–vertical plane** (plane of cutting velocity). It controls chip flow direction.
2. **Side Rake Angle (α_s)** – Angle between the tool face and a plane parallel to the tool base, measured in the **transverse–vertical plane** (perpendicular to cutting velocity). It controls chip thickness and shear.
3. **End Relief Angle (ε₁)** – Angle between the **end flank (primary clearance face)** and a plane perpendicular to the tool base, measured in the **transverse–vertical plane**. It prevents the flank from rubbing the workpiece.
4. **End Cutting Edge Angle (C_s)** – Angle between the **end cutting edge** and a plane parallel to the tool shank, measured in the **transverse–vertical plane**. It allows the cutting edge to clear the workpiece and is a relief-providing angle in the feed direction.
5. **Side Relief Angle (ε₂')** – Angle between the **side flank** and a plane perpendicular to the tool base, measured in the **longitudinal–vertical plane** (plane of cutting velocity). Prevents rubbing along the side of the tool.
6. **Side Cutting Edge Angle (C_e)** – Angle between the **side cutting edge** and a plane parallel to the tool shank, measured in the **longitudinal–vertical plane**. Reduces the radial component of cutting force.
7. **Nose Radius (r)** – Small radius provided at the junction of side and end cutting edges for surface finish improvement.

A typical signature: **α_b – α_s – ε₁ – ε₂' – C_s – C_e – r**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tip:** In the ASA system the order is **Back Rake – Side Rake – End Relief – Side Relief – End Cutting Edge Angle – Side Cutting Edge Angle – Nose Radius.**

### 2.2 Functions of Each Angle — "Why and How"

- **Back Rake (α_b):**
  - *Why:* Reduces cutting force, controls chip flow upward and over the tool.
  - *How:* Positive rake (most common for steel) of **+5° to +15°**; negative rake (–5° to –10°) for carbide on hard materials.
  - *Effect on chip:* Curls the chip backward over the tool face.

- **Side Rake (α_s):**
  - *Why:* Reduces chip thickness, improves shear angle.
  - *How:* Typically **+5° to +15°** for steel, used to direct chip sideways away from the operator.
  - *Effect:* Reduces power consumption.

- **End Relief (ε₁):**
  - *Why:* Prevents rubbing of the end flank on the workpiece's newly cut surface.
  - *How:* **6° to 10°** (steel), **10° to 15°** (aluminium, brass).
  - *Effect:* Allows the cutting edge to engage the workpiece freshly.

- **Side Relief (ε₂'):**
  - *Why:* Prevents rubbing of the side flank on the workpiece along the feed direction.
  - *How:* **6° to 10°** (steel).
  - *Effect:* Critical when feed is applied; without it, the tool would burnish the workpiece.

- **End Cutting Edge Angle (C_s):**
  - *Why:* Provides clearance on the end cutting edge to allow the tool to advance into the workpiece.
  - *How:* Typically **5° to 15°**.
  - *Effect:* Small angle increases tool-tip strength; large angle reduces radial forces.

- **Side Cutting Edge Angle (C_e):**
  - *Why:* Controls chip thickness, radial force, and chatter.
  - *How:* Usually **15° to 30°**; **0°** for parting tools.
  - *Effect:* Large angle reduces radial force and chatter at the cost of chip-thinning.

- **Nose Radius (r):**
  - *Why:* Improves surface finish, increases tool-tip strength, distributes cutting forces.
  - *How:* **0.4 to 0.8 mm** for finishing; **1.0 to 2.0 mm** for roughing.
  - *Effect:* A larger nose radius reduces surface roughness Ra, but increases radial cutting force and chatter tendency.

### 2.3 The Three-Plane Reference System (Orthogonal Cutting Model)

A right-handed **orthogonal coordinate system (OXYZ)** is attached to the tool tip:
- **Z-axis** = cutting velocity (V) direction.
- **X-axis** = radial / depth-of-cut direction.
- **Y-axis** = feed direction (perpendicular to both).

The **tool cutting edge plane reference (P_F)** is the plane containing the principal cutting edge and the cutting velocity vector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three planes?** A single cutting edge lives in **3D space**. Without a 3-plane reference, the angles cannot be uniquely defined. The ASA system uses the **tool base**, **longitudinal–vertical**, **transverse–vertical**, and **tool cutting edge plane** as references.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for Quick Revision — All Formulas You'll Need**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tool Signature | $\alpha_b - \alpha_s - \epsilon_1 - \epsilon_2' - C_s - C_e - r$ | degrees, mm | ASA system |
| 2 | Orthogonal Rake Angle | $\tan \gamma_o = \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e$ | degrees | Valid for turning |
| 3 | Orthogonal Clearance | $\tan \alpha_o = \tan \epsilon_1 \cos C_e + \tan \epsilon_2' \sin C_e$ | degrees | Valid for turning |
| 4 | Inclination Angle (λ_s) | $\tan \lambda_s = \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e - \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e$ | degrees | Edge tilt relative to horizontal |
| 5 | True Rake Angle | $\tan \gamma = \tan \alpha_b \cos\lambda + \tan \alpha_s \sin\lambda$ | degrees | (generalised) |
| 6 | Cutting Velocity | $V = \pi D N / 60$ | m/s | $D$ = workpiece dia (m), $N$ = rpm |
| 7 | Feed Rate | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f$ = feed/rev (mm) |
| 8 | Material Removal Rate | $MRR = V \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/s | $d$ = depth of cut |
| 9 | Surface Roughness (theor.) | $R_a = f^2 / 8r$ | µm | With nose radius $r$ |
| 10 | Grinding Wheel Speed | $V_w = \pi D_w N_w / 60$ | m/s | $D_w$ = wheel dia, $N_w$ = rpm |

> **Pitfall note for tables:** In the formulas above the symbols $\alpha_b$, $\alpha_s$, $\epsilon_1$, $\epsilon_2'$, $C_s$, $C_e$ denote angles; use $\tan$ (tan) before each. Do not use a vertical bar `|` for absolute value inside the table; the symbols are angles, not mod values.

### 2.5 The Standard Tool Geometry for Mild Steel (Bench-mark Values)

| Angle / Parameter | Symbol | Value for Mild Steel (HSS tool) |
|---|---|---|
| Back Rake | $\alpha_b$ | +10° |
| Side Rake | $\alpha_s$ | +10° |
| End Relief | $\epsilon_1$ | 8° |
| Side Relief | $\epsilon_2'$ | 8° |
| End Cutting Edge Angle | $C_s$ | 8° |
| Side Cutting Edge Angle | $C_e$ | 20° |
| Nose Radius | $r$ | 0.8 mm |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Production Turning:** Selection of proper rake/clearance angles increases tool life by **2–4×** and reduces power consumption by **15–25%**.
- **CNC Machining:** Modern CNC lathes use **indexable inserts** whose geometry is equivalent to the ASA 7-element signature; understanding it is essential for tool selection.
- **Tool Re-sharpening:** Knowing the original geometry is critical — a wrongly re-ground HSS tool fails prematurely even at low cutting speeds.
- **Form Tools:** The same TCG machine is used to grind **circular form tools** and **threading tools** used on automat and capstan lathes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion from ASA Tool Signature to Orthogonal Cutting Angles

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** Modern chip-flow analysis, FEM simulations, and Merchant's circle diagram all use the **orthogonal rake angle $\gamma_o$** and **orthogonal clearance $\alpha_o$**, not the ASA angles. So we MUST convert.

**Starting Reference Frames:**
- Let the **principal cutting edge** lie in the **longitudinal–vertical plane** (P-V plane) and make an angle $C_e$ with the longitudinal direction.
- The **side cutting edge** (or end cutting edge) lies in the **transverse–vertical plane** (P-T plane).

**Given:** $\alpha_b$ (back rake), $\alpha_s$ (side rake), $C_e$ (side cutting edge angle).

**Step 1 — Resolve the back rake into a component perpendicular to the cutting edge projection:**
- The back rake is measured in the plane of the cutting velocity. Its component along a plane perpendicular to the side cutting edge is $\alpha_b \cos C_e$.

**Step 2 — Resolve the side rake into a component along the same plane:**
- The side rake is measured in the transverse–vertical plane (perpendicular to the cutting velocity). Its component along a plane perpendicular to the side cutting edge is $\alpha_s \sin C_e$.

**Step 3 — Superpose the two components using the tangent addition (small-angle approximation):**
- The orthogonal rake angle $\gamma_o$ is given by the sum of projections of the two rake angles onto the plane perpendicular to the cutting edge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \gamma_o &= \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual note:** This equation says the orthogonal rake is the vector sum of back-rake and side-rake contributions, weighted by $\cos C_e$ and $\sin C_e$ respectively, because the side cutting edge is tilted by $C_e$ from the longitudinal direction.

**Step 4 — When $C_e = 90°$** (tool pointed at the workpiece axis, as in parting), the side cutting edge aligns with the feed direction and the orthogonal rake reduces to the side rake:
- $\tan \gamma_o = \tan \alpha_s$ (which is consistent with the formula since $\cos 90° = 0$ and $\sin 90° = 1$).

**Step 5 — When $C_e = 0°$** (side cutting edge parallel to feed), the orthogonal rake reduces to the back rake:
- $\tan \gamma_o = \tan \alpha_b$ (since $\cos 0° = 1$ and $\sin 0° = 0$).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion for the Orthogonal Clearance Angle

By identical geometric reasoning applied to the **clearance angles** (end relief $\epsilon_1$ and side relief $\epsilon_2'$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \alpha_o &= \tan \epsilon_1 \cos C_e + \tan \epsilon_2' \sin C_e
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual note:** The clearance on the orthogonal plane is the sum of the projected end relief and side relief onto the plane perpendicular to the cutting edge. The formula form is identical to the rake equation, with $\epsilon$ replacing $\alpha$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inclination Angle (Cutting Edge Tilt)

The **cutting edge inclination angle** $\lambda_s$ is the angle the cutting edge makes with a horizontal plane through the tool tip. It is the third component (out-of-plane) needed for full 3D description:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \lambda_s = \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e - \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual note:** The sign convention: positive $\lambda_s$ means the cutting edge slopes downward toward the workpiece; negative means it slopes upward. This angle is typically **0°** for most turning tools and becomes important for tools with deliberately inclined edges (e.g., end mills, form tools).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Merchant's Chip-Formation Model (Quick Reference)

Merchant (1945) showed that the **shear angle $\phi$** in orthogonal cutting is determined by the orthogonal rake angle $\gamma_o$ and the tool–chip friction angle $\beta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\phi + \beta - \gamma_o = 90°
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for the shear angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi = 45° - \frac{\beta}{2} + \frac{\gamma_o}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual note:** Increasing the orthogonal rake (more positive) **increases the shear angle** $\phi$, which **decreases chip thickness** and **reduces cutting force** $F_c$. This is why positive rake tools require less power.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Finding Orthogonal Rake

**Given:** For a turning tool the ASA signature is:
- $\alpha_b = +12°$, $\alpha_s = +10°$, $C_e = 20°$.

**Find:** Orthogonal rake angle $\gamma_o$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the conversion formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \gamma_o &= \tan(12°)\cos(20°) + \tan(10°)\sin(20°)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the trigonometric values numerically:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 12° &= 0.2126 \\
\cos 20° &= 0.9397 \\
\tan 10° &= 0.1763 \\
\sin 20° &= 0.3420
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \alpha_b \cos C_e &= 0.2126 \times 0.9397 = 0.1998 \\
\tan \alpha_s \sin C_e &= 0.1763 \times 0.3420 = 0.0603
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the contributions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \gamma_o &= 0.1998 + 0.0603 = 0.2601
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the inverse tangent:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_o &= \arctan(0.2601) \approx 14.58° \approx 14°\,35'
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\gamma_o \approx 14.6°$ for a tool with $\alpha_b = 12°$, $\alpha_s = 10°$, $C_e = 20°$.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting Angles for Various Workpiece Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-favourite table — commit to memory:**

| Workpiece Material | $\alpha_b$ | $\alpha_s$ | $\epsilon_1$ | $\epsilon_2'$ | $C_s$ | $C_e$ | $r$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (HSS tool) | +10° | +10° | 8° | 8° | 8° | 20° | 0.8 |
| Hard Steel (HSS tool) | +5° | +5° | 6° | 6° | 5° | 15° | 0.4 |
| Cast Iron (HSS tool) | 0° | 0° | 6° | 6° | 5° | 15° | 0.4 |
| Aluminium (HSS tool) | +25° | +20° | 12° | 12° | 15° | 30° | 0.8 |
| Brass (HSS tool) | +5° | +5° | 8° | 8° | 8° | 0° | 0.4 |
| Hardened Steel (Carbide) | –5° | –5° | 6° | 6° | 5° | 15° | 0.4 |
| Stainless Steel (HSS) | +12° | +12° | 8° | 8° | 8° | 20° | 0.8 |

### 3.7 Tool Inspection Geometry (Setting Angles on TCG)

The **tool and cutter grinder rest** is a universal work-holding fixture that can be tilted in **two perpendicular planes** to set the desired clearance and rake angles.

| Setting on Rest | Action | Angle Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| Tilt rest forward (away from operator) by angle $\alpha_b$ | Tool face inclines upward | Back Rake |
| Swivel rest left/right by angle $\alpha_s$ | Tool face inclines sideways | Side Rake |
| Tilt rest sideways to set clearance | Flank tilts away from workpiece | Relief/clearance angles |
| Set tool point projection from rest centre | Controls nose radius location | Nose radius |

> **Practical tip (viva):** A good TCG operator can set a tool geometry to within $\pm 0.5°$ using a protractor scale, and finish with a **dressable, spark-free** contact across the entire clearance face.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tool and Cutter Grinding Machine

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a typical TCG machine. Because a true engineering drawing would require a CAD block, this functional flow shows how the **wheel head, work rest, and table** interact to produce the required tool geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base and Column] --> B[Grinding Wheel Head]
    A --> C[Work Table XY Movement]
    A --> D[Universal Tool and Cutter Rest]
    B --> B1[Wheel Spindle Motor 0.5 to 2 HP]
    B --> B2[Grinding Wheel Al2O3 or SiC]
    B --> B3[Wheel Guard and Coolant Nozzle]
    C --> C1[Longitudinal Traverse]
    C --> C2[Cross Traverse]
    D --> D1[Tilt Table for Back Rake]
    D --> D2[Swivel Base for Side Rake]
    D --> D3[Tool Blank Clamp Vice]
    E[HSS Tool Blank 20x20 mm] --> D3
    F[Operator Sets Signature Angles] --> D1
    F --> D2
    F --> D3
    D --> G[Grinding Cycle Phase 1 Grind Rake Face]
    D --> H[Grinding Cycle Phase 2 Grind End Flank]
    D --> I[Grinding Cycle Phase 3 Grind Side Flank]
    D --> J[Grinding Cycle Phase 4 Form Nose Radius]
    G --> K[Inspection with Angle Protractor]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Finished Single-Point Cutting Tool with ASA Signature]
```

### 4.2 3D Schematic of a Standard Right-Hand Turning Tool (ASCII Equivalent)

```
                       Tool Tip (Nose)
                            *
                          / | \
                        /   |   \
       Side Flank   /     |     \   End Flank
   (Side Relief) /        |        \  (End Relief)
              /     Nose radius r   \
            /           ___            \
          /          __/   \__           \
        /         _/         \_           \
      /         _/             \_          \
    /         _/    Rake Face     \_        \
  /         _/    (Back Rake        \_     \
/         _/      & Side Rake)        \_    \
Shank   ________________________________________
                                              |
        <-----  Side Cutting Edge  ----->
        <--  C_e = 20 deg  -->
        |---- C_s = 8 deg ----| (End Cutting Edge)
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The tool tip is at the right. The **shank** sits on the left. The **rake face** (top) carries the back-rake and side-rake angles. The **end flank** (upper-right) provides the end-relief angle. The **side flank** (lower-right) provides the side-relief angle. The **nose radius** rounds the corner where the two cutting edges meet.

### 4.3 Process Flow for Grinding a Single-Point Tool

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Select HSS Blank 20x20 mm] --> B[Mark Tool Geometry on Blank]
    B --> C[Mount Blank in Rest Vice]
    C --> D[Set Back Rake on Rest Tilt Table]
    D --> E[Grind Rake Face Al2O3 Wheel]
    E --> F[Dress Wheel with Diamond Tool]
    F --> G[Set End Relief Angle]
    G --> H[Grind End Flank]
    H --> I[Reset Rest for Side Relief]
    I --> J[Grind Side Flank]
    J --> K[Form Nose Radius on Rake Face]
    K --> L[Inspect with Bevel Protractor and Optical Projector]
    L --> M{Geometry OK?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Mark Tool and Store]
    M -->|No| O[Re-grind Offending Surface]
    O --> L
```

### 4.4 Reference Plane Geometry Diagram (3-Plane System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reference System] --> P1[Plane P1 Longitudinal-Vertical Plane of Cutting Velocity V]
    R --> P2[Plane P2 Transverse-Vertical Plane Perpendicular to V]
    R --> P3[Plane P3 Tool Cutting Edge Plane TCEP]
    P1 --> A1[Back Rake alpha_b measured here]
    P1 --> A2[Side Relief epsilon_2 prime measured here]
    P1 --> A3[Side Cutting Edge Angle C_e measured here]
    P2 --> B1[Side Rake alpha_s measured here]
    P2 --> B2[End Relief epsilon_1 measured here]
    P2 --> B3[End Cutting Edge Angle C_s measured here]
    P3 --> C1[Tool Cutting Edge Lies in this Plane]
    P3 --> C2[Nose Radius r lies in this Plane]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagram:** All **back**-names live in the **longitudinal–vertical plane (P1)**; all **side/end** angle pairs split between the two planes according to whether the angle controls the axial or radial clearance.

### 4.5 Coolant and Wheel Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Tool Material?] --> Q1[HSS]
    Q --> Q2[Carbide]
    Q1 --> R1[Grinding Wheel Al2O3 White or Pink Resin Bond]
    Q1 --> R2[Coolant Soluble Oil 1 to 20 Emulsion]
    Q2 --> S1[Grinding Wheel Green SiC or Diamond Resin Bond]
    Q2 --> S2[Coolant Dry Grinding or Oil Mist]
    R1 --> T[Surface Speed 20 to 30 m per s]
    R2 --> T
    S1 --> U[Surface Speed 12 to 20 m per s Softer Grade]
    S2 --> U
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Define a single-point cutting tool. List the seven elements of its ASA tool signature.*

**Model Answer:**
A **single-point cutting tool** is a cutting tool that has only one effective cutting edge engaged with the workpiece at any instant during material removal. It is generally ground on a high-speed steel (HSS) or carbide blank and is used on lathes, shapers, planers, and boring bars.

The **seven elements of the ASA tool signature** are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Signature} = \alpha_b - \alpha_s - \epsilon_1 - \epsilon_2' - C_s - C_e - r
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\alpha_b$ = Back Rake Angle
- $\alpha_s$ = Side Rake Angle
- $\epsilon_1$ = End Relief Angle
- $\epsilon_2'$ = Side Relief Angle
- $C_s$ = End Cutting Edge Angle
- $C_e$ = Side Cutting Edge Angle
- $r$ = Nose Radius

*(Marks distribution: Definition 1, Listing 7 elements with symbols 1, Order explanation 1 = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*What is the function of (i) nose radius and (ii) side relief angle in a single-point cutting tool?*

**Model Answer:**
**(i) Nose radius (r):** It is the small radius provided at the junction of the side and end cutting edges. Its functions are:
- Improves the **surface finish** of the machined workpiece.
- Increases the **strength of the tool tip**, reducing the likelihood of chipping or breakage.
- Distributes the **cutting forces** over a longer portion of the cutting edge, reducing localised stress.
- Reduces the **stress concentration** at the corner.

**(ii) Side relief angle ($\epsilon_2'$):** It is the angle ground on the **side flank** of the tool measured in the longitudinal–vertical plane. Its function is to:
- Prevent **rubbing** between the side flank of the tool and the workpiece's machined surface during feed motion.
- Provide **clearance** in the feed direction.
- Allow the cutting edge to remain sharp and effective over longer durations.

*(Marks distribution: Nose radius 1.5 + Side relief 1.5 = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A — Module Internal Choice Option 1

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module Experiment 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Tool and Cutter Grinding (TCG) machine**. List any **four** common operations performed on it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A turning tool has ASA tool signature: **10° – 10° – 8° – 8° – 8° – 20° – 0.8 mm**. Identify each element and convert the rake-angle system to the **orthogonal rake angle $\gamma_o$** and **inclination angle $\lambda_s$**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction of a TCG Machine:**

A Tool and Cutter Grinding machine consists of the following main parts:

1. **Base and Column** – Provides rigidity and houses the spindle bearings.
2. **Grinding Wheel Head** – Mounted on the column; can be swivelled in two perpendicular planes (horizontal and vertical) to position the grinding wheel at various angles relative to the workpiece.
3. **Wheel Spindle and Motor** – Drives the grinding wheel at surface speeds of **20–30 m/s** (1–2 HP motor typical).
4. **Work Table** – Provided with longitudinal and cross-traverse (X-Y) movements for controlled feed during grinding.
5. **Universal Tool and Cutter Rest** – A work-holding fixture that can be tilted and swivelled; holds the tool blank at the desired angles. Includes a vice clamp.
6. **Coolant Supply** – Soluble-oil emulsion nozzle for HSS grinding; dry-grinding attachment for carbide.

**Working Principle:**
The grinding wheel is brought into contact with the tool blank held in the universal rest. The rest is tilted/swivelled to set the required angle. The wheel grinds the rake face, flanks, and nose radius in successive passes, with intermediate wheel-dressing using a diamond tool. Coolant is supplied during grinding to prevent thermal damage to the HSS blank.

**Four Common Operations:**
1. Grinding of single-point turning tools (this experiment).
2. Re-sharpening of milling cutters.
3. Re-sharpening of twist drills.
4. Grinding of form tools and reamers.

**Sketch** — see the block-level functional architecture in SECTION_4 of these notes (replace with proper labelled diagram in the answer book).

*(Marks distribution: Description 3, Sketch 2, Four operations 2 = 7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify each element of the given signature 10° – 10° – 8° – 8° – 8° – 20° – 0.8 mm:**

| Element | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Back Rake | $\alpha_b$ | 10° |
| Side Rake | $\alpha_s$ | 10° |
| End Relief | $\epsilon_1$ | 8° |
| Side Relief | $\epsilon_2'$ | 8° |
| End Cutting Edge Angle | $C_s$ | 8° |
