| Side Cutting Edge Angle | $C_e$ | 20° |
| Nose Radius | $r$ | 0.8 mm |

**Step 2 — Convert to orthogonal rake angle using the formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \gamma_o &= \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e \\
&= \tan 10° \cos 20° + \tan 10° \sin 20° \\
&= 0.1763 \times 0.9397 + 0.1763 \times 0.3420 \\
&= 0.1656 + 0.0603 \\
&= 0.2259
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_o &= \arctan(0.2259) \approx 12.74° \approx 12°\,44'
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inclination angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \lambda_s &= \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e - \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e \\
&= \tan 10° \sin 20° - \tan 10° \cos 20° \\
&= 0.1763 \times 0.3420 - 0.1763 \times 0.9397 \\
&= 0.0603 - 0.1656 \\
&= -0.1053
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_s &= \arctan(-0.1053) \approx -6.01° \approx -6°\,00'
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — State the result:**

- **Orthogonal rake angle** $\gamma_o \approx 12.7°$
- **Inclination angle** $\lambda_s \approx -6.0°$ (the negative sign indicates the cutting edge slopes slightly upward away from the workpiece, which is acceptable for a standard turning tool).

*(Marks distribution: Identification 1, Formula 1, Substitutions 2, Numerical evaluation 2, Result + interpretation 1 = 7 marks)*

---

#### Question B — Module Internal Choice Option 2

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module Experiment 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **functions of each of the seven angles** of a single-point cutting tool and discuss how **rake angle and clearance angle** affect the cutting performance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A turning tool has signature **12° – 10° – 8° – 8° – 8° – 20° – 0.8 mm**. The work material is **mild steel (180 BHN)**, depth of cut = **2 mm**, feed = **0.2 mm/rev**, cutting speed = **30 m/min**, workpiece diameter = **60 mm**.
   - (i) Calculate the **spindle rpm**.
   - (ii) Calculate the **material removal rate (MRR)**.
   - (iii) Comment on the **suitability** of the chosen geometry for mild steel. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The seven angles of a single-point cutting tool (ASA signature) and their functions are:

**1. Back Rake Angle ($\alpha_b$):**
- Angle on the rake face, measured in the longitudinal–vertical plane (plane of cutting velocity).
- **Functions:** Reduces cutting force, controls chip flow, lowers power consumption. Positive rake softens the workpiece ahead of the tool.

**2. Side Rake Angle ($\alpha_s$):**
- Angle on the rake face, measured in the transverse–vertical plane (perpendicular to cutting velocity).
- **Functions:** Directs the chip sideways away from the operator, reduces chip thickness, improves shear angle, decreases vibration and chatter.

**3. End Relief Angle ($\epsilon_1$):**
- Angle on the **end flank** (primary clearance face), measured in the transverse–vertical plane.
- **Functions:** Prevents rubbing of the end flank with the workpiece's newly formed surface. Allows the cutting edge to engage the workpiece freshly.

**4. Side Relief Angle ($\epsilon_2'$):**
- Angle on the **side flank**, measured in the longitudinal–vertical plane.
- **Functions:** Prevents rubbing along the side of the tool in the feed direction. Critical for tool life and surface finish.

**5. End Cutting Edge Angle ($C_s$):**
- Angle between the end cutting edge and the tool shank (longitudinal direction).
- **Functions:** Provides clearance on the end cutting edge; small $C_s$ strengthens the tool tip but increases radial force; large $C_s$ weakens the tip.

**6. Side Cutting Edge Angle ($C_e$):**
- Angle between the side cutting edge and the tool shank.
- **Functions:** Controls the chip thickness, radial cutting force component, and chatter. $C_e = 0°$ for parting tools (radial force = 100%).

**7. Nose Radius ($r$):**
- Small radius at the intersection of side and end cutting edges.
- **Functions:** Improves surface finish (reduces $R_a$), strengthens the tool tip, distributes cutting force. A larger $r$ increases radial force and risk of chatter.

**Effect of Rake Angle on Cutting Performance:**
- **Positive rake** (typical for ductile materials like mild steel): reduces cutting force, power, and tool-tip temperature; produces thinner, longer chips with higher shear angle.
- **Negative rake** (for hard, brittle materials like hardened steel): increases tool-tip strength, supports the cutting edge against impact loads, but at the cost of higher cutting force and power.

**Effect of Clearance Angle on Cutting Performance:**
- **Too small a clearance angle:** the flank rubs the workpiece → heat generation → tool-tip wear, poor surface finish, vibration.
- **Too large a clearance angle:** weakens the cutting edge → chipping, premature failure.
- **Optimal range (6°–10° for steel):** provides a balance between edge strength and clearance.

*(Marks distribution: Listing with functions 4, Rake discussion 1.5, Clearance discussion 1.5 = 7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $\alpha_b = 12°$, $\alpha_s = 10°$, $C_e = 20°$, mild steel, $d = 2$ mm, $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, $V = 30$ m/min, $D = 60$ mm.

**(i) Spindle Speed (N):**

The relationship between cutting speed $V$ and spindle speed $N$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad \text{(with $D$ in mm, $V$ in m/min)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 V}{\pi D} \\
  &= \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 60} \\
  &= \frac{30000}{188.496} \\
  &\approx 159.15 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, the operator would select the nearest available standard speed of **160 rpm** on the lathe.

**(ii) Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MRR} &= V \times f \times d \\
\end{aligned}
$$

But first convert cutting speed to mm/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 30 \text{ m/min} = 30000 \text{ mm/min} = 500 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Or use the direct formula with all dimensions in mm/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MRR} &= 500 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.2 \text{ mm} \times 2 \text{ mm} = 200 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

In mm³/min:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MRR} &= 30000 \times 0.2 \times 2 = 12000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 12 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Suitability of the Geometry:**

The chosen geometry has $\alpha_b = 12°$ (slightly higher than the standard 10° for mild steel), $\alpha_s = 10°$, $C_e = 20°$, and a nose radius of 0.8 mm.

- **Positive rakes** of $\alpha_b = 12°$ and $\alpha_s = 10°$ are **suitable** for mild steel — they reduce cutting force, power, and temperature.
- **Clearance angles** of 8° are within the recommended 6°–10° range.
- **$C_e = 20°$** is appropriate for general-purpose turning.
- **Nose radius 0.8 mm** is suitable for a finishing-to-medium cut at feed of 0.2 mm/rev.
- **Conclusion:** The geometry is **well-suited** for finish turning of mild steel at the given cutting parameters. For roughing, the nose radius could be increased to 1.2 mm and the feed increased to 0.3–0.4 mm/rev.

*(Marks distribution: Spindle speed 2, MRR 2, Suitability comment 3 = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Wrong order in the ASA signature.** The examiner expects exactly: **Back Rake – Side Rake – End Relief – Side Relief – End Cutting Edge Angle – Side Cutting Edge Angle – Nose Radius**. Writing them out of order costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $\epsilon_1$ and $\epsilon_2'$ with the rake angles.** Remember: $\alpha$ (alpha) = rake, $\epsilon$ (epsilon) = relief. This is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units in MRR/numericals.** Mixing mm and cm in volume calculations loses 1 mark easily.
> 4. **Not labelling the sketch of a TCG machine** with at least 5 parts (base, head, table, rest, wheel). A blank sketch = 0 marks for the diagram portion.
> 5. **Converting back rake to orthogonal rake using $\tan \gamma_o = \alpha_b$ alone** — this is WRONG unless $C_e = 0°$. The full conversion formula must be used.
> 6. **Forgetting the sign convention on $\lambda_s$.** Always state the sign (positive/negative) explicitly.
> 7. **Not mentioning coolant** in the working of TCG — for HSS grinding, soluble oil is mandatory; for carbide, dry grinding or oil-mist is used.
> 8. **Missing inspection step.** A full KTU-quality answer must include a note on using a **bevel protractor / optical projector / tool maker's microscope** to verify the geometry after grinding.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **Single-point tool signature (ASA):** $\alpha_b - \alpha_s - \epsilon_1 - \epsilon_2' - C_s - C_e - r$ (always in this order).
- **Three reference planes:** Longitudinal–Vertical (P-V), Transverse–Vertical (P-T), Tool Cutting Edge Plane (P_F or TCEP).
- **Rake angles** ($\alpha_b$, $\alpha_s$) reduce cutting force and direct chip flow.
- **Relief angles** ($\epsilon_1$, $\epsilon_2'$) provide clearance to prevent rubbing.
- **Cutting edge angles** ($C_s$, $C_e$) control the geometry of the cutting edges.
- **Nose radius** $r$ improves surface finish; trade-off with radial force and chatter.
- **Conversion formula:** $\tan \gamma_o = \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e$.
- **Inclination formula:** $\tan \lambda_s = \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e - \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e$.
- **Merchant's equation:** $2\phi + \beta - \gamma_o = 90°$ → $\phi = 45° - \beta/2 + \gamma_o/2$.
- **Standard mild-steel signature:** **10° – 10° – 8° – 8° – 8° – 20° – 0.8 mm**.
- **Standard aluminium signature:** higher rake (~25°), higher clearance (~12°), nose radius 0.8–1.2 mm.
- **Standard cast iron signature:** zero rake (0° – 0° – 6° – 6° – 5° – 15° – 0.4 mm).
- **TCG operations:** grinding of SPCT, milling cutter resharpening, drill resharpening, form-tool grinding, reamer grinding.
- **Grinding wheel for HSS:** Aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃), white or pink, resin bond, grade K/L, structure 5–8.
- **Grinding wheel for carbide:** Silicon carbide (SiC) green, or diamond wheel; dry grinding or oil-mist.
- **Surface speed:** 20–30 m/s for HSS; 12–20 m/s for carbide.
- **Coolant for HSS:** water-soluble oil 1:20 emulsion. For carbide: dry or oil-mist (water-based coolant causes thermal cracking of carbide).
- **Inspection instruments:** Bevel protractor, tool maker's microscope, optical projector, gauge for nose radius.
- **Sign convention on inclination angle $\lambda_s$:** positive = cutting edge slopes downward to the workpiece; negative = slopes upward.
- **Common workpiece material classifications for tool geometry selection:**
  - **Long-chipping ductile (steel, aluminium):** high positive rake.
  - **Short-chipping brittle (cast iron, bronze):** zero or low rake.
  - **Hard/tough (hardened steel):** negative rake with carbide tool.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Milling Machine: Plane and Pocket Milling Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Milling Machine: Plane and Pocket Milling Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔩 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Milling** is a fundamental material-removal machining process in which a multi-point rotary cutter (the **milling cutter**) is advanced against a stationary or slowly moving workpiece to remove material in the form of discrete **chips**. The geometry of the finished surface is governed by the controlled relative motion between the cutter teeth and the workpiece along multiple axes.

In the context of **Module Experiment 2**, two canonical milling exercises are mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus:

1. **Plane Milling (Surface Milling / Slab Milling):** Production of a flat surface parallel to the cutter axis by traversing the workpiece beneath a peripheral or face milling cutter.
2. **Pocket Milling:** Enclosed-cavity milling in which a pocket (rectangular, circular, or irregular) is produced inside a workpiece face by profiling along a closed toolpath, typically using an **end mill**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCSFL308 – 2024 Scheme):** *Module 2 — Exercises on milling machine: plane and pocket milling operations.* This experiment carries direct **CO (Course Outcome) mapping to CO1 and CO2** and tests the student's ability to *select cutting parameters, set up the machine, execute the cut, and measure the resulting surface.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Plow & Field" Analogy:**
Imagine a **rotary lawn mower** moving across a field of grass. The spinning blades (cutting teeth) shear the grass (workpiece material) at a controlled rate as the mower is pushed forward (table feed). Now scale this up — replace grass with steel, the mower with a horizontal-spindle milling machine, and the blades with a hardened High-Speed Steel (HSS) or Carbide milling cutter. That is **milling in essence**.

- **Plane Milling** = Mowing the **entire field uniformly** to one flat height.
- **Pocket Milling** = Mowing **only a rectangular patch** inside the field, lowering the soil to a specified depth, leaving the surrounding ground untouched — like digging a **rectangular pond inside a flat meadow**.

The chips produced are **discontinuous** (unlike turning, which produces a continuous chip), giving milling better thermal dissipation but generating **cyclic cutting forces** that demand rigid machine-tool design.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Speed ($V_c$)** | m/min | Peripheral velocity of the cutter |
| **Spindle Speed ($N$)** | rpm | Rotational speed of the cutter arbor |
| **Table Feed ($V_f$)** | mm/min | Linear feed of the workpiece |
| **Feed per Tooth ($f_z$)** | mm/tooth | Critical for chip load |
| **Depth of Cut ($a_p$)** | mm | Radial/axial penetration |
| **Machine Time ($T_m$)** | min | Total machining duration |

---

## 1.4 Safety & Operational Pre-Requisites (Workshop Mandate)

> [!WARNING]
> **Workshop Safety Protocol:** Milling cutters rotating at high RPM store enormous kinetic energy. Loose clothing, jewelry, and unsecured workpieces can cause fatal injuries. Always wear **safety goggles, gloves (when handling blanks), and closed-toe shoes**. Ensure the **workpiece is clamped rigidly** before powering the spindle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Milling Process

### 🔹 Step 1 — Mechanics of Chip Formation
Each tooth of the milling cutter engages the workpiece **intermittently**. As the tooth rotates into the cut, it begins at **zero chip thickness** (entry angle), reaches a **maximum chip thickness** at the tooth's angular midpoint, and tapers back to **zero** at exit. This is why milling is called an **interrupted cutting process**.

- The undeformed chip thickness $h$ varies sinusoidally with the cutter rotation angle.
- **Average chip thickness** determines the chip load $f_z$ on each tooth.

### 🔹 Step 2 — Up Milling vs. Down Milling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite — This distinction is asked almost every semester.**

| Feature | **Conventional (Up) Milling** | **Climb (Down) Milling** |
|---|---|---|
| Cutter rotation vs. Feed | Opposite directions | Same direction |
| Chip thickness | Starts thick → thin | Starts thin → thick |
| Tool life | Lower (tooth rubs on entry) | Higher (clean cutting action) |
| Surface finish | Rougher | Smoother |
| Machine backlash demand | Tolerates backlash | **Requires backlash-free table** |
| Typical application | Old machines, roughing | CNC, finishing |

### 🔹 Step 3 — Plane Milling Operation
- Uses **plain milling cutters** (cylindrical, peripheral teeth) or **face milling cutters** (inserts on the face).
- Cutter axis is **parallel to the machined surface**.
- Workpiece traverses in a direction **perpendicular to the cutter axis**.
- Depth of cut $a_p$ = thickness of the layer removed per pass.

### 🔹 Step 4 — Pocket Milling Operation
- Uses **end mills** (square, ball-nose, or bull-nose) — never a peripheral plain cutter.
- Requires a **closed toolpath**: linear passes, circular interpolation, or trochoidal milling.
- **Step-over ($a_e$)** controls scallop height between adjacent passes.
- **Engagement angle** is critical — full immersion can stall small-diameter end mills.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these six equations — they account for ~70% of calculation marks in Module-2 viva and ESE questions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Derived from (1) |
| 3 | Table Feed | $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | $z$ = number of effective teeth |
| 4 | Feed per Tooth | $f_z = \dfrac{V_f}{z \cdot N}$ | mm/tooth | Critical chip-load parameter |
| 5 | Metal Removal Rate | $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$ | mm³/min | Pocket milling: $a_e$ = step-over |
| 6 | Machining Time | $T_m = \dfrac{L + L_a}{V_f}$ | min | $L_a$ = cutter approach + overtravel |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry:** Pocket milling of aluminium wing ribs requires climb milling at high $V_f$ to achieve **5-µm surface finish**.
- **Automotive Die & Mould:** Pocket milling of forging dies demands trochoidal toolpaths to maintain **constant chip load**.
- **Production Tooling:** Plane milling of cast iron machine beds uses indexable carbide face mills with ceramic inserts at $V_c$ exceeding **800 m/min**.

The principles learned in this Module Experiment directly scale up to **5-axis CNC machining centres** used in modern industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation of Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

**Starting principle:** Cutting speed is defined as the **peripheral velocity of the cutter tip** with respect to the workpiece surface.

$$
V_c = \frac{\text{Peripheral distance per minute}}{\text{Unit conversion}}
$$

In one revolution, the cutter tip travels a circumferential distance equal to $\pi D$.

$$
\text{Distance per revolution} = \pi D \quad [\text{mm}]
$$

If the spindle rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, then the peripheral velocity is:

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi D \cdot N}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}]
$$

where the factor $1000$ converts **mm to metres**.

**Solving for $N$ (Spindle Speed):**

$$
\pi D \cdot N = 1000 \cdot V_c
$$

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi D} \quad [\text{rpm}]
$$

**Solving for $D$ (Cutter Diameter):**

$$
D = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi N} \quad [\text{mm}]
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Machining Time for Plane Milling

**Setup:** A workpiece of length $L$ mm is to be plane-milled with a cutter of diameter $D$ mm. The cutter must first **approach** the workpiece by a distance equal to the cutter radius (to achieve full engagement) and then **overtravel** by an equal distance for clean exit.

$$
\text{Total table travel} = L + D
$$

Wait — strictly, the **approach + overtravel = cutter diameter $D$**, because:
- Approach distance = $D/2$ (to bring full diameter into contact).
- Overtravel distance = $D/2$ (to clear the workpiece).
- Sum = $D/2 + D/2 = D$.

Therefore:

$$
T_m = \frac{L + D}{V_f}
$$

with $V_f$ in mm/min and $T_m$ in minutes.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 1 — Plane Milling Calculation (KTU Standard)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
>
> A **plain milling cutter of diameter 80 mm** having **8 teeth** is used to machine a cast-iron workpiece of length **300 mm**. The recommended cutting speed is **20 m/min** and the feed per tooth is **0.25 mm/tooth**. Calculate: (a) Spindle speed, (b) Table feed, (c) Machining time.

### 🔸 Part (a) — Spindle Speed

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi D}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 80}
$$

$$
N = \frac{20000}{251.327}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 79.6 \text{ rpm}}
$$

### 🔸 Part (b) — Table Feed

$$
V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N
$$

$$
V_f = 0.25 \times 8 \times 79.6
$$

$$
\boxed{V_f \approx 159.2 \text{ mm/min}}
$$

### 🔸 Part (c) — Machining Time

$$
T_m = \frac{L + D}{V_f}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{300 + 80}{159.2}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{380}{159.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_m \approx 2.39 \text{ min}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Problem 2 — Pocket Milling Calculation

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
>
> A **rectangular pocket of dimensions 100 mm × 60 mm** and depth **8 mm** is to be machined on a mild-steel workpiece using a **12 mm diameter 4-flute end mill**. The cutting parameters are: $V_c = 30$ m/min, $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth. The step-over (radial depth) is taken as **40% of cutter diameter**. Calculate: (a) Spindle speed, (b) Table feed, (c) Number of passes, (d) MRR.

### 🔸 Part (a) — Spindle Speed

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi D}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 12}
$$

$$
N = \frac{30000}{37.699}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 795.8 \text{ rpm}}
$$

### 🔸 Part (b) — Table Feed

$$
V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N
$$

$$
V_f = 0.05 \times 4 \times 795.8
$$

$$
\boxed{V_f \approx 159.16 \text{ mm/min}}
$$

### 🔸 Part (c) — Number of Passes

$$
a_e = 0.40 \times D = 0.40 \times 12 = 4.8 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Number of passes} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Pocket width}}{a_e} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{60}{4.8} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 12.5 \right\rceil = 13
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Number of passes} = 13}
$$

### 🔸 Part (d) — Metal Removal Rate

$$
MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f
$$

$$
MRR = 8 \times 4.8 \times 159.16
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR \approx 6111.7 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 6.11 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Cutter Selection

| Workpiece Material | Recommended Cutter | $V_c$ Range (m/min) | Coolant |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | HSS End Mill | 20 – 30 | Cutting oil |
| Cast Iron | Carbide Face Mill | 60 – 80 | Dry (or MQL) |
| Aluminium | HSS / Carbide End Mill | 100 – 300 | Cutting fluid / kerosene |
| Brass | HSS Plain Cutter | 40 – 60 | Dry |
| Hardened Steel (>HRC 45) | Carbide / Coated Carbide | 50 – 100 | Flood coolant |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Horizontal Milling Machine Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base & Column] --> B[Spindle Housing & Quill]
    B --> C[Cutter Arbor + Milling Cutter]
    D[Worktable - X Axis Saddle] --> E[Knee - Y Axis Vertical Slide]
    E --> F[Workpiece + Vice / Clamp]
    G[Feed Handle - X Axis] --> D
    H[Feed Handle - Y Axis] --> E
    I[Feed Handle - Z Axis] --> D
    J[Power Feed Selector] --> G
    J --> H
    K[Coolant Nozzle] --> F
    L[Electric Motor Drive] --> B
    M[Dial Gauge for Table Feed Calibration] --> D
    N[Depth Stop] --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Up Milling vs. Down Milling Cutter Engagement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UP[Conventional Up Milling]
        U1[Cutter rotates CW] --> U2[Workpiece feeds opposite to cutter]
        U2 --> U3[Chip starts thick - thinner at exit]
        U3 --> U4[Tooth rubs on entry - heat buildup]
    end

    subgraph DOWN[Climb Down Milling]
        D1[Cutter rotates CW] --> D2[Workpiece feeds in same direction]
        D2 --> D3[Chip starts thin - thicker at exit]
        D3 --> D4[Heat carried away with chip - cooler cut]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Plane Milling Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Read Drawing & Identify Dimensions] --> P2[Select Cutter and Check Sharpness]
    P2 --> P3[Mount Cutter on Arbor - Tighten Drawbar]
    P3 --> P4[Clamp Workpiece in Vice on Table]
    P4 --> P5[Set Spindle Speed using Vc and D]
    P5 --> P6[Set Table Feed using fz and z]
    P6 --> P7[Engage Safety - Wear PPE]
    P7 --> P8[Start Spindle - Reach Set RPM]
    P8 --> P9[Engage Table Feed - Approach Cut]
    P9 --> P10[Monitor Chip Color and Sound]
    P10 --> P11[Complete Pass - Stop Feed then Spindle]
    P11 --> P12[Measure Surface with Vernier Micrometer]
    P12 --> P13{Acceptable Dimension?}
    P13 -- No --> P6
    P13 -- Yes --> P14[Record Observations in Lab Manual]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Pocket Milling Toolpath Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Define Pocket Boundary] --> Q2[Choose Entry Strategy - Ramping or Plunge]
    Q2 --> Q3[Plunge Cutter to Depth ap at Safe Helical Entry]
    Q3 --> Q4[Execute First Peripheral Pass - Profile Cut]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step Inward by ae - 40 percent D]
    Q5 --> Q6[Execute Linear Pass - Raster Scan]
    Q6 --> Q7{Pocket Floor Reached?}
    Q7 -- No --> Q5
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8[Execute Final Finishing Pass - Stepover reduced]
    Q8 --> Q9[Retract Cutter to Safe Z]
    Q9 --> Q10[Inspect Pocket - Depth, Wall Finish, Squareness]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define milling. Differentiate between up milling and down milling.**

**Model Answer:**
Milling is a machining process that uses a rotating multi-point cutter to remove material from a workpiece.

- **Up Milling (Conventional):** Workpiece feeds opposite to cutter rotation at the contact zone; chip thickness decreases from maximum to zero; tooth tends to rub and seize on entry; suitable for old machines with backlash.
- **Down Milling (Climb):** Workpiece feeds in the same direction as cutter rotation; chip thickness increases from zero to maximum; cleaner cutting, better finish, longer tool life; requires backlash-free table drive.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six milling cutters used in the machine shop and state one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Cutter Name | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plain Milling Cutter | Plane/slab milling of flat surfaces |
| 2 | End Mill | Pocket milling, slotting, profiling |
| 3 | Face Milling Cutter | Large flat surface generation |
| 4 | Side and Face Cutter | Machining steps and slots |
| 5 | T-Slot Cutter | Cutting T-slots in machine tables |
| 6 | Slitting Saw | Narrow slot cutting |
| 7 | Angular Cutter | V-grooves and dovetails |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

### 🔸 **Question A (14 Marks)** — Plane Milling Dominant

> **[KTU ESE Model – Module 2, 14-Mark Pattern]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **horizontal knee-and-column milling machine**. Label all major parts.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A plane milling operation is to be performed on a mild-steel workpiece of length **250 mm** and width **100 mm** using a **plain milling cutter of 75 mm diameter with 10 teeth**. Recommended cutting speed is **25 m/min** and feed per tooth is **0.20 mm/tooth**. Determine: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Table feed, (iii) Machining time, (iv) Material removal rate if depth of cut is **3 mm**.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

A horizontal knee-and-column milling machine consists of:

- **Base:** Supports the entire machine and houses the coolant and lubrication system.
- **Column:** Vertical structure supporting the spindle and arbor.
- **Knee:** Vertical slide mounted on the column face; supports the saddle and worktable.
- **Saddle:** Mounted on the knee; travels horizontally (cross-traverse, Y-axis).
- **Worktable:** Topmost sliding surface; provides longitudinal feed (X-axis).
- **Spindle:** Horizontal rotating shaft driven by an electric motor through a gear box.
- **Arbor:** Holds the milling cutter; supported between the spindle nose and an overarm.
- **Overarm & Arbor Support:** Provide rigidity to the arbor at the far end.
- **Feed Mechanism:** Hand wheels and power-feed selector for X, Y, Z axes.
- **Coolant System:** Nozzle directing cutting fluid to the cutter-workpiece interface.

> *Diagram may be drawn in the answer script using the Mermaid schematic from Section 4.1 as reference.*

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $D = 75$ mm, $z = 10$, $V_c = 25$ m/min, $f_z = 0.20$ mm/tooth, $L = 250$ mm, $a_p = 3$ mm.

**(i) Spindle Speed:**
$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 75} = \frac{25000}{235.62} \approx 106.1 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) Table Feed:**
$$
V_f = 0.20 \times 10 \times 106.1 = 212.2 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**(iii) Machining Time:**
$$
T_m = \frac{L + D}{V_f} = \frac{250 + 75}{212.2} = \frac{325}{212.2} \approx 1.53 \text{ min}
$$

**(iv) Material Removal Rate:**
$$
MRR = a_p \cdot W \cdot V_f = 3 \times 100 \times 212.2 = 63660 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 63.66 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}
$$

---

### 🔸 **Question B (14 Marks)** — Pocket Milling Dominant (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Model – Module 2, 14-Mark Pattern]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **pocket milling operation** in detail. Discuss different toolpath strategies (raster, trochoidal, contour-parallel) with neat sketches.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A rectangular pocket **80 mm × 50 mm × 6 mm** deep is to be machined in an aluminium plate using a **10 mm diameter, 2-flute HSS end mill**. Cutting speed = **60 m/min**, feed per tooth = **0.08 mm/tooth**, and step-over = **50% of cutter diameter**. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Feed rate, (iii) Number of passes, (iv) Total machining time for a single Z-level.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

Pocket milling is a closed-cavity milling process where an end mill removes material enclosed within a defined boundary on a workpiece face. The cutter executes a closed toolpath that progressively reduces the unmachined area.

**Toolpath Strategies:**

- **Raster (Line-by-line):** Cutter moves in parallel linear passes back and forth; simple but creates sharp corners at direction changes.
- **Contour-Parallel (Spiral):** Toolpath offset inward from the pocket boundary; yields uniform scallop height and better finish.
- **Trochoidal:** Cutter moves in circular arcs while advancing — reduces radial engagement, ideal for deep pockets in tough materials.

**Procedure:**
1. Define pocket boundary in CAD.
2. Choose entry strategy (ramping preferred over direct plunge for HSS end mills).
3. Helical ramp to depth to avoid end-mill breakage.
4. Execute roughing passes at calculated step-over.
5. Final finishing pass at reduced step-over for surface quality.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** Pocket = 80 × 50 × 6 mm, $D = 10$ mm, $z = 2$, $V_c = 60$ m/min, $f_z = 0.08$ mm/tooth, $a_e = 0.5 \times 10 = 5$ mm.

**(i) Spindle Speed:**
$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 60}{\pi \times 10} = \frac{60000}{31.416} \approx 1909.9 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**
$$
V_f = 0.08 \times 2 \times 1909.9 = 305.6 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**(iii) Number of Passes:**
$$
\text{Passes} = \left\lceil \frac{50}{5} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 10 \right\rceil = 10 \text{ passes}
$$

**(iv) Machining Time (single Z-level):**

Assuming each pass covers the full 80 mm length plus approach/overtravel of cutter diameter:
$$
T_m = \frac{\text{Passes} \times (L + D)}{V_f} = \frac{10 \times (80 + 10)}{305.6} = \frac{900}{305.6} \approx 2.95 \text{ min}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert mm to m (or vice versa) in $V_c$ equation costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Approach + Overtravel:** Many students write only $T_m = L / V_f$, ignoring the cutter approach distance. Deduct **2 marks** if missed.
> 3. **Number of Teeth Confusion:** Plain cutters have peripheral teeth (typically 6–12), face mills have insert counts, end mills have 2–6 flutes — do not interchange.
> 4. **Direction of Milling:** Marks awarded only if up/down milling is correctly justified with chip-thickness behaviour.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise Before Exam Day**

- ✅ Milling = **interrupted cutting process**; chips are **discontinuous**.
- ✅ **Up Milling** = conventional; **Down Milling** = climb.
- ✅ Cutting speed $V_c$ in **m/min**; spindle speed $N$ in **rpm**; feed in **mm/min**.
- ✅ The single most-tested formula: $\;N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}\;$
- ✅ Table feed equation: $\;V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N\;$
- ✅ Machining time for plane milling: $\;T_m = \dfrac{L + D}{V_f}\;$ — **never forget the cutter diameter**.
- ✅ MRR for plane milling: $\;a_p \cdot W \cdot V_f\;$ (use $a_e$ instead of $W$ for pocket milling).
- ✅ **Plain milling cutter** → plane/slab milling; **End mill** → pocket milling and profiling.
- ✅ Depth of cut $a_p$ is **axial**; step-over $a_e$ is **radial** (perpendicular to cutter axis).
- ✅ Pocket milling always uses an **end mill** with closed toolpath strategy.
- ✅ **Safety first:** PPE, workpiece clamping, no loose clothing, drawbar tightened.
- ✅ Step-over commonly chosen as **40–60% of cutter diameter** for roughing, **10–20%** for finishing.
- ✅ Lab Viva favourites: difference between horizontal and vertical milling machine; between HSS and carbide cutters; meaning of "spiral milling cutter" (cuts with shearing action vs. straight-tooth cutter).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Milling Machine: Spur Gear Cutting Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Milling Machine: Spur Gear Cutting Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Spur Gear** is a cylindrical, parallel-axis gear with straight teeth cut parallel to the axis of rotation, used to transmit motion and power between two parallel shafts without any axial thrust. **Spur gear cutting on a milling machine** is a **form-cutting (generating) operation** in which a specially shaped, profile-ground **involute gear cutter** is mounted on a horizontal milling machine arbor, and a gear blank held between the centering point and tail stock of an **indexing head (dividing head)** is fed radially across the cutter to generate one tooth space at a time. After each tooth space is cut, the indexing head is rotated by a calculated angle to position the blank for cutting the next tooth.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In a Machine Shop laboratory, the term "Exercises on Milling Machine" refers to a *form-cutting method* (NOT a generating method like a gear hobber). Students must clearly distinguish between **form cutting** (milling machine + disc cutter) and **generation cutting** (gear hobbing machine). Marks are awarded for using the phrase *"form-cutting method using an involute profile cutter."*

### 1.2 Key Terminology Used in the Experiment

The following are the **standard gear parameters** that every KTU student must know and define in the record:

- **Module (m)** — the ratio of pitch circle diameter to the number of teeth; the *metric standard* for gear size.
- **Pitch Circle Diameter (D)** — the imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll.
- **Addendum (a)** — radial height of the tooth above the pitch circle.
- **Dedendum (b)** — radial depth of the tooth space below the pitch circle.
- **Circular Pitch (p_c)** — the arc length measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points of adjacent teeth.
- **Pressure Angle (φ)** — the angle between the line of action and the common tangent to the pitch circles, **standard value = 20°**.
- **Tooth Thickness (t)** — thickness of the tooth measured along the pitch circle.
- **Tooth Space** — the gap between two adjacent teeth along the pitch circle.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Cookie Cutter" Concept

Imagine you have a flat slab of dough (your **gear blank**) and you press a shaped metal cutter (your **involute gear cutter**) into it vertically. Each press makes **one tooth-shaped impression** (one tooth space). You then rotate the slab a small, fixed angle, and press again. Repeat this 40 times around the circle, and you end up with a perfect gear with 40 teeth.

This is *exactly* what a horizontal milling machine + dividing head does. The milling cutter has the **exact involute cross-section of a single tooth space** ground into it, and the indexing head is the "small angle rotating" mechanism. The "small angle" between successive cuts is mathematically linked to the number of teeth through the **plain indexing formula**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use 8 cutters and not just 1?**
> An involute curve's shape changes slightly depending on the number of teeth. A cutter designed for 20 teeth will *not* perfectly form a 40-tooth gear's flanks. Hence the industry uses a **set of 8 cutters** (No. 1 to No. 8), each ground for a specific range of teeth. This is a *direct application of the involute profile variation principle*.

### 1.4 Why This Operation Matters in Manufacturing

Spur gears are the **most commonly produced gear type** in industry. They are found in every gearbox, transmission, lathe headstock, automobile gearbox, clock, and conveyor drive. The milling machine method, although slower than hobbing, is widely used in **tool rooms, maintenance workshops, R&D labs, and small-batch production** because it requires only a standard horizontal milling machine — no specialized gear hobbing machine is needed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The gear cutting operation is built on **three pillars** that the KTU examiner expects you to state in the record:

#### Pillar 1: Form Cutting (NOT Generation)

In form cutting, the **cutter has the exact shape of the tooth space**. The cutter simply *plunges* into the blank to form the tooth profile. There is no rolling motion between cutter and blank. This is fundamentally different from gear hobbing, where the hob and blank *roll* together (generation principle).

#### Pillar 2: The Indexing Head (Dividing Head)

The indexing head is a precision gear mechanism mounted on the milling machine table. It allows the gear blank to be rotated through **precise angular increments** between successive cuts. The standard indexing head has:

- A **worm gear with 40 teeth** on a fixed circle plate.
- A **single-start worm** driving this gear.
- Hence **one full turn of the crank = 1/40 of a full blank rotation**.

To cut a gear of **n teeth**, the crank must turn **1/n** of a full rotation between cuts. Since one turn = 1/40 rotation, the required crank turns are:

$$N = \frac{40}{n}$$

This is the famous **plain indexing formula**.

#### Pillar 3: The Cutter Set (8 Cutters)

Standard gear cutter sets contain **8 cutters**, each ground for a specific tooth range. The reasoning is mathematical: the involute profile *changes shape* with tooth count, and a single cutter can only approximate the involute over a narrow range of teeth.

### 2.2 Gear Cutter Selection Chart (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table** — it appears in nearly every KTU question on spur gear cutting.

| Cutter No. | Range of Teeth | Approximation Basis |
|:----------:|:--------------:|:-------------------:|
| 1 | 135 teeth and above (Rack) | Treated as straight-sided rack |
| 2 | 55 to 134 | Curvature at root ≈ rack |
| 3 | 35 to 54 | Standard involute |
| 4 | 26 to 34 | Standard involute |
| 5 | 21 to 25 | Standard involute |
| 6 | 17 to 20 | Standard involute |
| 7 | 14 to 16 | Standard involute |
| 8 | 12 to 13 | Coarse — minimum tooth count |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU numerical problems on this topic are solved using ONLY the formulas below.** Commit them to memory in the order shown.

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Standard Value / Unit |
|:-------:|:---------:|:------:|:-------:|:---------------------:|
| 1 | Module | m | Given / Standard | mm |
| 2 | Number of Teeth | Z | Given | — |
| 3 | Pitch Circle Diameter | D | $D = m \times Z$ | mm |
| 4 | Circular Pitch | $p_c$ | $p_c = \pi m$ | mm |
| 5 | Addendum | a | $a = m$ (i.e., **1 module**) | mm |
| 6 | Dedendum | b | $b = 1.25\,m$ | mm |
| 7 | Whole Depth (Cutting Depth) | h | $h = 2.25\,m$ | mm |
| 8 | Outside (Blank) Diameter | $D_o$ | $D_o = m(Z + 2)$ | mm |
| 9 | Root Diameter | $D_r$ | $D_r = m(Z - 2.5)$ | mm |
| 10 | Tooth Thickness (at P.C.) | t | $t = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$ | mm |
| 11 | Clearance | c | $c = 0.25\,m$ | mm |
| 12 | Pressure Angle | φ | Standard | **20°** |
| 13 | Indexing Crank Movement | N | $N = \dfrac{40}{Z}$ | turns |
| 14 | Pitch Circle Radius (for setting) | R | $R = \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{mZ}{2}$ | mm |

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering

- The **module (m)** is the *master parameter*. In metric gear design, once you choose a module, the entire gear's size is locked. This is why module is the first parameter specified on every engineering drawing.
- The **outside diameter (D_o)** is what the machinist measures with a vernier before starting — it tells them the *size of the blank* required.
- The **whole depth (h)** is the *radial depth of cut* the milling machine must plunge — the vertical feed setting on the machine.
- The **indexing crank movement (N)** tells the operator exactly *how many turns* to advance the crank between teeth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure angle 20°** is the universal ISO standard. The older 14.5° system (Brown & Sharpe) is now obsolete. KTU questions always use 20° unless specified otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pitch Circle Diameter Formula

**Given:** A spur gear with module $m$ and number of teeth $Z$.

By definition, the **module** is the ratio of the pitch circle diameter to the number of teeth:

$$m = \frac{D}{Z}$$

Solving this algebraic equation for $D$ (multiply both sides by $Z$):

$$D = m \times Z$$

This is the **fundamental gear size equation** in the metric system.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Whole Depth (Cutting Depth)

The whole depth $h$ is the sum of addendum and dedendum:

$$h = a + b$$

By KTU / AGMA standard, $a = m$ and $b = 1.25\,m$. Substituting:

$$h = m + 1.25\,m = 2.25\,m$$

This is the **radial depth the cutter must plunge** into the blank.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Outside (Blank) Diameter

The outside diameter is the pitch circle diameter plus **two addenda** (one on each side):

$$D_o = D + 2a$$

Substituting $D = mZ$ and $a = m$:

$$D_o = mZ + 2m = m(Z + 2)$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Root Diameter

The root diameter is the pitch circle diameter minus **two dedenda**:

$$D_r = D - 2b$$

Substituting $D = mZ$ and $b = 1.25\,m$:

$$D_r = mZ - 2(1.25\,m) = mZ - 2.5\,m = m(Z - 2.5)$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Tooth Thickness at Pitch Circle

The pitch circle is divided into **Z equal arcs**, alternating between tooth thickness and tooth space. Hence, **one tooth + one space = circular pitch**. Since tooth thickness equals tooth space at the pitch circle (standard proportion):

$$t = \frac{p_c}{2} = \frac{\pi m}{2}$$

---

### 3.6 Derivation of the Indexing Formula (Plain Indexing)

The indexing head has a **40-tooth worm wheel** driven by a **single-start worm**. Therefore, **one full turn of the crank rotates the worm wheel by exactly 1/40 of a full revolution**:

$$1 \text{ crank turn} = \frac{1}{40} \text{ of full blank rotation}$$

For a gear with $Z$ teeth, the angular step between consecutive teeth is $\frac{1}{Z}$ of a full rotation. Therefore the crank turns required per step is:

$$N = \frac{1/Z}{1/40} = \frac{40}{Z}$$

Hence the **plain indexing formula**:

$$N = \frac{40}{Z}$$

---

### 3.7 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Numericals)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**
A spur gear of **40 teeth** and **module 2 mm** is to be cut on a horizontal milling machine using an indexing head. Calculate all gear dimensions, select the appropriate cutter, and determine the indexing movement.

#### Given Data

$$Z = 40, \quad m = 2\text{ mm}, \quad \phi = 20°$$

#### Step 1: Pitch Circle Diameter

$$D = mZ = 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 2: Circular Pitch

$$p_c = \pi m = \pi \times 2 = 6.283 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 3: Addendum

$$a = m = 2 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 4: Dedendum

$$b = 1.25\,m = 1.25 \times 2 = 2.5 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 5: Whole Depth (Radial Cutting Depth)

$$h = 2.25\,m = 2.25 \times 2 = 4.5 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 6: Outside (Blank) Diameter

$$D_o = m(Z + 2) = 2 \times (40 + 2) = 84 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 7: Root Diameter

$$D_r = m(Z - 2.5) = 2 \times (40 - 2.5) = 75 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 8: Tooth Thickness at Pitch Circle

$$t = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 2}{2} = \pi = 3.1416 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 9: Cutter Selection

For $Z = 40$ teeth, the value lies in the range **35 to 54**, hence **Cutter No. 3** is selected from the standard 8-cutter set.

#### Step 10: Indexing Movement

$$N = \frac{40}{Z} = \frac{40}{40} = 1 \text{ full turn}$$

The crank must be turned **1 complete revolution** between successive tooth cuts.

#### Step 11: Center Distance / Pitch Radius for Cutter Setup

$$R = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{80}{2} = 40 \text{ mm}$$

This is the radial distance from the blank's center to its pitch circle — used to set the cutter's vertical position above the blank center.

### 3.8 Result Summary Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|:---------:|:------:|:-----:|
| Pitch Circle Diameter | D | **80 mm** |
| Circular Pitch | $p_c$ | **6.283 mm** |
| Addendum | a | **2 mm** |
| Dedendum | b | **2.5 mm** |
| Whole Depth | h | **4.5 mm** |
| Outside Diameter | $D_o$ | **84 mm** |
| Root Diameter | $D_r$ | **75 mm** |
| Tooth Thickness | t | **3.1416 mm** |
| Cutter Number | — | **No. 3** |
| Indexing | N | **1 turn** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step mark allocation (KTU pattern):** The 7-mark numerical part awards 1 mark for each of steps 1 through 6, and 1 mark for cutter selection + indexing. Skip a step and you lose the mark — the examiner has no discretion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Setup Architecture on Horizontal Milling Machine

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **physical arrangement** of the gear cutting setup, mapping the interaction between every component of the system. This is the schematic students must draw in their KTU record (typically as a labeled line diagram in the observation section).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Horizontal Milling Machine] --> B[Machine Spindle]
    B --> C[Involute Gear Cutter mounted on Arbor]
    D[Indexing Head on Machine Table] --> E[Worm and Worm Wheel 40:1]
    E --> F[Index Crank and Handle]
    G[Tail Stock on Machine Table] --> H[Centering Point / Dead Center]
    I[Gear Blank mounted on Mandrel] --> J[Mounted between Indexing Head Center and Tail Stock Center]
    J --> K[Vertical Feed - Depth of Cut 2.25m]
    C -.cuts.-> I
    F -.rotates by 40 divided by Z.-> D
```

### 4.2 Operational Process Flow

The following Mermaid flow diagram maps the **step-by-step operational sequence** for cutting the gear. Students must describe each block of this diagram in the KTU record's "Procedure" section to score full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Gear Blank] --> B[Measure Blank Diameter and Verify with D equals m times Z plus 2m]
    B --> C[Mount Blank on Mandrel between Indexing Head and Tail Stock]
    C --> D[Select Correct Gear Cutter from 8 Cutter Set]
    D --> E[Mount Cutter on Milling Machine Arbor]
    E --> F[Set Cutter Tooth Profile Center exactly at Blank Center Height]
    F --> G[Set Vertical Depth of Cut equals 2.25m using Dial Gauge or Paper Slip Method]
    G --> H[Engage Half Nut and Lock Longitudinal Feed]
    H --> I[Cut First Tooth Space by Traversing Blank Across Cutter]
    I --> J[Retract Blank and Release Index Crank]
    J --> K[Turn Index Crank by 40 divided by Z turns]
    K --> L{Have all Z teeth been cut?}
    L -- No --> I
    L -- Yes --> M[Reduce Depth Slightly and Take Spring Cut for Finish]
    M --> N[Stop Machine, Remove Finished Gear, Inspect]
    N --> O[End]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Cutter Selection Logic

The block diagram below shows the **decision logic** the operator follows when selecting the correct involute gear cutter. This is a frequent theory question in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given: Number of Teeth Z] --> B{Z greater than or equal to 135}
    B -- Yes --> C1[Use Cutter No 1 Rack Profile]
    B -- No --> D{Z between 55 and 134}
    D -- Yes --> C2[Use Cutter No 2]
    D -- No --> E{Z between 35 and 54}
    E -- Yes --> C3[Use Cutter No 3]
    E -- No --> F{Z between 26 and 34}
    F -- Yes --> C4[Use Cutter No 4]
    F -- No --> G{Z between 21 and 25}
    G -- Yes --> C5[Use Cutter No 5]
    G -- No --> H{Z between 17 and 20}
    H -- Yes --> C6[Use Cutter No 6]
    H -- No --> I{Z between 14 and 16}
    I -- Yes --> C7[Use Cutter No 7]
    I -- No --> J{Z equals 12 or 13}
    J -- Yes --> C8[Use Cutter No 8]
```

### 4.4 Indexing Head Working Principle (Block Form)

A physical sketch of the indexing head is mandatory in the KTU record. Below is the functional block representation showing how the crank rotation is transmitted to the blank.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hand Crank with Handle] --> B[Single Start Worm]
    B --> C[40 Tooth Worm Wheel]
    C --> D[Indexing Spindle of Dividing Head]
    D --> E[Work Holding Mandrel with Gear Blank]
    E --> F[Tail Stock Center for Support]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Drawing Tip:** When drawing the indexing head in the record, always show the **crank**, the **worm**, the **40-tooth worm wheel**, the **spindle axis**, and the **plate with concentric circles of holes** (the index plate). Labeling the worm wheel as "40 teeth" is mandatory — the examiner will not award full marks without it.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define a spur gear. List any six important gear terminology with their meaning.**

**Model Answer:**

A **spur gear** is a cylindrical gear with straight teeth cut parallel to the axis of rotation, used to transmit power between two parallel shafts without axial thrust. The six important terms are:

- **Module (m):** Ratio of pitch circle diameter to number of teeth. Unit: mm.
- **Pitch Circle Diameter (D):** Imaginary circle on which the gear effectively rolls. $D = mZ$.
- **Addendum (a):** Radial height of tooth above the pitch circle. $a = m$.
- **Dedendum (b):** Radial depth of tooth space below pitch circle. $b = 1.25\,m$.
- **Pressure Angle (φ):** Angle between the line of action and the common tangent. Standard = **20°**.
- **Circular Pitch ($p_c$):** Distance between corresponding points of adjacent teeth on the pitch circle. $p_c = \pi m$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**What is the principle of gear cutting on a horizontal milling machine? Why is a set of 8 cutters used instead of a single cutter?**

**Model Answer:**

The **principle** is the *form-cutting method* using an *involute profile cutter*. A gear cutter, ground to the exact shape of a tooth space, is mounted on the milling machine arbor. The gear blank, held between the indexing head and tail stock, is fed radially across the rotating cutter to cut one tooth space at a time. The indexing head rotates the blank by a calculated angle of $\frac{360°}{Z}$ between cuts.

A **set of 8 cutters** is used because the involute profile shape varies with the number of teeth. A single cutter cannot accurately produce the involute for all tooth counts. The 8 cutters are ground for 8 different ranges (e.g., 12–13, 14–16, 17–20, 21–25, 26–34, 35–54, 55–134, and 135+), and the correct cutter is selected based on the number of teeth.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the procedure for cutting a spur gear of **36 teeth** and **module 4 mm** on a horizontal milling machine using an indexing head. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate all the dimensions required to cut the above gear and select the suitable cutter from the standard cutter set. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Procedure

1. **Preparation of the blank:** Calculate the blank (outside) diameter using $D_o = m(Z + 2) = 4(36 + 2) = 152$ mm. Machine a mild steel blank of 152 mm diameter to the calculated size and face both sides flat.
2. **Mounting the blank:** Mount the blank on a mandrel and place it between the indexing head center and the tail stock center on the milling machine table. Lock the table.
3. **Cutter selection:** From the standard 8-cutter set, for $Z = 36$ (lies in range 35–54), select **Cutter No. 3**.
4. **Cutter mounting:** Mount the involute gear cutter on the milling machine arbor with a suitable spacer. The cutter must rotate in a direction that pulls the blank under it (climb milling preferred).
5. **Centering the cutter above the blank:** Adjust the table height so that the **cutter tooth profile center** (the midpoint of the cutter's tooth space) is exactly at the height of the blank's centerline.
6. **Setting depth of cut:** Calculate depth $h = 2.25\,m = 2.25 \times 4 = 9$ mm. Set this radial depth on the vertical hand wheel using a slip gauge or paper slip method.
7. **Cutting the first tooth:** Lock the longitudinal feed, engage the vertical feed, and traverse the blank across the cutter to cut one tooth space.
8. **Indexing:** Retract the blank, release the index crank, and turn it by $N = \frac{40}{Z} = \frac{40}{36} = 1\frac{4}{36} = 1\frac{1}{9}$ turns (i.e., 1 full turn + 4 holes on the 36-hole circle plate).
9. **Cutting remaining teeth:** Repeat steps 7 and 8 until all 36 teeth are cut.
10. **Spring / finish cut:** Reduce the depth by ~0.2 mm and take a final light pass across all teeth for a smooth finish.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Calculations

**Given:** $Z = 36$, $m = 4$ mm, $\phi = 20°$

**(i) Pitch Circle Diameter:**

$$D = mZ = 4 \times 36 = 144 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Circular Pitch:**

$$p_c = \pi m = \pi \times 4 = 12.566 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Addendum:**

$$a = m = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Dedendum:**

$$b = 1.25\,m = 1.25 \times 4 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**(v) Whole Depth (Radial Cut):**

$$h = 2.25\,m = 2.25 \times 4 = 9 \text{ mm}$$

**(vi) Outside (Blank) Diameter:**

$$D_o = m(Z + 2) = 4 \times (36 + 2) = 152 \text{ mm}$$

**(vii) Root Diameter:**

$$D_r = m(Z - 2.5) = 4 \times (36 - 2.5) = 4 \times 33.5 = 134 \text{ mm}$$

**(viii) Tooth Thickness at Pitch Circle:**

$$t = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 4}{2} = 2\pi = 6.283 \text{ mm}$$

**(ix) Cutter Selection:** For $Z = 36$ (range 35–54), select **Cutter No. 3**.

**(x) Indexing Movement:**

$$N = \frac{40}{Z} = \frac{40}{36} = 1\frac{4}{36} = 1\frac{1}{9} \text{ turns}$$

i.e., 1 full turn + 4 holes in the 36-hole circle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 1:**
> Many students write $N = \frac{40}{36} = 1.111$ and stop. **This loses 1 mark.** You must state the interpretation: *"1 full turn of the crank, followed by 4 holes on the 36-hole circle plate."* This is a standard KTU deduction. The fraction $\frac{1}{9}$ turn **must be converted into holes** on the index plate.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **indexing head (dividing head)** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a gear of **48 teeth** and **module 3 mm**, calculate: (i) Pitch circle diameter, (ii) Addendum, (iii) Dedendum, (iv) Blank diameter, (v) Tooth thickness, (vi) Depth of cut, (vii) Cutter number to be used, and (viii) Indexing movement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Indexing Head

**Construction:**

An indexing head consists of:

- A **headstock** containing a **worm and worm wheel** (40 teeth) mounted on the output spindle.
- A **single-start worm** driven by an **index crank** through a gear train.
- A **rotating spindle** with a **center** for mounting the work (gear blank on mandrel).
- An **index plate** with multiple concentric circles of equally spaced holes (typically 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37, 39, 41, 43, 47 holes) used to position the crank precisely for fractional turns.
- A **locking mechanism** to lock the spindle once a tooth is cut.

**Working:**

One complete revolution of the index crank turns the single-start worm by one thread, which rotates the 40-tooth worm wheel by $\frac{1}{40}$ of a full revolution. This rotates the spindle (and the work mounted on it) by $\frac{360°}{40} = 9°$. The blank is locked, one tooth is cut, the crank is advanced by the calculated number of turns and holes on the index plate, and the next tooth is cut. This process repeats for all $Z$ teeth.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Calculations

**Given:** $Z = 48$, $m = 3$ mm, $\phi = 20°$

**(i) Pitch Circle Diameter:**

$$D = mZ = 3 \times 48 = 144 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Addendum:**

$$a = m = 3 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Dedendum:**

$$b = 1.25\,m = 1.25 \times 3 = 3.75 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Blank (Outside) Diameter:**

$$D_o = m(Z + 2) = 3 \times (48 + 2) = 150 \text{ mm}$$

**(v) Tooth Thickness:**

$$t = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 3}{2} = 4.712 \text{ mm}$$

**(vi) Depth of Cut (Whole Depth):**

$$h = 2.25\,m = 2.25 \times 3 = 6.75 \text{ mm}$$

**(vii) Cutter Selection:** For $Z = 48$ (range 35–54), select **Cutter No. 3**.

**(viii) Indexing Movement:**

$$N = \frac{40}{Z} = \frac{40}{48} = \frac{5}{6} \text{ turn}$$

Since $\frac{5}{6}$ is not a whole turn, express it as holes on a plate divisible by 6. On the 24-hole plate (or the 18-hole plate, both with 6 as a factor), $\frac{5}{6} \times 24 = 20$ holes.

So: turn the crank by **20 holes on the 24-hole circle** (equivalently, 15 holes on the 18-hole circle).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 2:**
> When $N$ is a non-integer (e.g., $\frac{40}{48} = \frac{5}{6}$), students often forget to convert the fraction into a *specific hole count on a specific index plate*. KTU's mark scheme **explicitly requires the plate number and hole count**. For example, "20 holes on the 24-hole plate" or "15 holes on the 18-hole plate." Writing only "5/6 turn" without identifying the plate loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-hour revision bullets — read these 30 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- The operation is a **form-cutting method** (not gear generation); the cutter has the exact involute shape of a tooth space.
- The standard **module formula** is $m = \frac{D}{Z}$, so always begin with this when data is given.
- The **standard pressure angle** in KTU problems is always **20°** unless stated otherwise.
- **Addendum = 1 module** and **Dedendum = 1.25 module** — these ratios are the *single most-asked values* in KTU.
- **Whole depth = 2.25 module** — this is the *radial plunge depth* of the cutter.
- **Blank diameter = $m(Z + 2)$** — this is what the machinist actually machines to.
- **Tooth thickness at pitch circle = $\frac{\pi m}{2}$** — do not confuse with circular pitch ($\pi m$).
- **Plain indexing formula** is $N = \frac{40}{Z}$ — applies when $Z$ divides 40 evenly. For other $Z$ values, use the *index plate* method (universal indexing).
- **Cutter No. 3** is the most commonly selected cutter because the range 35–54 teeth is the most common design range.
- The **8-cutter set** is mandatory because a single cutter cannot produce the correct involute for all tooth counts — this is a *form vs generation* limitation.
- The **indexing head has a 40-tooth worm wheel** driven by a **single-start worm** — this is why one crank turn = $\frac{1}{40}$ of a rotation.
- Always convert fractional indexing movements like $\frac{5}{6}$ turn into a *hole count* on a specific *index plate* (e.g., 20 holes on the 24-hole plate).
- A "spring cut" (light finishing pass) is taken at the end to improve surface finish and dimensional accuracy.
- **Climb milling** is preferred for gear cutting to get a better finish and to avoid work lifting.
- Always align the **cutter tooth profile center** (not the cutter tooth tip) with the **blank's centerline** — this is the most common practical mistake in the laboratory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Arc Welding: Butt Welding and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Arc Welding: Butt Welding and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Arc Welding** is a fusion welding process in which coalescence of metals is achieved by heating the workpieces to a suitable temperature using the heat generated by an **electric arc** struck between an electrode and the base metal. In the KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Shop laboratory context, the specific variant performed is **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** — also known as *Manual Metal Arc (MMA) Welding* — using a consumable flux-coated electrode on **Mild Steel (M.S.) sheets**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *Arc welding is a metal joining process in which the heat required for fusion is produced by an electric arc maintained between a covered metal electrode and the base metal. The arc melts the base metal and the electrode, forming a molten weld pool that solidifies to produce a strong metallurgical bond.*

## 1.2 The Two Joints Under Study

| S.No. | Joint Type | KTU Exam Definition | Geometry |
|-------|------------|--------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Butt Joint** | Two M.S. sheets placed edge-to-edge in the same plane, fused along their abutting edges. | End-to-end alignment |
| 2 | **Lap Joint** | Two M.S. sheets overlapping each other for a finite length, fused by a *fillet weld* along the contact line. | Overlapping configuration |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sparkler" Intuition

Imagine striking a matchstick against a striking strip — the friction generates intense heat and a tiny glowing spark. **Arc welding works on the same principle**, except the "friction" is replaced by an electrical discharge, and the heat is so intense (**around 6000 °C to 7000 °C** at the arc core) that solid steel literally melts into a glowing liquid pool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Candle-Wax Joining Trick":**
> - If you have two pieces of wax and want to fuse them, you heat both edges until they become soft and sticky, then press them together until they solidify as one piece.
> - **Butt welding** is like melting the *ends* of two candles and pressing them tip-to-tip.
> - **Lap welding** is like placing two candles parallel and overlapping, then melting the *side of one* onto the *side of the other* — this is essentially a *fillet weld*.
> - The electric arc in welding is the "heat source" that replaces the candle flame — but thousands of times hotter and more controlled.

## 1.4 Why Mild Steel (M.S.) Sheets?

Mild Steel is a **low-carbon steel** (carbon content ≈ **0.15% to 0.25%**) which offers the following laboratory-friendly properties:

- **Excellent weldability** — no pre-heating or post-heating required for thin sections.
- **No cracking tendency** during solidification (unlike high-carbon or alloy steels).
- **Easily available and inexpensive** for student lab consumption.
- **Forgiving to parameter variations** — a perfect teaching medium.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Mild Steel Sheet Specification for KTU Lab:**
> Thickness: **2 mm to 6 mm**, Width: **50 mm to 100 mm**, Length: **150 mm to 200 mm** (varies by institution).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Arc Welding Process

The complete process can be broken down into five sequential phenomena:

1. **Arc Initiation (Arc Striking):** The electrode is touched against the workpiece momentarily to complete the circuit, then withdrawn by **2 mm to 4 mm** to establish a stable arc. This is called the *touch-and-lift* method. The arc voltage stabilizes around **20 V to 30 V**.
2. **Metal Transfer:** The arc heat melts both the electrode tip and the base metal surface simultaneously. The molten metal forms a small crater — the **weld pool**.
3. **Flux Decomposition:** The flux coating on the electrode decomposes to release:
   - **Shielding gases** (CO₂, H₂) to protect the molten pool from atmospheric N₂ and O₂.
   - **Slag** (floating oxide layer) to insulate the cooling weld from oxidation.
   - **Arc stabilizers** (e.g., potassium compounds) to maintain a smooth DC arc.
4. **Solidification:** As the electrode is moved forward, the weld pool cools and **solidifies**, forming a metallurgical bond — the *weld bead*.
5. **Slag Removal:** After cooling, the brittle slag layer is chipped away using a chipping hammer and wire brush.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Zones of a Weld Bead (Theory Question Favorite!):**
> 1. **Weld Metal (Fusion Zone):** The fully melted and re-solidified region — the strongest part.
> 2. **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ):** The region adjacent to the weld that did not melt but experienced microstructural changes due to heat. This is the **weakest zone**.
> 3. **Base Metal (Unaffected Zone):** The original parent metal, untouched by heat.

## 2.2 Butt Welding — Detailed Joint Geometry

A **butt joint** requires the two M.S. sheets to be aligned in the same plane with a **root gap** of approximately **1.5 mm to 2.5 mm** between them. For thin sheets (under 3 mm), a **square butt** (no edge preparation) suffices. For thicker plates, the edges are bevelled to form a **V-groove** or **U-groove** to ensure full penetration.

| Butt Joint Sub-Type | Edge Preparation | Typical Thickness Range | Applications in Lab |
|---------------------|------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Square Butt | None | ≤ 3 mm | Most common KTU lab specimen |
| Single-V Butt | One side bevelled at 30°–37.5° | 3 mm to 12 mm | Plate fabrication |
| Double-V Butt | Both sides bevelled | > 12 mm | Heavy structural work |
| U-Groove Butt | Curved groove machined | > 10 mm | Pressure vessels |

## 2.3 Lap Welding — Detailed Joint Geometry

A **lap joint** is the simplest joint to set up because **no edge preparation is required**. The two M.S. sheets overlap by a minimum of **3 × t to 5 × t** (where *t* = sheet thickness). A single-pass **fillet weld** is deposited along the edge of the top sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Overlap Length Matters:**
> The overlap must be at least **3 times the sheet thickness** to provide sufficient load-bearing area. For example, for a 2 mm M.S. sheet, the overlap should be at least **6 mm**. Insufficient overlap is a **common KTU lab error** leading to weld failure.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **board-exam favorites**. Memorize the units carefully — unit errors cost marks in KTU valuation.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Description |
|---|---------------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Arc Heat Input** | $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Total heat delivered per unit weld length |
| 2 | **Welding Current (Rule of Thumb)** | $I = 35 \times t$ (for SMAW, M.S.) | Amperes | Empirical formula — *t* = plate thickness in mm |
| 3 | **Welding Speed** | $S = \dfrac{L}{T}$ | mm/min | *L* = weld length, *T* = time to deposit |
| 4 | **Deposition Rate** | $D = \dfrac{W_{e} \times I}{1000}$ | kg/hr | *W_e* = electrode consumption (g/A·min) |
| 5 | **Strength Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{weld}}}{\sigma_{\text{base}}} \times 100$ | % | Weld strength as % of base metal |
| 6 | **Power Consumed** | $P = V \times I$ | Watts | Electrical power drawn by arc |
| 7 | **Energy per Unit Length** | $E = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{V \times I}{S}$ | J/mm | Energy delivered per mm of travel |

### Where Each Variable Means (Keep These Definitions Handy):

- **V** = Arc voltage (Volts) — typically 20–30 V for SMAW
- **I** = Welding current (Amperes) — typically 60–150 A for thin M.S. sheets
- **S** = Travel speed (mm/min) — typically 50–200 mm/min for hand welding
- **t** = Plate thickness (mm)
- **L** = Total weld length (mm)
- **T** = Welding time (min)

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

This lab exercise is the **gateway experiment** to industrial applications such as:

- **Structural steel fabrication** (I-beams, girders, trusses) — uses butt joints.
- **Sheet metal enclosures** (electrical panels, cabinets) — uses lap joints.
- **Automotive body assembly** — extensive use of lap and spot welds.
- **Pressure vessel manufacturing** — uses full-penetration butt welds.
- **Shipbuilding and pipeline construction** — circumferential butt welds.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The heat input formula $H$ directly controls the **HAZ width** and **residual stress**. Excessive heat input (> 2.0 kJ/mm for M.S.) causes **grain coarsening** in the HAZ, reducing toughness. This is why welders are trained to maintain a steady, fast travel speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Arc Heat Input Formula

The derivation of the heat input equation is a recurring 7-mark question in KTU university exams. We derive it step-by-step from fundamental electrical principles.

### Step 1 — Electrical Power Dissipated in the Arc

The welding arc behaves as an **electrical resistor** through which current flows. By the basic definition of electrical power:

$$P = V \times I$$

where $P$ is in **Watts (W)**, $V$ is the **arc voltage in Volts**, and $I$ is the **welding current in Amperes**. This is the *rate* at which electrical energy is converted into heat energy at the arc.

### Step 2 — Total Heat Energy in a Given Welding Time

If the arc is sustained for a time duration $T$ (in **seconds**), the total heat energy $Q$ generated is:

$$Q = P \times T = V \times I \times T \quad \text{(in Joules)}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Per Unit Length of Weld

In practical welding, the electrode moves along the joint. The **travel speed** $S$ is defined as the distance covered per unit time:

$$S = \dfrac{L}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \dfrac{L}{S}$$

where $L$ is the length of the weld (in mm) and $S$ is the travel speed (in **mm/sec** if $T$ is in seconds). Substituting this into the heat energy equation:

$$Q = V \times I \times \dfrac{L}{S}$$

The **energy per unit length** $E$ is obtained by dividing both sides by $L$:

$$E = \dfrac{Q}{L} = \dfrac{V \times I}{S} \quad \text{(in J/mm)}$$

### Step 4 — Unit Conversion to kJ/mm (Standard Welding Unit)

In international welding standards (ISO 857 / AWS), heat input is reported in **kilojoules per millimetre (kJ/mm)**. The speed $S$ in shop-floor practice is often given in **mm/min** (not mm/sec). So we convert:

$$1 \text{ mm/sec} = 60 \text{ mm/min}$$

Therefore, if $S$ is given in **mm/min**, the energy per unit length becomes:

$$E = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S} \quad \text{(in J/mm)}$$

To convert Joules to kilojoules, divide by 1000:

$$\boxed{H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{(in kJ/mm)}}$$

### Step 5 — Verification with a Numerical Example

> **Given:** $V = 25$ V, $I = 100$ A, $S = 150$ mm/min, weld length $L = 200$ mm.
> **Find:** Heat input $H$ in kJ/mm and total energy delivered.

**Solution:**

$$H = \dfrac{25 \times 100 \times 60}{150 \times 1000}$$

$$H = \dfrac{150000}{150000}$$

$$H = 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

Total energy for the 200 mm weld:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = H \times L = 1.0 \times 200 = 200 \text{ kJ}$$

This is the standard heat input range for a **2 mm to 3 mm M.S. sheet** using SMAW.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Welding Current "Rule of Thumb" for M.S.

For SMAW of mild steel using a general-purpose electrode (e.g., **E6013** or **E7018**), the recommended current is empirically related to plate thickness as:

$$I = k \times t$$

For M.S. with rutile-coated electrodes, the constant $k \approx 35$ A/mm. This comes from electrode manufacturer datasheets (e.g., ESAB, Lincoln Electric) and is derived from the need to maintain adequate **arc stability** and **penetration depth** of approximately **0.7 × t to 1.0 × t** per pass.

**For a 3 mm M.S. sheet:**

$$I = 35 \times 3 = 105 \text{ A} \quad (\text{typical welding current used})$$

**For a 2 mm M.S. sheet:**

$$I = 35 \times 2 = 70 \text{ A}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Butt vs. Lap

| Selection Criterion | Butt Joint | Lap Joint | KTU Exam Tip |
|---------------------|------------|-----------|--------------|
| Sheet thickness | Any, with edge prep for > 3 mm | Best for thin sheets ≤ 3 mm | Mention for thicker plate, butt is preferred |
| Edge preparation required | Yes (for thick plates) | No | Lap saves machining time |
| Material consumption | Less (no overlap) | More (overlap length wasted) | Butt is more economical |
| Load-bearing capacity | High — full cross-section | Lower — depends on fillet size | Butt is structurally superior |
| Skill required to align | High — needs tack welds + clamping | Low — sheets self-align when clamped | Lap is recommended for beginners |
| Distortion tendency | Higher (symmetric shrinkage) | Lower (asymmetric geometry) | Lap has less distortion |
| Inspection ease | Difficult (root side hidden) | Easy (fillet visible from outside) | Lap is easier to inspect |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Welding Procedure (for Lab Record)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the standard KTU 2024 lab procedure. Write it verbatim in your record.**

### A. Pre-Welding Preparation
1. **Sheets Selection:** Two M.S. sheets, **150 mm × 50 mm × 3 mm**.
2. **Surface Cleaning:** Remove rust, oil, paint using a wire brush, emery paper, or acetone.
3. **Edge Preparation:** For butt joint, square-cut edges with a root gap of **1.5 mm to 2 mm**. For lap joint, no edge prep needed.
4. **Clamping & Tack Welding:** Position the sheets on the welding table, clamp using **C-clamps or jigs**, and apply **2 to 3 small tack welds** (5 mm to 10 mm long) spaced 30 mm apart to hold alignment.

### B. Equipment Setup
1. **Welding Machine:** AC or DC transformer-rectifier, set to **80 A to 110 A** for 3 mm M.S.
2. **Electrode:** **E6013** (rutile-coated, Ø 2.5 mm or 3.15 mm) — most common in KTU labs.
3. **Electrode Holder & Earth Clamp:** Connect electrode holder to the negative terminal (DCEN — Direct Current Electrode Negative) for **E6013**; earth clamp to the workpiece.
4. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment):** Welding helmet (with **shade 10 to 13** filter glass), leather gloves, leather apron, safety shoes.

### C. Welding Operation
1. **Arc Strike:** Touch the electrode tip to the workpiece and immediately lift it by **2 mm to 3 mm** to establish the arc.
2. **Travel Direction:** For butt joint, weld in a **straight line** along the seam. For lap joint, weld along the edge of the top sheet, maintaining a **20° to 30° electrode angle** (drag angle).
3. **Travel Speed:** Maintain uniform speed of **80 mm/min to 150 mm/min**. The arc should be **1.5× to 2×** the diameter of the electrode ahead of the weld pool.
4. **Weld Length:** Deposit a bead of **100 mm to 150 mm** in a single pass.
5. **Arc Termination:** At the end of the weld, move the electrode back over the crater and quickly lift — this is the **crater-filling technique** to prevent crater cracks.

### D. Post-Welding
1. Allow the weld to cool in still air — **do not quench** (rapid cooling causes hardening and cracking).
2. **Slag Removal:** Use a chipping hammer and wire brush to remove the slag layer.
3. **Visual Inspection:** Check for visible defects (cracks, porosity, undercut, spatter).
4. **Dimensional Check:** Measure weld length, throat thickness, and leg length (for fillet).

## 3.5 Fillet Weld Geometry — Key Dimensions for Lap Joints

A **fillet weld** in a lap joint is characterized by two key dimensions (highly testable):

$$L_{\text{leg}} = 0.707 \times t_{\text{throat}}$$

$$t_{\text{throat}} = L_{\text{leg}} \times \cos(45°) = 0.707 \times L_{\text{leg}}$$

| Symbol | Name | Definition |
|--------|------|------------|
| $L_{\text{leg}}$ | Leg length | Distance from the joint root to the toe of the fillet (visible) |
| $t_{\text{throat}}$ | Throat thickness | The shortest distance from the root to the face of the weld (the load-bearing dimension) |

**Standard Fillet Weld Size for M.S. Sheet:**

For a 3 mm sheet, the minimum fillet leg size is **3 mm to 5 mm**, and the throat thickness is therefore:

$$t_{\text{throat}} = 0.707 \times 4 = 2.83 \text{ mm (typical)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Arc Welding Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional flow of the SMAW process — from power input to the finished weld bead.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B1[Welding Transformer Rectifier]
    B1 --> C1[DC Output Terminals]
    C1 --> D1[Electrode Holder with E6013 Rod]
    C1 --> D2[Earth Clamp on Workpiece]
    D1 --> E1[Electric Arc 6000C Core]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Base Metal Melts and Forms Weld Pool]
    E1 --> F2[Electrode Tip Melts and Adds Filler]
    F1 --> G1[Shielding Gas from Flux protects Pool]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Cooling and Solidification of Weld Bead]
    H1 --> I1[Slag Formation on Top of Bead]
    I1 --> J1[Slag Removal by Chipping Hammer]
    J1 --> K1[Finished Weld Joint with Full Penetration]
```

## 4.2 Types of Butt Joints — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph J1[Butt Joint Family]
        J1A[Square Butt Joint] --> J1B[Single V Butt Joint]
        J1B --> J1C[Double V Butt Joint]
        J1C --> J1D[U Groove Butt Joint]
        J1D --> J1E[J Groove Butt Joint]
    end
    J1A -.-> K1A[Thickness less than 3mm No edge prep]
    J1B -.-> K1B[Thickness 3 to 12 mm One side bevelled 30 to 37.5 degrees]
    J1C -.-> K1C[Thickness above 12 mm Both sides bevelled]
    J1D -.-> K1D[Thickness above 10 mm Curved U groove]
    J1E -.-> K1E[One side curved other side square]
```

## 4.3 Lap Joint — Fillet Weld Cross-Section

The following block diagram maps the cross-sectional view of a lap joint, showing the geometric relationships between the two sheets and the fillet weld.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LJ1[Lap Joint Cross Section Block View]
        B1A[Top MS Sheet] --> B1B[Fillet Weld Region]
        B1B --> B1C[Bottom MS Sheet]
    end
    B1B -.-> M1[Leg Length L visible dimension]
    B1B -.-> M2[Throat Thickness T load bearing]
    B1B -.-> M3[Overlap Length OL minimum 3 times thickness]
    B1B -.-> M4[Electrode Travel Direction T20 to T30 degrees drag]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow of the Lab Exercise
