```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Material Selection MS Sheet 150x50x3mm] --> S2[Step 2 Surface Cleaning by Wire Brush and Emery]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Edge Preparation Square for Butt None for Lap]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Clamping and Tack Welding 2 to 3 spots]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 PPE Check Helmet Gloves Apron]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Set Current 80 to 110 A for 3mm MS]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Arc Strike Touch and Lift Method]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Welding Travel Speed 80 to 150 mm per min]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Crater Fill at End of Bead]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Natural Cooling No Quenching]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Slag Removal Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Visual Inspection and Dimensional Check]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13 Record Observations in Lab Manual]
```

## 4.5 Heat Input Parameter Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Arc Heat Input H in kJ per mm] --> P2[Voltage V in Volts]
    P1 --> P3[Current I in Amperes]
    P1 --> P4[Travel Speed S in mm per min]
    P2 -.-> R1[Higher V increases H and broadens HAZ]
    P3 -.-> R2[Higher I increases penetration and deposition rate]
    P4 -.-> R3[Higher S reduces H and limits HAZ width]
    P1 --> P5[Quality Outcome]
    P5 --> P6[Low H below 0.8 kJ per mm causes lack of fusion]
    P5 --> P7[High H above 2.0 kJ per mm causes grain coarsening and distortion]
    P5 --> P8[Optimal H 1.0 to 1.5 kJ per mm gives best mechanical properties]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for Students:**
> The block diagrams above represent the *functional architecture* of the welding process. In a lab record, you should supplement these with a **hand-drawn cross-sectional sketch** of the actual weld geometry (showing the fusion zone, HAZ, and base metal), which carries additional marks in the KTU continuous evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — Definition Type) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Arc Welding" and list any four safety precautions to be followed in the machine shop during arc welding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Arc welding is a fusion welding process in which the heat required to melt and join the metals is generated by striking an electric arc between an electrode and the base metal. The arc temperature reaches approximately **6000 °C**, which is sufficient to melt mild steel. A consumable flux-coated electrode (e.g., E6013) is used, which simultaneously provides filler metal and shielding gas to protect the weld pool from atmospheric contamination.

**Four Safety Precautions (any four):**

1. **Welding Helmet with Shade Glass:** Always wear a welding helmet fitted with a **shade 10 to 13** filter glass to protect the eyes from the intense UV and IR radiation of the arc (a condition called **arc eye** or photokeratitis).
2. **Fire-Resistant Clothing:** Wear a leather apron, full-sleeve leather gloves, and closed safety shoes. Synthetic clothing is strictly prohibited as it may catch fire from sparks.
3. **Proper Ventilation:** Perform welding only in a well-ventilated area or use an **fume extractor** — welding fumes (especially from the flux coating) contain harmful **manganese oxide** and **fluoride compounds**.
4. **Earth Clamp Safety:** Ensure the earth clamp is firmly attached to the workpiece *before* striking the arc. A loose earth clamp can cause the current to find alternative paths through the operator's body, leading to **electric shock**.
5. **Hot Metal Handling:** Allow welds to cool naturally; never touch a recently welded joint with bare hands. Use tongs or pliers to handle hot coupons.

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four precautions — 0.5 mark each.)*

### Question 2 (Conceptual — Application Type) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Butt Joint and a Lap Joint with a neat sketch. Mention one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Butt Joint | Lap Joint |
|-------|-----------|------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Geometry** | Two sheets placed edge-to-edge in the same plane | Two sheets overlap each other for a finite length |
| 2 | **Edge Preparation** | Required (square cut, V-groove for thick plates) | Not required |
| 3 | **Weld Type** | Groove weld (full penetration) | Fillet weld |
| 4 | **Strength** | Higher — full cross-section carries load | Lower — relies on fillet throat |
| 5 | **Material Wastage** | Less | More (overlap region) |
| 6 | **Industrial Application** | Fabrication of pressure vessels, boiler shells, pipelines | Sheet metal enclosures, automotive body panels, electrical cabinets |

*(2 marks for clear differences, 1 mark for applications with sketch mention.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** process used for joining M.S. sheets. Explain the functions of the flux coating on the electrode. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A welder is joining two **3 mm M.S. sheets** using SMAW with the following parameters: Arc voltage $V = 25$ V, welding current $I = 100$ A, and travel speed $S = 150$ mm/min. Calculate (i) the heat input in kJ/mm, (ii) the total heat energy for a weld of length **180 mm**, and (iii) the time required to complete the weld. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Diagram:** (Student should draw a labelled block diagram of the SMAW setup — 2 marks)

**Components and Process Description (3 marks):**

1. **Power Source:** An AC or DC welding rectifier supplies a constant current (CC) output. The output voltage range is typically 20–30 V, and the current is adjustable from 50 A to 400 A.
2. **Electrode Holder:** A clamping device that holds the consumable electrode (E6013) and connects it to the welding circuit.
3. **Earth Clamp:** Connected to the workpiece, completes the electrical circuit.
4. **Electrode:** A flux-coated consumable rod (e.g., E6013, Ø 2.5 mm) — provides filler metal and arc stabilizers.
5. **Arc:** A sustained electric discharge between the electrode tip and the workpiece. The arc is initiated by the *touch-and-lift* method.
6. **Weld Pool:** The molten metal formed by arc heat; it solidifies as the electrode moves forward to form the weld bead.

**Functions of the Flux Coating (2 marks):**

| Function | Explanation |
|----------|-------------|
| **Shielding** | Flux decomposes in the arc heat to release CO₂ and H₂ gases, which displace atmospheric O₂ and N₂, preventing **porosity** and **nitrogen embrittlement**. |
| **Slag Formation** | A molten slag layer floats on the weld pool, protecting it from oxidation during solidification. The slag is later chipped off. |
| **Arc Stabilization** | Compounds like **potassium titanate** and **sodium silicate** in the flux lower the arc's ionization potential, making the arc smooth and stable. |
| **Deoxidation and Alloying** | Flux contains **Fe-Mn** and **Fe-Si** which scavenge oxygen and add manganese and silicon to the weld, improving strength. |
| **Weld Bead Shaping** | Flux controls the surface tension of the molten pool, producing a smooth, well-contoured bead. |

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Arc voltage, $V = 25$ V
- Welding current, $I = 100$ A
- Travel speed, $S = 150$ mm/min
- Weld length, $L = 180$ mm

**(i) Heat Input in kJ/mm (3 marks):**

$$H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

$$H = \dfrac{25 \times 100 \times 60}{150 \times 1000}$$

$$H = \dfrac{150000}{150000}$$

$$\boxed{H = 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

**(ii) Total Heat Energy for 180 mm Weld (2 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = H \times L$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 1.0 \times 180 = 180 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) Time Required to Complete the Weld (2 marks):**

Travel speed $S = 150$ mm/min means the welder covers 150 mm in 1 minute.

$$T = \dfrac{L}{S} = \dfrac{180}{150}$$

$$T = 1.2 \text{ minutes} = 72 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\boxed{T = 72 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with neat sketches the procedure for performing a **Butt Welding** exercise on two M.S. sheets in the machine shop. List the equipment and materials required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the common **welding defects** encountered in arc welding of M.S. sheets? Explain the causes and remedies for any **four** defects in detail. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Equipment and Materials (2 marks):**

1. M.S. sheets (2 Nos., 150 mm × 50 mm × 3 mm)
2. Arc welding transformer-rectifier (200 A capacity)
3. E6013 electrodes (Ø 2.5 mm or 3.15 mm)
4. Electrode holder and earth clamp with cables
5. C-clamps or welding jigs
6. Wire brush, chipping hammer, emery paper
7. Filler rod (if required for V-prep)
8. PPE: Helmet (shade 10–13), leather gloves, apron

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Sheets Preparation:** Cut two M.S. sheets to size. Clean the edges and surfaces with a wire brush and emery paper to remove rust, mill scale, and oil.
2. **Root Gap Setting:** Place the sheets edge-to-edge on the welding table with a **root gap of 1.5 mm to 2 mm** between them. Use a **spacer gauge** for accuracy.
3. **Alignment & Clamping:** Ensure the sheets are in the same plane (no step or mismatch). Clamp with C-clamps at both ends.
4. **Tack Welding:** Deposit 3 to 4 small tack welds of **5 mm to 10 mm** length, spaced **30 mm to 40 mm apart**, on the back side of the joint to hold the alignment.
5. **Welding Machine Setup:** Set current to **90 A to 110 A** (rule of thumb: $I = 35 \times 3 = 105$ A for 3 mm sheet) and voltage to **22 V to 26 V**.
6. **Welding:** Strike the arc at one end of the tack weld using the touch-and-lift method. Maintain a travel speed of **100 mm/min to 150 mm/min**. Move in a straight line along the seam. Use a slight **weaving motion (1.5× to 2× electrode diameter amplitude)** if the gap is wider than 2 mm.
7. **Crater Fill:** At the end of the bead, briefly reverse the travel direction over the crater and lift the electrode to fill any shrinkage cavity.
8. **Cooling & Cleaning:** Allow the weld to cool in still air. Use the chipping hammer and wire brush to remove the slag.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Four Common Welding Defects — Causes and Remedies:**

| S.No. | Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|-------|--------|-------|--------|
| 1 | **Porosity** (small cavities in weld) | Contamination by oil, rust, moisture; excessive travel speed; low current. | Clean surfaces thoroughly with acetone/wire brush; reduce travel speed; increase current slightly. |
| 2 | **Lack of Penetration** (weld metal does not reach root) | Low current, excessive travel speed, large root gap, wrong electrode angle. | Increase current; reduce speed; maintain correct root gap (1.5–2 mm); use proper 80°–90° electrode angle. |
| 3 | **Undercut** (groove at weld toe) | Excessive current, high travel speed, wrong electrode manipulation. | Reduce current; slow down travel; use weave motion to allow filler to fill the groove. |
| 4 | **Crater Cracks** (star-shaped crack at end of bead) | Sudden arc termination leaving a shrinkage cavity. | Always use the **crater-filling technique** — reverse travel over the crater before lifting the electrode. |
| 5 | **Spatter** (molten metal droplets around the bead) | High current, long arc length, damp electrodes. | Set correct current; maintain short arc (2–3 mm); dry electrodes in oven at 100 °C for 1 hour. |
| 6 | **Slag Inclusion** (non-metallic particles trapped in weld) | Insufficient slag removal between passes; shallow penetration. | Chip slag thoroughly between passes; increase current for deeper penetration. |

*(For 7 marks, present 4 defects × 1.75 marks each — 0.5 mark for cause + 0.5 mark for remedy + 0.75 mark for explanation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in the Welding Lab Viva and Written Exam:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mistake in Heat Input Formula:** Students frequently forget to convert the travel speed from **mm/min to mm/s** (multiply by 60 in numerator) and Joules to **kJ** (divide by 1000). This is a **2-mark direct deduction** in ESE.
> 2. **Confusing Leg Length with Throat Thickness:** Many students wrongly report only the leg length as the "weld size." Remember, the **throat thickness** ($0.707 \times L$) is the **load-bearing dimension**, not the leg.
> 3. **Forgetting Flux Functions:** Writing "flux produces slag" is incomplete. Examiners expect at least **3 functions** (shielding, slag formation, deoxidation) for full marks.
> 4. **Improper Electrode Polarity:** For E6013, the correct polarity is **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** — stating "DCEP" will lose marks.
> 5. **No PPE Mention:** Any welding-related question that touches on safety *must* include **at least one PPE item** (helmet, gloves, apron) for full marks.
> 6. **Crater-Filling Omission:** Ending the weld abruptly without crater fill is treated as a **defect (crater crack)** in the lab record's "observations" section.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Arc welding** = fusion welding using electric arc heat (~6000 °C).
- [ ] **SMAW** is the lab variant — uses flux-coated consumable electrode (E6013).
- [ ] **Butt joint** = edge-to-edge sheets; **Lap joint** = overlapping sheets with fillet weld.
- [ ] **Heat Input Formula:** $H = (V \times I \times 60) / (S \times 1000)$ **kJ/mm** — *board-exam favorite*.
- [ ] **Rule of thumb current for M.S.:** $I = 35 \times t$ (Amperes, $t$ in mm).
- [ ] **Electrode polarity for E6013:** **DCEN (Electrode Negative)**.
- [ ] **PPE checklist:** Helmet (shade 10–13), leather gloves, leather apron, safety shoes.
- [ ] **Arc striking method:** *Touch-and-lift*; arc gap maintained at **2–3 mm**.
- [ ] **Optimal heat input for thin M.S.:** **0.8 to 1.5 kJ/mm** (do not exceed 2.0 kJ/mm).
- [ ] **Travel speed for 3 mm M.S.:** **100–150 mm/min** (hand welding).
- [ ] **Three zones of weld:** Weld metal, **HAZ (weakest)**, base metal.
- [ ] **Fillet weld throat thickness:** $t_{throat} = 0.707 \times L_{leg}$.
- [ ] **Root gap for square butt joint:** **1.5 to 2 mm**.
- [ ] **Minimum overlap for lap joint:** **3 × t** (t = sheet thickness).
- [ ] **Slag removal tools:** Chipping hammer + wire brush.
- [ ] **Cooling rule:** Natural air cool — **never quench** in water.
- [ ] **Flux functions (5):** Shielding, slag formation, arc stabilization, deoxidation, alloying.
- [ ] **Common defects (must know 4):** Porosity, lack of penetration, undercut, crater cracks.
- [ ] **E6013 polarity:** DCEN; electrode diameter for 3 mm sheet: **2.5 mm or 3.15 mm**.
- [ ] **Crater fill technique:** Reverse travel at end of bead, then lift electrode.
- [ ] **Industrial use of butt joint:** Pressure vessels, pipelines, structural beams.
- [ ] **Industrial use of lap joint:** Sheet metal enclosures, automotive panels, electrical cabinets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Gas Welding: Butt Welding and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Gas Welding: Butt Welding and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Gas Welding?

**Gas Welding** (specifically **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)**) is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining two metal pieces is produced by the controlled combustion of a fuel gas (Acetylene, C₂H₂) mixed with pure oxygen (O₂) at the tip of a welding torch. It belongs to the family of **Fusion Welding** under **Pressure-less (Non-pressure) Gas Welding** as per KTU 2024 Scheme Classification of Welding Processes.

The combustion reaction at the heart of the process is:

$$2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O + Heat$$

The flame temperature achieved in a neutral oxy-acetylene flame reaches approximately **3200 °C (3473 K)**, which is more than sufficient to melt Mild Steel (M.S.), whose melting point is around **1500 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Highlight):**
> Gas Welding is a *manual fusion welding process* where the edges of the metal to be joined are melted by an oxy-fuel flame, with or without the addition of a filler rod. Since no external mechanical pressure is applied, it is classified as a **Non-Pressure Gas Welding Process**.

### 1.2 Butt Welding vs. Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets

| Joint Type | Definition | Position of Sheets |
|---|---|---|
| **Butt Joint** | Two M.S. sheets are placed edge-to-edge in the same plane and joined along their abutting edges. | Sheets lie in the same plane. |
| **Lap Joint** | Two M.S. sheets are made to overlap each other for a certain distance and joined at the overlap zone. | Sheets lie on top of one another with an overlap. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are heating two metal sheets with a **highly controlled, intensely hot miniature blowtorch flame** until their edges glow red and turn into a molten puddle. While still molten, you stir them together (with a filler wire) and let them cool — they fuse into a single piece.

- **Butt Welding** is like gluing two ends of a piece of paper together — the joint is at the meeting line of the edges.
- **Lap Welding** is like taping one piece of paper on top of another — the joint is over the overlapping region and is welded at **both edges** (a *double fillet* configuration).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications:**
> - **Butt Joints:** Used in fabricating tanks, boilers, pipelines, and sheet-metal enclosures where a flush, continuous surface is needed.
> - **Lap Joints:** Used in automobile body panels, ductwork, and structural frames where higher joint strength (due to larger weld area) is required and slight increase in thickness is acceptable.

### 1.4 Working Principle Summary

The oxy-acetylene flame has three concentric zones (called the *flame cone structure*):

1. **Inner Cone (White/Bright)** — Unburnt mixture of acetylene + oxygen at **~900 °C**.
2. **Middle Cone (Reducing Zone)** — Partial combustion; rich in hot carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H₂); the hottest zone used for welding at **~3200 °C**.
3. **Outer Cone (Oxidizing Sheath)** — Final combustion with atmospheric oxygen; bluish envelope at **~2500 °C**.

The work-piece is heated at a distance of **2 mm to 6 mm** from the inner cone tip — this is called the **Working Distance** or **Standoff Distance**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Oxy-Acetylene Flames

By adjusting the Oxygen : Acetylene ratio at the nozzle, three distinct flames are produced. Selecting the correct flame is **critical** for a defect-free weld on M.S.

| Flame Type | O₂ : C₂H₂ Ratio | Visual Appearance | Effect on M.S. |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Neutral Flame** | **1.1 : 1** | Sharp, clear inner cone; rounded bluish outer envelope. | No chemical action on molten pool — **Standard for M.S. welding.** |
| **Oxidizing Flame** | **> 1.1 : 1** | Shorter, pointed inner cone; harsh "hissing" sound. | Burns out carbon → brittle, porous weld. |
| **Reducing (Carburizing) Flame** | **< 1.1 : 1** | Long, feathery, fluffy inner cone with excess acetylene feather. | Adds carbon to weld → hard, brittle weld. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** For **M.S. Sheets**, ALWAYS use a **Neutral Flame** with a slight excess of acetylene (about 1.05:1 to 1.1:1) to ensure a clean, ductile weld.

### 2.2 Heat Transfer Mechanism

Heat is transferred from the flame to the work-piece by three modes:

1. **Conduction** — Through the solid metal from the molten pool.
2. **Convection** — By the hot gases of the flame envelope.
3. **Radiation** — By emission of thermal radiation from the incandescent flame core.

The **effective heat input (Q)** delivered to the weld zone for a given time *t* can be approximated by:

$$Q = \dot{V}_{C_2H_2} \times \rho_{C_2H_2} \times C_v \times \eta$$

where:
- $\dot{V}_{C_2H_2}$ = Volume flow rate of acetylene (m³/s)
- $\rho_{C_2H_2}$ = Density of acetylene = **1.097 kg/m³** at STP
- $C_v$ = Calorific value of acetylene = **48.8 MJ/kg** (≈ **56,000 kJ/m³** at NTP)
- $\eta$ = Thermal efficiency of gas welding = **0.7 to 0.85** (typical ≈ 0.75)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Neutral Flame Ratio** | O₂ : C₂H₂ = **1.1 : 1** | — | Memorize this for KTU exams. |
| **Acetylene Flame Temperature** | **≈ 3200 °C** | °C | Highest among common fuel gases. |
| **M.S. Melting Point** | **≈ 1500 °C** | °C | |
| **Calorific Value of C₂H₂** | **48.8 MJ/kg** | MJ/kg | Or 1450 BTU/ft³. |
| **Oxygen Pressure (working)** | **1.0 – 2.5 kg/cm²** | kg/cm² | Depends on sheet thickness. |
| **Acetylene Pressure (working)** | **0.3 – 0.7 kg/cm²** | kg/cm² | Never exceed 1 kg/cm² (safety). |
| **Filler Rod Diameter** | $d_r = t/2 + 1 \text{ mm}$ (approx.) | mm | $t$ = sheet thickness. |
| **Acetylene Flow Rate (rule of thumb)** | $\dot{V} \approx (100 \text{ to } 150) \times t$ | L/h | $t$ in mm; used for nozzle selection. |
| **Heat Input** | $Q = \dot{V}_{C_2H_2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_v \cdot \eta$ | J/s (W) | $\eta \approx 0.75$ for OA welding. |
| **Specific Heat of M.S.** | **0.486 J/(g·°C)** | J/(g·°C) | Used for temperature-rise problems. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This entire process underpins the fabrication of M.S. tanks, pressure vessels, automobile chassis, and structural frames. Mastering the flame ratio is the single most-examined KTU topic from this experiment.

### 2.4 Selection of Nozzle and Filler Rod

- **Nozzle (Tip) Size** depends on sheet thickness. As a rule, use the **largest nozzle** that does not overheat the work-piece.
- **Filler Rod (Welding Rod)** for M.S. is a low-carbon steel rod (≈ 0.1 % C) with a melting point slightly **below** the parent metal so it melts first and flows into the joint.
- Filler rod diameter selection: **d_r ≈ t / 2 + 1 mm**, where *t* is sheet thickness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Pre-Welding Preparation (Common to Both Joints)

1. **Sheet Selection:** Cut two M.S. sheets of dimensions **100 mm × 50 mm × 3 mm** (thickness ≈ 3 mm typical for lab).
2. **Surface Cleaning:** Remove rust, scale, oil, and paint using a wire brush, emery paper, or file. Cleaning zone ≈ **20 mm** from the joint line.
3. **Edge Preparation:**
   - For **Butt Joint** of sheets ≤ 3 mm thick → **Square butt preparation** (no bevel).
   - For thicker sheets → single-V or double-V bevel at **60°–90°** included angle with a **root gap of 1.5–2 mm**.
4. **Filler Rod Selection:** Mild steel filler rod of diameter **d_r ≈ t/2 + 1 ≈ 2.5 mm** for *t* = 3 mm.
5. **Tacking:** Place short weld beads (≈ 10 mm) at both ends to hold the sheets in correct alignment and maintain the root gap.
6. **Safety Check:** Verify regulator settings (O₂ at 2 kg/cm², C₂H₂ at 0.5 kg/cm²), flashback arrestor fitted, hoses crack-free, goggles worn.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Butt Welding Procedure (Leftward Technique)

The **leftward (backhand) technique** is preferred for sheets ≤ 3 mm because the heat is directed away from the molten pool, giving better control.

| Step | Action | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hold the torch with the **flame pointing in the direction of welding** and the rod ahead of the flame. | Leftward technique — used for thin sheets. |
| 2 | Position nozzle tip at **2–6 mm** from the sheet surface, inclined at **45°** to the sheet plane. | Standard working distance & torch angle. |
| 3 | Heat both edges **simultaneously**, oscillating the torch in a small circular or side-to-side motion. | Ensures both edges melt uniformly, forming a common puddle. |
| 4 | Dip the filler rod into the leading edge of the puddle at **30°–45°** to the sheet. | Allows filler metal to flow in by capillary action. |
| 5 | Maintain steady forward speed — too slow → burn-through; too fast → lack of fusion. | Optimal speed yields a smooth, ripple-free bead. |
| 6 | Stop at the end; **back-step** the torch slightly to fill the crater before withdrawing. | Prevents crater cracks. |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Lap Welding Procedure (Rightward Technique)

The **rightward (forehand) technique** is preferred for lap joints because the flame pre-heats the overlapping plate ahead of the pool, ensuring deeper penetration.

| Step | Action | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overlap the two M.S. sheets by a distance equal to **3× to 5× the sheet thickness** (i.e., 9–15 mm for 3 mm sheet). | Provides adequate weld area for strength. |
| 2 | Tack-weld at both ends of the overlap. | Holds sheets firmly during welding. |
| 3 | Hold the torch at **60°–75°** to the sheet, with flame directed toward the unwelded portion. | Rightward technique — for thicker sections. |
| 4 | First pass: weld along the **leading edge of the top sheet** to fuse it with the bottom sheet. | Forms the first fillet. |
| 5 | Flip the assembly; weld along the **other edge** from the reverse side. | Forms the second fillet, completing the *double-fillet lap joint*. |
| 6 | Use a **filler rod** at 30°–45°, dipping it into the inner cone of the flame occasionally to add material. | Builds up the fillet profile. |

### 3.4 Mathematical Insight — Heat Required to Raise M.S. Temperature

To verify whether the flame's energy is sufficient to melt the joint zone, consider a typical lab weld zone:

Let sheet thickness **t = 3 mm = 0.003 m**, weld bead width **w = 6 mm = 0.006 m**, length **L = 100 mm = 0.1 m**, and weld velocity **v = 100 mm/min = 1.67 × 10⁻³ m/s**.

**Step 1 — Mass of the weld zone:**
$$m = \rho_{M.S.} \times V = \rho_{M.S.} \times (t \times w \times L)$$

$$m = 7850 \times (0.003 \times 0.006 \times 0.1)$$

$$m = 7850 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-6} = 1.413 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg} = 14.13 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Heat to raise M.S. from 25 °C to melting point 1500 °C:**
$$Q_{sense} = m \times c \times \Delta T = 14.13 \times 0.486 \times (1500 - 25)$$

$$Q_{sense} = 14.13 \times 0.486 \times 1475 = 10{,}128 \text{ J} \approx 10.13 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Time available at welding speed:**
$$t_{weld} = L / v = 0.1 / (1.67 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 60 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Required average power:**
$$P_{req} = Q_{sense} / t_{weld} = 10{,}128 / 60 \approx 168.8 \text{ W}$$

This is easily delivered by an oxy-acetylene flame (typical nozzle delivers **2–10 kW**), confirming the process is **thermodynamically viable** for M.S. sheets.

### 3.5 Post-Welding Operations

1. **Slag & Spatter Removal:** Chip off loosely adherent oxide/slag with a chipping hammer; brush with a wire brush.
2. **Visual Inspection:** Look for cracks, porosity, undercut, lack of penetration, or burn-through.
3. **Dimensional Check:** Use a try-square for butt joint alignment; use a fillet gauge for lap joint leg length (should be **≥ 3 mm** minimum).
4. **Optional Destructive Test:** Bend test — bend the welded specimen 180° around a mandrel of **3 × thickness**; the weld should not crack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Neutral Flame is Critical for M.S.:** If excess oxygen → **FeO formation** → porosity & brittleness. If excess acetylene → carbon pick-up → hardness & cracking. Only a **neutral flame** keeps the weld chemistry balanced.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Oxy-Acetylene Welding Equipment Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["OXYGEN CYLINDER\nBLACK BODY\n99 PERCENT PURE O2"] --> B1["OXYGEN REGULATOR\n0 TO 30 KG PER CM2\nGAUGE"]
    A2["ACETYLENE CYLINDER\nMAROON BODY\nDISSOLVED ACETYLENE"] --> B2["ACETYLENE REGULATOR\n0 TO 3.5 KG PER CM2\nGAUGE"]
    B1 --> C1["FLASHBACK ARRESTOR\nONE WAY VALVE\nSAFETY DEVICE"]
    B2 --> C2["FLASHBACK ARRESTOR\nONE WAY VALVE\nSAFETY DEVICE"]
    C1 --> D["WELDING TORCH\nHANDLE WITH VALVES\nINLET FOR O2 AND C2H2"]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E["NOZZLE OR TIP\nM.S. SHEET JOINT\nMOLTEN PUDDLE"]
    H["FILLER ROD\nMILD STEEL\nLOW CARBON"] --> E
    E --> F["WELDED JOINT\nBUTT OR LAP\nCOOLED IN STILL AIR"]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Gas Welding Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP A\nIDENTIFY TOOLS\nGOGGLES TORCH CYLINDERS\nREGULATORS HOSES"] --> S2["STEP B\nCHECK CYLINDER PRESSURES\nOXYGEN 10 TO 15 KG PER CM2\nACETYLENE 5 TO 7 KG PER CM2"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP C\nPREPARE M.S. SHEETS\nCLEAN EDGES\nSET JOINT GEOMETRY"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP D\nTACK WELD AT BOTH ENDS\n10 MM BEADS\nMAINTAIN ROOT GAP"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP E\nSET NEUTRAL FLAME\nADJUST O2 AND C2H2 VALVES\nON THE TORCH"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP F\nDEPOSIT CONTINUOUS WELD BEAD\nLEFTWARD FOR BUTT\nRIGHTWARD FOR LAP"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP G\nBACK STEP TO FILL CRATER\nWITHDRAW TORCH SLOWLY"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP H\nPOST WELD CLEANING\nWIRE BRUSH CHIPPING HAMMER"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP I\nINSPECTION\nVISUAL BEND TEST\nDIMENSIONAL CHECK"]
```

### 4.3 Joint Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BUTT["BUTT JOINT GEOMETRY"]
        B1A["M.S. SHEET 1\nTHICKNESS T"] -->|ABUTTING EDGE| B1B["WELD BEAD\nTOP SURFACE"]
        B1A --> B1C["M.S. SHEET 2\nTHICKNESS T"]
        B1C --> B1B
    end
    subgraph LAP["LAP JOINT GEOMETRY"]
        L1A["M.S. SHEET 1\nBOTTOM"] --> L1B["OVERLAP ZONE\nLENGTH 3T TO 5T"]
        L1B --> L1C["M.S. SHEET 2\nTOP"]
        L1D["FILLET WELD 1\nLEADING EDGE"] --> L1B
        L1E["FILLET WELD 2\nTRAILING EDGE"] --> L1B
    end
```

### 4.4 Flame Structure Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1["OUTER CONE\nFINAL COMBUSTION\nWITH ATMOSPHERIC O2\nTEMPERATURE 2500 C"] --> F2["MIDDLE CONE\nREDUCING ZONE\nCO AND H2 RICH\nTEMPERATURE 3200 C\nWORKING ZONE"]
    F2 --> F3["INNER CONE\nUNBURNT MIXTURE\nO2 AND C2H2\nTEMPERATURE 900 C"]
    F3 --> F4["NOZZLE TIP\nGAS OUTLET\nMIXING CHAMBER"]
    F4 --> F3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
*What is a neutral flame in oxy-acetylene welding? Why is it preferred for welding Mild Steel sheets?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **neutral flame** is obtained when the oxygen-to-acetylene ratio at the torch nozzle is **1.1 : 1**, meaning all the acetylene is completely combusted with no excess of either gas. The inner cone is short, sharp, and clearly defined, surrounded by a rounded bluish envelope.
It is preferred for **Mild Steel welding** because:
1. It does **not oxidize** the molten pool (no excess O₂ → no FeO, no porosity).
2. It does **not add carbon** to the weld (no excess C₂H₂ → no carburization, no hardness).
3. It produces a clean, ductile weld with **balanced chemistry**, ensuring mechanical strength.
*(1 + 2 = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*List any six safety precautions to be observed during oxy-acetylene gas welding of M.S. sheets.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Wear** approved welding goggles with shade filter (shade 4–6) to protect eyes.
2. Ensure **flashback arrestors** are fitted on both oxygen and acetylene lines.
3. **Never** use acetylene at a working pressure **above 1 kg/cm²** to avoid self-decomposition.
4. Keep the acetylene cylinder **upright** and away from heat sources.
5. **Purge** the hoses and regulators before lighting the torch (open O₂ → bleed → close; repeat for C₂H₂).
6. Keep a **fire extinguisher** (CO₂ or dry powder) nearby; clear the area of flammable materials.
7. Do **not** strike the nozzle on the work-piece to extinguish the flame.
*(6 × 0.5 = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

---

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of an **oxy-acetylene welding setup**. List the function of each component. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe in detail the **leftward technique** of gas welding as applied to a **butt joint** in 3 mm M.S. sheet. Mention flame setting, torch angle, and filler rod specification. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Construction & Working of OA Welding Setup:**

The oxy-acetylene welding setup consists of the following components:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Oxygen Cylinder** | Stores O₂ at ~150 kg/cm², black body, identified by the word "OXYGEN". |
| **Acetylene Cylinder** | Stores C₂H₂ dissolved in acetone, maroon body, max pressure ~16 kg/cm². |
| **Oxygen Regulator** | Reduces O₂ pressure to working value (~2 kg/cm²); has two gauges (cylinder & delivery). |
| **Acetylene Regulator** | Reduces C₂H₂ pressure to working value (~0.5 kg/cm²); different thread to prevent interchange. |
| **Flashback Arrestors** | One-way valves that prevent flame propagation back into the cylinder in case of reverse flow. |
| **Hoses** | Oxygen → **blue**; Acetylene → **red** (colour-coded). |
| **Welding Torch (Blowpipe)** | Mixes O₂ and C₂H₂ in correct ratio and delivers the flame. Has two needle valves (one for each gas). |
| **Nozzle / Tip** | Sized according to sheet thickness; delivers the flame at the right velocity. |
| **Filler Rod** | Low-carbon steel rod, dipped in the molten pool to add filler metal. |
| **Welding Goggles** | Protect eyes from UV/IR radiation and bright flame. |

**Working Principle:** Oxygen from the cylinder is regulated to **~2 kg/cm²**, acetylene to **~0.5 kg/cm²**, and both gases are delivered through their respective hoses to the torch. Inside the torch mixing chamber, the gases blend in the **1.1 : 1** ratio. Upon ignition at the nozzle tip, they combust, producing a flame of **~3200 °C** that melts the joint edges. The filler rod is dipped into the molten puddle to deposit filler metal. The molten puddle solidifies to form a continuous weld bead, joining the two M.S. sheets metallurgically. *(7 marks — component table = 4 marks, working = 3 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Leftward Technique for Butt Joint:**

**1. Joint Preparation:**
- Sheets: **100 mm × 50 mm × 3 mm M.S.**
- Square edge preparation (no bevel for ≤ 3 mm thickness).
- Root gap: **1.5–2 mm**.
- Cleaning: File/wire-brush a 20 mm band along each edge to bright metal.

**2. Tack Welding:** Short 10 mm beads at both ends of the joint to maintain the root gap and prevent distortion.

**3. Flame Setting:**
- Open acetylene valve slightly, light the torch, then gradually add oxygen.
- Adjust until a **neutral flame** is obtained — sharp inner cone, no feather, no hiss.
- O₂ pressure ≈ 2 kg/cm², C₂H₂ pressure ≈ 0.5 kg/cm².

**4. Torch Position (Leftward Technique):**
- Hold the torch with the nozzle pointing **backwards (against the direction of travel)**.
- The flame **leads the molten pool** — heat is directed away from the just-deposited bead.
- Torch angle to sheet surface = **45°**.
- Working distance = **2–6 mm** from the inner cone tip to the sheet.

**5. Filler Rod Specification:**
- Material: Low-carbon M.S. filler rod, **AWS E-6010 / E-6013** classification.
- Diameter: **d_r ≈ t/2 + 1 ≈ 2.5 mm** for a 3 mm sheet.
- Held at **30°–45°** to the sheet, on the **leading side** of the puddle (ahead of the torch).

**6. Welding Sequence:**
- Oscillate the torch in a small circular or side-to-side motion to spread the heat across both edges.
- Dip the filler rod into the leading edge of the puddle to deposit filler.
- Maintain a steady travel speed of **~100 mm/min**.
- At the end of the joint, **back-step** the torch to fill the crater, then slowly withdraw.

**7. Inspection:** After cooling, chip slag, wire-brush, and check for:
- Uniform bead width (≈ 6 mm).
- No under-cut, porosity, or cracks.
- Square alignment of the two sheets.
*(7 marks: prep = 1, flame = 2, technique = 2, rod = 1, inspection = 1)*

---

#### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Alternative]

**(a)** Differentiate between **leftward and rightward welding techniques** of gas welding. State which one is used for lap joints on M.S. sheets and justify. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the step-by-step procedure to perform a **lap joint** on two M.S. sheets by gas welding. List three common defects observed and their remedies. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Leftward vs. Rightward Technique:**

| Feature | Leftward (Backhand) | Rightward (Forehand) |
|---|---|---|
| **Flame direction** | Flame is directed **backward** (opposite to direction of travel). | Flame is directed **forward** (along the direction of travel). |
| **Torch angle to sheet** | **45°** | **60°–75°** |
| **Filler rod position** | Ahead of the flame (leading side). | Behind the flame (trailing side). |
| **Welding speed** | Slower (for thin sheets). | Faster (for thicker sheets). |
| **Heat effect** | Heat does **not** preheat the parent metal ahead → lower heat input. | Heat **preheats** the parent metal ahead → higher heat input, deeper penetration. |
| **Application** | Thin sheets (≤ 3 mm), butt joints. | Thick sheets (≥ 5 mm), lap joints. |
| **Operator view** | Poor view of the puddle. | Excellent view of the puddle. |
| **Distortion** | Less distortion. | More distortion possible. |

**Preferred technique for Lap Joint on M.S. sheets:** **Rightward (forehand) technique.**

**Justification:**
1. Lap joints require **deeper penetration** to fuse the top sheet with the bottom sheet — the rightward technique's preheating effect delivers this.
2. The lap joint has a **larger weld pool** (a fillet triangle); the rightward technique allows the operator a clear view of the pool as he progresses.
3. Rightward welding enables **faster deposition**, suitable for the larger weld volume of a fillet.
4. The torch's leading flame preheats the next portion of the joint, reducing the risk of **lack of fusion** at the root.
*(7 marks: comparison table = 5, justification = 2)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Lap Joint Procedure & Defects:**

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Marking & Cutting:** Cut two M.S. sheets of **100 mm × 50 mm × 3 mm**.
2. **Cleaning:** Wire-brush both surfaces in the overlap region to bright metal.
3. **Overlap:** Place one sheet over the other with an overlap of **3t to 5t = 9 to 15 mm** (use 12 mm).
4. **Tack Welding:** Place 10 mm tack welds at both corners of the overlap to hold the sheets in position.
5. **Set Rightward Technique:** Torch angle **60°–75°**, flame pointing in the direction of travel.
6. **Neutral Flame:** Adjust O₂ : C₂H₂ to **1.1 : 1**.
7. **First Pass — Weld the Leading Edge:**
   - Direct flame to the **junction of the top and bottom sheet on the leading edge**.
   - Melt a small puddle at the corner; dip the filler rod at 30°–45° to build up a fillet.
   - Maintain working distance of 3–5 mm; travel along the entire edge.
8. **Flip the Assembly:** Allow to cool slightly, then flip to expose the other edge.
9. **Second Pass — Weld the Trailing Edge:**
   - Repeat Step 7 on the other edge to form a **double-fillet lap joint**.
10. **Post-Weld:** Wire-brush, chip slag, visually inspect.

**Joint Schematic (text description):**
```
Top Sheet       →   ___________________
                            ↓ (Fillet Weld 1)
Bottom Sheet    →   ___________________
                            ↓ (Fillet Weld 2, after flipping)
Top Sheet       →   ___________________
```

**Three Common Defects & Remedies:**

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Porosity (pin-holes in weld)** | Excessive O₂ in flame; dirty surfaces; rapid solidification. | Use **neutral flame**; clean surfaces; reduce travel speed. |
| **Lack of Fusion** (un-melted parent metal at joint root) | Torch too far; insufficient heat; rightward technique not used. | Reduce working distance; increase O₂ flow; use rightward technique for lap. |
| **Undercut** (groove at weld toe) | Excessive heat; wrong torch angle; fast travel. | Lower flame intensity; correct torch angle (60°); reduce travel speed. |
| *(Bonus: Burn-through)* | Too much heat on thin sheet. | Use smaller nozzle; faster travel; intermittent welding. |
*(7 marks: procedure = 4, defects = 2, remedies = 1)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong flame ratio:** Writing "1:1" instead of "1.1:1" costs **1 full mark** — examiners are strict about this.
> 2. **Confusing leftward & rightward:** Many students state "rightward for butt joint" — this is **wrong** for thin M.S. sheets. Memorize: **Leftward = Butt, Rightward = Lap** (general lab rule).
> 3. **Forgetting flashback arrestor:** Listing safety without the **flashback arrestor** is a guaranteed **0.5–1 mark deduction**.
> 4. **Omitting tack welding:** Failing to mention tacking in the procedure = **1 mark lost** (it is the first welding step).
> 5. **No crater-fill step:** The end of every weld has a **crater**; examiners look for the back-step / crater-fill action — missing it = **0.5 mark lost**.
> 6. **Wrong unit for pressure:** Acetylene pressure **must** be in **kg/cm²**, not bar or psi (KTU convention).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Gas Welding** = **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)** = Non-pressure fusion welding.
- **Neutral Flame Ratio:** **O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1.1 : 1** — used for M.S.
- **Flame Temperature:** **~3200 °C** in the reducing zone (middle cone).
- **Three Flames:** Neutral, Oxidizing (excess O₂), Reducing/Carburizing (excess C₂H₂).
- **Three Flame Cones:** Inner (~900 °C), Middle/Reducing (~3200 °C, working zone), Outer (~2500 °C).
- **Working Distance:** **2–6 mm** from inner cone tip.
- **Torch Angle:** **45°** (leftward, butt) or **60°–75°** (rightward, lap).
- **Filler Rod:** M.S. low-carbon rod; **d_r ≈ t/2 + 1 mm**.
- **Butt Joint:** Sheets in same plane; **leftward technique** for ≤ 3 mm sheets; square edge prep.
- **Lap Joint:** Sheets overlap by **3t–5t**; **rightward technique**; double fillet (weld both edges).
- **Acetylene Pressure:** Never exceed **1 kg/cm²** working (safety against decomposition).
- **Calorific Value of Acetylene:** **48.8 MJ/kg** = **~56,000 kJ/m³** at NTP.
- **Tack Welding:** Always done first at both ends of the joint.
- **Crater Fill:** Always back-step at the end to prevent crater cracks.
- **Safety:** Goggles (shade 4–6), flashback arrestors, fire extinguisher, vertical acetylene cylinder.
- **Post-weld:** Wire-brush, chip slag, visual + dimensional inspection.
- **Heat Input Formula:** $Q = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot C_v \cdot \eta$ with $\eta \approx 0.75$.
- **Specific Heat of M.S.:** **0.486 J/(g·°C)** — useful in heat-balance problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Preparation of Program, Simulation and Exercise on CNC Lathe: Turning, Step Turning, Taper Turning, Thread Cutting, Ball and Cup Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL308 - MACHINE SHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Preparation of Program, Simulation and Exercise on CNC Lathe: Turning, Step Turning, Taper Turning, Thread Cutting, Ball and Cup Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 CNC Lathe – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CNC Lathe (Computer Numerical Control Lathe)** is a programmable turning machine tool that automatically performs material removal operations on a rotating workpiece using a cutting tool whose motions (traverse, depth, feed) are governed by **G-code and M-code instructions** generated from a part program, replacing manual hand-wheel manipulation of conventional lathes.

The CNC Lathe used in the Machine Shop laboratory is typically a **2-axis CNC Turning Center** where:
- **Z-axis** → Longitudinal axis (along the spindle axis, tool moves parallel to workpiece rotation)
- **X-axis** → Cross-slide / Radial axis (perpendicular to spindle, controls depth of cut)
- Workpiece is **chucked and rotated** (spindle motor)
- Tool is **stationary radially** and is fed along Z and X digitally

> [!NOTE]
> **Work Coordinate System (WCS) Origin**: Conventionally set at the **intersection of the spindle nose face and the spindle rotation axis** (G54 default). All tool motions are referenced to this zero point in the part program.

## 1.2 Concept Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Think of a CNC Lathe as a **robotic wood-turner's apprentice**. Instead of the craftsman manually pushing a chisel (cross-slide handwheel) along a spinning piece of wood (chucked workpiece), you write a **recipe card** (the part program) listing each step: *"Start the spin, bring the chisel here, push it this deep, move it this fast, stop here."* The apprentice (CNC controller) reads the recipe and performs every cut identically every time. The **simulation** is the apprentice practising on a **virtual block of wood** before touching the real one — catching mistakes (crashes, wrong dimensions) without breaking tools or scrapping material.

In our KTU lab experiment, you will:
1. **Study** the CNC lathe console, axes, tool turret, chuck, tailstock.
2. **Prepare** a part program using standard G/M-codes for a specific geometry.
3. **Simulate** the program on the controller's screen (or in software like **CNC Simulator Pro / SinuTrain / FANUC NCGuide**).
4. **Exercise / Execute** the program on the actual machine (or witness the instructor's run).

## 1.3 The Five Operations – Overview

| # | Operation | Geometry Produced | Key G-codes | Typical Tool |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Plain Turning** | Cylindrical diameter reduction | G00, G01, G90 | CNMG / DNMG insert |
| 2 | **Step Turning** | Multiple discrete diameters (shoulders) | G01 with multiple Z-X moves | CNMG insert |
| 3 | **Taper Turning** | Conical surface | G01 with X-Z simultaneous motion, or **G90** | CNMG insert |
| 4 | **Thread Cutting** | External helical thread | **G92 / G32** (single-pass) or **G76** (multi-pass) | Threading insert (60° partial profile) |
| 5 | **Ball & Cup Turning** | Spherical (ball) / Concave hemispherical (cup) | G02/G03 circular interpolation with **R** or **I, K** | Round-nose / ball-nose insert |

> [!NOTE]
> **Spindle convention**: Right-hand rule — looking from the tailstock toward the chuck, the spindle rotates **counter-clockwise (M03)** for external turning. This gives **up-milling-equivalent chip flow** against the tool tip.

## 1.4 Physical Standards Highlighted in Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard metric thread profile**: ISO 60° (e.g., M16×1.5 — major dia **16 mm**, pitch **1.5 mm**).
> - **Default feed unit**: mm/rev for turning, mm/min for milling — set by **G98 (rev-based)** / **G99 (time-based)**.
> - **Default coordinate system**: Incremental = **G91**, Absolute = **G90**.
> - **Default unit**: **G21 (metric, mm)** — never use G20 (inch) in KTU lab without specific reason.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental CNC Lathe Programming Rules

### 2.1.1 Program Structure (Block-by-Block Anatomy)

A CNC part program is a sequence of **blocks (lines)**. Each block contains one or more **words** (address + number). Example:

```
N10  G21 G99 G97            ; Metric, feed in mm/rev, spindle in RPM
N20  G54                    ; Select Work Coordinate System 1
N30  M03 S1500              ; Spindle CW at 1500 RPM
N40  T0101                  ; Tool 1, offset register 01
N50  G00 X50.0 Z2.0         ; Rapid to safe start position
N60  G00 X30.0              ; Rapid to just above workpiece dia
N70  G01 Z-40.0 F0.2        ; Linear feed to depth Z=-40 at feed 0.2 mm/rev
N80  G00 X50.0 Z2.0         ; Retract to safe position
N90  M05                    ; Spindle stop
N100 M30                    ; Program end, rewind
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety**: The block **N50** (rapid) moves to a **safe clearance** (`Z2.0`) before plunging radially in **N60**. This prevents the tool from crashing into the chuck or workpiece shoulder during rapid traverse.

### 2.1.2 Modal vs Non-Modal Codes

- **Modal codes** stay in effect until overridden (e.g., G00, G01, G90, G21, M03, F, S, T).
- **Non-modal codes** act only in the block they appear (e.g., G04 dwell, G92 thread cycle in some controllers).

### 2.1.3 Tool Nose Radius Compensation (TNRC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When programming a **tapered or curved surface**, the **programmed point** (tool tip reference) and the **actual cutting point** differ by the **tool nose radius (r)**. Without compensation (**G41/G42**), tapered/arc dimensions will be off by ≈ **r × (1 − cos θ)** where θ is the taper half-angle.

For the KTU lab exercise, taper turning is usually programmed **without** TNRC and accepted with a small tolerance, but the concept is examined.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Governing Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|-----------|-------------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | **Spindle Speed** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $V_c$ = cutting speed, $D$ = workpiece dia | $N$ in RPM, $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm |
| 2 | **Cutting Speed** | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | Same as above | m/min |
| 3 | **Feed Rate (mm/rev)** | $f_n$ — given per tool/rev | Programmed via **F word** under **G99** | mm/rev |
| 4 | **Feed Rate (mm/min)** | $f_m = f_n \cdot N$ | Time-based feed under **G98** | mm/min |
| 5 | **Material Removal Rate** | $MRR = V_c \cdot f_n \cdot a_p$ | $a_p$ = depth of cut | mm³/min (× 10⁻³ = cm³/min) |
| 6 | **Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ | $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²), $\eta$ = efficiency | kW |
| 7 | **Taper Angle (full)** | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L}$ | $D$ = large dia, $d$ = small dia, $L$ = taper length | degrees |
| 8 | **Taper per mm / TPI** | $TPI = \dfrac{25.4}{P}$ | $P$ = pitch in mm | threads/inch |
| 9 | **Thread Depth (ISO metric)** | $h = 0.6134 \cdot P$ | $P$ = pitch | mm |
| 10 | **Thread Major Diameter** | $D_{maj} = d_{nominal}$ | Nominal thread size | mm |
| 11 | **Thread Minor Diameter** | $D_{min} = D_{maj} - 2h$ | Drilling size for internal | mm |
| 12 | **Thread Root (Pitch) Dia** | $D_p = D_{maj} - 0.6495 \cdot P$ | For thread fit | mm |
| 13 | **Pitch Dia (metric thread)** | $D_p = D_{maj} - 0.6495P$ | Same as above | mm |
| 14 | **Thread Lead = Pitch** (single-start) | $L = P$ | For single-start metric threads | mm |
| 15 | **Ball Radius vs Cup Radius** | $R_{ball} = R_{cup}$ | Same spherical radius | mm |
| 16 | **Ball Chord Height** | $H = R - \sqrt{R^2 - (D/2)^2}$ | Sagitta of spherical segment | mm |
| 17 | **CNC Taper Half-Angle** | $\tan(\theta/2) = \dfrac{X_{end} - X_{start}}{2(Z_{end} - Z_{start})}$ | Slope of X-Z move | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitch vs Lead**: For **single-start** metric threads (M-series), **Lead = Pitch**. For multi-start threads (rare in KTU lab), Lead = Pitch × number of starts.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Operation | Industrial Application |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Plain Turning | Shafts, pins, spacers, journal surfaces |
| Step Turning | Spindles, gear blanks, flanged shafts, bearing seats |
| Taper Turning | Morse tapers (MT2, MT3), pins for machine tool alignment, pipe-thread adapters |
| Thread Cutting | Bolts, studs, lead screws, valve stems, hose nipples |
| Ball/Cup Turning | Ball valves, pivot joints, bearing balls preforms, decorative knobs |

> [!TIP]
> The **Morse Taper** (MT1, MT2, MT3) used in drill chucks and tailstock sleeves is a classic taper-turning exercise — it has standard angles (e.g., MT2 ≈ **1:26 = 1.789°** ≈ 1°29'26").

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spindle Speed from Cutting Velocity

**Given**: Workpiece diameter $D$ (mm), recommended cutting speed $V_c$ (m/min) for the workpiece material and tool.

**Required**: Spindle speed $N$ (RPM) to program in the CNC (S-word).

### Step-by-step Derivation

Step 1 — Write the kinematic definition of cutting speed. The cutting speed is the linear velocity at which the cutting edge engages the workpiece surface:

$$
V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot \frac{1}{1000}
$$

(divide by 1000 to convert mm/min → m/min).

Step 2 — Solve algebraically for $N$:

$$
N = \dfrac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

Step 3 — Worked numerical example for KTU lab (Mild Steel workpiece Ø30 mm, $V_c$ = 120 m/min for HSS insert):

$$
N = \dfrac{120 \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot 30}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{120000}{94.248}
$$

$$
N = 1273.24 \;\;\text{RPM}
$$

Step 4 — Round to a safe programmed value on the next decade (most controllers accept integer S-values):

$$
N_{\text{programmed}} = 1200 \;\;\text{RPM}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU lab tolerance: a 5–10% deviation in RPM is acceptable; over-RPMing risks insert chipping; under-RPMing causes built-up edge.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ISO Metric Thread Cutting Parameters

**Given**: Nominal thread **M16 × 1.5** (major diameter 16 mm, pitch 1.5 mm).

**Required**: Thread depth $h$, pitch diameter $D_p$, minor diameter $D_{min}$, drill size for tapping (internal reference), threading infeed per pass.

### Step-by-step Derivation

Step 1 — Thread depth (theoretical sharp V):

$$
h = 0.6134 \cdot P
$$

$$
h = 0.6134 \cdot 1.5 = 0.9201 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

Step 2 — Pitch diameter (where fit occurs):

$$
D_p = D_{maj} - 0.6495 \cdot P
$$

$$
D_p = 16 - 0.6495 \cdot 1.5
$$

$$
D_p = 16 - 0.97425 = 15.02575 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

Step 3 — Minor diameter (root of thread, also drill size for internal tapping):

$$
D_{min} = D_{maj} - 2h
$$

$$
D_{min} = 16 - 2 \cdot 0.9201
$$

$$
D_{min} = 16 - 1.8402 = 14.1598 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

Step 4 — Programmed infeed depth (radial X-direction):
In **G92 single-block threading**, the first cut is typically **0.1–0.3 mm**, with subsequent passes of decreasing depth (flank-angle compensation).
Total radial infeed = thread depth = **0.9201 mm** for sharp-V; for a 60° partial-profile insert (which cuts flat-bottom flanks), total = **0.920 mm**.

Step 5 — Spindle speed for threading (must be **synchronized** with feed = pitch):
For thread cutting, $f_n = P = 1.5$ mm/rev (lead = pitch for single-start). Spindle and Z-feed are coupled by the controller — you **cannot override F** during the threading block.

Step 6 — Thread length programming:
For a 30 mm long thread on a Ø16 shaft starting at Z = 0 and ending at Z = -30, add a **run-in** and **run-out** of about 3–5 mm to allow tool acceleration/deceleration (so programmed length ≈ **35–38 mm**).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Taper Turning by Direct Linear Interpolation (G01)

**Given**: Taper from Ø40 mm at Z = 0 to Ø30 mm at Z = -50 mm (length 50 mm). Taper angle = ?

### Step-by-step Derivation

Step 1 — Taper ratio:

$$
\text{Taper} = \dfrac{D - d}{L} = \dfrac{40 - 30}{50} = \dfrac{10}{50} = 1:5
$$

Step 2 — Full included angle $\alpha$:

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{D - d}{L} = \dfrac{10}{50} = 0.2
$$

$$
\alpha = \arctan(0.2) = 11.3099° \approx 11°18'35"
$$

Step 3 — Half-angle (slope of one flank):

$$
\alpha/2 = 5.655°
$$

Step 4 — CNC programming (using direct G01 with simultaneous X-Z motion):

Assume tool is at **X40.0 Z2.0** (safe start), and the start of taper is at **Z=0** with **Ø40**.

```
N60  G00 X42.0 Z2.0           ; Rapid to safe clearance
N70  G00 X40.0 Z0.5           ; Rapid to just above start
N80  G01 X30.0 Z-50.0 F0.15   ; Linear interpolation from Ø40,Z0.5 to Ø30,Z-50
```

Step 5 — Verify slope:
The X-difference is **30 − 40 = −10 mm** (radial direction).
The Z-difference is **−50 − 0.5 = −50.5 mm** (longitudinal).
Slope = $\Delta X / \Delta Z = (-10)/(-50.5) = 0.198$ ≈ **0.2** → matches the 1:5 taper. ✅

> [!TIP]
> For **Morse taper MT2** (1:26 ≈ 0.03846 taper ratio), the X-difference for a 50 mm length would only be ≈ 1.92 mm — very shallow. Always use a sharp-pointed threading/turning tool with minimal nose radius for tapers.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ball / Cup Spherical Geometry

**Given**: Produce a **hemispherical ball (cup)** of radius $R$ = 15 mm at the end of a Ø30 mm shaft.

### Step-by-step Derivation

Step 1 — Ball/Cup geometry:
- A **ball** at the end of a shaft requires external spherical turning from Ø30 down to a point.
- A **cup** requires internal spherical turning (or a matching external ball pressed in).

Step 2 — Calculate the sagitta (chord height $H$) of a sphere of radius $R$ with chord (stem) diameter equal to the shaft $D = 30$ mm:

$$
H = R - \sqrt{R^2 - \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^2}
$$

$$
H = 15 - \sqrt{15^2 - 15^2} = 15 - \sqrt{0} = 15 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

Wait — this means the stem diameter **equals** the sphere diameter (since the ball radius = half of 30 = 15 mm). In this case, the ball is a full hemisphere built ON TOP of the existing shaft diameter, requiring turning **from Ø30 down to Ø0** over a Z-distance of 15 mm using a circular arc.

Step 3 — Generic case for a ball **larger than the shaft**:
Suppose $R = 20$ mm, shaft $D = 30$ mm. Then:

$$
H = 20 - \sqrt{20^2 - 15^2} = 20 - \sqrt{400 - 225} = 20 - \sqrt{175} = 20 - 13.229 = 6.771 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

The Z-travel needed to sweep the ball profile = **6.771 mm**.

Step 4 — CNC programming (using G02/G03 circular interpolation):

For an **external ball** (protruding outward) on the right end of the workpiece, the arc goes from a **starting point at X30 Z0** (shaft diameter at ball start) to **ending point at X0 Z-H** (apex of ball). With CW arc (looking from +Z toward origin, top view), the path is a **G02** (CCW in tool-tip view) or **G03** depending on the controller convention. **Most FANUC-style controllers use G02 = CW, G03 = CCW when viewed from the +Y axis looking toward origin** — but for lathes (XZ plane, no Y), this maps to G02 = CW around the spindle axis.

```
N70  G01 X30.0 Z0 F0.15         ; Approach start of ball at Ø30, Z0
N80  G02 X0 Z-6.771 R20.0 F0.10 ; CW arc to apex, radius 20
```

(Using **R-format** is simpler for full arcs; **I, K-format** is used for partial arcs not centered on start/end.)

Step 5 — For a **cup (concave ball socket)**, the same arc is reversed (CCW = G03) on the inside:

```
N80  G03 X0 Z-6.771 R20.0 F0.10
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool selection for ball/cup**: Use a **ball-nose insert** (full radius insert) so the spherical surface is true. A sharp-cornered tool leaves a faceted finish.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) and Power Check

**Given**: Turning Ø40 to Ø30 (depth = 5 mm radial), feed = 0.2 mm/rev, $V_c$ = 120 m/min on Mild Steel ($k_c$ ≈ 2000 N/mm²).

### Step-by-step Derivation

Step 1 — Spindle speed at average dia Ø35:

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 120}{\pi \cdot 35} = 1091 \;\;\text{RPM}
$$

Step 2 — MRR:

$$
MRR = V_c \cdot f_n \cdot a_p
$$

$$
MRR = 120 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 5 = 120 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

Step 3 — Power:

$$
P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{120 \cdot 2000}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 0.8} = \dfrac{240000}{48 \cdot 10^6} = 0.005 \;\;\text{kW}
$$

Wait — let me recompute with consistent units. MRR is in cm³/min → convert to mm³/min: $120 \times 1000 = 120000$ mm³/min.

$$
P = \dfrac{120000 \cdot 2000}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 0.8} = \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^8}{4.8 \times 10^7} = 5 \;\;\text{kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical KTU lab CNC lathe has a 5–7.5 kW spindle motor, so 5 kW MRR is **at the limit** — a milder depth (e.g., 2 mm) is safer for student exercises.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNC Lathe Axis Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Console] --> B[CNC Controller Unit]
    B --> C[Servo Drive Z-axis]
    B --> D[Servo Drive X-axis]
    B --> E[Spindle Drive]
    C --> F[Ball Screw Z]
    D --> G[Ball Screw X]
    F --> H[Tool Turret Carriage]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Cutting Tool T0101]
    E --> J[Spindle Motor]
    J --> K[Chuck]
    K --> L[Workpiece ØD]
    L --> M[Tailstock Optional]
    style A fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#ef5350,color:#fff
    style L fill:#aed581,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- The **controller** is the brain; receives G-code, sends pulses.
- **Servo drives** amplify pulses to drive the **ball screws** which linearly move the carriage.
- **Spindle motor** rotates the chuck via a belt or gear train.
- The **tool turret** indexes (rotates) to bring **Tool 1** into position.

## 4.2 Part Program Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Program Start]) --> Init[Initialize G21 G90 G54]
    Init --> SpindleOn[M03 S1200 Spindle ON]
    SpindleOn --> ToolChange[T0101 Tool Change]
    ToolChange --> RapidSafe[G00 Rapid to Safe Position]
    RapidSafe --> Approach[G00 to Approach Point]
    Approach --> Cut{G-code?}
    Cut -->|G01| LinearFeed[Linear Feed at F]
    Cut -->|G02| CWArc[Clockwise Arc]
    Cut -->|G03| CCWArc[Counter-CW Arc]
    Cut -->|G92| ThreadCycle[Threading Cut]
    Cut -->|G76| ThreadMulti[Multi-pass Threading]
    LinearFeed --> CheckDimen{Dimension Reached?}
    CWArc --> CheckDimen
    CCWArc --> CheckDimen
    ThreadCycle --> CheckDimen
    ThreadMulti --> CheckDimen
    CheckDimen -->|No| Cut
    CheckDimen -->|Yes| Retract[G00 Retract]
    Retract --> M05[Spindle Stop]
    M05 --> M30[Program End]
    M30 --> Stop([Rewind to Start])
```

## 4.3 Five Operations on a CNC Lathe — Geometry Comparison

```mermaid

    P1[Bar Ø30] --> P2[Cylinder Ø20 over length L]
    end

    end

    end

    style Step_Turning fill:#c8e6c9
    style Taper_Turning fill:#ffccbc

```

sequenceDiagram
    participant Ctrl as CNC Controller

    Note over Ctrl,WP: G76 P(m)(r)(a) Q(d_min) R(fin)
